entries: This carbonated liquid, a potent distillation of faerie magic, is bright fuchsia and tastes of autumn rain. A Humanoid who drinks a Toadstool Toddy has {@variantrule Immunity|XPHB} to Fire and Cold damage for 1 hour.

item.name: Vilesmog Bomb
entries: This smoky concoction is a favorite of poisoners and assassins. You can use a {@action Magic|XPHB} action to throw this vial up to 30 Feet away from you, at which point it breaks and releases a dark vapor that fills a 15-foot-radius {@variantrule Sphere [Area of Effect]|XPHB|Sphere} centered on it for 2 rounds. A creature that enters the area or starts its turn there loses any {@variantrule Resistance|XPHB} or {@variantrule Immunity|XPHB} to Poison damage and has {@variantrule Vulnerability|XPHB} to Poison damage. This effect lasts for 1 minute.

item.name: Wand of Silence
entries: This wand has 7 charges. While holding it, you can expend 1 charge to cast {@spell Silence|XPHB} from it (save {@dc 15}). The wand regains {@dice 1d6 + 1} expended charges daily at dawn. If you expend the wand’s last charge, roll a {@dice d20}. On a 1, the wand crumbles into ashes and is destroyed.

item.name: Wand of the Poltergeist
entries: This wand is a swirl of dark and white wood, topped with a carved screaming skull. The wand has 7 charges.

With the wand in hand, you can expend 1 or more charges to cast one of the following spells from it, using your spell save DC: {@spell Animate Objects|XPHB} (4 charges), {@spell Major Image|XPHB} (3 charges), or {@spell Shatter|XPHB} (2 charges).

entries.name: Regaining Charges
entries: The wand regains {@dice 1d6 + 1} expended charges daily at dusk. If you expend the wand’s last charge, roll a {@dice d20}. On a 20, the wand explodes into splinters and is destroyed.

item.name: Wraps of Aphaeleon
entries: Aphaeleon is the Arch Seraph of temperance and spiritual balance, a patron to many monks. While wearing these simple cloth fist wraps endowed with his blessing, you can spend 3 Focus Points on your turn to end the {@condition Incapacitated|XPHB}, {@condition Paralyzed|XPHB}, or {@condition Stunned|XPHB} condition on yourself (no action required).

item.name: Zeal
entries: This warhammer glows with an inner light. When the celestial realm nearly fell, a heavenly smith received a vision, to craft a hammer like no other. With the might of Zeal, heaven's armies turned back their foes, restoring the cosmic balance.

Zeal lies lost somewhere in Etharis, waiting to be wielded against the forces of darkness. The celestials don’t realize that the forces of darkness hold an equally powerful weapon, as the fates that inspired Zeal's creation sent a similar vision to an infernal weaponsmith to maintain the balance of power.

entries.name: Hammer of Heaven
entries: This magic warhammer grants a +3 bonus to attack and damage rolls made with it. When you hit with it, the target takes an extra {@damage 2d10} Radiant damage.

entries.name: Champion of Good
entries: While holding Zeal, you have {@variantrule Immunity|XPHB} to the {@condition Charmed|XPHB}, {@condition Frightened|XPHB}, {@condition Paralyzed|XPHB}, and {@condition Stunned|XPHB} conditions.

entries.name: Zealous Pursuit
entries: You can take a {@variantrule Bonus Action|XPHB} to toss the hammer into the air, causing it to hover magically in your space. While the weapon hovers, you can take a {@variantrule Bonus Action|XPHB} to cause it to fly up to 50 Feet to another spot within 50 Feet of you. As part of the same {@variantrule Bonus Action|XPHB}, you can either grasp the weapon if it is within reach, or cause it to attack one creature within 5 Feet of the weapon. The weapon uses your attack roll and adds your ability modifier to the damage rolls. The weapon ceases to hover if you grasp it, if you are more than 50 Feet away from it, or if you die.

entries.name: Spells
entries: While the hammer is on your person, you can cast {@spell Beacon of Hope|XPHB}, {@spell Earthquake|XPHB}, or {@spell Heroism|XPHB} (save {@dc 18}) from the weapon. Once you use Zeal to cast a spell, that spell can’t be cast again from it until the next dawn.

entries.name: Destroying the Hammer
entries: The only thing capable of damaging Zeal is its fiendish twin {@item Ardor|GrimHollowCG24}. A creature wielding {@item Ardor|GrimHollowCG24} can choose to attack Zeal, which has an {@variantrule Armor Class|XPHB} of 21 and 300 {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB}. No other creature or object can damage Zeal.

item.name: Dämglesvir
entries: Few ever saw the gleaming blade Dämglesvir before the Gods’ End, as only the emperor was permitted to draw the sword from its sheath. Now its blade and hilt are black, like a weapon dipped in shadow.

entries.name: Aspirant
entries: At this tier of Attunement, you have the following benefits:

items: You gain a +2 bonus to attack and damage rolls made with Dämglesvir.
Any weapon you wield deals an extra {@damage 1d6} Force damage.

entries: You also gain the following curse:

items: You become pallid and of ill health. You have {@variantrule Disadvantage|XPHB} on Constitution saving throws.

entries.name: Adept
entries: At the Adept tier of Attunement, you have the following additional benefits:

items: A creature that takes damage from the sword can’t regain {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB} until the start of your next turn.

When you take the Attack action, you may declare one of your attacks with the sword as a sweeping strike. Instead of a single attack, make an attack with the sword against each creature of your choice within reach. You can use this sweeping strike a number of times equal to your {@variantrule Proficiency|XPHB|Proficiency Bonus}, and you regain all uses when you finish a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}.

entries: You also gain the following curse:

items: Drawing on the power of the sword takes a physical toll on your body. When you hit a creature with the sword, your {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB|Hit Point} maximum is reduced by 1. This reduction lasts until you finish a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}.

entries.name: Master
entries: While at the Master tier of Attunement, you have the following additional benefits:

items: When you hit a creature with the sword, you can attempt to slay it instantly if it has 100 {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB} or less. The creature makes a {@dc 20} Constitution saving throw. On a failed save it dies, and on a success it takes normal damage from the attack. After using this effect, you can't use it again until you finish a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}.

entries: You also gain the following curse:

items: Your body has atrophied and is slow to heal. After you hit with Dämglesvir, you can’t regain {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB} until the start of your next turn. Instead, any effect that causes you to regain {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB} during that time grants you an equal number of {@variantrule Temporary Hit Points|XPHB} instead.

item.name: Hearthfire Breastplate
entries: The Hearthfire Breastplate has been profoundly transformed by its corruption. Once it was emblazoned with a golden sun and radiated light. Now its surface is black and distorted, wriggling as if the metal were alive.

entries.name: Aspirant
entries: At this tier of Attunement, you have the following benefits while wearing the Hearthfire Breastplate:

items: You can cast the {@spell Shield|XPHB} spell a number of times equal to your {@variantrule Proficiency|XPHB|Proficiency Bonus}. You regain all expended uses when you finish a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}.
You gain a +2 bonus to {@variantrule Armor Class|XPHB} and saving throws.

entries: You also gain the following curse:

items: Though the breastplate isn’t heavy, it feels burdensome, even when you aren't wearing it. You have {@variantrule Disadvantage|XPHB} on Dexterity saving throws.

entries.name: Adept
entries: At the Adept tier of Attunement, you have the following additional benefits while wearing the Hearthfire Breastplate:

items: Take a {@action Magic|XPHB} action to choose up to six creatures you can see within 30 Feet of you. Each creature gains {@dice 1d4 + 5} {@variantrule Temporary Hit Points|XPHB}. You can use this property a number of times equal to your {@variantrule Proficiency|XPHB|Proficiency Bonus}, regaining all expended uses when you finish a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}.
Creatures other than you have a +2 bonus to {@variantrule Armor Class|XPHB} while they have {@variantrule Temporary Hit Points|XPHB} provided by the breastplate.

entries: You also gain the following curse:

items: You feel the pain of the companions you try to protect. When an ally within 60 Feet takes damage that reduces their {@variantrule Temporary Hit Points|XPHB}, you take Psychic damage equal to half the number of {@variantrule Temporary Hit Points|XPHB} lost. This damage can’t be reduced or avoided in any way.

entries.name: Master
entries: While at the Master tier of Attunement, you have the following additional benefit while wearing the Hearthfire Breastplate:

items: When a creature you can see within 30 Feet of you takes damage, you can take a {@variantrule Reaction|XPHB} to ward that creature. It has {@variantrule Immunity|XPHB} to all damage until the start of your next turn, including against the triggering damage. You can use this property once, regaining the ability to do so after you finish a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}.

entries: You also gain the following curse:

items: Seeing your companions fall fills you with dreadful guilt. When an ally that you can see drops to 0 {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB}, you have the {@condition Frightened|XPHB} condition for 1 minute. If the creature dies, you have the {@condition Paralyzed|XPHB} condition instead. At the end of each of your turns, you can make a {@dc 16} Wisdom saving throw, ending the condition on a success.

item.name: Indorius's Crown
entries: Indorius's Crown bestows authority on whoever wears it, making their reasoning seem stronger and their coercions harder to resist. The crown does not, however, provide its wearer with wisdom.

entries.name: Aspirant
entries: At this tier of Attunement, you have the following benefits while wearing Indorius's Crown:

items: You have {@variantrule Advantage|XPHB} on Charisma ability checks and saving throws.

The crown has 12 charges. You can expend 1 or more charges to cast one of the following spells (save {@dc 18}) from it: {@spell Compulsion|XPHB} (4 charges), {@spell Dominate Person|XPHB} (5 charges), {@spell Hellish Rebuke|XPHB} (1 charge), {@spell Hold Person|XPHB} (2 charges), or {@spell Suggestion|XPHB} (2 charges). The crown regains {@dice 2d4 + 4} expended charges at dawn.

entries: You also gain the following curse:

items: Your sense of authority means you tend to pay less attention to the needs of others. While attuned to the crown, you have {@variantrule Disadvantage|XPHB} on Wisdom ({@skill Insight|XPHB}) checks and Wisdom saving throws.

entries.name: Adept
entries: At the Adept tier of Attunement, you have the following additional benefits while wearing Indorius's Crown:

items: You can take a {@variantrule Bonus Action|XPHB} to choose a {@variantrule Friendly [Attitude]|XPHB|Friendly} creature who can see or hear you. That creature must immediately use its {@variantrule Reaction|XPHB} to make a Weapon Attack or {@variantrule Unarmed Strike|XPHB}, or to cast a cantrip or spell of level 2 or lower with a casting time of Action. You can use this property a number of times equal to your {@variantrule Proficiency|XPHB|Proficiency Bonus}, regaining all expended uses when you finish a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}.

{@variantrule Friendly [Attitude]|XPHB|Friendly} creatures within 10 Feet of you who can see or hear you add {@dice 1d4} to attack rolls and saving throws. You do not grant this benefit while you have the {@condition Unconscious|XPHB} condition.

entries: You also gain the following curse:

items: Your judgmental stare intimidates your companions. When an ally within 10 Feet of you misses with an attack roll or fails an ability check, they have the {@condition Frightened|XPHB} condition from you until the end of their next turn, even if they have {@variantrule Immunity|XPHB} to the {@condition Frightened|XPHB} condition.

entries.name: Master
entries: While at the Master tier of Attunement, you have the following additional benefits while wearing Indorius's Crown:

items: After you make a Charisma check, you can replace the number you roll with a 14.

You can cast the following spells (save {@dc 18}) from the crown: {@spell Dominate Monster|XPHB} and {@spell Power Word Stun|XPHB}. Once you have cast one of these spells from the crown, you can’t cast either spell from the crown until you finish a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}.

entries: You also gain the following curse:

items: You feel a sickening terror and {@variantrule Vulnerability|XPHB} when you’re alone. If you can’t see or hear any {@variantrule Friendly [Attitude]|XPHB|Friendly} creatures within 10 Feet of you, you have {@variantrule Disadvantage|XPHB} on attack rolls and can’t concentrate on spells. Creatures conjured or summoned by magic, such as the {@spell Find Familiar|XPHB} spell, don’t count for this effect.

item.name: Ulmyr's Eye
entries: Ulmyr's Eye appears to be a crystal ball filled with ever-shifting smoke. Peering into the orb reveals hazy visions, many of which are disturbing.

entries.name: Aspirant
entries: At this tier of Attunement, you have the following benefits:

items: You know the {@spell Guidance|XPHB} cantrip and can cast it even if you don't have the Spellcasting class feature.

The orb has 12 charges. You can expend 1 or more charges to cast one of the following spells (save {@dc 18}) from it: {@spell Augury|XPHB} (2 charges), {@spell Clairvoyance|XPHB} (3 charges), {@spell Detect Thoughts|XPHB} (2 charges), {@spell Divination|XPHB} (4 charges), or {@spell Scrying|XPHB} (5 charges). The orb regains {@dice 2d4 + 4} expended charges at dawn.

entries: You also gain the following curse:

items: Your fears of the future diminish your attention in the present. While attuned to Ulmyr's Eye, you have {@variantrule Disadvantage|XPHB} on Wisdom ({@skill Perception|XPHB}) and Intelligence ({@skill Investigation|XPHB}) checks.

entries.name: Adept
entries: At the Adept tier of Attunement, you have the following additional benefits:

items: While holding the orb, you can use your {@variantrule Reaction|XPHB} to give {@variantrule Advantage|XPHB} or {@variantrule Disadvantage|XPHB} on a roll when a creature you can see within 60 Feet makes {@variantrule D20 Test|XPHB}. The triggering creature rerolls and uses the new result. You can use this property a number of times equal to your {@variantrule Proficiency|XPHB|Proficiency Bonus}, regaining all expended uses when you finish a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}.
While holding the orb, you can expend 1 or more of its charges to cast one of your prepared spells without expending a spell slot. The number of charges expended is equal to the spell's level.

entries: You also gain the following curse:

items: Ulmyr's Eye fills your dreams and meditations with nightmarish visions, making it difficult for you to rest. When you finish a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}, you must succeed a {@dc 14} Wisdom saving throw or gain only the benefits of a {@variantrule Short Rest|XPHB} instead.

entries.name: Master
entries: While at the Master tier of Attunement, you have the following additional benefit:

items: While holding the orb, you can cast the {@spell Foresight|XPHB} spell from it, requiring no Material component. When you cast the spell in this way, its duration is reduced to 1 hour. Once you have done so, you can’t cast the spell from the orb again until you finish a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}.

entries: You also gain the following curse:

items: You are so filled with dread that you suffer frightful visions difficult to discern from reality. When you roll {@variantrule Initiative|XPHB}, the orb casts {@spell Phantasmal Killer|XPHB} on you (save {@dc 14}). This effect can also be triggered by moments of extreme emotional or physical distress, at the GM's discretion.

entries: Made from the claws, teeth, and bones of lycanthropes, these weapons are particularly effective against other lycanthropes or vampires. You gain a +2 bonus to attack and damage rolls made with this magic weapon.

Additionally, on a successful attack against a lycanthrope, the target is forced back into its humanoid form unless it succeeds on a {@dc 10} Charisma saving throw. Also, damage done to vampires with Lycan Weapons cannot be regenerated until the creature finishes a {@variantrule Short Rest|XPHB}.

These serpent tooth-tipped crossbow bolts cause a target’s muscles to become rigid. When a creature takes damage from this bolt, it has the {@condition Paralyzed|XPHB} condition until the end of your next turn unless they succeed on a {@dc 15} Constitution saving throw. Once it hits a target, the ammunition is no longer magical.

Crafted from the dark material known as Shadowsteel, these weapons seem to ignore most armor, instead slicing through the spiritual essence of living flesh while delivering excruciating pain to their victims. Survivors of an encounter with a Shadowsteel Blade often report lasting pain that lingers for hours.

Attack rolls made with this magic weapon have {@variantrule Advantage|XPHB} if the target wears Medium or Heavy Armor. A creature hit by a Shadowsteel Blade must succeed on a {@dc 14} Constitution saving throw or have the {@condition Stunned|XPHB} condition until the end of its next turn. Undead creatures automatically succeed on this save.

If you roll a natural 1 on the first attack roll you make with a Shadowsteel Blade during your turn, you must succeed on a {@dc 14} Constitution saving throw or have the {@condition Stunned|XPHB} condition until the end of your next turn.

Dedicated followers of Tormach sometimes gain the blessing of the Lord of Blood Oaths. When they do, their weapons gain a spark of the Marauder’s power. When you hit with this weapon, you can cause the target to succeed on a {@dc 15} Strength saving throw or have the {@condition Prone|XPHB} condition. Once a creature fails this save, you can't use this ability again until the next dawn.
At each dawn, roll {@dice 1d20}. On a 1 or 2 the blessing ends, removing this effect from the weapon.

entries.name: The Four Sacred Artifacts
entries: Four Sacred Artifacts appeared at the dawn of the Bürach Empire, each a divine gift from the four gods: Aurelia, Galt, Maligant, and Ulmyr. These relics were given to Bürach’s first emperor, Indorius I, and inherited by each of his successors.

Maligant provided the mighty sword, Dämglesvir, to slay Bürach’s enemies. Galt placed a crown on Indorius I’s head to signify his divine right. Ulmyr gifted the emperor an orb of foresight to make him a wise ruler. Aurelia clad him in a breastplate, wishing only to protect him and his heirs.

Many centuries later, after Emperor Leopold I was assassinated, custody of the artifacts sparked a civil war. Each of the holy treasures disappeared amid the fighting. One religiously fervent tale claims they blazed with radiant light before banishing themselves beyond mortal reach. Other rumors suggest they were each stolen or lost in various separate conspiracies. It’s certain that whoever holds the Sacred Artifacts controls the fate of the Bürach Empire.

entries.name: Wielding An Artifact
entries: Before disappearing, the artifacts were tarnished and corrupted by Emperor Leopold I’s delusions, cruelty, and malice. Darkness had seeped into them, transforming their divine power and purpose. Though it’s heretical to suggest, some believe the artifacts’ befoulment is somehow linked to the death of the gods and the growing influence of the Great Beast.

Wielding even one Sacred Artifact is difficult. Their true power can only be unlocked by practicing their use. Yet this necessitates more time exposed to the darkness that now infuses them, which can infest the wielder’s body and mind.

entries.name: Attunement and Proficiency
entries: Each Sacred Artifact has three tiers of Attunement available to its user. When a character attunes to the item, they have access only to the benefits (and the curse) listed under the Aspirant tier. The Adept and Master tiers of {@variantrule Attunement|XPHB} are unlocked over time (see Attunement Milestones, below).
Attunement to a Sacred Artifact can't be voluntarily ended. If the Attunement does end, the character can attune to the item again at the Aspirant tier.

Each artifact also curses its wielder. In addition to the curse effects listed for each tier, a character attuned to a Sacred Artifact is unwilling to part with the item, keeping it within reach at all times. If the artifact's curse is ended or suppressed, the character's Attunement with that item is broken.

entries.name: Attunement Milestones
entries: To ascend to a higher tier of Attunement and unlock additional powers of a Sacred Artifact, the attuned character must achieve a specific milestone. There is no minimum level required to achieve a milestone. However, Adept-tier artifacts are appropriate for characters of level 5 to 10, while Master-tier artifacts are appropriate for characters of level 11 and above.
The GM and players should work together to create appropriate milestones for the campaign, using these examples (themed to the four artifacts) as guidance:

items: Wield {@i Dämglesvir} to slay a mighty foe.
Take no damage during a difficult combat.
Earn the fealty of an important ruler.
You alter a future witnessed in {@i Ulmyr's Eye}.

entries.name: Shared Delusions
entries: The darkness that seeped into the Sacred Artifacts causes their wielders to experience the same paranoid delusions that plagued Emperor Leopold I.

All characters attuned to a Sacred Artifact suffer the same delusions, which could include the sense of being watched, believing a {@variantrule Friendly [Attitude]|XPHB|Friendly} NPC to be {@variantrule Hostile [Attitude]|XPHB|Hostile} , or mistaking an innocent person or household pet for a monster. Characters can even experience multiple simultaneous delusions.
A character suffering a delusion can use the Search action to discern the truth, typically by succeeding on a {@dc 20} (or higher) Wisdom ({@skill Insight|XPHB} or {@skill Perception|XPHB}) check.

If the GM is using the {@variantrule Influence Dice|GrimHollowCG24|Influence Dice rules} rules, the frequency and intensity of delusions increases while the number of dice in the Beast Pool exceeds the number of dice in the Resolve Pool.

entries.name: Might of Heaven
entries: Dämglesvir was one of the many weapons wielded by Maligant himself during the Aetheric War. One of the god’s most powerful armaments, its name means {@i “might of heaven.”}

Dämglesvir was brought to Emperor Indorius I by a celestial honor guard. The weapon’s siblings are wielded by Maligant’s former lieutenants: the Arch Seraph Empyreus wields Judgement, while the Arch Daemon Tormach brandishes Wrath.

entries.name: The Four Sacred Artifacts
entries: Four Sacred Artifacts appeared at the dawn of the Bürach Empire, each a divine gift from the four gods: Aurelia, Galt, Maligant, and Ulmyr. These relics were given to Bürach’s first emperor, Indorius I, and inherited by each of his successors.

entries.name: Goddess’s Protection
entries: After the Aetheric War, Aurelia was desperate to protect what remained of mortal life. Yet the goddess was heartsick from the slaughter and did not wish to create another weapon. Instead, she ensured that no danger to the emperor’s life could ever threaten the stability of the realm.
The Hearthfire Breastplate sheathed the emperor in golden light, making their presence a beacon of hope and safety in the Bürach Empire.

entries.name: The Four Sacred Artifacts
entries: Four Sacred Artifacts appeared at the dawn of the Bürach Empire, each a divine gift from the four gods: Aurelia, Galt, Maligant, and Ulmyr. These relics were given to Bürach’s first emperor, Indorius I, and inherited by each of his successors.

Maligant provided the mighty sword, Dämglesvir, to slay Bürach’s enemies. Galt placed a crown on Indorius I’s head to signify his divine right. Ulmyr gifted the emperor an orb of foresight to make him a wise ruler. Aurelia clad him in a breastplate, wishing only to protect him and his heirs.

Many centuries later, after Emperor Leopold I was assassinated, custody of the artifacts sparked a civil war. Each of the holy treasures disappeared amid the fighting. One religiously fervent tale claims they blazed with radiant light before banishing themselves beyond mortal reach. Other rumors suggest they were each stolen or lost in various separate conspiracies. It’s certain that whoever holds the Sacred Artifacts controls the fate of the Bürach Empire.

entries.name: Wielding An Artifact
entries: Before disappearing, the artifacts were tarnished and corrupted by Emperor Leopold I’s delusions, cruelty, and malice. Darkness had seeped into them, transforming their divine power and purpose. Though it’s heretical to suggest, some believe the artifacts’ befoulment is somehow linked to the death of the gods and the growing influence of the Great Beast.

Wielding even one Sacred Artifact is difficult. Their true power can only be unlocked by practicing their use. Yet this necessitates more time exposed to the darkness that now infuses them, which can infest the wielder’s body and mind.

entries.name: Attunement and Proficiency
entries: Each Sacred Artifact has three tiers of Attunement available to its user. When a character attunes to the item, they have access only to the benefits (and the curse) listed under the Aspirant tier. The Adept and Master tiers of {@variantrule Attunement|XPHB} are unlocked over time (see Attunement Milestones, below).
Attunement to a Sacred Artifact can't be voluntarily ended. If the Attunement does end, the character can attune to the item again at the Aspirant tier.

Each artifact also curses its wielder. In addition to the curse effects listed for each tier, a character attuned to a Sacred Artifact is unwilling to part with the item, keeping it within reach at all times. If the artifact's curse is ended or suppressed, the character's Attunement with that item is broken.

entries.name: Attunement Milestones
entries: To ascend to a higher tier of Attunement and unlock additional powers of a Sacred Artifact, the attuned character must achieve a specific milestone. There is no minimum level required to achieve a milestone. However, Adept-tier artifacts are appropriate for characters of level 5 to 10, while Master-tier artifacts are appropriate for characters of level 11 and above.
The GM and players should work together to create appropriate milestones for the campaign, using these examples (themed to the four artifacts) as guidance:

items: Wield {@i Dämglesvir} to slay a mighty foe.
Take no damage during a difficult combat.
Earn the fealty of an important ruler.
You alter a future witnessed in {@i Ulmyr's Eye}.

entries.name: Shared Delusions
entries: The darkness that seeped into the Sacred Artifacts causes their wielders to experience the same paranoid delusions that plagued Emperor Leopold I.

All characters attuned to a Sacred Artifact suffer the same delusions, which could include the sense of being watched, believing a {@variantrule Friendly [Attitude]|XPHB|Friendly} NPC to be {@variantrule Hostile [Attitude]|XPHB|Hostile} , or mistaking an innocent person or household pet for a monster. Characters can even experience multiple simultaneous delusions.
A character suffering a delusion can use the Search action to discern the truth, typically by succeeding on a {@dc 20} (or higher) Wisdom ({@skill Insight|XPHB} or {@skill Perception|XPHB}) check.

If the GM is using the {@variantrule Influence Dice|GrimHollowCG24|Influence Dice rules} rules, the frequency and intensity of delusions increases while the number of dice in the Beast Pool exceeds the number of dice in the Resolve Pool.

entries.name: Divine Authority
entries: Galt imagined the Bürach Empire as a glorious society of fairness and order. To ensure that it operated effectively, it would need a unifying voice that spoke with divine authority.
Indorius's Crown is named for the emperor who wore it first, and each of Indorius’s heirs have felt the weight of authority upon their own head.

entries.name: The Four Sacred Artifacts
entries: Four Sacred Artifacts appeared at the dawn of the Bürach Empire, each a divine gift from the four gods: Aurelia, Galt, Maligant, and Ulmyr. These relics were given to Bürach’s first emperor, Indorius I, and inherited by each of his successors.

Maligant provided the mighty sword, Dämglesvir, to slay Bürach’s enemies. Galt placed a crown on Indorius I’s head to signify his divine right. Ulmyr gifted the emperor an orb of foresight to make him a wise ruler. Aurelia clad him in a breastplate, wishing only to protect him and his heirs.

Many centuries later, after Emperor Leopold I was assassinated, custody of the artifacts sparked a civil war. Each of the holy treasures disappeared amid the fighting. One religiously fervent tale claims they blazed with radiant light before banishing themselves beyond mortal reach. Other rumors suggest they were each stolen or lost in various separate conspiracies. It’s certain that whoever holds the Sacred Artifacts controls the fate of the Bürach Empire.

When a character attunes to the item, they have access only to the benefits (and the curse) listed under the Aspirant tier. The Adept and Master tiers of {@variantrule Attunement|XPHB} are unlocked over time (see Attunement Milestones, below).

Each artifact also curses its wielder. In addition to the curse effects listed for each tier, a character attuned to a Sacred Artifact is unwilling to part with the item, keeping it within reach at all times. If the artifact's curse is ended or suppressed, the character's Attunement with that item is broken.

There is no minimum level required to achieve a milestone. However, Adept-tier artifacts are appropriate for characters of level 5 to 10, while Master-tier artifacts are appropriate for characters of level 11 and above.
The GM and players should work together to create appropriate milestones for the campaign, using these examples (themed to the four artifacts) as guidance:

items: Wield {@i Dämglesvir} to slay a mighty foe.
Take no damage during a difficult combat.
Earn the fealty of an important ruler.
You alter a future witnessed in {@i Ulmyr's Eye}.

entries.name: Shared Delusions
entries: The darkness that seeped into the Sacred Artifacts causes their wielders to experience the same paranoid delusions that plagued Emperor Leopold I.

All characters attuned to a Sacred Artifact suffer the same delusions, which could include the sense of being watched, believing a {@variantrule Friendly [Attitude]|XPHB|Friendly} NPC to be {@variantrule Hostile [Attitude]|XPHB|Hostile} , or mistaking an innocent person or household pet for a monster. Characters can even experience multiple simultaneous delusions.
A character suffering a delusion can use the Search action to discern the truth, typically by succeeding on a {@dc 20} (or higher) Wisdom ({@skill Insight|XPHB} or {@skill Perception|XPHB}) check.

If the GM is using the {@variantrule Influence Dice|GrimHollowCG24|Influence Dice rules} rules, the frequency and intensity of delusions increases while the number of dice in the Beast Pool exceeds the number of dice in the Resolve Pool.

entries.name: Blessed Wisdom
entries: Religious texts claim Ulmyr plucked one of their own eyes as a gift for the first emperor. With it, Emperor Indorius I and his descendants would act with a measure of Ulmyr’s foresight and wisdom.

The eye was kept inside the Sovereign’s Orb, a golden reliquary inlaid with jewels mined from each of Bürach’s provinces. These would remind the emperor of their duty to each of the realms they ruled.

variantrule.name: Alternative Rest System
entries: Rests are crucial for characters to regain their resources, including {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB}, class features, and spell slots. For players seeking a grittier or more challenging campaign experience, this alternative rest system replaces the rest mechanics found in the core fifth edition rules. Any references to Short or Long Rests elsewhere in the core rules now refer to these rules.

entries.name: Quick Rest
entries: A Quick Rest is a 1-hour period of relaxation, where a creature does nothing more strenuous than reading, talking, eating, or standing watch. A Quick Rest can occur anywhere. To start a Quick Rest, you must have at least 1 {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB|Hit Point}.

entries.name: Benefits of a Quick Rest
entries: When you finish the Quick Rest, you gain the following benefits:

entries.name: Spend {@variantrule Hit Point Dice|XPHB}
entries: You can spend one or more of your {@variantrule Hit Point Dice|XPHB} to regain {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB}. For each {@variantrule Hit Point Dice|XPHB} spent in this way, roll the {@variantrule Hit Point Dice|XPHB} and add your Constitution modifier to it. You regain {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB} equal to the total (minimum of 1 Hit Point). You can decide to spend an additional {@variantrule Hit Point Dice|XPHB} after each roll.

entries.name: Interrupting a Quick Rest
entries: A Quick Rest is stopped by the following interruptions:

items: Rolling {@variantrule Initiative|XPHB}
Casting a spell other than a cantrip
Taking any damage

entries: An interrupted Quick Rest confers no benefits and must be restarted to gain any benefits.

entries.name: Short Rest
entries: A {@variantrule Short Rest|XPHB} is an extended period of rest—at least 8 hours— available to any creature. During a {@variantrule Short Rest|XPHB}, you sleep for at least 6 hours and perform no more than 2 hours of light activity, such as reading, talking, eating, or standing watch.

A {@variantrule Short Rest|XPHB} can occur in any location where you can sleep without interruption. This might take place in a dangerous location that has been temporarily secured and guarded. During sleep, you have the {@condition Unconscious|XPHB} condition. After you finish a {@variantrule Short Rest|XPHB}, you must wait at least 8 hours before starting another one or starting a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}.

entries.name: Benefits of a {@variantrule Short Rest|XPHB}
entries: To start a {@variantrule Short Rest|XPHB}, you must have at least 1 {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB|Hit Point}. When you finish the rest, you gain the following benefits:

entries.name: Regain Half {@variantrule Hit Point Dice|XPHB}
entries: You regain a number of spent {@variantrule Hit Point Dice|XPHB} equal to half of your total number of {@variantrule Hit Point Dice|XPHB} (minimum of 1). For example, if you have eight {@variantrule Hit Point Dice|XPHB}, you regain up to four spent {@variantrule Hit Point Dice|XPHB}.

entries.name: Spend {@variantrule Hit Point Dice|XPHB}
entries: After you regain {@variantrule Hit Point Dice|XPHB}, you can spend one or more {@variantrule Hit Point Dice|XPHB} to regain {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB}. For each {@variantrule Hit Point Dice|XPHB} you spend in this way, roll the die and add your Constitution modifier to it. You regain {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB} equal to the total (minimum of 1 Hit Point). You can decide to spend an additional {@variantrule Hit Point Dice|XPHB} after each roll.

entries.name: Exhaustion Reduced
entries: If you have the {@condition Exhaustion|XPHB} condition, its level decreases by 1.

entries.name: Special Feature
entries: Some features are recharged by a {@variantrule Short Rest|XPHB}. If you have such a feature, it recharges in the way specified in its description.

entries.name: Interrupting a {@variantrule Short Rest|XPHB}
entries: A {@variantrule Short Rest|XPHB} is stopped by the following interruptions:

items: Rolling {@variantrule Initiative|XPHB}
Casting a spell other than a cantrip
Taking any damage

entries: An interrupted {@variantrule Short Rest|XPHB} confers no benefits. The interrupted {@variantrule Short Rest|XPHB} must be restarted from the beginning to gain its benefits. If the interrupted {@variantrule Short Rest|XPHB} goes for at least 1 hour, the party can treat it as a Quick Rest instead, but they cannot take another Quick Rest as part of this interrupted {@variantrule Short Rest|XPHB}.

entries.name: Long Rest
entries: A {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB} is a period of extended downtime—at least 32 hours—available to any creature. During a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}, you sleep for at least two 6-hour periods separated by 16 hours of light activity, such as reading, talking, eating, crafting, trading, or performing downtime activities.

A {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB} can only occur in safe locations that provide shelter, warmth, comfort, and resources like food and water. This could be a settlement, homestead, stronghold, inn, castle, or large city. A {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB} cannot be completed in areas of discomfort or danger, such as a wilderness, dungeon, alley, or sewer. If the location is dangerous enough that characters feel the need to post watch, it cannot accommodate a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}.

During sleep, you have the {@condition Unconscious|XPHB} condition. After you finish a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}, you must wait at least 16 hours before starting another one or a {@variantrule Short Rest|XPHB}.

entries.name: Benefits of a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}
entries: To start a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}, you must have at least 1 {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB|Hit Point}. When you finish the rest, you gain the following benefits:

entries.name: Regain All HP
entries: You regain all lost {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB} and all spent {@variantrule Hit Point Dice|XPHB}. If your {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB|Hit Point} maximum was reduced, it returns to normal.

entries.name: Ability Scores Restored
entries: If any of your ability scores were reduced, they return to normal.

entries.name: Exhaustion Recovered
entries: If you have the {@condition Exhaustion|XPHB} condition, its level decreases to 0.

entries.name: Grievous Wounds Recovered
entries: If you have one or more Grievous Wounds, you or another creature can attempt to treat them. See Grievous Wounds, below, for details.

entries.name: Special Feature
entries: Some features are recharged by a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}. If you have such a feature, it recharges in the way specified in its description.

entries.name: Interrupting the {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}
entries: A {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB} is stopped by the following interruptions:

items: Rolling {@variantrule Initiative|XPHB}
Casting a spell other than a cantrip
Taking any damage
Leaving the location of relative safety

entries: If you rested at least 8 hours, including at least 6 hours of sleep, before the interruption, you gain the benefits of a {@variantrule Short Rest|XPHB}. You can only take 1 {@variantrule Short Rest|XPHB} during an attempt of a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}.

entries.name: The Point of Alternative Resting
entries: Intentions of rules are just as important to understand as the rules themselves. The intention of the alternative resting rules is to challenge characters, not to destroy them. The main reason some campaigns seem too easy or not challenging enough is the abundance of resources that characters have, and those resources are regained through {@variantrule Short Rest|XPHB|Short}, or {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB|Long Rests}.

This alternative resting system is meant to ensure that characters can regain the {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB} necessary to take part in encounters without always having the most powerful of their abilities to use every time.

Even more important than survivability with this new resting variant is story-driven decision making. In order to take a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}, characters must not just rest longer, they must rest in a place of safety. This can mean making a hard choice: should we push further into the monster’s lair or the {@variantrule Hostile [Attitude]|XPHB|Hostile} territory, or should we retreat to a place of safety?

It also places an emphasis on the characters making it a priority to find, cultivate, or maybe even create a place of safety. That threatened village may not mean much to the characters in terms of the connections they’ve made, but it means much more when it might be the only place they are able to rest in the area. You’ll find that the people and places of Etharis become much more important to the players when their valuable adventuring resources hinge on having a safe place to sleep.

variantrule.name: Grievous Wounds
entries: A consistent theme throughout dark fantasy and related genres is the fragility of life and the consequences of actions. While the roleplaying ramifications of a party’s decision can be highlighted through story outcomes, combat ramifications can be a little harder to implement.
Grievous Wounds add grit and realism to combat encounters, bringing the consequences of a difficult fight to the forefront of storytelling.

entries.name: Implementing Grievous Wounds
entries: Grievous Wounds come into play whenever a character is reduced to 0 {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB}. When a character is reduced to 0 {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB}, they roll 2d10 and add them together. Then they subtract from the result the number of existing {@table Grievous Wounds|GrimHollowCG24} they have.

With that result, consult the Grievous Wound table. If a player rolls the same result as one they already have a wound for, they skip this result and select the next lowest result they do not have as a wound. Results with a {@sup *} next to them can be taken more than once.
For example, if the modified result is a 6, and the character already has the Grievous Wound listed as result 6, they gain the Grievous Wound listed as result 5.

Upon completing a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}, if the character or someone helping them succeeds at a Wisdom (Medicine) check, a player can remove all effects from Grievous Wounds, but not Permanent Wounds. The DC for the Ability Check equals 10 plus the number of Grievous Wounds the character has. A character with a Healer’s Kit automatically succeeds the check by expending one use of a Healer’s Kit.

rows.name: Permanent Wound
entries: You sustain a grisly wound that may haunt you forever.. Roll 1d10 on the {@table Permanent Wounds|GrimHollowCG24} table and apply the result.

rows.name: Festering Wound
entries: Your injury becomes infected, causing a fever. You have the {@condition Poisoned|XPHB} condition. While {@condition Poisoned|XPHB}, you have {@variantrule Disadvantage|XPHB} on Constitution saving throws. A creature cannot ignore this instance of the {@condition Poisoned|XPHB} condition through {@variantrule Immunity|XPHB}.

rows.name: Internal Bleeding{@sup *}
entries: You suffer an internal hemorrhage and bruising. Your {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB|Hit Point} maximum is reduced by three times your character level.

rows.name: Wracked With Pain
entries: Your injury causes intense pain that limits your agility. You have {@variantrule Disadvantage|XPHB} on Dexterity saving throws.

rows.name: Concussion
entries: A brain contusion leaves you struggling to focus. You gain the {@condition Dazed|GrimHollowCG24} condition.

rows.name: Laceration
entries: Blood wells unabaited from a deep injury. You gain the {@condition Bleeding|GrimHollowCG24} condition.

rows.name: Deep Wound{@sup *}
entries: You endure a blow that would kill many, but merely staggers you. When you are reduced to 0 {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB}, you start with one failed {@variantrule Death Saving Throw|XPHB}.

rows.name: Battered{@sup *}
entries: Your muscles ache and you have had the wind knocked out of you. You gain 1 {@condition Exhaustion|XPHB} level.

rows.name: Injured Wrist{@sup *}
entries: You have badly sprained your wrist or fractured a bone in your arm and are unable to use it effectively. You are unable to hold objects in one of your hands and you cannot take actions that require that arm.

rows.name: Damaged Eardrum
entries: A blow to your head impairs your hearing. You have the {@condition Deafened|XPHB} condition.

rows.name: System Shock
entries: You suffer a severe nervous shock. You have {@variantrule Disadvantage|XPHB} on {@variantrule Initiative|XPHB} rolls.

rows.name: Injured Leg{@sup *}
entries: An injury to your leg or foot slows your movement. Reduce your {@variantrule Speed|XPHB} by 10 Feet, to a minimum of 5 Feet. Your {@variantrule Speed|XPHB} can't be increased, and you can't take the {@action Dash|XPHB} action.

rows.name: Torn Muscle{@sup *}
entries: This injury reduces your combat prowess. When you deal damage with a Weapon Attack or an {@variantrule Unarmed Strike|XPHB}, roll {@dice 1d4;1d6;1d8;1d10;1d12|1d4} and subtract the result from the damage dealt. Each additional time you receive this wound, increase the size of the die rolled (to 1d6, 1d8, 1d10, and 1d12). After reaching the maximum die size listed, replace further Torn Muscle results with Injured Leg.

rows.name: Stymied Recovery
entries: Your injury slows your overall recovery. The total number of {@variantrule Hit Point Dice|XPHB} you can spend during a {@variantrule Short Rest|XPHB|Short} or {@variantrule Alternative Rest System|GrimHollowCG24|Quick Rest} is equal to your Constitution modifier (minimum of 1).

rows.name: Severe Swelling
entries: Your swollen injuries give you an uncouth appearance. You have {@variantrule Disadvantage|XPHB} on Charisma ({@skill Performance|XPHB}) and Charisma ({@skill Persuasion|XPHB}) checks.

rows.name: Close Call{@sup *}
entries: You narrowly miss an otherwise dangerous blow. Instead of being reduced to 0 {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB}, you have 1 {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB|Hit Point} and have the {@condition Prone|XPHB} condition.

entries.name: Permanent Wounds
entries: Permanent Wounds are injuries that were not necessarily lethal but left the recipient permanently injured. They represent a serious impediment that requires creativity to overcome, especially at lower levels.

The effects of a Permanent Wound are irreversible unless the recipient is targeted with the Regenerate spell, upon which one Permanent Wound is removed. Some of the effects of Permanent Wounds can be offset with Prosthetics or magical items.

rows.name: Fatal Wound
entries: Through an unrecoverable injury, you have been fatally wounded. You are now dead but can be brought back through normal methods.

rows.name: Lost Arm{@sup *}
entries: A serious injury disables or destroys one of your arms. You can't use that arm to wield a weapon or a {@item Shield|XPHB}, or to perform the Somatic component of a spell.

rows.name: Lost Leg{@sup *}
entries: A serious injury disables or destroys one of your legs. Reduce your {@variantrule Speed|XPHB} by 15 Feet, to a minimum of 5 Feet. Your {@variantrule Speed|XPHB} can't be increased, and you can't take the {@action Dash|XPHB} action. If you receive this result again your {@variantrule Speed|XPHB} is reduced by another 15 Feet (to a minimum of 5 Feet).

rows.name: Lost Eye{@sup *}
entries: You lose an eye. You have {@variantrule Disadvantage|XPHB} on Wisdom ({@skill Perception|XPHB}) and Intelligence ({@skill Investigation|XPHB}) checks that rely on sight. If you receive this result again, you gain the {@condition Blinded|XPHB} condition.

rows.name: Dizzy Spells
entries: Undertaking vigorous activities leaves you addled. On odd numbered combat rounds, if you take an action, you cannot take a {@variantrule Bonus Action|XPHB} until the start of your next turn. If you take a {@variantrule Bonus Action|XPHB}, you cannot take an action until the start of your next turn.

rows.name: Scarred Lungs
entries: Your injury makes it a struggle to breathe. Your {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB} maximum is permanently reduced by 1 for each of your character levels. Additionally, when you gain a level, your {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB|Hit Point} maximum is reduced by 1.

rows.name: Hideous Scar
entries: You are horribly and visibly scarred.You have {@variantrule Disadvantage|XPHB} on Charisma ({@skill Performance|XPHB}) and Charisma ({@skill Persuasion|XPHB}) checks and {@variantrule Advantage|XPHB} on Charisma ({@skill Intimidation|XPHB}) checks.

rows.name: Adrenaline Rush
entries: Your injuries make you vulnerable to further damage, but your survival instincts are heightened in moments of danger. If you are {@status Bloodied|XPHB}, any {@variantrule Critical Hit|XPHB} against you becomes a normal hit. If you are not {@status Bloodied|XPHB}, attack rolls made against you score a {@variantrule Critical Hit|XPHB} on a roll of 19 or 20 on the d20.

rows.name: Hesitant
entries: Your psyche has been scarred more than your body. The first time after a {@variantrule Short Rest|XPHB|Short}, or {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB} that you have to make a saving throw to avoid the {@condition Frightened|XPHB} condition, you are {@condition Frightened|XPHB} even if you succeed on the saving throw.

rows.name: Old Pain
entries: This lingering injury causes you intense pain when it flares up. When you roll a natural 1 on any {@variantrule D20 Test|XPHB}, you have the {@condition Incapacitated|XPHB} condition until the end of your next turn.

variantrule.name: Diseases and Afflictions
entries: Diseases hold an odd position in fantasy worlds. They are meant to be as dangerous as real-world epidemics; however, magical cures and remedies are readily available to characters at lower levels, making them difficult to use as either an interesting game mechanic or a viable storytelling hook or plot device. In fact, 2024 5e rules barely mention diseases as a game element.

Grim Hollow, however, wants diseases to be a relevant— and even crucial—factor in adventures and campaigns set in Etharis. The Weeping Pox is an important piece of lore in Etharis’s history, so to eliminate them completely flies in the face of everything Grim Hollow is about.
As a way of making diseases, contagions, and afflictions a viable tool for GMs to tell stories, use the following rules.

entries.name: Natural Diseases
entries: Various naturally occurring viruses and bacteria regularly rampage through Etharis, causing both isolated cases of a disease as well as more dire epidemics. These diseases, however, can be combated with normal medicine, as well as eradicated by magic. Sometimes the symptoms of even the most contagious and debilitating disease can be managed with lower-level magic, or cured completely with spells such as {@spell Lesser Restoration|XPHB}.

entries.name: Rules for Natural Diseases
entries: For a standard, nonmagical disease or affliction, an exposed creature can make a DC 10 Constitution saving throw. For harsher diseases, the DC could increase as necessary to enforce the deadly nature of the disease.
On a successful save, the character might show very mild symptoms but suffer no other game-mechanically relevant effects. On a failed save, the character gains 1 {@condition Exhaustion|XPHB} level.

After two days have passed, the character can repeat the saving throw. On a failed save, they gain another {@condition Exhaustion|XPHB} level. If they did not complete a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB} over that 2-day stretch, they have {@variantrule Disadvantage|XPHB} on the check. On a success, they have resisted the disease.

This process continues until the character has either resisted the disease by succeeding on a saving throw, been cured via medicine or magic, or die from gaining 6 {@condition Exhaustion|XPHB} levels.

A GM can determine the ways in which {@skill Medicine|XPHB} checks or other healing efforts might succeed. It’s important to let characters with proficiency in skills or tools use those avenues for healing.

entries.name: Magical Diseases and Contagions
entries: The Core Rules for 2024 fifth edition have rules and examples of magical contagions, but the rules here could supplement them to show the particularly deadly place these contagions hold in Etharis.

Magical diseases and contagions have a similar effect and method of resolution to natural diseases. However, there may be affects in addition to {@condition Exhaustion|XPHB} levels. The DC for resisting the magical diseases is 15 instead of 10, and typical magic or rest cannot remove {@condition Exhaustion|XPHB} levels.
The other rules for suffering from and resting the magical diseases and contagions should follow the pattern set in the previous section.

entries.name: Curse Diseases and Afflictions
entries: Even more virulent, pervasive, and deadly are curse diseases and afflictions. These are not just magical in nature, they are harder to shake and have more potentially dire consequences if not healed in a reasonable time. The {@disease Weeping Pox|GrimHollowCG24} is an example of a curse disease that is highly unlikely to be cured easily, even with magic.

Unlike the lesser varieties of diseases and magical contagions, curse diseases and afflictions cannot be cured by {@spell Lesser Restoration|XPHB} or other lower-level spells or powers. Magic on the level of Wish is needed to even have a chance of curing such afflictions, and even then it is not a sure thing.

entries.name: The Weeping Pox
entries: An example of a curse disease in Etharis is the Weeping Pox. A person subjected to the disease must succeed on a {@dc 20} Constitution saving throw once each day they are exposed. If they succeed, they are safe for 24 hours. On a failed save, they have the affliction and begin to show symptoms within 24 hours.

At first a sick person will be subject to fever causing sharpened senses, overexcitement, and a sense of wellbeing. For the first three days, creatures suffering from the Weeping Pox must succeed on a {@dc 20} Constitution saving throw each day. If any of those saving throws fail, the creature reaches critical stages at the end of those 72 hours.

After 72 hours, these original symptoms transition to delirium, burning fever, insatiable thirst, and weltering lesions that have given the illness its name. Beyond this, nearly all who contract the disease die withing the next 72 hours. Most people succumb to the pox within the week, while the most resilient may hold on for almost two weeks.

variantrule.name: Creating Legendary Foes
entries: When creating encounters, it is often situationally appropriate to pit the party against a singular enemy. However, what is meant to be a dangerous and climactic boss fight sometimes ends as a one-way romp, not presenting a challenge for the players. This is largely due to the single creature taking far fewer actions per round of combat than the party. Legendary templates are designed to assist in this process and help make non-legendary creatures present a greater threat.

entries.name: Legendary Templates
entries: To implement a legendary template, select a monster that does not already have Legendary Actions. This monster should be CR 3 or higher, and it should be one that you want to be a difficult challenge for the characters to overcome.

Next, select a template from the options below. Add the Legendary Actions listed to the monster’s stats. You should select a template that fits the existing abilities of the monster, such as the Spellcaster template for a Mage or a Commander for a Hobgoblin Captain.

entries.name: Bruiser
entries: {@i Legendary Action Uses: 3. Immediately after another creature's turn, the bruiser can expend a use to take one of the following actions. The bruiser regains all expended uses at the start of each of its turns.}

entries.name: Charge
entries: The bruiser moves up to half its {@variantrule Speed|XPHB} directly toward an enemy creature. If this move brings it within 5 Feet of that target, it can make a Weapon Attack or an {@variantrule Unarmed Strike|XPHB} against that creature. If this attack hits, the target has the {@condition Prone|XPHB} condition.

entries.name: Fortify
entries: The bruiser gains a +3 bonus to its {@variantrule Armor Class|XPHB|AC} until the end of its next turn. The Bruiser can't take this action again until the start of its next turn.

entries.name: Heal
entries: The bruiser regains {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB} equal to {@dice 1d10 + #$prompt_number:min=1,title=Enter a CR,default=0$#|1d10 + its CR}. The bruiser can't take this action again until the start of its next turn.

entries.name: Commander
entries: {@i Legendary Action Uses: 3 (4 in Lair). Immediately after another creature's turn, the commander can expend a use to take one of the following actions. The commander regains all expended uses at the start of each of its turns.}

entries.name: Maneuver Ally
entries: The commander chooses up to three allies it can see within 30 Feet of it. If a target can see and hear the commander, it can take a {@variantrule Reaction|XPHB} to move up to half its {@variantrule Speed|XPHB} without provoking {@action Opportunity Attack|XPHB|Opportunity Attacks}.

entries.name: Command Ally
entries: The commander chooses one ally it can see within 30 Feet of it. If the target can see and hear the Commander, it can take a {@variantrule Reaction|XPHB} to make one Weapon Attack or {@variantrule Unarmed Strike|XPHB} with {@variantrule Advantage|XPHB} on the attack roll. If the attack hits, the target regains the use of its {@variantrule Reaction|XPHB}.

entries.name: Summon Allies (Costs 3 Actions)
entries: The Commander summons {@dice 1d4+1} {@variantrule Friendly [Attitude]|XPHB|Friendly} creatures of a CR no greater than one quarter the Commander’s CR (rounded down), assuming such creatures would be available at the location. The creatures appear within 30 Feet of the Commander in an unoccupied space. The commander can't take this action again for 1 hour.

entries.name: Practiced Spellcaster
entries: {@i Legendary Action Uses: 3. Immediately after another creature's turn, the practiced spellcaster can expend a use to take one of the following actions. The practiced spellcaster regains all expended uses at the start of each of its turns.}

entries.name: Cast Cantrip
entries: The practiced spellcaster casts a cantrip it knows, or a spell in its stat block that can be cast at will.

entries.name: Teleport
entries: The practiced spellcaster magically teleports up to 120 Feet to an unoccupied space it can see. The practiced spellcaster can't take this action again until the start of its next turn.

entries.name: Cast a Spell (Costs 3 Actions)
entries: The Practiced Spellcaster casts a spell from its list of spells, using a spell slot as normal.

entries.name: Skirmisher
entries: {@i Legendary Action Uses: 3. Immediately after another creature's turn, the skirmisher can expend a use to take one of the following actions. The skirmisher regains all expended uses at the start of each of its turns.}

entries.name: Move
entries: The skirmisher moves up to its {@variantrule Speed|XPHB} without provoking {@action Opportunity Attack|XPHB|Opportunity Attacks}.

entries.name: Weapon Attack
entries: The skirmisher makes a Weapon Attack or an {@variantrule Unarmed Strike|XPHB}.

entries.name: Calculated Strike (Costs 3 Actions)
entries: The Skirmisher makes a Weapon Attack or {@variantrule Unarmed Strike|XPHB} against a target. If the attack hits, it scores a {@variantrule Critical Hit|XPHB}.

variantrule.name: Heroism and Corruption
entries: Though the gods of Etharis are gone, the weave of Fate still governs the lives of its inhabitants. Without oversight, however, that once-indomitable force has become unStable and susceptible to outside influence. The interplay between heroism and corruption plays an important role in the lives of the people and creatures of Etharis. This is handled by Resolve Dice and Beast Dice.

While playing in Grim Hollow, the players and the GM each have a pool of six-sided dice to draw upon in order to shift Fate in their favor. The players’ dice are known as Resolve Dice, while the GM’s pool consists of Beast Dice. The dice in each pool can be spent to activate a number of different abilities, representing the ways in which Fate has been altered for good or for ill.

At the start of a campaign, each pool contains a single d6, but events during the game add or remove dice from the pools. Also, at the end of each session, if either pool has at least 2 fewer dice than the other, the GM should add one d6 to that pool. This represents Fate attempting to correct the imbalance.
The size of each dice pool is known to the GM and players at all times. This allows players to consider the dice pools when plotting their next course of action.

The dice pools are also great tools for storytelling and roleplaying. For example, as dice are added to the Beast Pool, NPCs likely grow wearier or more scared by current events. News may reach the party of a supernatural phenomenon that has locals on edge, or an informant might warn of increased monster attacks in the area. Omens and portents foretell disaster. Monsters can appear more twisted, and even the environment itself might become more disturbing, all to represent the growing threat.

By contrast, a growing Resolve Pool can spur a more positive attitude in the characters. Confidence and good humor should increase while the Resolve Pool outweighs the Beast Pool, as characters grasp a rare and fleeting moment of optimism.

entries.name: Resolve Pool
entries: The characters in a Grim Hollow campaign face a world of overwhelming odds. In order to triumph, they must draw upon deep personal reservoirs of courage and willpower. This is represented by the Resolve Pool, a special pool of six-sided dice that the players can use to thwart Fate and seize victory from the jaws of defeat.

The Resolve Pool starts with a single d6. Events happening within the game can add dice to that pool, up to a maximum of six d6. The GM award Resolve Dice to the party based on their actions and at specific plot moments within the game. If a player accurately and faithfully represents their character’s heroic attributes in difficult circumstances, it’s appropriate for the GM to awards a Resolve Die based on their roleplaying.

Furthermore, events or actions that dramatically bolster the party's determination can also add a Resolve Die to the pool. These rare instances of self-sacrifice, kindness, and charity provide a light in the darkness, helping the party to forge ahead and achieve success.

Finally, if the characters manage to heroically overcome a horrific event that had added a die to the Beast Pool (see below), add a die to the Resolve Pool. Overcoming dire challenges is the surest way to gain courage.

entries.name: Resolve Abilities
entries: Once there are dice in the Resolve Pool, the party can draw upon them to activate special abilities throughout the game. Resolve Abilities can only be activated by spending dice from the Resolve Pool, and each ability has a certain cost as detailed in the description. Feel free to come up with your own list of abilities for the players to use their Resolve Dice on, but certain examples may include the following:

entries.name: Hasty Recovery
entries: The character spends 1 Resolve Die during a {@variantrule Short Rest|XPHB} to remove 1 {@condition Exhaustion|XPHB} level. Once during that rest, the character may roll the Resolve Die and add it to a saving throw they make.

entries.name: Inner Strength
entries: When a character would expend a {@variantrule Hit Point Dice|XPHB}, they may instead spend 1 Resolve Die, rolling and using its result if necessary.

entries.name: Final Push
entries: After a character rolls a {@variantrule Death Saving Throw|XPHB}, they may spend 2 Resolve Dice. Roll both dice and add the result to the triggering Death Save.

entries.name: I’ve Just the Thing
entries: During their turn, a character may spend any number of Resolve Dice. They immediately gain a non-magical item whose GP cost does not exceed 10 times the number of Resolve Dice spent.

entries.name: Blaze of Glory
entries: During their turn, a character can spend 6 Resolve Dice to instill heroic might into up to 6 creatures within 120 Feet. Until the end of that character's next turn, each target has {@variantrule Advantage|XPHB} on D20 Tests and has {@variantrule Resistance|XPHB} to all types of damage. In addition, damage rolls by each affected character are doubled.

entries.name: Beast Pool
entries: The Great Beast is a powerful supernatural entity that stalks the lands of Etharis, and the Beast Pool represents how its influence twists the weave of Fate to oppose the characters' efforts. The Beast Pool begins with a single d6, but certain callous, evil, or horrific actions and events add more dice to represent the growing threat. Like the Resolve Pool, the Beast Pool can hold a maximum of six d6.

Ironically, the characters themselves may be the most significant cause for a growing Beast Pool. Whenever a character takes an action that darkens the tone of the game, the GM may add another d6 to the Beast Pool. Such actions include killing an innocent creature, choosing selfishness or callousness at the expense of others, working to undermine the common good, and so forth.

Horrific events can also add dice to the Beast Pool. When the party encounters a particularly strong evil creature, or when they witness something extremely disturbing, the GM can add a d6 to the Beast Pool. Reserve this for rare moments that truly shake the resolve or threaten the psyches of the characters. Simple fear effects and the {@condition Frightened|XPHB} condition aren't enough to increase the Beast Pool.

entries.name: Spending Beast Dice
entries: The GM can spend Beast Dice to hinder characters or bolster enemies, altering the balance of encounters at critical moments. This option is best used to build tension and raise the stakes of an important encounter, rather than as a blunt tool to simply defeat the party.
Here are some sample effects the GM can use or adapt:

entries.name: Bitter Disposition
entries: The GM may spend one Beast Die in order to decrease the starting disposition of an NPC from Friendly to {@variantrule Indifferent [Attitude]|XPHB|Indifferent}, or from {@variantrule Indifferent [Attitude]|XPHB|Indifferent} to {@variantrule Hostile [Attitude]|XPHB|Hostile}, in order to make the scene more memorable or challenging.

entries.name: Death Rattle
entries: When a character is reduced to 0 {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB}, the GM may spend one Beast Die and roll it. On a 5 or 6, that character immediately suffers a {@variantrule Death Saving Throw|XPHB} failure.

entries.name: Outnumbered
entries: The GM may spend any number of Beast Dice at the start of an encounter to increase the number of creatures facing the party. For each die spent, the GM may add an additional creature with {@variantrule Challenge Rating|XPHB} no greater than half the party’s average level, rounded down.

entries.name: Affliction
entries: Before or during a session, the GM can spend 4 Beast Dice to inflict a serious ailment upon a character or significant NPC important to the party. The target gains a {@book Shadowsteel Curse|GrimHollowPG24|7|Shadowsteel Curses}, a {@variantrule Diseases and Afflictions|GrimHollowCG24|cursed affliction}, or a {@filter Transformation|charcreationoptions|feature type=transformation} of the GM's choice. Before applying a Transformation to a character, check with the player to ensure this is acceptable.

entries.name: The Beast
entries: Before or during a session, the GM may spend 6 Beast Dice to have the Great Beast manifest near the characters' location. The effects of its presence are soon felt throughout the region, perhaps as an onslaught of vicious gnolls or the transformation of a forest into a writhing nest of corrupted plants. Alternatively, this option may be used to represent a different major and lasting threat to the local populace, such as an outbreak of coldfire in the Valikan Clans or the arrival of a Crimson Court member in Ostoya.

entries.name: Etharis Abilities
entries: Apart from Resolve Abilities and Beast Abilities, there are a set of abilities that can be activated with dice drawn from either pool. Both the GM and the party have access to Etharis Abilities, which represent more general methods of shaping the whims of Fate in your favor.

entries.name: Minor Fate
entries: Roll one die and add the total to a single {@variantrule D20 Test|XPHB} after the check has been made but before the result is known.

entries.name: Bolster
entries: Roll two dice and increase the {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB|Hit Point} maximum of a creature by the total for eight hours.

entries.name: Brutal Strike
entries: Whenever a creature is hit by an attack roll, the attacker may spend two dice and inflict a condition on the target for one round. This condition may be {@condition Bleeding|GrimHollowCG24}, {@condition Dazed|GrimHollowCG24}, or {@condition Stunned|XPHB}.

entries.name: Stroke of Luck
entries: By spending two dice, the GM or a player may cause a minor alteration to the environment, such as adding a small item or feature, moving an existing object five Feet, or slightly altering weather conditions or lighting. Examples would be a dropped weapon rolling out of reach or suggesting that phosphorescent fungi might bring a cave’s lighting up to {@variantrule Dim Light|XPHB} rather than total darkness.

disease.name: Weeping Pox
entries: A person subjected to the disease must succeed on a {@dc 20} Constitution saving throw once each day they are exposed. If they succeed, they are safe for 24 hours. On a failed save, they have the affliction and begin to show symptoms within 24 hours.

At first a sick person will be subject to fever causing sharpened senses, overexcitement, and a sense of wellbeing. For the first three days, creatures suffering from the Weeping Pox must succeed on a {@dc 20} Constitution saving throw each day. If any of those saving throws fail, the creature reaches critical stages at the end of those 72 hours.

After 72 hours, these original symptoms transition to delirium, burning fever, insatiable thirst, and weltering lesions that have given the illness its name. Beyond this, nearly all who contract the disease die withing the next 72 hours. Most people succumb to the pox within the week, while the most resilient may hold on for almost two weeks.

deity.name: Empyreus
deity.title: Arch Seraph of Valor
deity.symbol: Shattered chains between two defiant fists
entries: {@i Arch Seraph of Valor}

The patron of righteous warriors, Empyreus emboldens the strong to punish the horrors of Etharis that prey upon the weak. In battle, Empyreus favors bravery and conviction. His uncompromising nature differs from that of Maligant, his predecessor. The Arch Seraph drives his followers to become zealots bent on cleansing the world of whatever they perceive as unworthy or corrupt.

Empyreus competes with his brother, {@deity Tormach|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}, for the title of god of war. In this struggle, the Sword Saint encourages strategy and heroism among his knights and paladins, who shine as beacons of hope to the foot soldiers of Empyreus's armies and the downtrodden of Etharis. Warfare without righteousness is merely butchery.

The enemies of Empyreus's warriors have much to fear. Terror is a righteous weapon to turn against those who victimize the innocent, and one which Empyreus' faithful wield through violence and torture. {@deity Beleth|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24} smiles to watch their moralizing belligerence. Nobody knows better than the Arch Daemon of fear that mortals frequently manifest their own greatest terror—as Empyreus' zealots grow further inflamed, so do their deeds become more monstrous. For every charlatan, mage, and heretic Empyereus terrorizes, Beleth's grin grows wider.

Empyreus is glorified as a knight clad in heavy plate Armor. His wings are made from sword blades. Empyreus often wears a helmet, yet no heresy or wickedness escapes his stern gaze from within his visor.

entries.name: Followers
entries: Questing knights, war clerics, and warriors of noble houses are among those who wear Empyreus' sigils on their Armor and shields. Anyone who takes up arms might offer a prayer to the Sword Saint. Mercenary companies and even brigands might whisper a prayer before battle, asking for strength and courage.

As the prime deity among the Arch Seraphs in Castinella, countless inquisitions and holy crusades have been waged in Empyreus' name. The Arcanist Inquisition frequently employs fear to discourage the use of magic. The Sword Saint's most holy warriors are imbued with their own divine gifts, empowered to smite all that is filthy and blasphemous in the Arch Seraph's sight.

deity.name: Zabriel
deity.title: Arch Seraph of Truth
deity.symbol: A giant eye, the iris shaped like a keyhole, wreathed by countless pairs of angel wings
entries: {@i Arch Seraph of Truth}

All shadows and deceits are stripped bare by Zabriel's all-seeing eye. The Watcher inherited her domain from the goddess of knowledge, Typharia, for whom Zabriel acted as librarian. Now, truths are the only knowledge the Arch Seraph considers worth keeping. Lies, euphemisms, allegories, and folktales are of no interest to Zabriel, who believes ignorance on any scale is a dangerous weapon in the hands of tyrants and deceivers.

Zabriel's enemies are spies, assassins, and illusionists. These cunning operators, shrouded by {@deity Venin|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}, the Arch Daemon of deceit, spread disruption and misinformation supports harmful conspiracies and oppressive regimes. Ignorance is a chain around society's neck. Truth is the key to liberation.

It's said that Zabriel disguises herself as a robed figure to treat with mortals. Her face glows so brightly from beneath her hood that her features cannot be seen, and she carries a tome with the names of every being in existence scribed inside. The Watcher's true form is that of a giant eye, the iris shaped like a keyhole, wreathed by countless pairs of angel wings.

entries.name: Followers
entries: Churches devoted to Zabriel are places of learning. The Arch Seraph's priests are studious and generous with their knowledge, wishing to spread the light of understanding among the folk of Etharis as widely as possible. If a priest does not know an answer, they usually know where to look.

However, among the Watcher's adherents, their predilection for the truth can outweigh their sense of nuance or morality. Zabriel's inquisitors hunt criminals and spies with clinical tenacity, never mind the reasons for the crime. Artificers pray to Zabriel for a flash of inspiration, hoping it will lead them to a new revelation. They plunge into the depths of science without regard for ethics or safety.

deity.name: Aphaeleon
deity.title: Arch Seraph of Temperance
deity.symbol: Golden sphere within a blue crescent
entries: {@i Arch Seraph of Temperance}

The most contemplative and peaceful of the Arch Seraphs, Aphaeleon is a patron to the strong-minded and the spiritual. His presence is felt in the simple joy of contentment; whatever form it takes. The gentle warmth of a breeze. The satisfaction of a finished book. The quiet contemplation of meditation.

Aphaeleon was an angel of Myria, goddess of joy. The Arch Seraph has witnessed little joy left in the dwindling light of Etharis and has chosen instead to nurture those who seek spiritual freedom and enlightenment. Indulgence invariably leads to craving more. Aphaeleon aims to liberate mortals from their addictions and worldly desires.

A rivalry over mortal souls exists between Aphaeleon and his seraphic sibling, {@deity Sitri|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}. The Arch Daemon of hedonism gifts joy to mortals through all manner of corporeal delights, tipping the soul towards cravings that can turn into desperation. Aphaeleon offers a harder path, one that leads to a more enduring form of fulfillment.
Aphaeleon appears as an old, bearded elf floating above a sphere of golden light. Such is his stillness, sparrows land upon his shoulders to sing to him their whistling tunes.

entries.name: Followers
entries: Aphaeleon's priests can be found leading austere lives in secluded monasteries and peaceful wildernesses. They're pleased to share the teachings of their patron and the arts of spiritual healing with folk who come to them with peace and curiosity. On the other hand, monks in service to Aphaeleon are not to be underestimated if confronted with violence. Mastery of the body is part of mastery of the soul.

Worshippers of Aphaeleon also provide absolution to the wicked who truly desire spiritual freedom. Repentance might be achieved through a donation of worldly possessions to the poor, or by receiving last rites before burning upon the pyre. The form of atonement is dependent upon the nature of the sins.

deity.name: Solyma
deity.title: Arch Seraph of Justice
deity.symbol: A vertical sword gripped by gauntleted hands against a starburst
entries: {@i Arch Seraph of Justice}

Solyma has been worshipped since antiquity. Statues and epithets glorifying the Great Judge are found in ancient tombs and ruins that long predate the Gods' End. Her deity was destroyed during The Aetheric War, so long ago that no one today remembers his name, and Solyma has sworn a vow of silence until her former master is avenged. She will not speak until all cosmic horrors are defeated.

Solyma is the Arch Seraph of justice. Her silence endures so long as evil persists. She detests all that is corrupt, abject, or villainous. In particular, she loathes the Arch Daemon of avarice, {@deity Gorodyn|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}, who turns the hearts of mortals black over such a venal and petty thing as money.

In addition to her epithets of the Great Judge and the Burning Scales, Solyma has also earned the moniker of Lady Vengeance. She is a patron of the downtrodden and the persecuted. A warrior deity who wields a flaming sword, dealing punishment to evildoers until they grasp the full extent of the suffering they've inflicted upon others.

Lady Solyma appears as a long-haired angel with countless sets of wings, a sword glowing white-hot in her grasp. She is sometimes blindfolded. Her lips may be stitched closed, or otherwise her hair hangs in front of her face and down to her feet, which never touch the ground.

entries.name: Followers
entries: In Ostoya, Solyma exceeds {@deity Empyreus|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24} as the primary warrior deity. She is invoked by paladins, particularly in the Order of Dawn, and is also associated with monster hunters, vigilantes, judges, and those who root out evil. Prayers are offered to Solyma throughout Etharis, wherever oppression exists.

Lady Vengeance's most devout tend towards zealotry. Her silence offers no guidance, leaving her followers to stray toward more extreme ideologies of retribution and intolerance, rather than justice. Her hatred towards Aether Kindred, daemons, and undead has led her warriors to pursue any who dabble in the eldritch or occult, including pact-making warlocks or blood-cursed dhampir.
Nevertheless, Solyma's worshippers always rejoice and offer rewards when evil is destroyed. Priests of her temples may provide quests for hunting vampires, aberrations, and all manner of vile things.

deity.name: Morael
deity.title: Arch Seraph of Sacrifice
deity.symbol: A figure carrying a bladed wheel upon his shoulders
entries: {@i Arch Seraph of Sacrifice}
