Morael is the patron of martyrs and heroes who seek no glory, sacrificing themselves to build hope for a future they'll never see. The Arch Seraph teaches that sacrifice is the truest virtue. The purest expression of love is to put others' needs before oneself, or to suffer pain or anguish so that others may be spared. Morael offers strength in moments of desperate decision-making or surrender in the name of a great cause.

Morael was shaped by Vetara, goddess of love, before her destruction in The Aetheric War. He continues her legacy of compassion and selfless devotion. His efforts are frequently undermined by the Arch Daemon {@deity Malikir|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}, who whispers in mortals' ears that the central focus of love should be themselves.

Morael is portrayed carrying a giant wheel upon his shoulders, which is so burdensome that it forces him to his knees. Blades extend from the spokes of the wheel. Morael's arms and shoulders are scored by lacerations, blood dribbling down his back and reddening his beard. The wheel represents the unending nature of sacrifice. Without sacrifice, love cannot exist. Only pride.

entries.name: Followers
entries: Many commoners invoke Morael's name when burdened by pain or misfortune, accepting their punishments as true and just.

Morael's most ardent followers believe that virtue cannot exist without suffering. The Flagellants, priests of Morael, teach their congregations to forsake and tear down the comforts of society. Nobles are dragged from their homes. Taverns are burned down. In some regions, Morael's frightening reputation and image is more akin to an Arch Daemon of pain.

A noble house might hire mercenaries to protect them from Morael's worshippers, who see tyrants everywhere. Conversely, a priest might offer blessings to adventurers about to undertake a difficult quest. One they're unlikely to escape from unscathed.

entries.name: Healing Magic in Etharis
entries: The gods are dead. Divine power has waned in Etharis. Many temples that once revered the gods have now been converted to glorify the Arch Seraphs instead, and any new churches built are constructed in the Arch Seraphs' names.

Most of the clergy are simple priests. Perhaps the most devout among them can conjure basic magic, but truly talented divine healers such as clerics or paladins are extremely rare. Viewed as living prophets, these individuals are celebrated in Etharis—or feared.

As possible wielders of divine magic, your characters are truly remarkable. This blessing is both a gift and a burden. Commoners are starved for guidance and want to believe in a power greater than themselves. They may look to a cleric as a beacon of hope and beg for aid or counsel, while equally, they flee from the gaze of a paladin who they fear has come to judge them.

The characters cannot rely on divine healers outside of their own group. A donation to the church does not guarantee a cure for disease or a resurrection. Such things are beyond the capabilities of common priests. Indeed, necromancy is reviled in many places, even when performed with good intentions, meaning most temples won't revive a character even if it were within their capabilities.

If your characters are in need of healing, they're more likely to find success seeking the aid of a surgeon, apothecary, or plague doctor. Such individuals are found in crowded cities where disease and danger are common, and there's plenty of coin to pay for their services or Potions of Healing. They might also be found travelling the roads between settlements. Alternatively, stories of herbalists living on the edge of villages or deep in the woods exist across Etharis, if you dare to trust such folk.

deity.name: Miklas
deity.title: Arch Seraph of Mercy
deity.symbol: A silver chalice overflowing with golden light, held by two hands
entries: {@i Arch Seraph of Mercy}

With the loss of Aurelia, mortals have been left without protection. Miklas, the goddess's former servant, simply doesn't have the strength to fulfil Aurelia's domain, and can only offer aid to those already in pain. The sick, wounded, impoverished, and distressed all cry for Miklas' mercy. Her work is endless. The extent of suffering is unfathomable in Etharis's new dark age.

Opposing Miklas are spreading plagues like the Weeping Pox, virulent curses like those wrought with Shadowsteel, and the endless cycles of violence and war inflamed by tyrants and battles for resources. {@deity Tormach|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24} laughs at Miklas' wasted efforts. The two struggle over mortal lives on every battlefield, the Arch Seraph unable to prevent the carnage. Nevertheless, she foils Tormach's bloodlust with every life rescued.
Miklas resembles a tall, golden-haired figure clothed in radiant white robes. She bears a staff hewn from an ash tree whose leaves continually wither and grow back.

entries.name: Followers
entries: Doctors, apothecaries, herbalists, and surgeons offer prayers to Miklas, that she might guide their hand in mending the sick and injured. Miklas has many temples throughout Etharis. Her priests can offer medicines and Potions, though a divine healer is a truly rare and remarkable individual, blessed by the Arch Seraph. Her worship is popular, and commoners are more trusting of folk who wear Miklas' sigil.

More than any other Arch Seraph, Miklas' followers tend towards nonviolence. That doesn't mean they're not prone to zealotry. Folk who suffer from a curse, an eldritch heritage, a haunting, or a perceived mental anguish might be held against their will by worshippers of the Redeemer. Rescuing them from such confines can be tricky. Commoners who witness Miklas' priests being attacked are quickly angered, and Paladins sworn to the Arch Seraph harshly rebuke any use of violence.

deity.name: Beleth
deity.title: Arch Daemon of Fear
deity.symbol: A dark, horned gargoyle face with a cruel smile
entries: {@i Arch Daemon of Fear}

Most mortals fear the Arch Daemons, but conversely, Beleth finds mortals atrociously funny. He delights in torturing them with their worst fears to witness their petrified antics, knowing that the terror induced is often far worse than the actual threat. The Arch Daemon of fear is endlessly amused that commoners most often create what they fear most. Tyrants induce uprisings. Sycophants fail to earn respect. Witch hunters see mages everywhere.

The Arch Daemon of fear goes by many names—the Trickster, the Laughing Man, and Blackjester. Beleth's favorite game is to vex {@deity Empyreus|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}, whose followers' greatest fear is a world they cannot control. The warriors and priests who revere the Arch Seraph have turned to wielding terror as their weapon against heretics and mages, which makes Blackjester smile whether they intend it to or not.

Beleth's likeness is that of a horned and goat-legged shadow. His eyes are jet-black, glinting with horrendous glee like dark pools of ink, and when his cruel smile curls the edges of his mouth, black ichor dribbles down his chin. Blackjester's arms are wrapped with spiked chains. These shackles stab into mortal hearts whenever they feel gripped by fear.

entries.name: Followers
entries: The Trickster has many worshippers among bards and assassins who take inspiration from Beleth's obscene antics. Anyone who wields fear as a weapon and can make the Arch Daemon laugh is worthy of his umbral blessings.

A paranoid aristocrat may hire mercenaries to protect against assassins, only for the adventurers to learn the threat is imagined and their patron is truly haunted by the Laughing Man. A vampire noble in Ostoya might wield fear to oppress their subjects, making dark sacraments to Beleth in return for heinous magics. In the Castinellan Provinces, the Arcanist Inquisition hunts for mages in the righteous name of {@deity Empyreus|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}, but perhaps they have been blinded by Beleth's trickery and inflict tortures in the Trickster's honor, instead.

deity.name: Venin
deity.title: Arch Daemon of Deceit
deity.symbol: A weeping eye with a blue iris and red pupil
entries: {@i Arch Daemon of Deceit}

Venin is the mistress of lies, illusions, and insanity. As an Arch Daemon of supernal deceit, she is known by many names and many faces—Dusktongue, the Hooded One, the Great Deceiver, and many others forgotten by most mortals. She relishes the lies people tell themselves to justify depravity and cruelty. Venin triumphs when objectivity dissolves and mortals scream at each other over whose perceived reality is truth.

The Great Deceiver's schemes are frequently disrupted by {@deity Zabriel|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}, whose searing light burns away the obscuring darkness. Venin rewards those who assassinate the Arch Seraph's priests.

Venin's true form is unknown. Often, she appears as a towering human wreathed in black furs and shadows. At other times, she's depicted as a hooded figure whose face is a mirror, reflecting a subtly distorted version of the observer. Her voice seems to echo from deep within the listener's head.

entries.name: Followers
entries: Rogues, thieves, and murderers whisper prayers to Venin so that her darkness may conceal their misdeeds. Her hidden cults include assassin guilds, spy networks, and information brokers. Venin's priests are tricksters and orators. They're skilled at convincing others of their versions of the truth, but eventually, even they lose track of what is real and what is not.

deity.name: Sitri
deity.title: Arch Daemon of Hedonism
deity.symbol: A hand clutching a bleeding rose
entries: {@i Arch Daemon of Hedonism}

Sitri. His name slips from the tongue like honey or blood. The Tempter. The Bloodrose. Sitri whispers into the hearts of mortals, turning their minds inexorably toward whatever they desire until a want becomes a craving. It's said that Sitri has never once forced a soul to give in to hedonism. Yet inevitably, those who succumb to the Arch Daemon's promises lose themselves to their addictions—lust, hunger, intoxication. Then, when desire becomes all-consuming, no evil is beyond Sitri's purview to ask of his followers.

Sitri's most prized possessions are the souls of {@deity Aphaeleon|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}'s followers. Both once servants to Myria, the dead goddess of joy, Sitri now derives his pleasure from seducing Aphaelon's followers who backslide on their addictions.

Sitri has many depictions. Most often, he appears as snaking tendrils of black mist, which weave together into the shape of the viewer's greatest desire. Other times, the Tempter resembles a beautiful person. He wears whichever mask suits his purposes, offering a luscious rose clutched in one hand, the thorns causing blood to trickle between the fingers.

entries.name: Followers
entries: Sitri has countless followers in Altenheim or Nov Ostoya, where every indulgence is readily at hand. Every bodily pleasure is seized upon. Music. Wine. Food. Sex. Drug-fueled parties can last for days on end.

Cults that glorify Sitri form in the most profligate corners of Etharis's cities. Folk may not even realize they're being snared by a cult when they're first invited to an event, lured by the promise of whatever they desire. Inevitably, desire becomes addiction.

Sitri is also frequently glorified by the vampires of Ostoya, who view indulgence as the purpose of their very existence. A member of the Crimson Court may weave dark magics gifted to them by the Bloodrose. A creature trying to inflict the sanguine curse upon themselves might hope to do so through worshipping Sitri.

Sitri enjoys offering temptations to anyone, even those who spend their life adventuring. He might flicker into their thoughts in the guise of their greatest desire, leading them in service of Sitri's purposes without the adventurer ever learning the Arch Daemon's name. What depths would such a person sink to, to fulfil their deepest wishes?

deity.name: Malikir
deity.title: Arch Daemon of Pride
deity.symbol: A flaming ram skull crowned with a golden circlet
entries: {@i Arch Daemon of Pride}

Malikir prefers the title "God." Known to be the most powerful among the Arch Daemons, she's the embodiment of pride, tyranny, and the abuse of authority. Malikir is obsessed with her power. Her influence seeps into bold sovereigns and mighty heroes, who might begin with honorable intentions but are overcome by ego and paranoia. Knights become conquerors, and kings become tyrants. Malikir promises power, fame, and dominion to those who worship her, yet all who rise in her favor will inevitably come to a terrible end.

Malikir considers {@deity Morael|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24} to be a fool. The Arch Daemon's vanity cannot possibly conceive of how love is expressed through self-punishment or sacrifice. Nobody is more deserving of love than oneself.
The Subjugator is depicted as a towering figure of undulating flesh. Her flaming ram skull is crowned with a golden circlet, and her long arms end with talons.

entries.name: Followers
entries: Ambitious nobles, fame-seeking bards, and paladins who crave glory are among those most likely to believe themselves worthy of Malikir's blessings. But Malikir is selective of her disciples. Her cults usually center upon a singular, powerful personality, such as a ruler, merchant lord, military leader, or priest. These exemplars of pride and greatness attract large followings of those folk willing to perform the cult leader's bidding out of a sense of admiration, and the hope that the leader's glory will be shared among the group.

Of course, Malikir's blessings do not come freely. For one who aspires to fame or influence, even for goodhearted reasons, there may be no limit to the sins they're willing to commit in secret to gain the Subjugator's aid.

deity.name: Tormach
deity.title: Arch Daemon of Wrath
deity.symbol: A bull-headed warrior wielding a warhammer
entries: {@i Arch Daemon of Wrath}

The only truth of war is death. The only virtue is to kill. The only victory is to survive. Heroism, honor, and glory are illusions for moralizing cowards. These are the edicts of Tormach, Arch Daemon of wrath and war, who is known by such names as Manslayer and Marauder.

Once a lieutenant of the dead god Maligant, and brother to the Arch Seraph {@deity Empyreus|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}, Tormach has since forsaken his familial ties to seize the mantle of war god for himself. Tormach gives no praise for strategy or valor. In the Arch Daemon's mind, the only purpose of armed conflict is to kill. Victory itself is unimportant. All that matters is that as much blood is spilled and as much carnage is dealt as is possible, during and after the battle. Tormach is the element of war at its most violent. Every battlefield is an altar to the Arch Daemon of wrath, though his tributes are stolen time and again by the healing light of {@deity Miklas|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24} the Redeemer—a fact that infuriates him to no end.

Depictions of the Arch Daemon show a flaming warrior with a bull's head. Sometimes it's a decapitated head sitting upon a man's shoulders, rivulets of blood running down his scarred chest. Other times, the bull is depicted as Tormach's true face. He rides a chariot pulled by a pair of chimeras and wields his warhammer, the {@i Wrath of Heaven}.

entries.name: Followers
entries: Tormach is a source of strength for many soldiers and militaries. A growing faith among the armies of Unterland honor Tormach as an Arch Seraph, and even noble warriors may invoke his name to draw upon his strength as a war deity. Commoners are nevertheless wary of any who wear his sigil. Tormach's unflinching demand for slaughter twists his truly devout into raging berserkers.

Adventures featuring the Arch Daemon are likely to include clashes with one of his cults. A cadre of soldiers may try to turn their entire legion to Tormach's ideals, or a troupe of bandits might capture civilians and commit blood sacrifices in his name. Bloodthirsty fiends conjured from the Netherworld may join the ranks of a particularly powerful warband or even lead one. Valikan raiders who have forsaken their heritage might invoke the Marauder when taking heads from an unprotected settlement. A serial killer could be motivated by the Manslayer, intoxicated by the scent of their exsanguinated victims.

deity.name: Gorodyn
deity.title: Arch Daemon of Avarice
entries: {@i Arch Daemon of Avarice}

Gorodyn proselytizes that greed is a virtue. The Arch Daemon lovingly encourages mortals to voraciously hoard anything of value that can be bought with a coin. Food. Opulence. Secrets. Greed is sustaining. Greed is a vital instinct for survival and prosperity. To the Arch Daemon of avarice, luxuriating atop a wealth of resources is the greatest happiness one can achieve, even if that means the rest go hungry.

Gorodyn recognizes that the valuable resources of Etharis are finite. One mortal's gain is always another's loss. The King of Red Coins was once servile to Jezra, god of commerce, until The Aetheric War left the domain empty for Gorodyn to seize. Forgotten was the spirit of fair trade and mutual ascendancy. Instead, Gorodyn rewards mortals who are strong or cunning enough to hoard their wealth by whatever means necessary.

Though he considers himself loving and pragmatic, Gorodyn fears the retribution of {@deity Solyma|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}. The rage of folk impoverished by the King of Red Coins can burn with enough intensity that it sparks a revolution, causing a violent redistribution of wealth.

Gorodyn is often depicted by a red or filthy coin. His true appearance is whispered to be an enormous and grotesque bag of rotting flesh enveloped in both flies and gaudy jewelry. His body is also covered with hundreds of mouths that all speak with the same voice.

entries.name: Followers
entries: Greedy nobles, ambitious courtiers, and even paupers all secretly pray to Gorodyn to grow their meagre wealth or protect what they've already hoarded. Hunger exists in all mortals. So, the King of Red Coins is worshipped throughout every level of society.

In {@book Morencia|GrimHollowCG24|9}, Gorodyn finds many followers, as the wealth of the entire continent flows through those free markets and guilds. Cults praising the King of Red Coins also exist in the opulent cities of Altenheim and Gräffendam, where nobles jealously amass their wealth whilst draining the countryside of resources.

deity.name: Vraigoroth
deity.title: Kindred of Devouring
entries: {@i Kindred of Devouring}

Vraigoroth is thought of as an enormous maw, ringed with fangs, that sucks all light and energy into itself, returning all existence to darkness. Others imagine it to be an unfathomable tumor, a growing mass of wriggling flesh with veinlike tendrils extending into reality, transmogrifying everything it touches into more of itself. Nobody truly knows its shape. It gnaws at the edges of existence, digesting in the dark.

Polyps of Vraigoroth bubble into the mortal realm wherever it has chewed a hole through reality. This can happen anywhere—deep within an unexplored cave, inside a dusty abandoned structure, or through the living flesh of a mortal host. These amorphous horrors twist and grow. They dissolve any organic matter they come into contact with, amalgamating it into their mass and growing in size as they consume. Unless destroyed, these nightmarish creatures can reach truly terrifying proportions.

Worshippers of Vraigoroth believe they've learned and accepted a horrifying truth—everything that exists was once part of this entity, and shall be again. The cycle has repeated endlessly. The devouring is inevitable. Therefore, Vraigoroth should be praised and its hunger fed. Once all of reality has been returned to its amalgamated mass, all suffering will end.

It's not always clear whether followers of Vraigoroth are terrified or jubilant at their patron's devouring. Warlocks who siphon some of the Aether Kindred's power tend to become gorgers themselves, entire settlements going missing as the arcanist consumes everyone and everything that comes within its grasp. Inevitably, they're transformed into another amorphous polyp of Vraigoroth itself.

deity.name: M'rorcameleth
deity.title: Kindred of Dreams
entries: {@i Kindred of Dreams}

Some folk fear to dream. The fleeting realities that unfold behind closed eyes are more than merely the fancies of an unconscious mind. Many tragedies have been preceded by the occurrence of bad dreams. The great King Aymeric Noblecoeur was said to have witnessed his death in a nightmare. Days later, he was gone.

M'rorcameleth, known as the Mind Hunter, slithers into mortal minds through the realm of dreams. In their waking moments, those who've witnessed it describe a landscape made of flesh, punctuated by bulbous eyes and wriggling, worm-like trees. Each tree bears a face. Stretched. Sleeping. It's unclear if M'rorcameleth is the master of this dreamscape realm or is the realm itself. Having visited this place, nobody wakes up the same as they were before.

Dreaming of M'rorcameleth is said to be an omen. Each night, the Aether Kindred tortures those it visits with horrifying visions of inevitable futures, causing them to wake knowing that they're doomed. The only escape is not to sleep at all.

The purpose of these nightmares, or M'rorcameleth's desires, is something mortals should not dare ask. Nevertheless, wizards and occultists have certainly tried. Oneiromancy is used to reach out to M'rorcameleth, with the few who succeed appearing to master psionic magics. Yet it isn't clear if the same person who closed their eyes is the entity who awoke, or whether they've become a puppet of the Aether Kindred's consciousness. Entire cults have formed around such individuals. Fanatics hope to dream of M'rorcameleth, so their mind too can be opened.

deity.name: Pharazorthok
deity.title: Kindred of Knowing
entries: {@i Kindred of Knowing}

Followers of Pharazorthok claim that its branches hold reality in place. They describe the eldritch being as resembling an enormous tentacled tree, with countless eyes upon its branches, twisting and growing beneath the surface of reality like roots through the soil. The mortal realm sits upon these branches. As does Numena, the Netherworld, and all realms.

It's whispered that Pharazorthok sees and knows all. Its branches pierce into reality. Its eyes watch from unseen corners to learn every secret and witness every deed. The tendrils of this Aether Kindred can also reach into mortal minds to listen and speak on the Material Plane, though its intentions for doing so are never clear.

Far from raving fanatics, Pharazorthok's worshippers are patient and learned. The grasp of the Aether Kindred's branches manifests visions of endless, frightening, cosmic understanding. Some break under such immense knowledge. Those who survive, and who aren't reduced to living their days in terrified silence, manifest sorcerous power and become educators of Pharazorthok's truth.

Cults devoted to Pharazorthok revolve around these enlightened spellcasters. Nobody can know for certain if what is being taught is true or the imaginings of a charlatan, but the cult will fervently protect their leader.

Wherever the branches of Pharazorthok wriggle through the veil and into the mortal realm, horrifying aberrations of eyes and teeth are spawned. Such abominations are pointed to as evidence of the claims made by Pharazorthok's followers.

deity.name: Lady Morakesh
deity.title: Primordial of Fire
entries: {@i Primordial of Fire}

The Burning Lady, Red Queen, and Emberheart are among her many names. The Primordial of Fire holds court in her realm of Flamebourne. She commands {@creature Azer|XMM|Azers}, {@creature Efreeti|XMM}, and {@creature Fire Giant|XMM|Fire Giants} in her army. Morakesh suffered the worst under {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}, as the Wyrm saw her as his most destructive weapon.

As such, she is highly capricious and easy to anger; she rewards characters with great riches as easily as she can turn them to cinders. Great care must be taken in petitioning her. Druidic cults of blood and fire are among her worshipers, who perform burning sacrifices of sentient beings to invoke Morakesh's favor.

deity.name: Alondo
deity.title: Primordial of Ice and Water
entries: {@i Primordial of Ice and Water}

Lord of the Deep and ruler of the Endless Sea, Alondo controls the denizens of lakes, seas, and oceans. He is worshipped by the laneshi. Aside from Kentigern, Alondo has no love for mortals and wishes to be left alone. That said, he is the most conscientious of his ilk, working diligently to prevent the return of {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}.

Still, rumors persist that he has something to do with the outbreak of coldfire plaguing Etharis. Blood druids who would invoke Alondo must first slay one of his hated enemies and hurl its heart into the sea.

deity.name: Citrolach
deity.title: Primordial of Earth and Stone
entries: {@i Primordial of Earth and Stone}

The Living Mountain masters over all those who pledge themselves to earth and stone. He is the progenitor of stoneborn trolls and earth elementals, who emerge from his underground realm of Stonereach. At times through history, Citrolach has been worshipped by dwarves and gnomes who delve for riches beneath the earth. He is also important to druids of the land and moon, who believe his power extends to plants and the denizens of the forests.

It is common for Citrolach's worshippers to bury their wealth in gems and {@item Gold|XDMG|Gold} back into the earth, returned to the Primordial's realm from where it was taken. Dwarves are said to inherit their stereotypical stubbornness from Citrolach, who has held to a particular stand for centuries. The saying goes that it is easier to move a mountain than convince Citrolach to change his mind.

deity.name: Ilhara
deity.title: Primordial of Air and Shadow
entries: {@i Primordial of Air and Shadow}

The Queen of Air and Shadow is the most ambitious Primordial of all. Her spies hide in the smoke of candles and lurk in empty shadows to find secrets from among mortals and spirits alike. Her ultimate aim is to be powerful enough to fill the void left by {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}. Ilhara requires that a dark secret be whispered into the air before she can be summoned.
Assassins and scouts who stalk the gloom or deal in Poisons may petition Ilhara for aid. She is of particular importance to the spies of Clan Morgöng in {@book Valika|GrimHollowCG24|7}.

deity.name: Gormadraug
deity.title: Primordial of All Elements
entries: {@i Primordial of All Elements}

The Great Prismatic Wyrm is also a powerful source of primal magic, though he doesn't know this in his current state. There is no known way to invoke him. Moreover, worship of the Wyrm is forbidden throughout the {@book Valikan Clans|GrimHollowCG24|7}. The Primordials also warn that drawing power from Gormadraug may even awaken him, and they readily smite any druids or rangers who try.

Much blood is spilled in the land of Thrull to keep Gormadraug in slumber. Raiders from that region of Valika take prisoners from the settlements they attack, specifically to be hung from trees and sacrificed for the good of all the world. Smaller cults also perform this practice. They might be found in other regions of Valika or the northern {@book Bürach Empire|GrimHollowCG24|3}.

entries.name: Welcome to Grim Hollow!
entries: Bound within these pages are countless stories of darkness, tragedy, and horror waiting to be told. If you're reading this, then you likely share our love of grim tales and dark fantasy—a storytelling genre which invites its audience to explore perilous worlds of violence and monsters.

Grim Hollow is a dark fantasy setting designed to tell such stories in the world of Etharis. Here, battles are hard fought. Choices are difficult. Characters may be forced to decide between rescuing innocent lives, defeating their enemies, and compromising their values. The path to justice is difficult to tread. Characters who explore this world may themselves be corrupted by an evil desire, vile curse, or traumatic past. Etharis leaves none unscathed.

Grim Hollow encapsulates many styles of dark fantasy, whether you wish to explore realms of gothic horror, twisted fairy tales, or eldritch dread. You might craft stories in which the greatest acts of evil are those humanity inflicts upon each other. Or you might create scenarios where survival is never guaranteed within a hostile landscape, and the only way to persist is by stealing what others have.

Despite the difficult circumstances the characters of your world face, their choices remain their own. It isn't the role of a storyteller to judge the actions of those partaking in the tale. We merely facilitate situations where dark themes are safely explored. Your characters might answer intolerance with wrath, greed with cruelty, or violence with overwhelming reprisal. Your role as Game Master is simply to enact the consequences of actions taken in a world that so rarely rewards heroic deeds.

Nevertheless, roleplaying games are intended for fun. Grim Hollow offers your players a different kind of power fantasy, where they'll face terrifying dangers and fight desperate battles only to achieve the smallest victories. But each victory should bring a small glimmer of hope into your broken world. The strength of your characters is in choosing to fight on, despite the challenges set against them, and the daemons they grapple within themselves.
It's now time to dim the lights. You've no fear of the darkness, for it's the very thing with which you will weave your tales of dread and woe.
Welcome to Etharis. This world is now your own!

data.name: Part 1: World of Etharis
entries: Etharis is a land of magic and terror, of teetering nations and crumbling empires. Leaders watch their tentative hold over their subjects slip away in the wake of disease, unrest, poverty, and disasters. Threats against the stability of nations lurk in the shadows and stand firmly in the open—both from forces outside their borders and from rebels within.

Humans dominated the continent for centuries, but since the death of the gods, every form of power is tenuous. From the ancient {@book Charneault Kingdom|GrimHollowCG24|5} to the bustling port of a {@book Morencia|GrimHollowCG24|9}, the ambitious and the desperate vie for control. From the frigid {@book Valikan|GrimHollowCG24|7} expanse to the sweltering {@book Castinellan|GrimHollowCG24|6} coast, societies mobilize to combat invading armies and mystical forces that threaten civilization itself.

Each nation faces its own specific forms of threat, and even the most evil autocrats, like the {@area Crimson Court of Ostoya|05c|x}, must be vigilant against forces that hope to tear them down. The threatening forces range from the magical to the mundane, from the subtle to the apocalyptic.

And everywhere there is strife, it is the common folks who suffer the most. The villages and outposts, the slums and the sanitariums: these are the places where the suffering of the whole of Etharis is made manifest. The powerless are always the first to fall, paying for their lack of options with their bodies and, far too often, their souls.

Many locations see the worst of the chaos and corruption, and this section of the {@i Grim Hollow Campaign Guide} provides details on these places. If they can survive the onslaught of both supernatural and manmade catastrophes remains to be seen.

And that's where the heroes come in. Are the troubles of the nations and city-states too much for those willing to lay down their lives for coin, glory, and the common good? Or will the so-called heroes end up adding to the suffering of the masses?

As the details of the nations, settlements, ruins, and unique location are revealed, ask yourself what tales of horror and hope, corruption and redemption, heroics and despair might take place there. When the fate of the village, city, nation, or world is resting in the balance, what will the heroes of Etharis be willing to do to save them? And can they save themselves in the process?

entries.name: Dark Fantasy Stories
entries: Dark fantasy stories are gripping and immersive by their very nature. The dangers feel more real. The emotional stakes are heightened. There is a thrill in choosing to confront something horrifying. Classic fantasy settings imagine noble rulers sending valorous adventurers to accomplish worthy deeds and vanquish great evils. Victory is rarely in question for the heroes. Their actions are presumed justified by the very nature of being the focus of the story.

Worlds of dark fantasy are less obsequious. Protagonists are likely to be as selfish, violent, vengeful, zealous, or grief stricken as the monsters and villains they hunt. Equally, no ruler is likely to have earned their powerful position without some measure of cunning or corruption.

Dark fantasy worlds are for characters to explore the dark side of humanity. That doesn't mean there can be no valor, no sacrifice, no heroics. Indeed, dark fantasy relies on those brief and rare moments. These special moments of selfless heroism illuminate what the world could be, what is lost because we are all flawed and often incapable of overcoming those flaws.

Victory for the central characters is never assured and always costly. Dangers lurking in the darkness—and sometimes right out in the open—are so great that each battle risks injury or death, making every monster encounter more frightening and thrilling as a result. The choices your characters make are more important because they could harm as many lives as they rescue. The question isn't about how they'll save everyone. Rather, who is worth saving?

Such impossible circumstances impact a character's journey and informs the stories that the Game Master presents. Dark fantasy protagonists can be terribly flawed. Players are invited to roleplay stories and emotions that differ from the valorous heroes they might typically play. Such experiences are as cathartic and escapist as any game set in a high fantasy world, and choosing whether your character fights the darkness or is consumed by it is often more personal.

The setting in this book provides Game Masters with the means to facilitate their own dark fantasy stories. Along with the rules content in the {@book Grim Hollow Player's Guide|GrimHollowPG24}, Game Masters and players have everything they need to envision and deliver a dark fantasy experience that resonates like the doleful tolling of a bell.

In this book, Game Masters have the people, places, Magic Items, curses, organizations, plots, and new rules to transition from a typical high fantasy setting into one that marks the dark contrast of a new type of campaign. Game play and storytelling may be different with these tools, but we feel it is just as fun!

As the Game Master, you can allow the players to use these tools to explore the inner-dimensional struggles of dark fantasy while you present stories of the more traditional conflicts: fighting the evil that monsters and humankind can undertake.

entries.name: Dark Themes of the Realms
entries: As you bring your own ideas and shades of light and dark to your campaigns and adventures, it's important to understand what Etharis, as the world of this Grim Hollow campaign setting, offers. Later in the chapter, a summary of each of the nations and city-states is provided.
Here, however, are the concepts and themes that Grim Hollow offers.

entries.name: The World is Broken
entries: Etharis is a world on the brink of destruction. The gods are dead, fear and superstition are rife, and every realm is beset by cataclysms threatening to collapse civilization entirely.

The bleak realities of Etharis make the realm incredibly dangerous. The common folk cower in their fragile settlements while monsters stalk freely across the land. Travel between population centers carries risk. Even within the walls of relative safety, greed and desperation foster a different kind of evil that preys upon the kind and unwary.

In times of such difficulty, the sins of hatred, prejudice, and cruelty often rise above kinder virtues. Etharis is filled with people who dream of a brighter tomorrow but are often willing to commit terrible deeds today to more easily survive the night. Raising and feeding an army requires taxes. A king cannot be a generous sovereign while defending his realm from monsters.

As the Game Master, you must decide how to portray the NPCs that live in this milieu. If empathy and sacrifice often end in ruin or death, does that change how a character acts and reacts? What angles do people take if they are commoners, powerful entities, and everyone else in between? Does the presence of powerful heroes give hope and encourage goodness, or are others more likely to take advantage of their presence for their own selfish means?

entries.name: The Gods are Dead
entries: The disappearance of the gods is the event that many blame for the current state of Etharis. Few people know the truth of their downfall, but all are aware that praying to the gods brings no aid and no relief. The divine magic that once offered healing and protection to those in need has dwindled, and few remain who can kindle its power.

The existence of the characters, especially those who wield the power of divine magic, is an anomaly in Etharis. They might be treated with awe, but they are just as likely to be seen as frauds or dangerous outliers. With no organized church structure behind these characters, there is no implicit trust or respect. If seen as a unique prophet of the Arch Seraphs, {@variantrule Reaction|XPHB}s could vary from cultlike worship to repulsion, hostility, or disbelief.
Power groups are just as likely to want to kill or discredit someone who wields the power of the dead gods. Threats against secular power are not tolerated by those with so much to lose.

The death of the gods has serious consequences, even for those who don't wish to partake in a divine class. Healing and curative magic is not readily available at shrines or temples. Similarly, resurrection is a near impossibility. Instead, those who can afford it rely on the medical sciences of apothecaries and surgeons for healing.

entries.name: Magic is Mistrusted
entries: Spellcasters in Etharis find themselves the target of hatred and mistrust. A magic inquisition has sprung up in the {@book Castinellan Provinces|GrimHollowCG24|6}, bureaucracies attempt to regulate and control the lives of mages elsewhere, and mobs hunt down those the common folk can blame for their misfortunes. Where once they delved into the mysteries of the universe, now wizards and their kin must seek to ensure their very survival.

What does this mean for a Grim Hollow adventure? Arcane casters who hurl flames or manipulate minds in an obvious manner to save a village are likely to find themselves shunned in the aftermath, or even being attacked by the townsfolk they just saved. When, in the mind of a naïve country farmer, magic is the tool of daemons and witches, being saved by magic is no salvation at all.

As the Game Master, you can decide the extent to which each person or location in your world mistrusts magic, and what form that mistrust takes. As long as the characters understand the theme is present, it becomes their responsibility to manage it to the best of their capabilities.

entries.name: Humanity is Flawed
entries: When faced with difficult circumstances, some rise to the occasion and help those in need. In a dark fantasy setting, many people fall on the other side of the coin. Etharis is populated by the desperate and fearful—those who look out for themselves, often to the detriment of others. Whether it's a shrewd noble murdering their political rivals over dinner or an impoverished farmer stealing vital medicine from their elderly neighbor to treat their own sickly child, the people of Etharis are rarely given a fair choice when deciding between decency and survival.

In short, Etharis is home to the scheming, the greedy, and the cruel. And even those who would otherwise eschew such evils may be forced into them through circumstances. Thus, Game Masters should remember that help is not something given freely by strangers, whether they have much, little, or nothing to lose. Bribery, intimidation, coercion, and sometimes outright blackmail are languages better understood in a world of mistrust and paranoia, when appeals for mercy or sympathy inevitably fail.

What is a helping hand in a traditional fantasy campaign should be looked at with suspicion in Grim Hollow. The danger of the monster guarding its lair might not be much different than an innkeeper guarding their hostel. After all, a bit of poison in the stew could net great wealth.

entries.name: Hope Within the Darkness
entries: Despite the dire circumstances facing the world, hope lingers. It may be scattered, dimmed, and assaulted on all sides, but it has not been extinguished. A farmer stands against the oncoming soldiers, giving his friends and family time to flee. A merchant, with a kindly smile, hands a loaf of bread to a poor beggar. A paladin, the last of his order, continues his quest to rid the world of a daemon.

Across Etharis, small acts of kindness, of bravery, of charity, all stand out amid the dark backdrop. Few and far between, their scarcity only serves to make them more special. As the Game Master, bringing these glimmers of hope into the world is part of what makes playing in a dark fantasy narrative rewarding for yourself and the players.

A dark fantasy setting is one in which good rarely vanquishes evil, and even then, it is only a temporary victory. However, those hard-fought victories, won at a steep price, deserve celebration. The secret of a successful dark fantasy campaign is knowing when to present that opportunity for heroism and selflessness, that ray of light in an otherwise dark world.

entries.name: The Realms of Etharis
entries: Etharis is an expansive continent occupied by empires and ancient kingdoms. Cultures are diverse and deep rooted. Each nation has been shaped by its people, history, power groups, relationship with magic, cataclysmic events, and neighboring sovereigns.

No realm is singular in its culture or values. Each represents a different genre of dark fantasy and horror, and therefore, different themes are best explored there. A single region could accommodate an entire campaign, or you can experience them all through your adventures by traveling across the continent.

The history, culture, and themes of each region are explored deeply in this book. Below, small snapshots of these realms are provided. There is no correct order to experience them, except that you should start wherever your inspiration takes you.

entries.name: The Bürach Empire
entries: Once a beacon of civilization that extended its territory into every realm, the {@book Bürach Empire|GrimHollowCG24|3} crumbles and rots, now clinging to its heartland provinces like an old lion ravaged by a lifetime of wounds and illness. Though still formidable in wealth and might, those within the empire live each day sensing that the end is approaching.

Stories of cosmic horror sit perfectly in the Bürach Empire, where the hopes of a better tomorrow are overshadowed by an impending and unavoidable doom. A shadow stalks the Bürach Empire, a creature of colossal size whose mere presence twists and corrupts the land around it. This beast has been seen by many yet never truly seen, for it is as much an archetype as it is a mortal being. But the horrors that spring up in its wake cannot be denied.

The provinces that comprise the remains of the Bürach Empire see the impending collapse of its leadership and society, and their leaders position themselves to benefit from that collapse—and perhaps even look to hasten it. Whether that end comes by war, rebellion, disease, or cults of strange gods, the empire may be witnessing its final days.

The dark fantasy themes of cosmic horror, gothic horror, and existential dread work well in a campaign set in the Bürach Empire. The corruption of the political and social spheres of Bürach are mirrored through the corruptive and mutative magic. The imperialism and colonialism that made Bürach strong have led to a soul-stealing disregard for others and their struggles. The "might makes right" attitudes of its people are now being torturously reversed, as the might slips away to be replaced by doubt and a proliferation of the might of monsters.

entries.name: The Ostoyan Empire
entries: Ostoya is a sunless realm of shadows and blood. The hearts of the folk who live in the {@book Ostoyan Empire|GrimHollowCG24|4} are as hard and cold as the land they live upon, forced to toil beneath a black sky and a callous nobility that wants to drain everything from its peasantry, including its blood.

Ostoya is ruled by a coterie of seven vampires named the Crimson Court, who both enjoy the perks of their power in gaudy displays, while also fretting moment by moment over the plots against them, both by hunters of vampires and by each other.

The gothic horror of vampires, werewolves, zombies, undead, ghosts, and ghouls pairs well with the themes of autocracy, civil unrest, resistance, rebellion, and personal safety. Stories and adventures in Ostoya can examine how much normal people are willing to endure politically and socially if their leaders promise to keep them safe from more local and realized threats. But when those same leaders also cause the threats, what are people willing to risk to fight for their freedom?

entries.name: The Charneault Kingdom
entries: The {@book Charneault Kingdom|GrimHollowCG24|5} is a realm of beauty that far exceeds any other. Hidden behind the mystical forest of the Grove Maze is a land of rolling emerald hills, mist-veiled glens, and countless flowing rivers. Charneault is patrolled by knights of virtue. A deep connection to nature has been entwined into Charnelaise culture from the elves who first settled these idyllic lands.

Yet all this beauty is merely an illusion. Stories told in the Charneault Kingdom are similar to children's fairytales—whimsical narratives that disguise the true brutality and danger at their core. The fairytales at the heart of Charneault are much darker and grimmer.

Capricious fey, worshipped on the one hand and feared on the other, take what they want with no fear of retribution. A corrupting mist veils their activities and decimates everything it touches with no end in sight. The tales and adventures of Charneault are those of nightmares—the supernatural horror of amoral fey creatures performing indescribable acts of cruelty and pain on mortal creatures just to see what will happen.

entries.name: The Castinellan Provinces
entries: Faith unifies the {@book Castinellan Provinces|GrimHollowCG24|6}. Fervently devoted to the edicts of the Arch Seraphs, the people of Castinella recognize the evils that can be performed through acts of arcane magic—and they do everything in their power to stop those acts from happening. If a few innocent or good-hearted people are killed or tortured in the process, it is considered a small price to pay in the name of safety.

Castinella is a realm where religion has transformed the populace from the best of intentions into monsters in their own right. The ignorance, misinformation, and blind devotion of the Arcanist Inquisition and its followers set up campaigns of psychological horror, where it only takes one wayward accusation to turn an otherwise innocent person's life into a nightmare. And the presence of actual monsters that need to be fought means choosing between utilizing the magic that kills monsters, or hiding the magic that will get you killed as one.

entries.name: The Valikan Clans
entries: Far to the north, at the frozen edge of the continent, lies the homeland of the {@book Valikan Clans|GrimHollowCG24|7}. Each day, the hardy folk who dwell in Valika must endure the unforgiving environment, rampaging elementals, and the blood-fueled conflicts that erupt between the various clans.

Life is short among the Great Clans. Yet the legacies of great heroes and clan chiefs live across generations through the sagas that the Valikans tell of their exalted history. Like the diverse seasons themselves, the honored and skilled bravery of Valikans is one side of a coin. The other, like the harsh winter, is about survival at any cost, including performing terrible and selfish acts to make sure you and your clan survive. Those acts include raiding settlements and taking captives—some of which may be sacrificed.

The tales of Valika include survival and supernatural horror. The arctic climate and magical coldfire make both the natural and the supernatural horror meld into one grim reality for those hoping to live or adventure there.

entries.name: Liesech and Morencia
entries: The two city-states of {@book Liesech|GrimHollowCG24|8} and {@book Morencia|GrimHollowCG24|9} are not full nations, but they are nonetheless thematically, geographically, politically, and socially distinct from the other areas. Each brings its own flavor and own sense of horror to a campaign.

Liesech is the putrid cauldron of disease where the Weeping Pox originated. Its people attempt to carry on their mercantile ways of life as their friends, family, peers, and even enemies die from the dreaded disease. Although the disease has a supernatural cause, its effects are nonetheless clear to see. Body horror and the corruption involved in that genre come not just through the disease itself, but from the sometimes questionable actions of those who attempt to cure it.

Morencia's disease is social rather than medical. The greedy and conniving residents of this center of banking and commerce smile and extend one hand while the other clutches a poisoned dagger. The vibrant and effusive façade of Morencia hides dark secrets and secret cults that would do anything for an ounce of power and one more {@item Gold|XDMG|Gold Coin}.

entries.name: An Incomplete History
entries: The complete histories, timelines, and myths of Etharis are meant to be illustrative rather than proscriptive. Each nation and people have their own sense of history and lore, much of which might be contradictory, as everyone tries to make themselves look like the hero or the victim, depending on the circumstances. Not every event is fully explored or every character detailed—such an undertaking would be impossible to contain within a single volume. There are many mysteries yet to be revealed.

While future volumes may delve even further into the recesses of Etharis, for now, they're left open for you to fill as the master of this game world. To use this book is to make Etharis your own. The ingredients of a fully realized world are provided to you within these pages to inspire your own tales, centered on characters who will change the history of the continent the moment they set foot upon it. Embrace this power. Grim Hollow and the world of Etharis belong to you now, and those you choose to share it with, to tell your own stories of darkness and hope.

data.name: Chapter 2: The Bürach Empire
entries: Languishing in the withered shadow of its former glory, the Bürach Empire is a land of relentless greed and infamous struggles for power and control. With its faith fractured and its people divided, the Hearthkeepers of Bürach manage the remains of this once-great nation. They siphon the flow of information to better control where it leads and upon which ears it lands, hoping to slow the dissolution of the empire's power and its people's hopes and dreams.

The empire still has an ambition that its might can no longer support, which only serves to fuel the flames of grim, and ultimately futile, determination. The Bürach people feel the growing urge to burst into a blaze of glory rather than allow their legacy to be left like an untended pyre of embers in winter, waiting to succumb to the cold.

Having once ruled over the greatest stretch of territory in Etharis, the Bürach Empire's influence exceeds its current borders. From north to south, the great expansion once saw the golden age of the empire's most daring dreams made reality, as nearly the entire continent flew the flag of the Bürach Empire.

However, with such a vast continent and foreboding barriers like the mountainous Grey Spines, the empire's conquests and desire for greener pastures ended. Soon after, the military resources necessary to maintain the ludicrous amount of territory grew too great. From this recession new nations were born, which drove the borders of the once-unstoppable superpower back to the heart of the continent.

From the ashes of what remains of the empire's most glorious days, echoes of their past victories ring out as its end draws near. In the face of its impending fate, this plagued lion is determined not to fade silently into obscurity.

entries.title: Abendland, Unterland, Bürach Empire, Rauland, Nordenland

entries.name: Geography
entries: The Bürach Empire's landscape has changed greatly over many years. Covered in a vast and deep forests, the tree line recedes ever so slowly to quench the empire's constant demand for lumber. While efforts to maintain the forests have proved effective, there is little that can prevent the empire from one day taking more from the forest than they plan to return.

entries.name: Western Bürach
entries: The density of trees in the west ranges from open and scenic to thick canopies that sunlight couldn't hope to pierce even on the brightest of days. Much is concealed inside of the woods that serve as the withering heart of the empire. The forest at the empire's center is home to a wide variety of tree types, tending toward oak, pine, and birch. However, the more westward one travels, isolated pockets of the forest occasionally receive bursts of humid air. This anomaly has transformed the local flora there into tangled jungles of unfamiliar plants.

While rain is plentiful in the forests of the empire, small valleys hidden within the deeper sections of the trees prove fatal as sudden flooding can catch unsuspecting travelers in a deluge that ends in unceremonious watery graves for the unwary.

The Empire's westernmost land comprises a rocky coast that hugs the Emerald Sea. The terrifying waters beyond the shore hold monstrosities and dangers far beyond what the imagination can conjure. The dual threat of sea monsters and Valikan raiders has made the coastal regions of {@area Abendland|01f|x} as treacherous for settlers as the deep forests of the heartland.

However, bold and brazen mercenaries and monster hunters of every type patrol the Bürach forests looking to make coin from commoners' troubles. The city of {@area Walstein|028|x}, now nothing more than an old ruin of ash and soot, has become a camping ground for adventurers seeking a challenge in the surrounding wilderness. It's said that even the Empire's soldiers are sent to Walstein to train and hone their combat prowess, as the area is riddled with monsters of all kinds.

For those who travel by the roads, the forests prove treacherous but remain traversable. Nevertheless, wise travelers choose the widest roads and the shortest routes between settlements wherever possible. Over the years, the roads have become broader as a result of lumberjacks shaving the forests' edges back from the established trade routes. Regardless of their size, in the Bürach Empire, all roads lead to Altenheim.

The capital city overlooks the miracle that is Lake Osea, crowning the great lake's southern shore with its high walls and great towers. The great City of Joy flourishes despite the hardships of the Empire's smaller settlements. Like a leech syphoning the nutrients from a healthy body, Altenheim draws all the wealth and produce from the surrounding provinces into its own coffers.

entries.name: Southern Bürach
entries: The southernmost part of the Bürach Empire is occupied by the Rock-Teeth Mountains. Narrow and dangerous roads wind through valleys and along cliffs to ancient mines and forgotten settlements. But the Rock-Teeth Mountains are also home to one of the greatest wonders the Bürach Empire has ever produced: the tiered city known as Stehlenwald.

{@area Stehlenwald|032|x} resembles a celestial stairway carved from granite and marble and adorned with great statues—a feat of architecture and engineering unrivalled throughout Etharis. The forges of Stehlenwald spew fire and ash endlessly. The city's main trade is in metalworks: jewelry, tools, machinery, and above all, weaponry.

Stehlenwald does not sit at the peak of the Rock-Teeth Mountains. The summit belongs to an enormous palace of opalescent towers and silken banners. Living inside this collection of spires is an archmage by the name of Martorius. Swearing no allegiance to any nation, he claims to predate the Bürach Empire and is invested in how it shall progress in the years to come.

entries.name: Eastern Bürach
entries: Following the eastern roads along the foot of the Grey Spine mountain range is {@area Unterland|025|x}, a marshland comprised of weathered buildings and battle-hardened soldiers. Great care has gone into maintaining the roads that pass through the swamps as it attempts to swallow any structures built there.

Reinforced and regularly repaired, the roads stand out among the old buildings that form the city of Olstenburg and the town of Vollendorf. Both locations rely heavily on the trade routes that pass through them, with the surrounding land yielding few resources beyond rich hunting grounds.

The fortress city Grabenstein blocks the mountain pass through the Grey Spine, guarding against the great shadow of Ostoya. The city's forges bellow. Its towers are guarded by hardened soldiers. Yet its ancient walls crumble, the city having been conquered and rebuilt by Bürach several times.

entries.name: Northern Bürach
entries: {@area Nordenland|021|x} stands between the icy chill of the north and the warm winds of the south. The people who call this place home are accustomed to constant rain and storms, and the greatest hazard by far is the regular river flooding. The endless turbulence makes traversing Nordenland arduous.

Settling the area is nearly impossible for outsiders. Many homes in this region are left abandoned to floodwater or ruinous winds, just as new homes are constructed in a safer location. Ultimately, most who can't tolerate this seminomadic lifestyle have since fled to the city of Landhaus or made the journey to reach Runeheim.

Mt. Vengeance lords over the surrounding mountains with a regal, volcanic fury. Its active eruptions disrupt travelers and worsen the looming storms covering the area. This once-dormant volcano awakened during Gods' End and has grown more restless ever since.

In the wake of divine devastation, the volcano has become a place of intrigue for adventurers and scholars alike. While few visited the volcano before the death of the gods, since its awakening, many have traveled there to investigate why its activity has become so hostile and so constant after centuries of silence. Among the ash and waves of heat are intense pulses of arcane power.

The ever-superstitious Norden fear the mountain must be casting judgment down upon the Empire's sorrowful state. Others believe it's a portal erupting up from the Netherworld in the aftermath of the Gods' deaths, spewing out all manner of hellish monsters into the mortal realm. Of the mercenaries, mages, and monster hunters who've dared to investigate the volcano, only scorched survivors emerge with frightful tales of the monsters lurking within.

Researchers are growing ever more concerned that Mt. Vengeance's eruptions and tremors are growing more frequent for seemingly no reason. The mountain spreads its ashes over the Empire as far as Duesenberg.

entries.name: Varied Landscapes
entries: The variety of land types that comprises the Bürach Empire has made its people diverse, adaptable, and innovative when it comes to surviving.

Between the marshlands, the forests, the mountain valleys, and the coastlines, Bürach has amassed a wealth of natural resources. What one region lacks due to constant storms or infertile land, another can provide through trade. Communal prosperity through commerce greatly contributed to the Empire's growth, and these mercantile practices leak into other nations.

Provided the plants continue to grow, the rains bring fresh water, and the mines yield enough minerals, the Empire has always been thought able to survive any calamity it may have to endure. A lack of trade, due to the hazards and monsters spreading through the empire, adds to the darkening mood of Bürach's citizenry.
Each citizen claims their province is the most beautiful. If you've seen one ugly mountain forest, you've seen them all.

entries.name: History
entries: A nation born in the {@area Era of Expansion|038|x} with the crowning of the first Emperor Indorius I, the Bürach Empire came into the world of Etharis as a child of necessity and emerged as a titan of prosperity. Their might was renowned. Their luxuries were plentiful. Standing at the pinnacle of what a civilization can achieve, progress became inertia propelled by their success. Not without its hardships or sacrifices, the Empire's determination to touch the heights of the sky began by claiming all that the soil had to offer.

The smaller kingdoms and tribes of central Etharis first became united by their faith in the Four Divines: Aurelia, Galt, Ulmyr, and Maligant. This pantheon chose Indorius I as the one to begin forging their new empire. As a boy named Thancred, the future emperor was discovered by an order of priests called the Hearthkeepers.

Acting on visions from their gods, the priests witnessed Thancred return a lamb to life with his bare hands, demonstrating his divine providence. As one, the priests fell to their knees and named him Emperor Indorius I, first of his line. To aid in his task of unifying the realms, Emperor Indorius I received four gifts filled with the divine power of each of the gods.
From the pure wish of healing and protection, Aurelia brought forth a breastplate like no other. Gifted to him, she hoped it would forevermore shield his bloodline from harm.

From the beacon of his everlasting authority, Galt forged a crowned circlet crafted from golden sovereignty. Bestowing it to Indorius I, Galt demanded that the Emperor's lineage know not only how a nation is valued, but how much the ruler serves a nation by the laws they enact.

From a longing to see and understand, Ulmyr removed their eye and placed it directly into the Emperor's hand. Blessing them with wisdom and the power of foresight, they imparted words of warning: "Consistency brings comfort while complacency can only bear the fruit of calamity."

From his side, Maligant unsheathed a longsword and presented it to the Emperor. In doing this, Maligant cut the emperor's finger and revealed the blade's sacred name as soon as his blood touched its edge: {@item Dämglesvir|GrimHollowCG24}, the "Might of Heaven." Maligant praised Indorius I on the victories yet to come and told him that a sword wielded by a coward can cut nothing. To be mighty is to bow only to the dawn of what cannot be changed.

With indomitable blessings laid down by the heavens upon the Bürach Empire's gleaming fate, there was none that could oppose them. One region after another fell into line, as the empire marched, slaughtered, negotiated, and conquered their neighboring territories. With their bold momentum, they saw fit to unite the world and stamp out their foes. For the glory of the Four Divines the provinces put their faith in the emperor's leadership and confidence.
An adventurer, huh?
Keep a safe distance and we'll get along famously.

entries.name: Gods' End
entries: The four Divine Gifts were far more valuable than anyone could have known. Within each of the Sacred Artifacts, all the gods had stowed a small part of themselves. More than just a fraction of their powers or will, inside these items held the manifestations of the very essence of each god.

None could have predicted the disaster that would come from the trust the gods put into Emperor Indorius I's descendants. Out of love for the first emperor's reign, the Hearthkeepers enacted the Edict of Eternal Blood, which decreed only the emperor's direct descendants could be raised to the throne. Purity was maintained within the coming imperial generations. Decade after decade, the emperors resembled their glorious ancestor less and less.

After the birth of Leopold I, the Bürach aristocracy feared the day he would be crowned. Leopold I's troubles revealed themselves in his youngest years. He was an impatient and insensitive child, very often cruel. He would imprison or torture servants and subjects for even minor infractions. He insisted on executions at noble feasts, like the executioners were minstrels performing for his guests. His early life was spent terrorizing those under his protection and betraying the people who wanted to believe in him the most.
But of all the mindlessly cruel acts he became the source of, the worst was how Leopold I tainted the Sacred Artifacts and by consequence, the very gods themselves.

In an apparent religious fervor—though it's debated whether his faith was genuine or another cruel manipulation—Leopold I declared an imperial order that the Bürach Empire would now only worship the One True Deity, branding the remaining three as usurper deities. He never named which god would serve as the Empire's sole patron.

Like wildfire, feuds sparked into the unforgivable inferno of war. Each region insisted their favored god was the One True Deity that would lead the Empire into its next glorious age of victory. The Four Divines themselves began to quarrel. The minds of the faithful were set aflame as the gods' once beautiful ideals tarnished into obsessive and unyielding rhetoric. The Four Divines no longer saw the potential in the Bürach Empire's capacity for greatness in unity, but now were bitter rivals that demanded absolute devotion to only themselves.

To end the violence, individuals from the four provinces conspired against the emperor. Their assassins swept into the imperial palace and killed Leopold I, and with his death his influence on the gods through the Sacred Artifacts diminished.

Aurelia was the first to awaken from her futile vanity and realize the gods' battles would soon destroy Etharis. Using all her divine strength, she shielded the mortal realm right at the moment of utter calamity.

From the sky, like comets or shooting stars, fell countless sparks of glittering light to the earth. Each one had been an angel in service to the gods, and their downfall was the devastating portent that the gods were no more.

entries.name: Aftermath
entries: In the wake of the Gods' End, every region of the Bürach Empire faces hardships threatening to crumble their greatest achievements and cripple their hopes of better days to come.

Though the Hearthkeepers ordained a new emperor with forged Sacred Artifacts, and the bonds of shared history kept Bürach from disbanding entirely, the civil war left deep divisions. As a result, border disputes have become more frequent, folk have grown paranoid, and travelers are often greeted with hostility.

Furthermore, the true Sacred Artifacts that had protected the realm and been a symbol of the Emperors' divine right of rule went missing. After absconding with them from the imperial palace, Leopold I's assassins intended to bring the Artifacts to {@area Nordenland|021|x}. They hoped the wizards could reverse whatever eldritch corruption had overtaken them. They never reached their intended destination.

To this day, the fate of the Sacred Artifacts remains a mystery. Most believe the assassins, each loyal to different provinces, quarreled among themselves for possession of the holy treasures. The treasures were separated to stop any single individual from controlling the fate of the Bürach Empire, and they have since been lost to history.

A more theological tale says the Artifacts blazed with holy light and vanished before the eyes of their possessors. Only one who can bring all four back together is worthy to wield them and claim Indorius I's legacy.

entries.name: Rising Shadows
entries: With the absence of the gods, a new force of dread now stalks the lands of the Bürach Empire, a powerful and corrupting presence that spreads through the realm like rot within an apple. Where it wanders, death follows. Hearts are poisoned and minds are turned to wickedness. No commoner dares even whisper its name; such is the fear of this rising shadow.

Aberrations have appeared in its wake. The forests of {@area Abendland|01f|x} are filled with many-limbed mutants, their flesh twisted into tortured shapes. They attack wayfarers and set upon villages. Nordenland is plagued by corrupted flora, including trees with human limbs and faces instead of knot holes and branches. Wolf-headed monsters prowl the hills of {@area Rauland|023|x}, leaving settlements abandoned wherever they roam. {@area Unterland|025|x} has reported sightings of corrupted warriors. Their Armor is melted into their flesh. Their Weapons are extensions of their limbs, and they hunt from place to place, killing indiscriminately.

The sigil of this horror has been found scrawled on church doors and in basements—a deer skull crowned with many antlers. There are no survivor accounts—at least none that can be trusted. It's described as a behemoth wreathed in shadows, whose body is that of a rotting corpse and whose bony crown rakes through the clouds. Its trumpeting shriek transforms people into monsters. None know what it is.
It has no other name than "the Great Beast."

entries.name: Provinces
entries: The mighty Bürach Empire is comprised of four provinces: {@area Abendland|01f|x} as its heart, {@area Nordenland|021|x} as its mind, {@area Rauland|023|x} holds a pickaxe, and {@area Unterland|025|x} carries a sword.

Each province began as its own unique kingdom or culture. The natural resources of Abendland's fertile woods made them a mercantile kingdom of great wealth and influence. Magic and philosophy defined the complex cultures of Nordenland. Rauland was a land of builders who resisted conquest through technology. Meanwhile, the proud Unterfolk keep a long military tradition stretching deep into their history as warring tribes.

At the Empire's height, the provinces worked in harmony for the sake of Bürach's bright future. Yet the Empire has never been homogenous. In the wake of the Gods' End, the cultural divides between the provinces have only grown deeper, and mutual prosperity has given way to fear and self-interest.

entries.name: Abendland
entries: Abendland is the region where Aurelia, goddess of protection, healing, and the hearth found worship. It is also where the holy church of the Hearthkeepers began. According to scripture, Aurelia instructed her first priestess, Amara, to strike the ground with a spear, from which a wellspring of water emerged. This spring swelled into the blessed Lake Osea, which fed the surrounding land until it grew into a verdant forest. The city of Altenheim was built upon Lake Osea's southern bank, and from its cathedral the Hearthkeepers spread their holy message.

Abendland is rich in farmland and timber, but its most valuable commodity has always been religion. Scripture marks the province as the birthplace of Indorius I. With such a blessed history and divine authority, the Hearthkeepers controlled the fates of many who sought the favor of the gods.

To maintain their influence in the aftermath of the Gods' End, the Hearthkeepers were swift to crown an imperial successor, a distant relation of Leopold I. However, in most recent times the church's grip on power may finally be loosening, for good or ill.

After Emperor Aratron II was tragically lost at sea along with his imperial ship, the pregnant Empress Valeria was sheltered and protected by the Hearthkeepers at all times. Her only public appearances were alongside Pope Roland Murrich, head of the church. After Empress Valeria gave birth, the Hearthkeepers were quick to coronate the newborn infant and shield him from public view or influence.

Yet the citizenry of the Bürach Empire cries out for governance. Faith in the Hearthkeepers has waned with the absence of divine magic. The realm teems with monsters, internal strife, and foreign threats—compounded by an empty throne. In answer, the nobility have established their own authority: the Säullan Council.

Self-appointed and defiant of church authority, the Säullan Council comprises powerful leaders from each of the Empire's provinces. Though far from politically united, the Council negotiates trade and resources between the provinces to deal with the Empire's many troubles. Once the young Emperor Odalric I grew into his station, the Säullan Council began serving as close advisors and soon superseded the Hearthkeepers as the Emperor's most trusted aids.

Over the years, Pope Murrich has grown weary and ineffectual. With his protégé, Aratron II, dead, and Emperor Odalric I distrustful of the church, many under Murrich have questioned how many years of service he has left.

Of all the choices that Emperor Odalric I has made during his ascension to the throne, the most controversial has been the permanent installation of the Säullan Council as an advisory body. While it was believed that the council would be disbanded when the emperor came of age, Odalric I insisted they remain to offer their knowledge and perspective.

At the time, the Emperor cited his inexperience as the primary reason for this choice. Others wonder if the seeds of mistrust had been planted in the young ruler's mind, and the Emperor clearly seeks the advice of people not directly tied to the Church of the Hearthkeepers.

Meanwhile, tensions within the Hearthkeepers rise. Many feel Pope Murrich no longer commands the influence over the throne he once did. For this reason, the church quietly searches for Murrich's successor while it waits for his death—timely or otherwise.

Meanwhile, a bishop in Altenheim has set the hearts of the faithful ablaze with his impassioned and prophetic sermons. His surging popularity among the crowds has led to an eventual summons to speak with Odalric I at the Imperial Palace. Little is known of Karlos Reinach's life before joining the Hearthkeepers, yet the church has seen mounting support for him to succeed Murrich, if indeed Karlos has captured the Emperor's ear.

entries.name: Notable Individuals

items.name: Emperor Odalric Pongor Aurelius I
items.entry: Practically coronated in the womb, no other child's birth was more anticipated than Odalric I. The babe knew nothing of his father, Aratron II, and was raised between his mother Valeria and Pope Murrich. Throughout his life, the empress and the pope would hover over him in desperate attempts to mold his mind.

entries: However, once introduced to the Säullan Council, Odalric I became fascinated in learning about the world outside Altenheim. He desires to understand the threats facing his empire. Odalric I spends long hours questioning members of the Säullan Council, learning economics from Lady Büchandt, strategy from {@area Lord General Roemer|026|x}, production from Master Lorand, and philosophy from Professor Welgirson. The council members share their vast expertise openly, fostering close relations with the young Emperor.
It isn't certain whether Odalric I has matured enough to understand the difference between honest guidance and political coercion from his councilors.

items.name: Bishop Karlos Reinach
items.entry: A quiet man of the cloth, Bishop Reinach's contemplative manner disguises the fiery passion of his faith, which explodes forth during his sermons. Reinach preaches that the Gods' End was only the first omen of the apocalypse. The second is the Great Beast.

entries: The Hearthkeepers have, until now, decried any public mention of the Great Beast as heresy. This makes Reinach a controversial figure within the church. Yet the Bishop's unorthodox preaching has captured the attention of the Emperor, creating a pathway to bring Odalric I back into the Hearthkeepers' sphere of influence.

Nevertheless, members of the church remain suspicious of Bishop Reinach, whose history before the Hearthkeepers is mysterious. Rumors designed to discredit him link Reinach to the Celestial Gate—a doomsday cult that praises the Great Beast and claims the world's end is inescapable.

items.name: Gatekeeper Chantal Sommerkin
items.entry: Gatekeeper Sommerkin is dead. Or that is what the rulers of Abendland wish to believe. Sommerkin led a dangerous cult called the Celestial Gate, preaching that the Great Beast was divine retribution for all humanity's corruption. The Hearthkeepers had her assassinated to prevent such heretical lies.

entries: Despite this, Sommerkin's blasphemous writings continue to circulate. Though the Celestial Gate was disbanded, her disciples quietly infiltrate each tier of society. Their goal has remained the same—turn Abendland into a theocracy in preparation for the end of the world. Previously, they aimed to depose the emperor for this cause. Now subtle influence is their weapon of choice. This change in direction has led to the frightening suspicion that the Gatekeeper herself is still alive, scribbling her heresy in some dark place.

entries.name: Nordenland
entries: Nordenland is old. Its living cultures predate recorded history. The land is rich with the spark of magic, entwining Norden traditions with arcane practices and spiritual communing. The people of Nordenland have less fear of magic than other Bürach citizens. Their leaders have weaved the power of the arcane since the god Ulmyr first taught them how to grasp it.

Diversity runs through the cultures of Nordenland. While folk who reside in the west hew to the Bürach Empire and its traditions, the further north and east one travels, the closer the Norden begin to resemble their Valikan neighbors.

When Emperor Indorius I was crowned, Ulmyr became part of a united pantheon. Many Norden followed the wisdom of their deity, and Nordenland became a province of the growing Bürach Empire. Yet the eastern settlements preferred their own ancient ways to the laws of a foreign power, resisting the absorption of their territory.

The Raulfolk came to harvest timber and metal for their workshops, logging the forests and cutting open the earth. The Hearthkeepers came to spread their religion, chasing away the primal spirits the Norden communed with. Soldiers from {@area Unterland|025|x} arrived to enforce the empire's laws and tithes. Norden fought for a time, but as they were outnumbered, they eventually had to accept the empire's embrace. Their simmering anger never waned.

In the aftermath of the Gods' End, the Norden has abandoned their oaths to the Bürach Empire and reverted to their old traditions. They live closer to nature, refusing to fell living trees or lift stones without first beseeching the primal spirits to which their ancestors spoke. While some villages make sacrifices to the Primordials, other settlements whisper to the faerie folk who dwell in the deep wildernesses of the north.

Nordenland has long been the focal point of magical studies in the Bürach Empire, both to their acclaim and their great folly. The city called Ulmyr's Gate is no longer found on any map.
