entries.name: Defeat the Gravekeeper
entries: Viscount Warlow has decreed it illegal to attack the Gravekeeper. Its undead visage gives credence to the Crimson Court's authority and keeps the populace afraid. However, Sister Abigail wants it destroyed. She's secretly offering a reward to anyone who can kill the Gravekeeper and prove it will never return. Doing so would require the player characters to descend deep into the mausoleum's undead-infested catacombs and discover what curse binds the Gravekeeper to its eternal servitude.

entries.name: Curse Breakers
entries: A common folktale shared in Fallowheart is about Thaddeus Grisham, the eccentric wizard who once lived in the tower in {@area Est Vale|08b|x}. The story goes that he was an advisor to the dukes of {@area Kaer Vena|090|x} before {@area The Darkfall|080|x}. The tower held the realm's criminals given to Grisham by the Grand Duke, and the mage would conduct hideous experiments upon them at his own whim. When the moon hides its face behind the clouds and Fallowheart is darkest, some say the screams of Grisham's prisoners can still be heard from within.

The truth is that Thaddeus Grisham was corrupted by Shadowsteel. He practiced weaving curses and crafting objects using the profane metal. His prisoners were indeed the victims of such magic; they now infest the tower as cursed monsters, escaped from the cages that once contained them.

Grisham himself still haunts the tower's highest level as a {@creature Shadowsteel Ghast|GHMG}. He's protected by the same arcane traps and conjured elementals that guard his treasures. Grisham's faculties are no longer entirely his own, but he is covetous of his Shadowsteel and his arcane library. He is hostile to anyone entering his tower unbidden.

From the outside, Grisham's Tower doesn't appear to pose any great danger to Fallowheart. Yet both Viscount Warlow and Sister Abigail know its rumored treasures and secrets could be powerful in the hands of their enemy.

entries.name: Church and State
entries: Two armies camp outside Fallowheart's walls like rival wolves snarling over a carcass. On the northern bank of the Arteriyan River fly the banners of the Raevo rebellion, while outside the southern gates are soldiers loyal to {@area Soma|06e|x} and the Court. Neither is eager for open bloodshed. The ties of kinship run through the city on both sides. But amongst this tension, even the slightest provocation could explode into open war.

Within this maelstrom, Sister Abigail quietly searches for brave confidants she can entrust with an inglorious quest—to assassinate the Viscount. She believes the only way to break the stalemate in Raevo's favor is to kill Warlow and prove the {@area Crimson Court|05c|x} isn't invincible. Sister Abigail can't risk open defiance or treachery herself; such provocation would give the Viscount pretext to arrest her as a traitor and demolish the cathedral, snuffing the last stronghold for Raevo within the city.
For these reasons, Sister Abigail only offers this quest to folk she trusts, such as those who might have destroyed the Gravekeeper or defended the cathedral from Volitant spies.

Entering Kaer Vena will be difficult. The player characters could arrive under false pretenses to petition the Viscount. They might alternatively discover the secret passages in the cliffs beneath the castle walls, which lead into Kaer Vena's tombs. Regardless, those who do gain entry would face many loyal soldiers, vampiric spawn, and other undead horrors beside the Viscount in Kaer Vena's darkened halls.

entries.name: Snuff the Candle of Hope
entries: Few events in Fallowheart occur without the Viscount's knowledge. Those who perform notable deeds are frequently summoned to Kaer Vena if the Viscount believes they may be of service to him. Such feats could include routing smugglers from the Ebon Syndicate or breaking the curse on Grisham's Tower. Currently, the Viscount seeks individuals of a particular skillset for an unscrupulous task—to kill every soul in Saint Dayla's Cathedral and burn it to the ground.

Unwilling to make martyrs of Sister Abigail and her congregation, the Viscount refuses to send soldiers to perform public slayings. Furthermore, Saint Dayla's Cathedral is consecrated ground; neither Warlow nor his vampiric spawn can enter it themselves. Therefore, this quest will be entrusted to those the Viscount can deny connection with.

Sister Abigail and her congregation are not formidable warriors. They're defended by a small militia of Raevo soldiers, though their true protector is {@area Solyma's Champion|094|x}, the {@b {@creature Pactbreaker|GHMG}}. Defeating the celestial construct and slaying Sister Abigail will close the Crimson Court's bloody grip on Fallowheart and earn high rewards from Viscount Warlow.

entries.name: The Free City of Malkovia
entries: The "Free City" of Malkovia is conflicted in its identity and allegiance. Bisected by the {@area Roiling River|09f|x} and split between warring provinces, Malkovia was founded as an international market that accepted business from everywhere in Etharis. The city has had to fight fiercely to maintain their open trade policies. After the Darkfall, Malkovia became torn, as both {@area Raevo|066|x} and {@area Soma|06e|x} clutched for control of the city's location and commerce, making it vulnerable to swindlers and opportunists. Anything goes in Malkovian markets, so long as you pay the proper taxes.

While dozens of bridges cross the Roiling River along its length, delivering merchants and wayfarers from one side to the other, Malkovia sits upon the arterial road that connects Raevo to the rest of Ostoya. No less than seven bridges provide access from one side of the river to the other. The city itself is a network of slick streets and cramped alleys, crowded with folk moving from one market or warehouse to the other. Something is being sold on every street and at every corner, either from shops, stalls, or sleeves. The affluent and empowered reap excessive profits from their vast ventures, while the common citizens on the street fight the crushing weight of poverty while fending off each other.

The Raevan rebellion and Crimson Court both endeavor to pressure the Free City into joining their cause. Although Malkovian leadership has cannily resisted these advances, the commoners of Malkovia have been keen to listen. The Northside favors the rebellion, denouncing the vampiric nobility of Ostoya as godless monsters. Conversely, the Southside favors the Court, preferring the security of a united empire and the imposing strength of supernatural leadership. Mariska Király, the Matriarch of Malkovia, remains staunch in her position that the city remains independent. But her subordinates may have other ideas.

entries.name: Geography
entries: Malkovia's architecture is as pointed, intricate, and arching as any city in Ostoya. Countless steeples point toward the night sky. Statues of weeping seraphs, hooded knights, and hackled werewolves brood beneath curved alcoves. While dark deeds go unnoticed in darkened alleys, the winding, cobblestoned streets are witnessed by the glowing eyes of each houses' lantern-lit windows.
The two halves of Malkovia are known simply as Northside and Southside of the Roiling River. This arbitrary divide that has nonetheless resulted in diverging communities.

Multistoried terraces of ornate stonework are far more common on the Northside. Here, Malkovia's wealthy and influential make their homes. The center of Northside is dominated by the wharf district, made of warehouses and dock workers, but wide roads make it easy for black carriages to ferry the nobility through the less-desirable parts of town.

By contrast, the infrastructure in Southside has fallen into disrepair. The oldest parts of the city were flooded during the Darkfall and never properly restored, making each home a breeding ground for mold, assuming it still has all four walls. Though Malkovia's vast markets operate on the Southside, the wealth they generate does not remain there.

entries.name: The Roiling River
entries: The Roiling River carves across the entire width of northern Ostoya. It starts beneath the fires Mt. Vengeance in the Grey Spine, boiling its way through the realm and cooling in temperature and temperament before it reaches Malkovia.

The river is the lifeblood of the city. It's essential for Malkovia's renowned mercantile industry, but also for daily use as a source of food, transport, and a place to dump refuse. The water is pierced with a network of drains and pipes. Downriver is littered with filth and sewage that obscure the murky depths below. Impoverished "River Rats" throw nets into the waste to catch discarded valuables or swim along the riverbed, raking up the muck and sediment in hopes of discovering small treasures.

The Roiling River averages nine hundred feet across as it passes through Malkovia. Tiny uninhabited islands dot the surface. Rushing waters cascade over muddy shoals and between bridge supports, requiring skilled captains to navigate their boats along the city's length. The banks are thick with mud and marshland reeds, home to several species of birds, fish, eels, and very often, monsters.

entries.name: Fissure Lake
entries: During {@area The Darkfall|080|x}, a destructive fissure opened on the Southside of Malkovia and split the Roiling River. As undead poured out of the earth, water torrented in, draining the river and dragging ships, buildings, and people into the darkness of the unknown caverns below. In the aftermath of the disaster, a dam was constructed to seal off the newly formed lake and correct the river's flow. Today, Fissure Lake remains as a haunting reminder of the countless souls consumed by the Darkfall, and the fragility of life within the Ostoyan Empire.

Fissure Lake is filled with ghost stories. Many fear that openings to the {@area City Below|073|x} are still present under the water. Indeed, sodden corpses shamble onto the banks from time to time, and spirits are said to drift upon the lake's surface. Nobody dares fish or sail upon those waters.

Perhaps the most frightening rumor is that the lake is slowly draining. That year after year, the surface is lowering. It is feared that one day soon it will be a wide-open door to the underworld once more.

entries.name: Founding the Free City
entries: Spreading north from the Elian Pass, many of the first Ostoyan migrants settled along the Roiling River. Others followed it eastward. They searched for the perfect place to call their home, trekking through the Dark Wood, eventually arrived at a looming structure built from rock and bone: an immense and imposing bridge. This solitary crossing empowered the settlers to utilize both sides of the river and send boats westward to trade with others. And so here, they started to build their city.

Malkovia quickly became a booming trade hub, attracting merchants from across Etharis wishing to access the riches of a burgeoning empire. Inevitably, as often occurs in mercantile cities, circles of illicit activity arose as Malkovia became an attractive base of operations for smugglers and thieves.

entries.name: The Fight for Freedom
entries: When the Bürach Empire marched to claim sovereignty over Ostoya, Malkovia became critical for funding the war effort and recruiting foreign mercenaries. Rarely had the warfront threatened the city directly. That was, until the Darkfall.

When the sun vanished, Malkovia was plunged into darkness and terror. Undead clambered from a gaping fissure that opened on the river's southern bank, unleashing death and destruction on an unprecedented scale. Unlike Nov Ostoya, there were no heroes or armies to protect Malkovia. Instead, there was simply the river. The crag filled with water, flooding away the undead who would have otherwise consumed the city, but dragging many commoners into the cold darkness with it. By the time the last glint of sunlight was gone, the fissure had become full. The black waters brimmed with wreckage and corpses.

The Büracher retreated, the Crimson Court seized power, and the Raevans rebelled. Malkovia suddenly found itself trapped on the front of a new war—one that expected the city to pick a side. Both the {@area Crimson Court|05c|x} and the Council of Mages have made their case for Malkovia's loyalty, wielding promises and threats in equal measure. As with {@area Fallowheart|07c|x}, the Court prefers not to incite rebellion through military action, which could unite the citizens of Malkovia with soldiers from Castalore. Meanwhile, Malkovia gives few concessions and pays no tax to Raevo, ensuring continued trade partnerships with southern Ostoya.
Unfortunately for both sides, Malkovia's preferred form of independence is absolute. The city's markets are open to all. Mortals and monsters alike.

entries.name: A Tenuous Balance
entries: Today, Malkovia remains a crowded city. Folk arrive every day seeking the safety of a larger settlement, a reprieve from the blood tax, or the opportunity for work. But as the population rises, so do tensions. The sprawling city is more divided than ever.

The prosperity of Northside is looked upon jealously by the commoners of the south. The free flow of resources from {@area Raevo|066|x} is on full display, alongside the untaxed wealth imported through trade with other nations.

Residents of Northside do not give their blood to the Ostoyan nobility. {@area Oleg the Butcher|0b4|x}, overboss of the northern districts, pays the hunting guilds personally to punish vampires who take blood from north of the river. Support for the Raevan rebellion is not uncommon, although outwardly campaigning for the cause is swiftly silenced, both because it diminishes the power of Matriarch Király and invites retribution from the Crimson Court.

Meanwhile, the influence of the Court is strongly felt in Southside. Their progeny hunt openly to exact the blood tax, while many residents give their blood willingly to win favor from agents of the vampires.

Malkovia has not received a direct visit from a member of the Crimson Court in some time. Many residents welcome it, however. A strange religion revering the vampires as seraphs has emerged in the Southside of Malkovia, spreading quite strongly, even bleeding into Northside.

entries.name: Notable Locations
entries: Besides the Northside and Southside, Malkovia is further divided into six smaller districts. This proves to be essential knowledge to any visitor who doesn't wish to become lost in Malkovia's dark and narrow alleyways.

entries.name: Old Malkovia
entries: The eastmost district north of the river, "Old" Malkovia is the place where the first structures of the city were built. It's often the first district incoming ships see when they glimpse Malkovia's spires, and its historic architecture from the early days of the city are among the most ornate and finely crafted. The proud bloodlines of old Malkovia date back to the city's founding. The district is wealthy, but it is mostly comprised of old nobility of lesser houses.

entries.name: The Ossuary Bridge
entries: There are seven major bridges within Malkovia that cross the {@area Roiling River|09f|x}. None is more recognizable or infamous as the Ossuary Bridge.

Constructed from the skulls, bones, and skeletons of countless people, the Ossuary Bridge was discovered by the migrant Ostoyans who would found Malkovia. Masterfully crafted with pillars of vertebrae, ribcage columns, and a wide street of teeth and bones laid like cobblestones, the bridge was a lone edifice surrounded by swamp. Unsettled by its appearance but in need of its utility, the first Malkovians settled the north bank.

The name "Malkovia" comes from this very bridge. Carved into the stone along its length, the phrase "KREKI STOMALK OVIAVINDI" is prominently carved. However, when the bridge was discovered, mud and rubble obscured most of the phrase, displaying only "MALK OVIA." After the discovery of the {@area City Below|073|x}, the phrase has since been translated to mean "all are equal in death."

The Ossuary Bridge remains to this day, unshaken and undamaged by even {@area The Darkfall|080|x}. As the eastmost bridge of Malkovia, every ship must enter and leave beneath it. An architectural wonder, its origin had remained a mystery until discovering the City Below, where familiar mosaics of tooth and bone can also be found.

entries.name: Ralakia
entries: Across the water from Old Malkovia is the Southside district named Ralakia, now derisively called Lake Town. Flooded during the Darkfall and the formation of {@area Fissure Lake|0a0|x}, most of the water never drained away, leaving behind permanent marshland.

The most destitute of the districts, Lake Town is a labyrinth of packed slums and tenements. The folk here are often overlooked and ignored. They survive through their own grit, scrounging for food in the waters, and begging for coins.

entries.name: The Clocktowers
entries: The three watch towers that overlook Malkovia from an eastern bend in the river were originally built to protect the city from Valikan longboats. Northern raiders were a frequent threat in Malkovia's earlier history. Since the Darkfall has enveloped Ostoya, only especially ambitious or brash Valikans would dare attack such an established settlement.

The towers have since been purchased by a gnomish engineer named Ciprian Johann, who modified each with brass plates and internal mechanisms. Every tower has its own clockface. Their collective chimes create an hourly symphony of thunderous bells that hauntingly echo over Malkovia.

The moonlit towers have become a landmark of the city. Their tolling begins and ends the day. Shipping crews keep their schedules synchronized to the hands of the clocks. However, what occurs inside the towers is ever a mystery, as Ciprian Johann permits only himself and his engineering apprentices inside.

entries.name: Chimera Wharf
entries: Chimera Wharf has grown to dominate Northside as the largest district, encompassing docks, workshops, warehouses, and union halls. Most residents are dockhands, sailors, merchants, or craftsmen. The workers here don't consider themselves wealthy, forced to pay union dues and city taxes, so when coin goes missing, most folk don't mind provided the amounts aren't undercutting profits.

The district draws its name from the monster hunters who helped found Malkovia, killing local threats to keep the infant settlement safe. A brass statue in their honor collects rust in a modest town square. It depicts a chimera slain beneath the blade of one of their forebears.

entries.name: Hunters Guildhall
entries: Today, the monster hunters' guildhall doesn't much reflect their warrior traditions. Unless a servant of the Crimson Court crosses over the river, there are fewer monsters to hunt in the heart of Malkovia compared to tax avoiders and petty thieves.

Only the older guild members have even been trained to hunt monsters properly. Newer recruits are little more than thuggish enforcers for the Matriarch, and their de facto guild leader, {@area Oleg the Butcher|0b4|x}.

entries.name: Óvrosz
entries: Óvrosz began as a separate settlement upriver from Malkovia, before urban expansion connected them. Home to the city's wealthiest, the nobles of Óvrosz enjoy the clearest waters of the Roiling River before it passes through the filth of Malkovia. More space means larger estates. The south end of the district opens entirely onto the Jade Marsh.

The marshland isn't suitable for building structures. Therefore, it remains a large midnight garden, groomed and cultivated on the coin of the nobility living in Óvrosz. Although open to the public, the nobility don't take kindly to the unwashed commoners who may visit.

entries.name: Mooreland Manor
entries: In the middle of Malkovia, the Roiling River splits around a low, crescent-shaped island known as the Mooreland. Once nothing but grass, reeds, and mud, the island now contains the large estate of Malkovia's ruler. For many generations that was the noble household, von Dülvet, until the family was massacred by the Matriarch of Malkovia.

Mooreland Manor is a lavish and multi-story mansion, surrounded by well-kept gardens and sculpted hedges. The manor and its grounds are used extensively for special occasions of both business and pleasure. An invitation from the Matriarch to attend her at her home could be a lucrative opportunity, but one that should be considered with caution. Business conducted on the Mooreland isn't always clean.

entries.name: Miskavir
entries: The most crowded district of Southside is Miskavir, home to the Etharin Market. Countless shops, stalls, and services shout for attention among the throng of visitors to Malkovia. The market is a gateway to the rest of the world. Charnelaise silks, Castinellan wines, Valikan tapestries, and even Büracher automatons find their way into Ostoya through Malkovia's markets, which isn't closely watched by either the {@area Crimson Court|05c|x} or the Council of Mages.

However, Miskavir is a perilous place. Deals are cut as often as throats. Such an unregulated marketplace attracts thieves and smugglers, some of which even sell body parts and viscera that can be offered as a blood tax on behalf of the Southside. Where these body parts are sourced would be a dangerous investigation.

entries.name: The Drain
entries: Somewhere deep underground, beneath the city streets of Miskavir, is a notorious fight pit known as the Drain. Combatants fight to the death within a grated cage, suspended above a funnel. The funnel catches all blood and gore lost from the warriors within, which is then offered to the Crimson Court to further cover Malkovia's blood tax. Fighters are encouraged to gut and bleed out their opponents, maximizing the blood collected and therefore the prize paid out.

The Drain is a common place for scum of all sorts to gather. Betting on fights is a grim avenue to escaping poverty. Voluntary fighters are rarer than involuntary ones, who are usually prisoners of the city, unfortunate travelers who upset a local, or political rivals to {@area Ylana Svarog|0b5|x}. The Drain is commonly a stage for executions and other tragic ends.

entries.name: Lupul
entries: To the far west in Southside is Lupul, casually called the banking district, where Malkovia stores much of its wealth. Most of the Southside nobility reside here. So too does a garrison of soldiers who protect the coin repositories and noble estates, though they seem more of a disheveled gang than a trained military.

Malkovia's money is reposited south of the {@area Roiling River|09f|x} to further avoid taxation from {@area Raevo|066|x}, in the event Castalore ever attempted to exert such authority over the city. Nevertheless, the banks are close to {@area Óvrosz|0ac|x}, so nobles never need travel far to count their coins.

In the darkness of Ostoya, the Ebon Syndicate uses the Malkovian banks to siphon funds for themselves. This arrangement is not entirely unknown, and the Syndicate is a quiet supporter of Matriarch Király for as long as she sanctions their activities. In exchange for the coin they steal from Malkovia, the Ebon Syndicate feeds information to the Matriarch from across Ostoya, acquires particularly difficult to obtain objects from distant places, and makes the Matriarch's most dangerous rivals disappear when the situation calls for subtlety.

entries.name: Using Malkovia in a Campaign
entries: Malkovia is a city in a perilous and unique situation, with its northern and southern sections split by the Roiling River, and its ruler attempting to keep the city free from the military and political turmoil that threatens to turn the place to a war zone. With so many potential conflicts and intrigues simmering just below the surface, and so many individuals and groups with competing goals in the same vicinity, Malkovia is a wonderful crucible in which to concoct memorable stories and adventures.

Many of the power groups with representatives in Malkovia are desperate to gain any kind of advantage over their enemies or to achieve their goals. People in this position, even those with a functional moral compass, can see the sacrifice of a few pawns in the larger game as a regrettable but necessary step in winning the larger conflict. These people may see the characters as just such pawns.

Characters might be sent on missions by any group, ostensibly to perform some noble tasks, when in reality the mission has a far more devious goal. If the characters realize that they've been misled, the people who've used them may blame the characters for any illegal or immoral activity—or try to eliminate them entirely from the board. Keep such traitorous and devious plots in mind as you imagine adventures taking place in Malkovia.

entries.name: Matriarch Mariska Király
entries: Mariska's story echoes many of the lost and nameless children of Malkovia. Born a street urchin and surviving only through cunning and skills she had beaten into her, Mariska's short life was almost ended when the waters broke the riverbanks during the Darkfall. A monster slayer named Grivr rescued her. A matter of right place, right time, as he often reminded Mariska.

Strict training and painful mutations preceded the young elf into adulthood. She was tempered into a deadly devourer -trained monster hunter whose legend quickly grew, and Grivr and Mariska hunted frequently south of Malkovia, where undead now roamed freely. This dangerous trade in a realm newly ruled by vampires cost Grivr his life.

Mariska's middle years were spent as a mercenary in the city, demanding a high price for her particular skills. While dissatisfaction mounted with Malkovia's rulership in the aftermath of the Darkfall, the monster hunter amassed wealth and connections with unions, syndicates, and merchants. When the ruling household had lost the faith of its people, Mariska landed her own militia upon the Mooreland and took Malkovia for herself.

Shortly after, she kicked down just about every wall of regulation that limited trade, flooding the city with wealth. She forged alliances among her connections to strengthen her militia. In Northside, the self-titled Matriarch is a champion of the people who keeps the armies of both {@area Soma|06e|x} and {@area Raevo|066|x} at bay. In Southside, Mariska is considered a thug, wielding threats and violence to enforce her regime.

Despite her success maintaining Malkovia's independence, Mariska is surrounded by vipers. Those who would rather give the city to Soma or Raevo, and those who would rather sit in the Matriarch's seat, wait for her downfall.

entries.name: Oleg the Butcher
entries: Oleg is the overboss of Malkovia's Northside, governing the districts and enforcing the city's laws on behalf of the Matriarch. Though order ultimately reigns in Malkovia, there's much Oleg is willing to let slide, provided city dues are still being paid.

Better known to his friends and enemies as the Butcher due to his skills learned as a monster hunter, Oleg's specialty is killing vampires who dare to cross the river. The grudgel's belt is laden with body parts harvested from his prey. When dealing with mortal criminals, Oleg doesn't hesitate to devour portions that inflict terrifying mutations, making himself stronger.

Having been trained by the Matriarch herself, Oleg's loyalty to Mariska has never been questioned. But he hates the vampires more than she. Becoming a devourer has changed his biology into a dhampir, a fact that Oleg mourns and loathes.

He meets frequently in secret with Raevan rebels and hopes to provide an edge to expanding their influence within the city. Should conflict erupt in Malkovia, the Butcher will gladly wet his axes with the blood of the damned.

entries.name: Ylana Svarog
entries: Once an exceptional student of the {@area Ravencourt Sanctuary|071|x}, Ylana Svarog is constantly underestimated in Malkovia due to her gnomish stature. Her enemies that consider Ylana easy prey are quickly reduced to windswept ashes.

Despite her arcane talents, Ylana never felt at ease among the rigid bureaucracy of Castalore or the Council of Mages, preferring the free-spirited culture of Malkovia. For a time, she was the court mage to the von Dülvet nobility. She won her position as overboss of Southside by helping facilitate Mariska's coup and has overseen the function of the city's markets and banks ever since.

Despite coming into power alongside the Matriarch, rumors persist that Ylana has become at odds with Mariska and Oleg. The gnomish wizard has found her own source of wealth and resources by dealing with delegates from the {@area Crimson Court|05c|x}, and she permits the spread of a dogma that worships the vampires as seraphs. Allowing this religiosity to grow in Malkovia erodes Mariska's, and therefore Ylana's, authority within the city. It seems unlikely the wizard would permit this if she didn't have certain assurances from {@area Soma|06e|x}.

entries.name: Marienne Marilyn
entries: Marienne was born into nobility but couldn't help herself from wanting more. Affluence bored her, and a dangerous thirst for excitement and eyes for opportunity led to her exploring a passion for thievery. Small thefts from other nobles weren't enough. Her indiscretions escalated into embezzlement, until she was caught and forced to flee from her former life in disgrace.

Starting over on the shadowy streets of Malkovia, Lady Marilyn already had contacts with the Ebon Syndicate and Matriarch Király. A confidant and mediator between the two, Marienne is now considered the head of the Ebon Syndicate in Malkovia and its most powerful agent in Ostoya. The moonlit city is the Syndicate's safest gateway into the empire. Its independence shields their activities from the talons of the Court or the eyes of the Council of Mages.

Despite her opulent upbringing, Lady Marilyn is not easily bullied or manipulated. What she lacks in grit she makes up for in charm and cunning. Her true occupation is a guarded secret. While Marienne and Mariska Király pretend at having a close friendship, the Matriarch is ever aware of the knife held against her throat, should her decisions inconvenience the Syndicate's operations in Malkovia.

entries.name: Father Elijah
entries: A tragic martyr. A divine prophet. A bloodthirsty deceiver. Father Elijah is all of these and more, depending on who is asked in Malkovia. Downcast from the heavens during the Gods' End, Elijah wandered Etharis without a cause. Faithless. Spent. That was, until he became a vampire.

Father Elijah's path is now strikingly clear. He is once more divine, a gift returned to him when the sanguine curse ran down his throat in rivulets. In his own words, this revealed to Elijah an ancient truth. Vampires are seraphs, born of a god buried in the furthest depths of the {@area City Below|073|x}, with a mandate to kill the faithless and elevate the worthy to conquer death entirely.

Elijah has proselytized across Ostoya, emboldening its dour citizens to hope, to yearn, to crave the promise of immortality. Most recently, the Father has taken up residence in southern Malkovia, intent to bring the Free City into the fold of the Crimson Court.

His speeches draw crowds who he enraptures with miracles made possible through both the power of vampirism and the dwindling sparks of his divinity. Father Elijah weaves songs, to which the congregation dance in celebration of life and weep at the finality of death, before he showers them with food and gifts brought from the broad farmlands of {@area Terinima|068|x}.

Even Malkovia's nobility find the Father fascinating. His promises of power through the divine providence of vampirism is intoxicating. He has masses of followers from every district of the city, threatening Malkovian leadership with a new banner for its people to unite beneath. Elijah has plans to do just that, and perhaps instead of indoctrinating the city into the influence of the Crimson Court, he'll take it for himself. After all, what's greater than a Grand Duke? A god.
Look at what he can do? How is that not a divine gift?

entries.name: Drowned Underworld
entries: Grimhilde is a strange and sordid Ralakian local. She often wanders into the Etharin Market, looking for daring individuals who might like to help her extract treasures from the depths of {@area Fissure Lake|0a0|x}. Having discovered {@item Gold|XDMG|Gold Coins} and strange trinkets on the lake's muddy banks, Grimhilde has convinced herself that further ancient riches must be hidden just below the dark water's surface. The urchin offers what she's found so far to help entice prospective divers.

The water beneath Fissure Lake is murky and filled with floating corpses. They drift in and out of view like fleeting nightmares, some thrashing about weightlessly in the silent gloom whenever light draws near.

Crushed buildings that fell into the lake during {@area The Darkfall|080|x} are scattered along the bottom, the haunting visage of a drowned city greeting divers who make it this far. Tunnels into the {@area City Below|073|x} proper can also be found. Bone-laced passageways and ancient chambers that have not been seen by mortal eyes in unknowable centuries. Some are still submerged in muddy water while others have been drained. The necropolis spreads out from beneath Fissure Lake to other parts of Malkovia, and possibly to other parts of Ostoya, if a journey far enough is made. The types of lost treasures and arcane artifacts that could be unearthed here are outnumbered only by the number of undead horrors lurking within this drowned underworld.

Fissure Lake can be explored across multiple expeditions, encountering different monsters and treasures each time. Grimhilde expects to share in any recovered wealth. They can, of course, be ignored but may seek restitution from the Southside overboss if they believe they've been cheated.

entries.name: Noble Prey
entries: Lord Rathulf is a wealthy {@area Óvrosz|0ac|x} noble obsessed with hunting. While not a warrior himself, he's a patron of the local monster-hunting guildhall, receiving certain benefits including guild appointed bodyguards and favors from {@area Oleg the Butcher|0b4|x}. He's traveled across Etharis killing monstrosities and building a fortune selling rare salvage in the Etharin Market.
Now with his coin and connections, Lord Rathulf enjoys playing a most dangerous game.

Rathulf has posted a bounty on a beast that's stalking the commonfolk of Malkovia from within the Jade Marsh. He believes it to be a rogue strigoi or ecoukrylo vampire. Too old to hunt it himself, and no longer trusting the local guildhall's aptitude against an actual monster, he contracts independent mercenaries, offering a considerable reward to whomever brings him a hunting trophy as proof of the deed.

The monster in question is a powerful werewolf ravager—a fact that becomes clear after some investigation. What isn't obvious is that the lycanthrope is Rathulf himself. He intentionally deceives mercenaries into pursuing him before turning them into his prey.
Oleg the Butcher is aware of Lord Rathulf's scheming pastime. If Rathulf is defeated and Oleg is informed, the overboss feigns ignorance but resolves to pay the bounty on the Lord's behalf.

entries.name: Gangs of Malkovia
entries: Tensions on Malkovia's streets threaten a violent gang war. Monster hunters from the Northside guildhall have slain vampires in the city's south, openly displaying the carcasses in defiance of the Crimson Court. {@area Ylana Svarog|0b5|x} has petitioned the Matriarch to punish Oleg and his thugs for these crimes. Guild hunters who cross the bridges into Southside are now attacked on sight by Ylana's own enforcers.

Meanwhile, the teachings of {@area Father Elijah|0b7|x} have spread into Northside and increase sympathy for the {@area Crimson Court|05c|x}. Oleg appears powerless to stop this religious infiltration. He's outlawed the faith on his side of the river, enraging his own citizens and southside believers.

Matriarch Király hires independent investigators to act as her secret and personal agents. She suspects a greater plot to destabilize Malkovia is afoot and intends to learn if the threat is coming from {@area Raevo|066|x} or {@area Soma|06e|x}. Her agents are asked to spy on both Oleg and Ylana in hopes of finding ways to ease tensions. The investigation involves discovering who has been inciting the skirmishes occurring in the streets.

The Matriarch's spies may eventually discover a young and talented monster hunter named Darius, who is partially responsible for the vampire killings. He was raised and trained by Oleg himself. However, Darius also secretly spreads Father Elijah's religious propaganda. The boy's full name is Darius von Dülvet. He is the last heir of Malkovia who survived the Matriarch's massacre of his family. Now old enough to fight, he intends to take his revenge on Mariska. The first step is destabilizing her authority.
The Matriarch's spies can choose to either capture Darius and bring him before Mariska or help with his vengeful crusade. Either choice may change the fate of Malkovia.

entries.name: For Whom the Bells Toll
entries: Malkovia's clock towers have stopped ringing. Additionally, commoners have recently gone missing near the three towers. Surely, this isn't a coincidence. This has prompted the Matriarch to send investigators to look into the matter.

Exploring the clocktowers discovers arcane libraries hoarded with blasphemous books. Eldritch laboratories are filled with nightmarish constructs. These half-living humanoids have had their bodies painfully grafted with fiery machinery and gruesome Weapons. Also found throughout the towers are the manic scribblings of Ciprian Johann. The gnomish eccentric owns the three buildings, and until now has permitted very few to see inside.

Johann's writings exalt his own brilliance. He believes the three clocktowers are his master work, unrivalled even in Stehlenwald. Johann obsessively turns away tourists who have come to marvel at the towers' magnificence, thinking the common rabble would desecrate his three holy edifices.

The gnomish artificer has been captured by his own pride. In the highest levels of the clocktowers, the mechanisms have become twisted and daemonic. The cogs are powered by fiery arcane objects. Malikirian imps flock like bats inside the belfry.

Ciprian himself is transforming into a fiend, having grafted his own body with hellish prosthetics. Unless he is stopped, he begins capturing commoners to subject them to the same horrific procedures.

data.name: Chapter 4: The Charneault Kingdom
entries: A land of broken vows, mystical fey, and a tense but strained peace between once-warring cultures, the Charneault Kingdom is a seemingly idyllic realm atop a tinderbox of complex intrigues and inevitable conflict waiting to ignite.

Unfolding in the southwestern corner of Etharis, beneath the rugged Rock-Teeth Mountains and the dense forest known as the {@area Grove Maze|0bf|x}, Charneault is renowned for its meandering hills, vast sky, and clear rivers. It's said that the residents of Charneault, both the elves and northerners who maintain a tentative peace, hold a closer connection to the land and the Spirits of Nature than any other nation. They also have the most to lose when the whims of those spirits take a horrific turn.

For generations, an impenetrable fog has descended over the lands of Charneault. The mist was harmless for centuries, just another natural sight to behold in a land of countless beauties and mysteries. In recent times, however, the fog has transformed into a malevolent force. Within the mist, plants grow twisted and beasts become wild. Roads and towns are plagued by wicked spirits and malevolent creatures wherever the fog rolls in.

The king of Charneault, {@area Thibault de Sauveterre|101|x}, and the elven Archdruid, {@area Aetherion|0dd|x}, work to discover the truth behind this sudden change. Despite their shared efforts, the kingdom remains divided in their opinions. Many elves believe the {@area dark mist|0c2|x} is a spiritual reckoning: a vengeful awakening in the land for the suffering inflicted upon their ancestors. Other settlements see nature itself as the problem, the dark mist a threat to be vanquished.

The Charneault Kingdom now faces the specter of its own history, an ambiguous future, and the mist which encompasses it all. Through this, Charneault's generous, fertile landscape belies a culture built upon mutual distrust, moral hypocrisy, and a well-earned fear of the unknown forces that exist just beyond the veil of nature.

entries.title: Iron Sparrow, Fairweathers, Severe Templars, Dawn Dreamers, Vermeil Guard

entries.name: Geography
entries: The natural barriers that protect Charneault create a profound veil from the ugliness of Etharis. Travelers who brave the twisting roads through these primal boundaries hardly believe the strange beauty they witness in the {@area Land of the Thousand Rivers|0c0|x}. Nevertheless, wayfarers are warned to remain alert. The splendor of this mystical realm belies the hidden danger of the fey, at whose whim awe can easily become terror.

entries.name: The Grove Maze
entries: The Kingdom of Charneault is nearly impossible to reach on foot. The Grove Maze chokes the pass between the Grey Spine and the Rock-Teeth Mountains, and creeps southward to partially conceal the Thousand Rivers from the east.

The forest is ancient. It's a tangled labyrinth of deeply wooded foothills, misted valleys, cascading rivers, and forested wetlands. The Grove Maze conceals scenic beauties, but it has just as many dark corners. Careless wayfarers become lost within. Even veteran explorers must watch the paths they've wandered, lest the Grove Maze rearrange itself to ensure there's no way out.

The twisted boughs of the trees creak heavily, as if the forest is constantly moving. Maps and landmarks can't be trusted. A beautiful green vale with a stream like a blue, silk ribbon running through its center may reveal itself to an explorer, never to be found again when they return just a day later. The Spirits of Nature show whatever they will of the forest, and they conceal anything they desire to be left undisturbed. The Grove Maze is the final resting place of many would-be trespassers who try to pierce such barriers, their corpses feeding the vegetation at the gates to the Realm of Faerie.

Folks who the archfey permit into their realm speak of their experiences in the Grove Maze as if they'd wandered through a dream. Some claim to have seen the glittering spires of a palace nestled in a flower garden, while others report fleeing from a green-scaled dragon snaking through a moldy bog. A wintery glade is dusted by a pillar of snowfall. Birds bicker in plain language before falling silent as wayfarers approach. Yet a person can explore the Grove Maze for a lifetime and witness none of these peculiar sights if the Spirits of Nature don't deem them worthy or interesting enough.

entries.name: Land of the Thousand Rivers
entries: Countless rivers flow through the heartland of the Charneault Kingdom between its rolling hills. Dense woods and fertile farmland spread across the entire kingdom, making the realm rich in natural resources. Aside from the larger cities, villages tend to be small. Each nestles beside a brook or spring teeming with fish and overgrown with foliage. The waters sparkle as if lined with diamonds. Between the curtains of silvery mist that drift over the land, Charneault is truly breathtaking.

The southwest portion of the Charneault Kingdom extends into a peninsula, with the Emerald Sea to the west and the Gulf of Lions to the east. The difficulty of traversing the Grove Maze makes maritime trade a necessity. Charneault's largest port is arguably Morencia, which remains its own city-state but whose independence is partially protected by the kingdom. This arrangement helps Charneault keep inland roads closed to other nations, shielding itself against the Bürach Empire's imperialism or Castinella's intolerance toward primal mysticism.

Yet life isn't without difficulty. The untamed nature of the wilderness means small settlements are preyed upon by dire wolves and brigands, who hide easily in the fog-laden valleys. The Spirits of Nature are capricious and covetous. Children go missing often. They might be kidnapped on a forest's edge or unknowingly exchanged with a wechselkind. Faeries take up residence in newly built homes if the timber used was cut from a tree that once belonged to them. Entire communities may disappear into the wilds to dance around a cauldron, forgetting their mortal lives to become playthings for the fey.

entries.name: Changing Seasons
entries: The ever-changing seasons transform the Charneault Kingdom. The four Courts of the Fey are made manifest in Etharis, and the folk of the Thousand Rivers celebrate each season for its own blessings and challenges. Those who revere the archfey perform rites as the seasons change to ensure favor with the Court who currently rules the land.

In the spring, heavy rain falls, followed by rays of sun painting the realm in lush greens and flowering colors. Yet the woodlands become wilder as the Spirits of Nature turn mischievous. Hunters and lumberjacks avoid the deep forests in spring. The allure of satyr songs drift on the breeze, and the trees twist their branches to trap explorers within their groves. Those seduced by the beauty of spring become lost forever in the verdant wilderness.

During summer the rivers recede. The days become warm and dry. Field hands know to avoid working beneath the heat of the midday sun, lest they be visited by a maiden or crone wielding a sickle, or the undead pestas she leaves in her wake. Those seeking relief from the hot sun are wise to leave certain pools or brooks alone. Fish-fey spirits called gaspers swim beneath the surface, waiting to drown careless fools who plunge their filthy hands in Charneault's pristine waters.

Autumn turns the realm brilliant shades of orange and brown. Cruel-spirited faeries awaken scarecrows and farm tools to wreak horror upon undeserving peasants. As the last of the grain is harvested for winter and put into storage, the final rites are performed to express gratitude to the Spirits of Nature and ensure they don't feel slighted by what has been taken from the land.

As winter grips the realm, the rivers freeze in the kingdom's north. Commoners huddle inside their firelit homes to endure the cold, dreaming of spring while sickness takes hold. Stories claim the {@area Winter Queen|129|x} watches Charneault through her frozen mirror and inflicts cruelties for each offense against the Spirits of Nature that went unpunished in the previous year. True or otherwise, malevolent fey slink into settlements looking to devour sufferers of such illness or sorrow. Their true names unknown, though monster hunters call these spirits grief eaters.

entries.name: The Mist
entries: The mists that permeate Charneault seem magical to behold, but in truth originate from magma vents along the nation's southern coast and beneath the Emerald Sea. That's not to say the mists aren't an instrument of the archfey. The largest of these heated fissures lies beneath a lake, inside a caldera where the Temple of Mist is built. Within that thick fog, a circle of druids speak with the Spirits of Nature directly. Their rituals, and the Temple itself, are never visible from outside the crater.

Sometimes the mist is harmless: a light veil that passes gently over the lands and hides the frolicking pixies. Silvery hosts of sprites are said to move unseen within the mist, marching to secret battles waged between the Fey Courts. When the mist has come and gone, mushrooms crop up in faerie circles. The goats have been milked and the milk stolen. The grass is longer, the trees regrown their leaves, and pollen drifts upon the breeze. But this gentle passing of the mist is no longer always the case.

In recent times, the mist has turned grey and frightening. The residents of Charneault flee when the dark mist comes to embrace the land in an umbral fog. Those who cannot escape must shelter as best they can.

Within the dark mist, the weather changes to that of other seasons. Fertile soil turns to dust, and crop fields lay barren in spring, while a blanket of snow freezes folks inside their homes during summer. In winter, a flash flood from melting rivers and pelting rain can wash away whole settlements.
The dark mist turns the natural foliage hostile. Spores become noxious. Vines awaken to strangle wayfarers. Treants begin bleeding a sickly sap, attracting swarms of vicious insects.

The Spirits of Nature themselves appear corrupted by the dark mist. Pixies lose their wings and transform into vengeful pucks—corpse gardeners who fertilize their groves with peoples' innards. Sylvan archers stalk into settlements and hunt the occupants as if for sport. Entire towns become a pale-spun nest of giant spiders. The sounds of fey merrily singing are gone—only mortals screaming in the darkness, and the echoes of whimsical laughter like children playing a game.

entries.name: History
entries: Before there was a kingdom called Charneault, there was Caer Neiada, the "land of countless rivers" in the Elvish tongue. A verdant paradise of rolling hills and fertile wilderness that was isolated from other nations, Caer Neiada flourished on its own for centuries before the Era of Expansion.

entries.name: The Ulufey
entries: The first settlers of Caer Neiada called themselves the ulufey: "mortal fey" in Elvish. Believing they were the direct descendants of the Spirits of Nature, the children of the archfey, in everything they did they gave respect and offerings to the seasonal courts. The ulufey considered Caer Neiada to be their birthright.

They hunted from the land, but always buried their bones and offal to nurture the soil. They gathered more often than farmed, always spreading the seeds to grow in new places. They felled trees whilst planting others. They listened for the voices on the wind and in the streams, the Spirits of Nature, who tutored them in the primal magics of their ancestors.

The cultures of the ulufey stretch back into the mists of history before the Era of Expansion. Fragments of their great works still exist in ancient stonework, faded tapestries, and half-remembered songs that the elves still celebrate to this day.

But much of it was also lost. Humankind first appeared in Caer Neiada like the embers of a forthcoming wildfire, drifting in from beyond the {@area Grove Maze|0bf|x}. When they first encountered the ulufey, they adopted the word into the human tongue—"elf."

entries.name: Arrival of the First Men
entries: Passing through the Grove Maze was not a simple feat for the folk of central Etharis. Many tried and failed. For a time, humans believed the Grove Maze and Rock-Teeth Mountains denoted the southern edge of the world. Eventually explorers did push through, and after them migrants, shortly before armies.

Beholding the natural splendor of Caer Neiada, many folks believed this place was a haven from the mud and black forests of central Etharis, a gift given to them by the gods. The first tribes settled in the northeastern part of the region, where the great rivers facilitated agriculture. They hunted freely and fed the scraps to their animals. They cut trees to build their homes and feed their fires. They tilled the earth and planted foreign grains. Without heeding the Spirits of Nature, the settlers drank deeply of all the land had to offer.

Nearby elves were horrified witnessing the ravaging of their lands. Worse, they feared the wrath of the archfey. Conflict between cultures was inevitable, as the elves tried to expel these foreigners from their realm and the northerners clung to their new homes. Armies arose on both sides, culminating in generations of war.

entries.name: Pact of the Sacred Land
entries: Many songs and chivalric tales illuminate the exploits and battles between the northern settlers of Caer Neiada and the elves who dwelled there since time began. Some of these accounts are recorded as history. Others are fables about heroes who may have lived but have had their tale retold so many times, the truth has become lost.

What nobody questions is the name of the first man who would be king of a realm called Charneault: Aymeric Noblecoeur. Once a knight in service to Castle Lamesdhonneur, Noblecoeur was said to have been knocked from his horse by an arrow and washed away in the river currents.

He was found deep in Caer Neiada by an elven sage named Thuridan Sarador, who bathed his feet in a stream. A leader among his own people, Sarador sat and spoke to Noblecoeur, explaining his vision for a united realm. Two cultures, one kingdom. The dream of Charneault.

Historians who don't believe this fable write that Aymeric Noblecoeur sought to bring northern domination to the land under a banner of diplomacy. Lord Sarador, knowing that an endless cycle of war would end with the extinction of the elves, chose to offer peace. Both leaders paid a personal toll for the ensuing pact. For their sacrifices, the sun dawned on the Charneault Kingdom.

The Pact of the Sacred Land outlines the peace between the northern folk and the elves, and their respective responsibilities in sharing the land. The Bondmakers commune with the Spirits of Nature directly. The king and his noble court rules, but the Bondmakers are their counsel to the whims of the archfey. The chapter knights protect the kingdom against invasion and maintain order between its peoples. Wealth and produce is shared. The sacred sites of elves are never disturbed.

Of Thuridan Sarador, the elven sage crowned Aymeric Noblecoeur and formed the Circle of Bondmakers. Counselling his friend, Sarador revealed one final demand that was made by the Spirits of Nature. King Aymeric Noblecoeur, and each regent that would succeed him, must send their firstborn child to the Temple of Mist. There, they would be trained in ancient customs. The heir would then ascend to the Fey Courts, never to be seen again. All attempts to circumvent this tribute have brought disaster upon the people of Charneault, and so it is still upheld diligently to this day.

entries.name: The Circle of Bondmakers
entries: There are two main faiths in the Charneault Kingdom. Belief in the Four Divines—Aurelia, Galt, Ulmyr, and Maligant—migrated south with the northern tribes. But the Gods' End did not impact Charneault as it did other realms. Many offer their prayers to the Arch Seraphs in the gods' absence, yet the kingdom's continued prosperity is said to be in no small part thanks to the Spirits of Nature.

The elves would not call their relationship with the fey a religion. They view the Spirits of Nature as their true ancestors and the Courts of the archfey as a ruling authority above their own. The fey are not distant gods who answer the prayers of the downtrodden through goodwill. Elven customs are far more transactional. The Spirits of Nature demand that the wilderness is maintained: for every animal hunted, fruit plucked, and tree felled, an offering of equal value is provided in gratitude for the paradise the Spirits of Nature have cultivated.

Offering prayers to the archfey is considered a faux pas. Though they are celebrated, it's improper to make appeals for their aid without an appropriate gift given to earn their favor. In recompense for an entire realm, the regents of the Charneault Kingdom have paid a steep price.

The Circle of Bondmakers are a community of druids whose responsibility is maintaining the connection between the people of Charneault and the Spirits of Nature. They wander the Kingdom, strengthening or restoring the bonds with the Spirits, wherever the fey cause trouble.

The Bondmakers' are a divisive group due to their secrecy and their connection to the archfey. Many suspect that their loyalty to Fey Courts outweighs their loyalty to the crown. They conceal their rituals and customs. Not even their own knightly escorts are permitted within the Temple of Mist, the druids' sanctum in southern Charneault.

Folk who've witnessed the cruelty of the fey distrust the Bondmakers innately. While fey can certainly be malicious, the druids contend that their wickedness is often the result of an insult inflicted by a commoner or community. In regions where the land is held sacred, the Bondmakers act as counselors, spiritualists, and exorcists. In places where chapels to the Arch Seraphs are built, the druids are thought of as pact makers and heretics.

Nevertheless, the two faiths coexist in Charneault, often within the same settlement. For centuries they've influenced each other. In modern times, northerners have created their own way of connecting with the Spirits of Nature through knighthood and quests. Elven ideals of romance and beauty have suffused the warrior cultures of the north to give rise to the Chapter Knights.

entries.name: Knighthood
entries: Knighthood is the greatest aspiration of any child who comes of age in the Charneault Kingdom. The traditions of the Chapter Knights date back to before the founding of the realm and have their roots in both northern and elven cultures.

A knight is expected to exemplify many virtues: courage, strength, and honor as required by any warrior. Justice, temperance, and mercy. A knight may be needed to act as a sheriff or judge in defense of the realm's peace. Faith, generosity, and hope. The knights are the light of Charneault, and their ability to spread beauty and mirth is as important as their skill in swordsmanship.

The Chapter Knights are highly codified institutions that prioritize differing virtues. All serve the Charneault Kingdom. Yet their specific purposes and ideals differ. Becoming a Chapter Master, the leader of each knightly order, involves years of dedication and service to a chapter's cause and virtues.

To be king or queen of Charneault, one must be regarded as a knight of the highest caliber. When a new ruler is to be coronated, the Chapter Masters convene and decide who among them shall be the next regent. Often this is merely a formality. The heir apparent is customarily a child or relative of the previous ruler, prepared from a young age to become a Chapter Master and eventually ascend the throne. But intrigues do happen.

There are five major knight chapters recognized as the most renowned in Charneault. Other, lesser chapters exist, though they attract fewer squires and tend to be specific in their cause. Knights-errant also wander the land looking for deeds to enact, though they bring no honor upon themselves. A knight without a chapter has no authority recognized by the crown.

entries.name: Vermeil Guard
entries: The Vermeil Guard acts as a personal guard of the {@area Bondmakers|0c7|x}, traversing Charneault with them wherever the druids may need. However, the Vermeil Guard are not allowed to witness any of the Bondmakers internal ceremonies or customs. They are responsible for escorting the firstborn of a new regent to the Temple of Mist as well.
The shield of the Vermeil Guard is green and gold, featuring crowned antlers and a sprig of holly. Their loyalty is to the throne and the Spirits of Nature in equal measure.

entries.name: Iron Sparrow
entries: The Iron Sparrow oversee the general defense of Charneault, with subchapters stationed outside the {@area Grove Maze|0bf|x}, along the Rock-Teeth Mountains, and on the coastline. Anyone entering Charneault from foreign lands likely comes across an Iron Sparrow patrol.
These knights carry a blue and silver shield, which displays a fortress that defends Charneault, and the song sparrow of their namesake.

entries.name: Fairweathers
entries: These knights patrol the mainland of Charneault, keeping the peace and rebuilding settlements after a conflict or {@area the mist|0c2|x} passing through. They're also in command of the justice system, though with matters that occur close to Tol Leyemil, they defer to the {@area Acen Elavain|119|x}.
The Fairweathers ride with a yellow shield adorned with an ivory mounted knight carrying a sword and the scales of justice.

entries.name: Dawn Dreamers
entries: The Dawn Dreamers protect Tol Leyemil, though they live on the outskirts of the great city. Their members have elven heritage. Though their knights rarely range far from the elven stronghold, some can be found throughout Charneault investigating threats to their home.
The Dawn Dreamers shield is the color of autumn. A bare tree spreads its branches above symbols of elven culture, a lyre and a bow: both stringed instruments of beauty and destruction.

entries.name: Severe Templars
entries: The most contentious of the knight chapters is the Severe Templars, whose purpose is to map the land of Charneault and study its environments. Many elves aren't comfortable with these Chapter Knights' research, which they view as intrusive to the Spirits of Nature. Nevertheless, the Severe Templars' explorations could uncover truths about the realm that have laid hidden for centuries.

The Severe Templars view their work as bringing to light the true extent of archfey power in Etharis, which they believe is an insidious force. Their loyalty is to the crown and no other. Displayed on their black shields is a coroneted knight alongside a flaming torch, which banishes the darkness of the wilderness and burns the Severe Templars' foes.

entries.name: The Quest
entries: Any citizen of the Charneault Kingdom can rise to knighthood. Most who do are inducted into a chapter at a young age, educated collectively before being chosen as a knight's squire.

Squires are expected to maintain their mentor's Weapons, clothing, and equipment. They're responsible for the good health of their and their master's horses. Each squire must memorize and embody the ideals of their chapter, and through their duties learn the skills and virtues necessary to be worthy of knighthood. When at last they're deemed ready, each squire must embark on a quest.

Lower-born commoners not recruited by a chapter must instead arm and educate themselves. Their road is far more difficult. These squires leave home to embark on their quest without any of the preparation or resources offered by a chapter.

All squires carry a blank shield. During their quest they must accomplish a worthy deed of great courage. Once achieved, the squire can assert the right to bear a coat of arms on their shield. Most are knighted by the chapter who trained them. Solitary squires must find a chapter willing to induct them, or else wander the land as a knight-errant.

Sometimes during their quest, a knight or squire might meet a Spirit of Nature and gain their trust through noble acts or behavior. Many renowned knights are those who have managed to enter the service of such a Spirit by taking an oath to protect and serve them, becoming guardians of the wilderness itself. In return, the knight is blessed with primal magics that vary depending on the nature of the Spirit to which the oath was taken.

Other squires might swear vows to an Arch Seraph and become anointed. It's not unheard of for divine power to manifest among the ranks of the Chapter Knights, and those who accomplish it are revered as paladins of the highest virtues.

entries.name: The Dark Mist
entries: After generations of relative peace between the cultures of the Charneault Kingdom, relations took a grim turn when {@area King Thibault de Sauveterre|101|x} ascended the throne.

The King's firstborn daughter, Tiphaine de Sauveterre, knew it was her destiny to walk to the Temple of Mist and disappear forever: a sacrifice she made happily for the kingdom she loved. But the Princess' heart was also given to another.

Sir Guilhem le Preux was a famed knight of the {@area Vermeil Guard|0c9|x}. He'd sworn oaths to the Spirits of Nature. He'd slain werewolves and toppled giants. And, despite the {@area Pact of the Sacred Land|0c6|x}, he also married Princess Tiphaine in secret.

When Thibault de Sauveterre was crowned, Sir Guilhem wept at his king's feet. The knight begged that Tiphaine be spared. It was the Princess herself who took her husband's hands and instructed him to be merry, for the sacrifice of their great love would forge the mightiest Kingdom.
But it was not to be.

Sir Guilhem broke his oath to the Vermeil Guard and forsook his duties, unable to bear walking beside his wife to the Temple of Mist. He swore instead to never love another and to live dishonorably as a knight-errant. But swiftly the Spirits of Nature came to him. It's said a song sparrow perched upon his shield and chirped in his ear, warning Sir Guilhem that the Princess was in grave danger.

The knight stirred his horse to follow Tiphaine's escort, his own Chapter brethren, but Sir Guilhem arrived too late. The Vermeil Guard were defeated. The {@area Bondmakers|0c7|x} were slaughtered. All was in ruin, and Princess Tiphaine was missing. Yet the hoof tracks of her captors led onwards to the Temple of Mist. Her husband pursued.
What happened next is the subject of mythmaking. Nobody is certain who Princess Tiphaine's assailants were, and her fate, along with Sir Guilhem's, remains a mystery.

Stories whispered in taverns say the Severe Templars did the killing, intentionally breaking the Pact of the Sacred Land in malice, and the dark mist is the archfeys' retribution. More elaborate conspiracies claim {@area Diyanthar|0dd|x}, a sorcerous elf of grand ambition and no honor, abducted Princess Tiphaine to enact his own fell ritual in the Temple of Mist. Perhaps Sir Guilhem interrupted that ritual too late for its effects to be completely prevented, and from that day forth the mist was tainted—sometimes as silvery as ever, other days dark and filled with terrors.

A romantic tale imagines Guilhem and Tiphaine reunited and fled far from Charneault to be happy in a distant land. But such fairy tales are rarely true, and both have been seen as apparitions in the dark mist, calling for help to lift their curse.

entries.name: Provinces
entries: Charneault is comprised of seven provinces, each with its own expressions of elven and northern culture. Gran Croix is the realm of the {@area Grove Maze|0bf|x}. The western provinces of Beaumont, De la Fontaine, and Faucher have higher elven populations and greater reverence for the Spirits of Nature. Gagnon, Lamar, and Chaplin are home to more urbanized settlements and tend toward worship of the Arch Seraphs. Dividing the provinces in this way doesn't paint the nuances of Charnelaise society, and influences from the Realm of Faerie are found everywhere.

Before the {@area Pact of the Sacred Land|0c6|x} united them, each province was its own territory or kingdom. Some were ruled by a noble house whose descendants still govern to this day, now bearing the titles of duke or duchess. It's common for these noble families to include Chapter Knights among their lineages.

Honor and reputation are influential in Charneault, even if the heads of house are not a knight themselves. Marriage and trade are other levers of power. As ambition circles the kingdom's throne, those who cannot wield a sword to wear the crown instead offer their children and riches to remain within the king or queen's graces.
Losing one's honor at a tournament is a setback. Fortunately, another tournament is never far away.

entries.name: Chaplin
entries: Bordering most other provinces, Chaplin is the crown jewel of the Charneault Kingdom and the birthplace of knightly virtue. It's the seat of the Crown's authority. It's also the wealthiest of any region, though its riches are not based on resources or even crown taxes. Folks pour into Chaplin from across Charneault in the hopes of witnessing greatness.

The annual Games of the White Dove take place each summer at Castle Lamesdhonneur, where knights and squires from every chapter compete for personal glory. Likewise, the Tournament of the Pink Rose is held at Houiville. This springtime festival emphasizes contests of poetry, song, and painting over that of martial prowess. Smaller competitions are held in every village throughout Chaplin year-round, and squires who survive the mud, blood, and pressure of local contests aspire to be remembered as champions of the Victor's Circle at the capital.

Chaplin immortalizes its heroes. The shields of knights long passed hang upon the outer walls of Castle Lamesdhonneur, reminders of their now-distant courageous deeds. The halls of Houiville tell these stories in greater detail. Woven tapestries depict knights blessed by Arch Seraphs, druids speaking with woodland creatures, and villagers banding together to defeat a ravening werewolf.

While Chaplin worships acts of glory and heroism, such stories of the Charneault Kingdom are often a lie. Within Castle Lamesdhonneur, endless conspiracies unfold. While the highest power rests with the crown, any regent is beholden to competing interests. The Royal Council are the crown's closest advisors, comprised of dignitaries who represent the interests of the nobility. While the ducal houses are meant to speak on behalf of their people, many use their influence in Castle Lamesdhonneur to further their personal desires and interests.

The group known as Acen Elavain are the voice of Tol Leyemil. Loyalty from half the realm relies on the elves feeling (if not always being) heard, and their wishes granted whenever the crown is able. But only the Chapter Masters can make a king. This gives the Knight Chapters inordinate power in deciding the future of the Charneault Kingdom, and what a previous regent's legacy will be.

Largely, the decrees of the king are obeyed. Peace would not reign otherwise. Even so, this doesn't stop plots and bargains from being arranged behind closed doors and in whispered hallways. The Charneault Kingdom, and Chaplin particularly, is a realm where courage, faith, and generosity are upheld as the ideal virtues of leadership. But secrecy, ambition, and cruelty are the true tools of power.

Tensions in Chaplin have only risen as the {@area dark mist|0c2|x} spreads. In the face of mounting calamity, Castle Lamesdhonneur has been transformed into a refuge for many Charnelaise seeking shelter against the horror outside. The once-vibrant city is rife with nervous fear. In a time when he is most needed to bring hope, the noble King de Sauveterre has retreated from public life and left the Severe Templars to maintain order within Chaplin.

entries.name: King Thibault de Sauveterre
entries: Former Chapter Master of the {@area Iron Sparrow|0ca|x}, King de Sauveterre struggled to claim the throne in the face of dire competition from Hermand le Sévère. Thibault prevailed, though narrowly. Kingship was never something he aspired to, especially knowing the sacrifices he would need to make. It was his deeply held belief in the knightly virtues that made him an inspiring choice from among his peers.

Thibault's supporters thought he would be a lion among kings once he ascended the throne. Instead, he became a broken man almost instantly after the tragedy that befell his daughter, Princess Tiphaine, and the subsequent spreading of the dark mist.

Frightened, indecisive, and disaffected are the words whispered behind the King's back. Thibault's reputation as a knight have sustained the love of his people, but without the leadership of the crown, his court has descended into violent suppression and grabs for power. Without the combined loyalty of his subjects, the King is powerless to fight the dark mist.
Though most doubt he could find the strength for it, bringing all people together beneath his bloody sword may be Thibault's only option left to unite his realm.

entries.name: Hermand de Sévère
entries: The man who would be king. Chapter Master of the Severe Templars, Hermand de Sévère, has an indomitable reputation as a knight who rarely fails at his goals. While he's celebrated for his swordsmanship and fearlessness, many a knight and squire have lain dead at his feet having faced de Sévère in combat during a tourney.

Losing the crown to Thibault de Sauveterre is considered Hermand's only failure in life. Yet in defeat he appears to have found success. His voice holds great influence in Castle Lamesdhonneur, and he attends more councils than the King. Though he has no official political power aside from his role as Chapter Master, whenever de Sévère is present in a room, decisions tend to be made toward his preferences.

Hermand joined the Severe Templars as a child. He dedicated his entire life to serving his chapter, and under his command the Severe Templars have become one of the most feared machines of warfare the Charneault Kingdom has seen.

The Templars' contempt for the Spirits of Nature is no secret. The dark mist only affirms their crusade to burn the archfey out of civilized lands wherever their influence is felt. Strong leadership from the crown has prevented open hostility between the elves and Templars in years past, but King de Sauveterre's retreat from public life has emboldened the Severe Templars to extend their authority beyond their usual bounds. This hasn't helped rumors that Hermand de Sévère ordered the killing of Princess Tiphaine.

entries.name: Gagnon
entries: Gagnon is the easternmost duchy of Charneault and borders the Castinellan Provinces. Forts are built along the rivers that divide the two nations. Open hostility is rare, but Charneault is alert to the intolerance Castinella expresses towards druidic magic and the Spirits of Nature.

Gagnon is the home to the {@area Iron Sparrow|0ca|x}, whose knightly tradition is entwined with an appreciation of the inherent beauty in the natural world. Music is common in the streets of Camont. Sometimes it's a knight singing ballads by a fountain, reciting the Iron Sparrow's deeds that keep the realm safe.

Neighborhoods in Camont are lush with verdant courtyards, potted flowerbeds, and overhanging trees. The extent to which the architecture reflects elven influences can be striking for a city this far east—a result of Camont's close proximity to the {@area Grove Maze|0bf|x}. Nevertheless, worship of the Arch Seraphs is common in Gagnon, especially given their eastern neighbors.

entries.name: Duchess Sylvana Briar Lorraine
entries: The young duchess of Camont is no more than five years old. Made an orphan by the recent death of her father, Duke Lothair Lorraine, intrigues loom around Sylvana, as her father's nobles' scheme to make her ducal throne their own.

Complicating matters, the Duchess appears to be cursed. When she's bored, which is often, pixies flit from thin air to torment her servants. When she's angered, nearby objects burst into flames. When Sylvana is amused, bubbles escape from her ears and mouth, which has earned her the moniker the Bubbling Duchess.

The nature of Sylvana's curse is unknown, though she seems quite incapable of controlling it. It remains to be seen whether she needs protection from her court, or whether the court needs protection from her.

entries.name: Steward Edwardine Evrétte
entries: The elven steward to Duke Lothair Lorraine, governance of Gagnon has fallen to Edwardine until Duchess Sylvana comes of age. The Steward actively searches for a solution to Sylvana's curse. Meanwhile, others in the court believe that Edwardine holds a secret about Sylvana's abilities that threatens civil unrest if revealed.

entries.name: Lamar
entries: Lamar is a bridge between east and west Charneault. Its midlands are mostly farms and roads, where commoners make humble livings whilst dreaming of knightly adventures and mystical Fey Courts.

Though often unseen, the Spirits of Nature are never far. The many bridges that cross the countless rivers, and guide stones marking directions beside the roads, are frequently destroyed—presumably by mischievous faeries. Superstitious folk who fear fey retribution tear down structures built too close to sacred woods or faerie mounds.

Mont de Lancey is the ducal house, and a coastal fortress built upon a rugged bluff brimming with rare minerals and stones. The town that circles the mountain has mined the rock for generations, earning considerable wealth for the ruling house. Their familial signet rings are inset with gemstones.

Alongside the mountain another castle is built, this one the stronghold of the Fairweathers—Castle de Esquire. The aspirants trained within are renowned for becoming exceptional squires, coveted by other chapters. Though a squire is always permitted to join any chapter that welcomes them, Castle de Esquire steadily swells the ranks of the Fairweathers for the protection of the realm.
The spread of the {@area dark mist|0c2|x} has brought a massive influx of students to the school, and Mont De Lancey acts as a symbol of hope and persistence against the mist.

entries.name: Vivian Descoteaux
entries: Chapter Master of the Fairweathers, and mother to the current Duchess of Lamar, Vivian Descoteaux is an acclaimed knight. Formidable and determined, she is fixated on being the one and only savior of Charneault's ideals and its citizens.

Vivian wasn't pleased that {@area Thibault de Sauveterre|101|x} was given the kingship, but she herself was not vying for it. A king rots in their golden cage at Castle Lamesdhonneur. The real work of leading happens on horseback, charging a foe down with sword and lance. King de Sauveterre is too kind in her eyes. His reliance on diplomacy is a weakness. She is a woman of deeds and few words, believing that it's in words where dishonesty hides.

Vivian is, underneath her steel facade, a deeply scarred individual with delusions of grandeur and a willingness to sacrifice anything or anyone while thinking she is saving them. She intentionally leaves villages unprotected to bait her foes. She'd sooner burn down grain stores and slaughter livestock than leave them for brigands or rebels. To Vivian Descoteaux, justice exists only at the end of a sword.

entries.name: Emy Descoteaux
entries: The third born child to Vivian Descoteaux, Duchess Emy Descoteaux is a disappointment to her mother. More comfortable with a book in her hand than a sword, Emy never showed much aptitude for knighthood nor much interest.
Duchess Descoteaux is a puzzle solver. She works to make the mines beneath Mont de Lancey more efficient and the exportation of Lamar's gems and produce more lucrative.

When the dark mist rolls over Mont de Lancey, the Duchess instructs her people to flee into the city's walls for better safety: another disheartening strategy to her mother. Vivian would rather have fresh corpses to study after the dark mist disperses.
I watched Lady Vivian slay a whole troupe of twisted goblins once. They came out of the mist, shrieking and gibbering like they do. Those were the last sounds they ever made.

entries.name: Grand Croix
entries: The {@area Grove Maze|0bf|x} has its own provincial name: Grand Croix. Most settlements are along the great forest's edge, though some communities choose to live within its fortress of trees. These villages are intimately shaped by elven culture. Homes are sometimes built aloft, among the branches of enormous trees, and connected by arching bridges and spiraling staircases. The Spirits of Nature may secretly reside in these hamlets, hiding themselves when they hear horses approach.
