Ember Cairn is a holy city of the Seraphs built on holy city of the dragons. The Seraphs are more powerful, of course. They are still present.

entries.name: History
entries: In the fabled Era of Antiquity, the dragonborn had settled the lands of the Castinellan peninsula all the way to the Grove Maze, which borders Charneault. Almost nothing is known about this neolithic culture. All that remains of it are cave reliefs, broken statues, and oral stories passed through generations of dragonborn. Nobody is certain if the dragonborn can trace their lineage back to true dragons. They share more in common with elves than wyverns.

Whether the dragonborn never wrote their histories, or the texts were lost in the calamity that destroyed their home, modern generations know almost nothing of their past. Half-remembered mythologies of long-silent gods. Ancient structures crumbled to their foundations. Granite carvings of a flame-breathing dragon, or perhaps a deity, lording over dragonborn, either bent in terror or worship. This is all that remains. Much of it was rediscovered in the old ruins of Ember Cairn.

The dragonborn became nomadic after the cataclysm. Their people spread to every reach within Etharis, even forming small communities in other folks' empires. But nowhere could they call home until Ember Cairn summoned them. The cultural hole left by their misplaced heritage has since been filled by the reconstruction of their broken city and worship of the Arch Seraphs.

Dragonborn are not the only people who dwell upon the Shattered Peaks. As with most places after the Era of Expansion, humans are populace within the holy city. Dwarves have also settled within Ember Cairn and formed small villages near the quarries. Dwarven and gnomish ingenuity is relied upon often in the city's reconstruction, establishing new granite mines, and the careful excavation of the cathedral's catacombs.
Of course, faith draws pilgrims of all cultures and species who wish to revere the Arch Seraphs and purify themselves from the taint of magic.

entries.name: Reconstruction
entries: After Sagrado Valieda formed the {@area Arcanist Inquisition|2f1|x}, he chose Ember Cairn as the order's stronghold—at the time, a secluded place to sequester dangerous arcanists. Valieda's broader reasons also included extending an invitation for the dragonborn to rebuild their ancestral home. But rumors persist that his true motives were less than generous.

Archeologists of {@area Therpena|14a|x} were already digging through the ruins of the ancient city, unearthing the history of Castinella's draconic ancestors. Some accounts suggest Valieda feared what might be discovered. Rather than permit a culture of arcanists or dragon worshippers to be revived, the Unifier erected his fortress above those ancient stones so they would never be disturbed. Other rumors suggest the catacombs beneath the {@area Cathedral of Embers|167|x} already existed: an umbral pit for the Inquisition to lock their enemies away, their pleas for mercy never to be heard again.

In the many years since, the Castinellan theocracy has spent an abundance of time, wealth, and labor reconstructing Ember Cairn. The church claims this was always Valieda's desire. Though the progress is ongoing, the benefits this effort has yielded to the provinces are countless.

The dragonborn have been loyal and fervent servants of the Arch Seraphs. Ember Cairn is a reward for their piety and has become a rallying place to recall their diaspora back to the ancestral lands from across Etharis. Often, dragonborn who return home likewise devote themselves to the Eternal Dogma.

Ember Cairn has become a powerful symbol in the minds of all Castinellans. It's both a projection of the nation's strength and righteousness, and a dire warning to all who would break the laws of the Eternal Dogma. Many aspire to visit the holy city and behold its splendor for themselves. None wish to be dragged there in inquisitorial chains.

The prosperity of Ember Cairn also spreads wealth to the rest of the Castinellan Provinces. Granite and other building stone are a lucrative export. So are divine statues, necklaces, clasps, and other holy trinkets crafted by the hands of priests in the most sacred city on the continent. These holy symbols reach as far as the Bürach Empire or Charneault Kingdom, where Chapter Knights sworn to the Arch Seraphs wear them. Donations also flow into church coffers at Ember Cairn, before being funneled to the rest of Castinella for the construction of more extravagant temples, finery for the ecclesiastic class, and Weapons for the Arcanist Inquisition.

entries.name: Wildfires
entries: Wildfires are a reality for Ember Cairn, as they've been for all the Castinellan Provinces. Casualties occur on the roads into the city when a blaze spreads without warning, and refugees from threatened or burnt-out villages come to the holy city to beg for alms.

Many advocate for better fire suppression. Shepherds who cross the mountains with goats or cattle provide a surprising mitigation to the danger of wildfires, as their animals eat some of the ground fuel, such as fallen sticks and dry plants. The shepherds of the Shattered Peaks have become glorified by the faithful. They're associated with {@deity Aphaeleon|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}, whose image is sometimes that of a penniless herder protecting his flock from the dangers of temptation.

entries.name: Notable Locations
entries: The reconstruction of Ember Cairn is progressing and reshaping the city. Less developed than other major cities, there are nonetheless many significant locations that have made Ember Cairn revered.

entries.name: Cathedral of Embers
entries: The Cathedral of Embers is perched atop a narrow summit. Encircled by a high curtain wall that's almost flush with the cliffs beneath it, the Cathedral gates open only for the Inquisition and the poor souls condemned to imprisonment. Countless pilgrims crowd the steps outside those gates. Their constant prayers are a mix of pleas and adulation for {@deity Empyreus|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24|Empyreus's} divine protection.

The Cathedral itself is a sizeable keep encircled within those walls. Its white towers are crowned with majestic domes, and countless arched windows keep vigilance over Ember Cairn. Despite projecting the image of a sacred temple, the Cathedral of Embers is a fortress capable of housing an army. Inquisitor knights are summoned here from across Castinella to receive blessings, purifications, and sacred orders before being dispatched on crusades. A legion of soldiers guards the cathedral at all times.

The Inquisition also houses judges, jailers, and interrogators within their stronghold. The domed towers encircled within their curtain wall are not all the Cathedral of Embers is, as there are tunnels delving into the rock beneath the cathedral and deep into the roots of the surrounding mountains. The upper levels contain quarters, armories, and offices for those whose duty is dealing with imprisoned apostates. None know the depth of the lower levels.

entries.name: Cathedral Catacombs
entries: The catacombs are a vast network of ancient tunnels that spread from beneath the Cathedral of Embers. The {@area Arcanist Inquisition|2f1|x} claims to have built these tunnels themselves, though most scholars presume this is a lie. The catacombs are simply too vast for that to be possible.

The darkness hold countless cells and crevices for incarcerated apostates to be thrown. Rumors suggest the Inquisition isn't even aware of how many mages they've locked away, condemned to rot within those tunnels and never be spoken of again. For some, the catacombs are a test. Not every room is locked, and the accused are permitted to wander the labyrinth freely to search for a way out. Those who find their way back through the Cathedral of Embers, their path said to be lit by {@deity Zabriel|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}, are offered a chance at redemption through confession or joining the Inquisition. Accused who discover another way to escape the catacombs are swiftly recaptured and condemned to death. None have accomplished such a feat without the aid of magic.

The jailers of the Inquisition are intimately familiar with the upper catacombs, aware of the many paths an apostate might take in their attempt to escape the labyrinth. Traps and torture chambers await those who try to wield their magic. Black runes inscribed in the stonework make it impossible to cast most types of spells, making it impossible to teleport or discorporate. Most apostate mages are cornered and punished before they've breathed a gasp of fresh air, never to see the sky again.

Deeper in the catacombs, the stonework becomes more ornate and vast, crumbling railings lining the edge of steps that descend into cavernous chambers. Great statues of draconic warriors hold aloft vaulted ceilings. Giant bas-reliefs illustrate intricate scenes of dragons being fought or worshipped, and astrological charts depicting images of the night sky.

An accused person who flees this deeply into the catacombs may lose the pursuit of their inquisitorial jailers, yet they are no more likely to ever experience freedom. Even worse monsters are said to lurk within that hidden labyrinth.

entries.name: Hidden Labyrinth
entries: An ancient labyrinth exists beneath Ember Cairn. It contains the history of the dragonborn, still largely buried under the holy city, hiding the true story of what happened to that ancient civilization. Stone slabs and fragments of dragon bone are occasionally discovered in the Shattered Peaks surrounding Ember Cairn. While the only well-known entrance into this labyrinth is through the catacombs beneath the Cathedral of Embers, other openings do exist in the crags and fissures of the mountains. Archaeologists explore these caves in secret.

The Inquisition takes a dim view of anybody trying to access this hidden labyrinth. Officially, their stance reflects a concern for public safety. Yet tension does exist between the Castinellan theocracy and the dragonborn who aren't swept up by faith, who believe that the church is intentionally trying to keep them separated from their heritage. These voices are small in Ember Cairn. It's not difficult for the Inquisition to draw a link between a person's interest in the hidden labyrinth and a probable curiosity in the occult.

The Inquisition's vigilance against harm from the hidden labyrinth isn't without merit, either. Deadly monsters, arcanists gone mad, and artifacts beyond modern understanding are sequestered in the dark, awaiting an explorer unlucky enough to find themselves within those depths.
Who knows what greater horrors could be slumbering in the dark?

It's rumored that Ember Cairn's black market knows of multiple secret entrances into the labyrinth, and can provide access for a price. Their clients include treasure hunters and historians. The hidden labyrinth is also sold as a path to sneak into the Cathedral catacombs and rescue a condemned mage.

entries.name: Marketplace
entries: Among the dense cluster of buildings near the heart of the city is where the bustling marketplace is loudest, constantly filled with merchants and customers. The stalls range from food vendors, fabric sellers, stonework artisans, bead makers, jewelers, and, unavoidably, counterfeiters. Both local and travelling merchants hawk their wares. The marketplace is always a mix of locals purchasing imported produce and pilgrims searching for mementos of their visit to the holy city.

Preachers stand on street corners screaming the will of the Arch Seraphs to whomever will listen. Divine healers make a modest living mending ailments. Acting as an unsanctioned prophet is a dangerous vocation in Ember Cairn, as the Inquisition is intolerant toward folks whose religious values may contravene the Eternal Dogma, or whose miraculous healing may unknowingly draw from the power of an Arch Daemon. Even the devout are not free from the Inquisition's suspicion.

Surprisingly, the smuggling trade is less scrutinized, provided the wares being peddled are not magical in nature. Souvenirs from the heritage of the dragonborn, counterfeit sacred baubles, stolen granite carvings, and rare contraband from other regions of Etharis can all be found in the narrowest alleys of the marketplace. However, the most lucrative illicit trade is in people and information.

The Inquisition can be difficult to extract information from, considering their members are fanatics and idealists. Nevertheless, the enemies of the ecclesiarch pay great sums for whatever secrets can be extracted from Ember Cairn, including the movements of Inquisitorial legions, the locations of high-ranking judges are ripe for assassination, and the identities of those interred in the Cathedral of Embers.

Folk who wish to visit the holy city unnoticed rely on the same smuggling rings. They might arrive with a desire to rescue a friend or loved one from the fires of the Inquisition, possibly even desperate enough to try entering the catacombs. Or they've come to verify information that could help save arcane lives in the broader Castinellan Provinces. In any case, far more people are smuggled into the city than ever survive to make the return journey.

entries.name: Empyrean Pit
entries: The Empyrean Pit is an elaborate fighting arena where warriors worship through combat. The walls of the indoor arena are carved with ornate artworks, featuring winged seraphs falling upon hordes of writhing daemons. The overhead dome is painted with an image of the heavens. Violent spectacles are presented in the arena throughout the year, under the Inquisition's gaze, who ensures every fight is in accordance with the Eternal Dogma.

The Empyrean Pit is, in fact, an opportunity for the Inquisition to build the reputation of its knights. Tortured arcanists are sometimes herded into the arena. Their enfeebled magics are no match for an armored inquisitor knight, who doles out violent executions in the name of {@deity Empyreus|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24} to be witnessed by an audience of the Arch Seraph's faithful.
Monsters are also caged and brought in for sanctioned warriors to fight against. Such entertainment is far too enticing for large crowds to pass up, and so audiences flock to see these battles.

These violent showcases are quite different from the tournaments held in the Charneault Kingdom, where death is rarely the aim and knights are admired for virtues such as grace and humility. In Ember Cairn, the goal is to eliminate creatures who offend the Arch Seraphs. Every battle begins with a prayer. Rather than a cheering crowd drunk on wine and merriment, the executions are accompanied by an audience chanting solemn hymns and righteous adulations. Every match is overseen by an Inquisitor Priest, ensuring it's performed with sanctity. Detractors ponder if {@deity Tormach|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24} derives an equal measure of satisfaction from the Empyrean Pit as its namesake does—a blasphemous thought.
Officially, gambling on the results of battles is illegal. This doesn't mean it isn't possible through bookies in Ember Cairn's black market.

entries.name: Important Individuals
entries: Notable people associated with Ember Cairn include those described below.

entries.name: Lord Inquisitor Crevax
entries: The commonfolk of Castinella have come to revere or dread Heretic's End, the flaming longsword of Lord Inquisitor Crevax. One of the deadliest Weapons in the provinces, according to body count, the Lord Inquisitor has swung it during enough executions of blasphemers, heretics, and mages to make the weapon famous. Crowds cheer at the scent of burnt blood. Never mind that Crevax has also wielded Heretic's End to slay daemons, aberrations, and all manner of monstrous horrors conjured to the mortal realm but reckless arcanists.

The golden dragonborn herself is the image of a warrior angel. Her scales reflect the gods' given sun. Her Armor, the same she's worn since taking office, shows every sign of repair and rehammering, like scars of battle proudly worn. Crevax is fearless in battle—even careless in zealotry.

Despite her seraphic reputation, Lord Inquisitor Crevax has aged. Still formidable, few would wish to face her in battle; yet Crevax no longer strikes out on crusades. Instead, she remains in Ember Cairn to guard the city itself. Warden of the Cathedral of Embers, the most dangerous apostates are brought before her to receive judgment. When she descends into Ember Cairn to perform executions, crowds gather in their hundreds to witness her deeds and hear her words.

From the Cathedral of Embers, the Lord Inquisitor also continues to make plans that would see the armies of the Arcanist Inquisition crusading across Etharis. She desires desperately to spread the Eternal Dogma into every realm.

entries.name: Hedric, the High Redeemer
entries: Hedric, a formidable inquisitor in his own right, commands the legions of the {@area Arcanist Inquisition|2f1|x} whilst crusading outside of Ember Cairn. Though not much younger than Lord Inquisitor Crevax, Hedric's frightening legend is beginning to eclipse his superior's own.

Hedric does not evoke the image of a seraph. His grizzled maw and curved horns make the dragonborn appear more like a daemon, which has aided in eliciting confessions from terrified heretics. Few apostates placed in Hedric's care go into the afterlife with the stench of sin still upon them, the cleansing of confession allowing each to die redeemed in the eyes of the Arch Seraphs.

Despite his infamy causing most enemies to flee and hide from the High Redeemer, Hedric sees danger everywhere. He is relentless in his pursuit of the eldritch and arcane. He would rather order his troops to raze an entire settlement than allow a single mage to escape his wrath—a matter of tension between the Inquisition and Castinella's great houses. The provinces are most at ease when Hedric's pursuits take him far from home, seeking evils in other realms.

entries.name: The Bleeding Man
entries: All cities have their share of urban myths and ghost stories. Not even sacred Ember Cairn is exempt. Over many years, a tale has emerged of the Bleeding Man, an arcanist who has sworn to topple the stones of the Cathedral of Embers and slaughter any who have sworn the oaths and joined the Inquisition. Many inquisitors have died already. Bodies of soldiers or priests have been displayed with the sigil of an Arch Seraph or Arch Daemon carved into the flesh of their skin. The victim is bloodless. A wall beneath their corpse is scrawled with a crimson manifesto and a promise that more inquisitors will meet their end.

The Inquisition is loath to acknowledge the Bleeding Man's existence. Twice they've claimed to have caught him and conducted public executions, yet both times his blood-written threats reappeared within days.

Some folk suspect the Bleeding Man is a sangromancer who escaped the cathedral's catacombs to terrorize Ember Cairn. Others believe he was tortured to death, and his crimson wraith now haunts the city. In truth, nothing is known about the Bleeding Man. His heritage. His gender. Whether he's a mortal or a monster.
An obvious theory is that he's more than one person. Some suspect an enclave of mages operates within the city to spy on the Arcanist Inquisition. If that's true, they'd be taking a tremendous risk.

entries.name: Sebastián, the Goat Shepherd
entries: Sebastián brings his grazing animals across the mountains periodically, like many shepherds. Unlike many shepherds, he leads his flock to the driest areas of the Shattered Peaks, most at risk of wildfire, and lets them eat up all the ground fuel to limit the intensity of the inevitable burn. Despite the ever-present danger, Sebastián has never been caught in a blaze.

Good-natured and maybe a little reckless, the goat herder often seems to appear at homesteads or villages in the aftermath of a blaze, offering what little comfort and aid he can. Most folk feel lucky to encounter his strange but calming hospitality. Sebastián reminds his countryfolk that suffering is temporary, but a necessary part of life for growth and humility. He does not consider the fires to be a reminder of the world's chaos or evils, but a message from the Arch Seraphs that life is to be cherished because everything is fleeting.

A rumor's begun that the humble shepherd is the {@deity Morael|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24|Arch Seraph Morael} in disguise. That he's come to bless those who've suffered and spurn those who haven't. Indeed, Sebastián does not speak highly of a church that gilds itself in glory while commoners burn and starve. Mortal or seraph, the Arcanist Inquisition has taken a keen interest in meeting this kindly goat herder.
Suffering is temporary. Compassion is eternal.

entries.name: Quest Hooks
entries: The following quest hooks can be used to flesh out adventures in Ember Cairn.

entries.name: Danger Beneath the Quarry
entries: Miners at a granite quarry have reported mysterious sounds coming from the lower levels of the mine. The rock has also felt unstable. Recently, a huge chunk of stone fell at the bottom of a mineshaft, almost causing a collapse and injuring several miners.

Furthermore, deep in the mines, the workers have discovered carvings of dragonborn antiquity. Many depict stars arranged into runic patterns. Other valuables, including ancient jewelry and precious stones, have also been found.

Alberto Yogren, foreman of the mine, would rather keep the Inquisition uninformed of these troubles. The gnome wishes to unearth whatever treasures might still be hiding in the ancient caves, despite the risks involved in concealing discoveries of possible arcane providence. However, his workers are uncooperative. The miners are almost as frightened of the grinding noises coming from deep inside the mountain as they are the Inquisition's wrath if Alberto's excavation is discovered.

Through Ember Cairn's marketplace, Alberto looks to hire mercenaries to brave the unstable mines. Where do the shafts lead? What lies at the bottom? How long can Alberto continue digging before the Arcanist Inquisition is alerted?

entries.name: Julianna's Rescue
entries: Julianna of Aurelia was downcast during the Gods' End. She was once a prominent member of the {@area Watchers of the Faithful|2d8|x} but was horrified to witness the violence mortals inflict on each other in the name of faith. Rather than strictness, Julianna taught the love of the Arch Seraphs through kindness.

For a time, she was a healer living in the Shattered Peaks, giving clemency to the sick and homeless. Then the Inquisition came. They accused Julianna of contravening the Eternal Dogma and performing sangromancy with her divine blood.
Now Julianna is housed within the Cathedral catacombs. Her congregation has, in secret, raised funds to hire mercenaries to rescue the downcast from torture and death.

Entering the Cathedral of Ember's will be its own undertaking, before even thinking of escape. Sneaking in would be nigh impossible. Voluntary capture would be the most expedient and most painful method, assuming the Inquisition did not decide upon execution over incarceration. Sneaking in through the hidden labyrinth would be no safer, unless a guide could be found to help light the way through the darkness and monsters.
Up there in the Shattered Peaks is an angel who can use magic to heal. I hope the Inquisitors don't fine her.

entries.name: Whispers in the Black Market
entries: A new commodity has begun circulating in Ember Cairn's marketplace—tomes and scrolls penned in blood: texts written by the hand of the {@area Bleeding Man|16f|x}. Many of these writings include actual sangromantic spells inscribed on their pages, which could be learned and cast by any mage able to read them. These texts are incredibly difficult to find. Obviously illegal, anyone found peddling them would be dragged immediately to the Cathedral of Embers. There is a doctrine of dissent and anarchy in the Bleeding Man's words.

Rumors in the market suggest the books and scrolls are being spread by a cult, disciples of the Bleeding Man, who call themselves the Whisperers. Their voices must be small to avoid being heard by an inquisitor's ear. Finding them through the black market requires patience and guile; many an inquisitor who has searched for them has been found hanging above the market square. Paranoia helps keep the Whisperers safe. They're suspicious of even other spellcasters, who could be used by the Inquisition to root out their members.

Once contact is made with the Whisperers, they offer many services in exchange for favors. They know of secret entrances into the hidden labyrinth and will help mages get inside. They'll sell scrolls, Potions, and spell components otherwise illegal in Ember Cairn. Meeting the Bleeding Man, if he exists, is only possible to the innermost circle of the Whisperers.

Of course, the Inquisition would also pay in gold or fire for any information they could learn about the Whisperers. The reward received would depend on whether that information was given willingly or yielded under duress in the depths of the Cathedral of Embers.

entries.name: The Empyrean Pit Murders
entries: A series of tournaments in the {@area Empyrean Pit|16b|x} has taken place, each crowning a new champion over the course of several weeks. Each night for the last four, one of those fighting champions has been murdered in a ritualistic manner. The Ember Cairn authorities have not been able to make any sense of the murders, nor can they find any leads.

A new tournament is set for this evening, and the characters are asked to have one of their group enter the tournament. The fights will be fixed in such a way that the character representative will win. Then the characters can set a trap for the murder, who will ostensibly come after the new champion.

A couple of nights after the tournament, a group of {@deity Tormach|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24} cultists, led by a {@creature Champion of Tormach|GHMG} (or some other level-appropriate {@filter servant of Tormach|bestiary|miscellaneous=!adventure npc|search=Tormach}) appears out of thin air right in front of the character who won the tournament. They attack as a genuine test of slaughter skills on behalf of {@deity Tormach|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}, who sees the fighting pit as a mockery of true battle.

data.name: Chapter 6: The Valikan Clans
entries: The lands of Valika cover the very northernmost region of Etharis, crowning the continent with an icy splendor that few outside of the clans can appreciate. The six largest {@area clans|17d|x}—Rune, Sýr, Völgr, Mithra, Morgöng, and Limgri—claim dominance over much of the land, though minor tribes can be found scattered throughout the harsh wilds.

Much like the clans themselves, the provinces of Valika each have distinctive traits that set them apart. {@area Grarjord|183|x} abuts the {@book Bürach Empire|GrimHollowCG24|3} along the southern border—with its vast pine forests. The barren lands of {@area Thrull|17f|x} act as home to those clans that rely on raiding and primordial magic, where dark traditions are held in the highest esteem. The vast province of {@area Kandar|181|x} comprises the eastern half of Valika, meeting the {@book Ostoyan Empire|GrimHollowCG24|4} along the mountains of the Grey Spine—who strive to change the perception that the rest of Etharis has about the Valikan clans. Through Valika, the land is as rugged and enduring as its people.

While those outside Valika may only think of the raiders of Thrull, with images of bloodthirsty barbarians brought to mind, the perception has begun to change. This has led to population growth along the southernmost borders as an influx of southerners arrive for diplomacy and trade.

Ever adaptable, the folk of Valika shall persevere through whatever awaits them. It should be known though, that despite the ever-shifting winds of change, some things do remain constant, chief among them the tradition of passing down stories that relate the rich history and tales of the lands of Valikan ancestors. These tales shape each generation, ensuring the strength of the land for years to come.

entries.title: Clan Mithra, Clan Morgöng, Clan Limgri, Clan Rune, Clan Sýr, Clan Völgr

entries.name: Geography
entries: Travelling across Valika requires one to be prepared for varied terrains and weather conditions. As the stories go, the region was once known as the Primordial Land, constantly in flux and uninhabitable by mere mortals.

Today, sudden elemental changes are infrequent, though not unheard of. Coldfire sweeps down from Volgen in the far northeastern reaches of Valika, consuming the land and transforming it into a harsh and unforgiving wasteland of ice. In the wake of the coldfire, an ice bridge remains connecting Volgen to the mainland. Many fear coldfire's continued spread, envisioning the entire region as a massive barren tundra if the worst were to occur.

Presently, one can find mighty fjords along the coastlines, leading into steep, ice-covered peaks that descend into an open tundra that makes up most of the landmass of Grarjord and Thrull. Snowcapped forests dot the edges of the fjords south of the city of {@area Halsfjord|186|x}—the seat of Clan Rune. To the north of the Teeth, frozen rivers wind down from the perpetually frozen lake Frostmere, which shares its name with the ice fortress that stands atop its treacherous surface.

The Thockan Pass makes cuts through the Haeysor Mountains to {@area Borgund|189|x}, the seat of Clan Sýr. Northwest of these mountains lies the Holgar Strait, providing access to the island of Holgar and the city of {@area Tyburn|188|x}. This bustling port is the seat of Clan Völgr and the primary launch point for Valikan reaver fleets that sow terror along the coast.

East of Borgund, forests bleed into the open tundra before climbing the Grensfal Mountains and {@area Foxwood Range|18e|x}, nominally delineating Kandar's boundary. {@area Hrist|187|x}, Kandar's capital, lies on the mountain's southeastern edge along the valley formed by the Isbryteralva river, which flows south from Lake Jakkan towards the Skuggiskogr Forest. The cities of {@area Summerhelm|18a|x} and Runeheim rest near the edges of the forest.

Continuing eastward, the Njucka Mountains complete the ring that protects Cold Iron Keep, with the Ormralva River flowing down from those peaks into Shadow Storm Gulf. The mountains continue eastward, meeting up with the coldfire bridge that spans the gap between the mainland and Volgen, meeting at {@area Fort Kentigern|18c|x}. The isle of {@area Cinderghast|18d|x} lies in a smoky haze off the northern coastline, in a stark counterpoint to the barren frozen wastelands of Volgen, host to the Snow Queen's palace in the Astrik Mountains.

entries.name: History
entries: The history of the Valikan {@area Clans|17d|x} is mostly passed down via oral tradition, but the occasional legends have been written down by scholars throughout the ages. Most of these legends chronicle the major milestones that shaped the face of the lands and influenced the cultural standards that are still practiced by most of the clans to this day. The greatest legends were compiled into a manuscript known as the {@i Lord Edda} by the scholars at Hrist, and copies of various versions remain in safekeeping.

entries.name: The Great Prismatic Wyrm
entries: Legends of the time before humans occupied the northern lands speak of the mighty primordials roaming this region. Their power was said to be so great as to rival that of the gods, drawing from each of the sacred elements—fire, water, air, and earth.

The mightiest among these elementals was a colossal serpent known as {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}, and he was worshiped even by his peers, treated as a prince whom they bowed before. Stories speak of his enormous size, and the strength and powers he held that outshone any other primordials. Of these stories, none sow quite as much fear as the depictions of the utter destruction he left in his wake as he traversed the lands, with coldfire erupting at his every step, utterly consuming all living things and leaving behind a barren wasteland.

Of course, even such a fearsome beast as {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24} must have a weakness, and this was found to be true. The Seven Heroes—for whom the current ruling clans are named—sought out the Great Wyrm to end his reign: Kentigern of the Wastes, the sorceress Rune, Sýr the Seven-Bladed, Völgr Clad-in-Iron, Limgri Lightbringer, Morgöng Shadestrider, and Mithra of the Silver Harp.

These heroes hoped to vanquish {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24} so as to allow the northern lands a chance to settle and become suitable for habitation. They fought battle after battle against countless hordes of {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}'s elemental forces, eventually being swallowed by the Great Wyrm himself. However, this was not to be the end, as they hatched a daring plan from within the belly of the beast to pierce the creature's heart and defeat him once and for all...or so their story goes.

Many, however, believe that the Great Wyrm will one day rise again to consume all of Etharis. Many of the clans believe this tale—and the potentially apocalyptic ending—to be literally true. Woe to those who stand between these fundamentalist Valikans and their goals.

entries.name: Thorgard's Saga
entries: The Seven Heroes hold a special place in the legends of Valika, but other heroes have risen in the days since the land was claimed. Thousands of years after the fall of {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}, the clans were divided over countless minor squabbles, and the need arose for a hero to unite the clans.

Thorgard, an orc of Clan Völgr, decided he would be the hero required by his people, and the mighty warrior sought a path to unite the clans. Aided by a hag who had visions of the future, Thorgard quested for the legendary weapon of Kentigern, the hero who assembled the Seven Heroes. With Bane-Slayer—a magical double-bladed sword wreathed in ice and flames—in hand, Thorgard began his campaign for unity, travelling from clan to clan gathering forces beneath his banner to defeat the common enemy of all clans: invading southerners.

By force or by diplomacy, one after another, each clan pledged their banners to Thorgard, sharpening their Weapons and preparing their longships for battle. A cunning plan was hatched to draw the southern forces out into the open by undertaking a series of raids against the coastal settlements they had established. Once the southern troops had been driven inland to the tundra, they were met by fierce warriors from each of the tribes. Battles raged for some time, but the united strength of the clans proved to be enough to drive the invaders back south beyond the border of Valika.

With his victory in hand, Thorgard then set out with a mighty fleet to conquer more lands in the west for Valika, but alas, that was the last time anyone saw the ferocious conqueror. Tales of his courage and determination remain staples of Valikan storytelling, and the saga of Thorgard is forever in the minds of his people.

entries.name: The Prismatic Circle
entries: The reputation of Valika as a land of brutal raiders has been propagated by the druids of the {@area Prismatic Circle|25d|x}. Years ago, an elemental druid known as Kybard claimed to have experienced prophetic visions which proved that the Seven Heroes had not vanquished {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}, but that he merely sleeps beneath the earth and sea. Kybard claimed that his visions showed a future where the Great Wyrm awakened to destroy the world. This future could only be prevented by sating the creature with magic and the blood of mortals.

The druids of the Prismatic Circle tirelessly travel the northern lands between the ritual sites known as {@area Sentinel Woods|18f|x}, making great sacrifices of magical trinkets triumphantly taken from the southlands, stolen livestock barged north throughout the raiding season, and humanoid victims—both the condemned of the Thrull-based clans and thralls marked as offerings to slake the hunger of {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}'s sleeping spirit.

The total number of Valikan warriors fallen over centuries of raiding, southlanders killed defending against the longboats' attacks, and victims slain as part of the Prismatic Circle's rituals might never be known and is certainly uncountable. With each new raiding season, the ground beneath the Sentinel Woods grows ever darker with blood.

The druids of the Prismatic Circle are led by the {@area Grand Druid Hjadana|1a5|x}, who steadfastly upholds the sanctity of Kybard's visions as the driving force behind their purpose. Her fanaticism to the cause has led to the vicious cycles of raiding and death that make most in the southern lands tremble at the thought of Valika.

The brutality that Hjadana oversees has also allowed the Prismatic Circle to maintain a firm hold on the fealty of the clans of {@area Thrull|17f|x} and {@area Grarjord|183|x}, especially that of Clan Völgr, whose might in battle serve them well as they functionally serve as the military might which enforces the Circle's edicts.

entries.name: Raiding and Thralls
entries: Valikan raids inspire a deep fear in the people of Etharis, all of whom have heard the tales of Clan Völgr's berserker fury, the strength of Clan Sýr's arms and Armor, and the potency of Clan Rune's destructive magic. When Valikan longships sail south early in spring, settlements along the coast from Kulen Sound to the Gulf of Lions look to the security of their food and trade goods, their livestock, and their valuables. While those items are precious, the most valuable treasure that the raiding parties return with is thralls.

Valika's harsh winters take a toll on the population. The more desperate and immoral clans bolster their population through captives brought back from raids. Raiding for thralls is so old a tradition among the clans that many Valikans, mostly those under the banners of the Thrull-based clans, have no perspective to even question the practice.

The north is a harsh land sparsely covered by small settlements, where folk understand that they are responsible for their own survival. But the number of people needed to work the rocky fields, to delve the mines, to crew fishing boats on the rough waters, and to hunt the fast-moving game herds of the north has never been matched by the available population of the clans. So, millennia ago, those clans looked southward for the folk they needed to keep their settlements and their culture alive.

The Valikan clans raid each other endlessly, to be sure. Folk captured by another clan can be bonded into servitude as easily as the folk of foreign lands. But such local raids effectively see the clans only trading members back and forth, and so they focus more on the theft of resources, livestock, lore, and portable wealth—leaving thrall raids to the reaving fleets of the south.

entries.name: Politics

entries.title: Grand Druid Hjadana and Chief Nolgr

entries: Most of the clans inhabiting the western Valikan territory of {@area Thrull|17f|x}, while not technically under the rule of the Prismatic Circle, follows the edicts and rules set forth by the Grand Druid. These edicts encourage the clans to participate in constant conflict, raiding enemies and neighbors alike in the hopes of appeasing {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24} the Great Wyrm and maintaining his slumbering vigil. The Grand Druid commands the circle from their base of operations on the island of Holgar. Technically, though, each clan has their own system of governance.

entries.name: Clan Rune
entries: Of the major Valikan clans, Clan Rune, enjoys one of the most representative political structures. An overseer controls each settlement. Larger towns typically have an overseer council of three elected officials, while the clan seat at {@area Halsfjord|186|x} is overseen by the Council of Rune—thirteen councilors elected by the folk of the city to govern the clan. The authority of local councilors is absolute in matters governed by clan laws, which have long been codified and recorded in written form. However, when clan members in dispute with each other disagree with a councilor's decree, appeals can be made to the Council of Rune.

entries.name: Clan Sýr
entries: The politics of Clan Sýr are tied to the hierarchy of the Järnförning. At ten years of age, each member of Clan Sýr is bonded to the Järnförning, a hierarchy of labor, responsibility, and authority that shapes the lives of all clanfolk. The Järnförning is both guild and school, set up as twelve tiers—called "nivåer" by clan tradition—of knowledge and skill running the range from apprentice to clan master.

Each settlement, large or small, is led by three wardens, each of which must be a member of the highest nivå found in that settlement, and who are voted into service by other members of the same nivå.

entries.name: Clan Völgr
entries: The hierarchy within Clan Völgr is established by dominance, with the strongest and hardiest inevitably rising to positions of authority in their raiding parties and home villages. But strength is reckoned in many ways among the Völgr—the most powerful warriors often willingly pay fealty to figures with weaker physical and martial attributes who have proven leadership and tactical acumen.

Standing atop the clan power structure, Chief {@area Nolgr Magnusson|1a5|x} has ruled the Völgr for nearly thirty years, and as clan leader, Nolgr is also Keeper of the Three Tribes, a title bestowed by the Prismatic Circle, which effectively makes him master of all the Thrull-based clans.

entries.name: Clan Mithra
entries: Like Clan Rune in Thrull, Clan Mithra has a representative political structure. Villages hold things—also known as moots—in which they elect an overseer. All townsfolk over the age of fifteen can vote at the thing. Overseers appoint a Voice, usually from among the best musicians and skalds, to travel to the larger things of the jarldom in which they reside. While jarls are also elected, their positions tend to stay within their families, blurring the lines between democracy and a hereditary monarchy.

At the fall equinox althing, set at the end of the annual harvest celebration, the jarls elect a king or queen from among themselves. The current monarch is {@area Queen Andrea Helsing|1a5|x}, who rules from the capital city of {@area Kandar|181|x}, {@area Hrist|187|x}. The greatest foes to Clan Mithra are the clans of Thrull, whose incursions have forced the Mithrans to develop and hold their standing armies. Preferring diplomacy, Queen Andrea still hopes that there can be a solution to the conflict beyond the continued loss of life. Thus far, violence seems to be the only answer their foes will accept.

entries.name: Clan Morgöng
entries: Clan Morgöng lacks anything resembling a centralized government. Jarl Summerhelm holds a hereditary title and village leaders are typically the eldest villagers, those who have survived the most raids or weathered the most winters. As a matter of pride, Clan Morgöng has no written records. Laws are memorized by a lawspeaker, typically a skald, who then interprets disputes based on prior precedence. Typical punishments include fines, brief indenturement as a thrall, or an alternate form of action that rights the wrong. Petty thievery against other villagers may be accepted once, but repeat offenders risk being exiled from the village. The serious crimes of murder and sexual assault are not tolerated; killing is allowed on raids, but never at home, and sexual assault is forbidden even on raids. These, along with intentional damage to any of the village's watercraft, are viewed as the most grievous crimes and are punishable by execution.

entries.name: Clan Limgri
entries: Like Clan Morgöng, there's very little central government ruling Clan Limgri. Family units operate independently, although they do gather for celebrations four times a year, on the solstices and equinoxes. As far as others are concerned, Jarl Angrboða Grata of {@area Solheim|18b|x} and {@area Taala Brightstar|1a5|x} speak for the clan. In most cases, this is true: while the jarl and the druid have little impact over how the families and communities are organized, they are the two most in touch with the rest of the elders and leaders among the clan. Among the small groups of traveling nomads, Limgri's druids and warlocks are most likely to take on leadership positions.

There are few codified laws among Clan Limgri, although each community may have specific laws and penalties for transgressions as enacted by their own leaders. Across the clan, those who commit the gravest transgressions are abandoned in the wilds on their own. Without supplies or a community, abandonment in the wilds means almost certain death.

Hospitality is the one governing law of Clan Limgri. Any strangers found in the northern wilds are welcomed to stay as long as they need. Strangers are never denied food and drink; all that the Limgri have is shared with those they encounter in the wilderness. For this reason, the Limgri frequently find themselves victims of Völgr raids; their generosity is taken advantage of by scouts, who then report the nomads' location to Völgr raiding parties. Unwilling to change their own cultural laws, the Limgri, through Jarl Grata and Taala Brightstar, have petitioned for the Clan Mithra armies to extend their outposts further north. Queen Andrea has provided those warriors in exchange for Clan Limgri taking control of Cold Iron Keep, which houses the most dangerous, non-Valikan criminals in Kandar.

entries.name: Provinces
entries: Unlike the other kingdoms, borders in Valika are more of a nebulous concept. Each province flows into the next without much regard for boundaries, unless there is a physical feature, such as a river or mountain range, that easily delineates the divide. Which often means that clan members might cross into the territory of a rival clan without knowing—until they come across a rival clan's hunting party.

entries.name: Thrull
entries: The area known as Thrull stretches from the island of Holgar in the far west of the northlands—home to the city of {@area Tyburn|188|x} and the seat of the Prismatic Circle—to the foothills of the Grensfal Mountains that shield the city of {@area Hrist|187|x} against land raids from Clan Völgr. Thrull is the dominant culture in Valika, at least as far as the major clans are concerned. And because those clans define the raiding culture by which Valika is known, so too do folk across Etharis acknowledge the primacy of this province and its people.

entries.name: Grand Druid Hjadana
entries: As the Grand Druid of the {@area Prismatic Circle|25d|x}, Hjadana holds a large influence on the political and cultural habits of many of Valika's clan and leaders. From her home in Tyburn on the island of Holgar, Hjadana presides over the ritualistic sacrifices that the circle claims are necessary to appease the sleeping Prismatic Wyrm, {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}, and keep him in his resting state. These rituals originated from the visions of the druid Kybard, and her fanaticism for his views drive the clans of Thrull to bloodlust, which can only be sated by reaving. Whether the victims be Valikan or foreign is often of little concern to Hjadana, only that blood is spilled to appease {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}. Hjadana's closeness to Chief Nolgr Magnusson of Clan Völgr, and the Prismatic Circle's presence in the Völgr clan seat of Tyburn, ensures that the bonds between the druids and that clan's warriors and raiders remains strong.

entries.name: Grenhildr
entries: Once a protégé of Hjadana and thought to be her successor, a traumatic near-death experience caused Grenhildr to reject the traditional beliefs of the Prismatic Circle, in turn causing a schism of faith within the circle.

Grenhildr had been separated from her party after traveling to complete a ritual sacrifice and got lost in a blizzard, where she was assumed to be dead. Much to the surprise of the great druid, she returned to Tyburn, bringing with her a tale of having been saved and fed by prismatic drakes, and spending the winter in a sheltered cavern where {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24} the Great Prismatic Wyrm spoke to her. Grenhildr told any who would listen of how {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}'s long sleep was causing the wyrm's power to fade, and how the north would come to apocalyptic ruin if that power ever ended.

Her followers are known as the Cult of the Great Wyrm. Much to the horror of Hjadana, their beliefs have been spreading and catching on across the lands of Valika, even in the province of Thrull, where the Prismatic Circle holds the most sway.

entries.name: Nolgr Magnusson
entries: Chief Nolgr Magnusson of the Völgr is effectively the leader of all Thrull, though his control over the three primary clans is not as absolute as some would think. As the chief of the Völgr clan, Nolgr is the war leader of his people, personally leading raids against lesser clans, the lands of Kandar, and the peoples of the south for nearly three decades. As Keeper of the Three Tribes, he has been invested by the Prismatic Circle with an extra level of authority over Clan Rune and Clan Sýr, at least ceremonially. And Nolgr is a master of ceremony, and a leader whose charisma and personal magnetism have proved nearly as deadly as his axe hand. Nolgr proudly acknowledges that his relationship with the Grand Druid Hjadana is the foundation of his power in Thrull, and rumors fly that he may have fathered at least one of her children.

entries.name: Ordun Doransson
entries: The chief of Clan Rune, Ordun cuts an imposing figure, with a shadowed complexion and sun-bright hair, under-shaved and braided. Protected against the elements by his magic, he usually goes bare-chested even in the coldest weather, presenting an intimidating presence to all those who meet him. By presenting himself not just as a formidable rune mage but a potent war chief, Ordun has positioned himself in his people's eyes as an equal to Nolgr Magnusson, chief of Clan Völgr and anointed as Keeper of the Three Tribes by the Prismatic Circle. And as Ordun has done so, the pride and independence of Clan Rune has grown.

entries.name: Kandar
entries: The area known as Kandar reaches as far to the northeast as Volgen, which was once an island but is now connected to the mainland by a coldfire glacial bridge. The more populated regions are far to the west, near the center of the peninsula, at the Grensfal Mountains and the disputed lands adjacent to Thrull. There, the united troops of the Kandar clans of Mithra, Limgri, and Morgöng keep watch. The more temperate {@area Summerhelm|18a|x} in the southwest provides a southern border for the major Kandar clans, though a city even further south, Runeheim, is nominally within Kandar's borders.

Despite Kandar's diplomatic efforts, the people of Etharis still first associate Valika with the greater violence of Thrull. Kandar's desire for peace has allowed them to make inroads with the southern nations, but the wilds of the region, the facial tattoos worn by many of the northerners, and the reputation of their neighbors leave the people of Kandar fighting against stereotypes from any foreigners within their lands.

entries.name: Queen Andrea Helsing
entries: Queen Helsing of {@area Hrist|187|x} is known to most of the people of Kandar. She is the leader of Clan Mithra, and the elected war chief of the united alliance of Kandar clans. Queen Andrea is a ruler who must balance the needs of the various clans with the threat of Thrull in the west. Her ambition and desire to drive change and progress in Kandar led her to become a good ruler, even though she may claim that she never desired the role.

Queen Andrea is dedicated to the survival of Kandar, free from the Prismatic Circle and the threat of Thrull. She has no philosophical or cultural problem with her fellow Valikans across the border, but she seeks to keep the clans within Kandar unconquered. This requires her to reach out to the southern lands. Fighting against the prejudice that all the people of the northlands are barbarians has been a struggle, and one of the ways Queen Andrea counters that image is by supporting Hrist's cosmopolitan look and atmosphere.

She welcomes trade with other nations and pursues alliances with those who would offer military or magical aid. In addition to her political alliances, she is savvy when it comes to working outside the rules. She frequently has her agents hire adventurers for tasks related to the war effort, or in creating situations that would lead other cities and nations to ally with Kandar. If some of these missions seem to walk a thin line along the edge of honor, well, she's not the one carrying them out—that ethical question rests solely on those she has hired.

Though much of her childhood was spent in Hrist, Andrea traveled throughout Kandar with a diplomatic mission from Clan Mithra to the other clans during her youth. She was part of the entourage, not as a diplomat herself, but as a bodyguard to Urda Longsight, a Clan Mithra seer and poet. The ancient woman took Andrea under her wing, and though the young warrior never took to the reading of runes or entrails, she learned a great deal about patience and listening—and how knowing what someone wants can be used as leverage when forming alliances or compromises.

Once known as a golden-haired shield maiden, Queen Andrea has grayed over the past several years, not only due to her growing age but also her worry over Kandar's fate. Queen Andrea shows her battle scars with honor, frequently wearing sleeveless tunics under a fur cape, so that the old injuries are obviously displayed on her pale arms.

entries.name: Taala Brightstar
entries: Taala was raised among the wandering Limgri with their family and was marked as a seer at a very young age. Their talent for nature magic blossomed while Taala was still a child, and they plagued their family by running with the reindeer—sometimes in the shape of a reindeer—making it difficult to keep track of the youth. As the speaker of Clan Limgri, Taala devotes their time to manifesting the Lightbringer philosophy to the clans, making life as full of beauty and peace for their people as possible.

While they have seen many threats to the people of Valika during their long life, but none so frightening as the dual disaster of war with Thrull while coldfire expands its grasp of the north. The elderly druid fears for the future of their people, and does everything they can to ensure that Clan Limgri, along with the other clans of Kandar, will see the new world when it comes. Despite all the troubles plaguing the world, Taala has not lost hope. Taala views themself as a spiritual heir to Limgri Lightbringer and is dedicated to shining that light against any shadows that encroach on Valika.

As they aged, Taala's temperament evened out, but they still prefer to wear a furred or feathered form to walking with humans. The latter has become a necessity, however, not only due to Taala's aging body, but also due to their position as an advisor in {@area Solheim|18b|x} and the central point for all of Clan Limgri's complaints and concerns.

They seek to support the efforts of the Order of Kentigern in staving off coldfire and speak on behalf of Clan Limgri at althings to request protection for the northern clan from Völgr raids. Taala has also tried to reach out to the Redclaws, hoping that the secretive order will accept aid and support from the united Clans to face off against the Prismatic Circle, but the Redclaws remain elusive, perhaps because Taala is only skilled at shapeshifting and not a true shapeshifter.

entries.name: Thrulldr
entries: A prime example of the close ties between nature and humans in Valika, Thrulldr is an enormous werebear who spends his time split between his human and bear forms. He claims his hulking appearance is due more to the transformation he underwent as a boy than his Valikan heritage. Even in human form, Thrulldr's body is covered with dark hair so thick it might as well be fur, and he proudly wears a braided beard extending down his chest.

As the leader of a group known as the Redclaw druids, Thrulldr can trace his heritage back to Kentigern of the Wastes, but his power draws not from that connection but from the force of his own personality. Among his people, Thrulldr is known for his great rumbling laugh and his generous spirit.

But until anyone enters the circle he considers his family, the huge man treats them with suspicion, or even derision. To Thrulldr, druids who have not undergone a final transformation to give their bodies a second, permanent shape, are not close enough to the wild to be trusted.

For clanfolk who fear Thrull's invasion, or coldfire's looming presence, the Redclaws offer a kind of hope for another path, one that embraces their harsh natural world rather than fighting against it. Thrulldr views all recruits to the circle as potential allies, but he withholds judgment until an older member of the circle can speak for them.

The Redclaws are primarily druids, but other shapeshifters, both natural-born and transformed, are among their number, and the final test—the transformation into one of the werecreatures within the ranks of the circle—is not determined by skill with nature magic, but strength of heart and will. In this way, Thrulldr has built a widespread following, all beholden to him as their alpha, but few gathered in one place.

entries.name: Njavesh Luunsdóttir
entries: While Njavesh is a druid, she does not belong to any of the typical orders found across Valika, but instead was raised in the wilds. Born in a druidic circle known as the Fox Whisperers, she is a gifted caster and shapechanger who holds a deep connection with the many awakened foxes who roam the northern tundra and Grensfal hills.

As the leader of the Fox Whisperers, Njavesh has a wide network of information available to her, gathered from her decades of wandering and from the secrets that the frostbite foxes return to her. Any who become injured while traveling through the territory the foxes roam, or those who respectfully request an audience through the frostbite foxes to seek answers can gain an audience with Njavesh.

entries.name: Sanrun
entries: Abbot Sanrun is head of the formerly contemplative Order of Kentigern. A bald man with grudgel features, old enough to remember a time before the current coldfire crisis—and yet still appearing in his prime, the abbot has a mournful aura. Though he maintains a welcoming and friendly air, especially when greeting recruits to the order, there is always a sense of how much the world and its crises weigh on this man, who intended a life of quiet solitude, not war.

Originally a member of Clan Mithra, Sanrun is fully devoted to the Order of Kentigern, and is dismayed at the lack of funding the order has been receiving since the border war began. In fact, Sanrun believes that {@area Kandar|181|x} and {@area Thrull|17f|x} must unite to face the coldfire crisis before it freezes the entire north, though his calls for unity are generally ignored in {@area Hrist|187|x}.

Despite this, Sanrun has expanded his work in recruiting those who would keep coldfire at bay, reaching out beyond the borders of Kandar and into the southern kingdoms. He is particularly interested in bringing evokers and alchemists into the order, as the first can combat coldfire directly with their spells, and the latter can create the tools with which the rest of the order fights. Alchemist's fire, burning oils, and exploding casks are among the explosives collected by the Order of Kentigern, and Abbot Sanrun knows that they will never have enough.

Still, despite his sorrowful attitude, Abbot Sanrun runs a tight organization, giving rank and status to each member so they know whose commands to obey and where they fit in the hierarchy. The order's training grounds within {@area Fort Kentigern|18c|x} are extensive, and their work with ice drakes, polar bears, and giant lynxes requires many riders and handlers who are skilled with animals. Druids who are unable to take the final step to join the Redclaws frequently make their way, after their failed test, to the Order of Kentigern, where Abbot Sanrun welcomes them with open arms.

entries.name: Grarjord
entries: Often, the entirety of the north is called Grarjord by southerners, and many from the southern lands still refer to the lands of the Valikan clans by this ancient name. While Grarjord still encompasses much of the land from the border of the {@book Bürach Empire|GrimHollowCG24|3} stretching up along the coastline, and outward across the open tundra, determining where the borders between it and Thrull or Kandar lie can be a tricky proposition.

The open tundra that comprises most of Grarjord is the home of many minor clans that enjoy the relative freedom they find here away from the influence of clans Rune, Sýr, and Völgr, and their constant clashes with the clans of Kandar. While technically, much of the tundra does fall under the authority of Thrull and its clans, there are very few resources worth squabbling over, and therefore, very little oversight of the people who settle there. The downside to this arrangement lies in the unforgiving nature of the land, the bitter cold, its barren soil, and the monstrous beasts that lurk outside the light of a warm campfire.

entries.name: Locations of Interest
entries: The following locations are places of power, importance, and adventure within the lands of the Valikan clans.

entries.name: Skarfanes
entries: This town once served as a watch post and waystation where the reaving fleets could stop and resupply. However, its proximity to the {@book Bürach Empire|GrimHollowCG24|3}, and to their prized city of Erlefurt, led to an eventual capture by the Bürach's fleet. Rather than using the town as a safehold to push further into Grarjord, the empire declared Skarfanes to be a free city and neutral trading post. Today, clan traders largely control Skarfanes, even as it's protected by imperial forces set up as a satellite of the garrison in Erlefurt.

The town is a sprawling settlement of huts and homes, clustered around longhouses and warehouses and surrounded by a formidable stockade wall built of imported timbers. Four gates mark the entry points into the city, but just as many locals live outside Skarfanes as inside, with most of the minor clans of Grarjord maintaining permanent steadings within sight of the walls. Moreover, after three generations, intermarriage between the imperial occupiers and local clanfolk has created a mixed culture unique to Skarfanes.

entries.name: Halsfjord
entries: Situated along one of the many peninsulas that make up the Teeth—the series of long, fjord-cut peninsulas that give the northlands the appearance of a great beast set to consume the south—Halsfjord serves as the seat of Clan Rune. This position makes it one of the most easily protected cities in all of Valika, a fact which its residents surely appreciate with their intimate knowledge of raiding.

Halsfjord also boasts a store of magical knowledge to rival any colleges in the {@book Bürach Empire|GrimHollowCG24|3}. The residents understand all too well the value of their knowledge and craft. Stealthy raids by war bands are a constant threat, with many minor clans intent on bringing knowledgeable Clan Rune mages or artificers into their service as thralls.

Halsfjord stands as a beacon on rocky terrain, high above its namesake fjord, its walls fortified by years of enchantments adding to its layers of defense. Magical beacons blaze in the night, leading the way for ships and merchants alike. Magic is the leading commodity that Clan Rune trades for all the food and sundries required by residents.

Halsfjor'ds might is mostly attributed to it being the final home of Rune, the clan's namesake sorcerer. She used powerful magic to imprison {@area Cinderghast's|18d|x} fire elementals before retiring to her village. In the years following, many of the world's best sorcerers and artificers visited Halsfjord seeking her knowledge, which resulted in the formation of numerous magical schools. These schools enabled Halsfjord to become the vibrant city that is seen today.

entries.name: Hrist
entries: Hrist is the seat of the {@area Kandar|181|x} province and Clan Mithra. It is where {@area Queen Andrea Helsing|1a5|x} holds court for her jarls in the Great Hall. It is a bustling city, inviting people from across Etharis to live and work. Most foreigners who don't have valuable skills to contribute to the clan—such as metalworking—find themselves relegated to the foreign quarter in the city's outer circle beyond its walls. Soldier barracks and a large marketplace where less reputable merchants hawk their wares can also be found in the outer circle.

The inner circle houses the Great Hall and the residences of most of the oldest families of Clan Mithra. Many strive to move up in reputation enough to gain a coveted residence in the first circle, but most Valikans and skilled craftspeople reside in the middle circle, which also houses the Kaupang, Hrist's largest market, and the Skald Library, which is renowned throughout Etharis for its extensive collection of relics and artifacts.

entries.name: Tyburn
entries: As the seat of Clan Völgr and the seat of the {@area Thrull Province|17f|x}, this bustling port city is spoken of with equal parts fear and reverence. The fearsome fleets of longships that deposit raiders upon the shores of their southern neighbors depart from the deep harbor on the southern corner of the island of Holgar.

Further adding to the city's reputation, the {@area Prismatic Circle|25d|x} and its leader, {@area Hjadana|1a5|x}, established their base of operations in Tyburn. As a result, the circle has been able to heavily project their influence into the government, enough so that one could argue that Thrull is a theocracy dictated by the edicts of the Prismatic Circle.

entries.name: Borgund
entries: The city of Borgund in the Armarka Mountains is the largest settlement in Thrull's north, and the seat of Clan Sýr. Said to have been first settled while Sýr the Seven-Bladed still lived, the upper levels of Borgund spread along a series of stepped mountainside terraces lined with stone-walled houses.

But the true size of the city becomes apparent only once visitors pass into Sýr's Well, a set of slow-sloping ramps that descend from a broad central court atop the highest terrace, down into the stony depths of the mountain. Long tunnels radiate out from the foot of the well like spokes, with hundreds of chambers and halls sharing space with the great underground forges—the legacy of Clan Sýr and its people's legendary skill with metalwork.

With much of Borgund set within the mountains of the far north, the half-year of night that descends across the region in winter is of little concern to those who dwell here. Borgund's halls and tunnels are a warm warren of magical rune light, making this one of the most pleasant places to spend the long winters in Valika. The heat of the forges balances the chill of stone, with fresh air constantly cycled into the depths through elaborate ventilation tunnels.

Borgund is a hub of manufacturing for Clan Sýr's hallmark Weapons and Armor, and the site of the classrooms and training forges where young clanfolk and thralls are trained. More so than in any other area of Thrull, many of Borgund's thralls elect to join Clan Sýr once their term of service is up, dedicating themselves to the artistry of metal. As a result, Borgund boasts more dwarves, elves, and other folk of the southlands than any other settlement in Thrull.

entries.name: Summerhelm
entries: The second largest settlement in {@area Kandar|181|x} after Hrist, Summerhelm is the seat of Jarl Birger av Summerhelm. The city is ringed by a tall stone wall, which casts shadows over the city, making it seem as if caught in a perpetual twilight.

This shadowy atmosphere perfectly suits the needs of the many illicit merchants that make the city their home. Though many storefronts appear to be on the right side of Kandar's cultural laws, the greater center of commerce is known as the Dream Market. As Jarl Summerhelm's attention towards the city has dwindled, the Dream Market has become more open about its wares. Weaponry, frequently ill-gotten, goes for reasonable prices, and magic charms, both real and fake, fill the market tables.

Unlike most of Kandar, Summerhelm's construction is more like what is found in the southern kingdoms, with the Summer Palace being the greatest example. The large fortress is built like an Ostoyan Castle and was likely held by the ancestors of that nation in the distant past. But deeper inside the building, it's clear that humans were not the original builders of this monument to strength.

entries.name: Solheim
entries: As the name suggests, this small city was once home to a grand temple to the sun. However, that temple fell into disrepair after the gods fell, and now sits prominently in the center of the city as a decaying reminder of how faith alone cannot save you.

The smallest of Kandar's three cities, Solheim sits on the Shadow Storm Gulf, bereft of faith, existing but no longer thriving. The shipyards are Solheim's one remaining pride, along with the well-kept great hall, where Jarl Angrboða Grata stores her things. Still, an overarching atmosphere of doom pervades the city, even on the rare occasions when the sun does shine down on them.

entries.name: Fort Kentigern
entries: Originally built as a monastery for an order of contemplative monks, Fort Kentigern has become the last shield between the coldfire waves and the rest of the northlands. The high keep is built in a style similar to the fortresses of the south, with thick stone walls shaped by both craftspeople and mages. The fort's curtain walls are 10 feet thick and sport defensive siege weaponry. The ramparts are lined with catapults, trebuchets, and ballista, all of which are designed to hurl flaming pitch into the cold blue flames. The ramparts also feature enclosures where evokers can hurl their own fireballs to the grounds below.

Here, the Order of Kentigern gathers, not for contemplation, but as defenders of the living—and, perhaps, the world. Unlike the rest of Valika, the clans hold no sway in Fort Kentigern. Once a person has joined the order, their past is forgotten; their life becomes devoted to one single cause. A majority of the order is drawn from Valikans, but people from all over Etharis count themselves among their number. While some members initially retain their suspicions concerning arcane magic or the faithful, they soon realize that any skills brought to bear against the coldfire have their value.

The Order of Kentigern's accepting and inclusive outlook is not out of philosophy, but out of necessity: all are welcome in the fight, for as long as they can fight. And many, many members of the order die in the line of duty. And yet, despite all the efforts of the Order of Kentigern, the coldfire continues to freeze Valika and its people. Of the few settlements that once existed on Volgen, only a handful now remain, and even those have shrunk as their residents flee the danger—or die before they are able to run.

entries.name: Cinderghast
entries: As tales go, Cinderghast has always been a place better suited to fire elementals than humans, and the state of the volcanic islands now supports this theory. The Svalr, though, tell of a time when the settlement of Varra flourished there, its soil fertile, its crops rich, its people happy. When Rune of the Seven Heroes trapped the salamander Ixlalu there with a curse, the land erupted into the poisoned landscape that now exists. Though few dare visit these islands with their active volcanoes and treacherous waters, a few scholars have braved the journey to find that the Svalr tales have merit. Cinderghast has a vast ruin, covered in ash and buried in rock, where an ancient city once stood.

The islands are bound by a curse that makes studying the ruins difficult. Some areas are blocked to mortal travel; though visitors can see where they would like to go in the distance, when they walk toward their destination, they find themselves turned around and back at their point of origin. This is the nature of the spell binding Ixlalu and his army of salamanders and fire elementals to Cinderghast. However, the bonds of that spell are weakening, and the smallest of the fire elementals are beginning to escape.

entries.name: The Foxwood Range
entries: The Foxwood Range is a narrow vale in the Grensfal Mountain foothills, comprising a network of warren tunnels and dens providing safe haven for frostbite foxes to whelp and raise their young in spring. The unique geography ensures a large number of foxes disperse back into the wilds for autumn and winter trapping. The frostbite foxes of the north, hunted and trapped for their resilient, weatherproof fur, are an important part of Valikan clan culture.

The moon druids of an order known as the Fox Whisperers are instrumental to the long-term health of fox stocks, shepherding packs of these valuable creatures and ensuring that hunting and trapping don't deplete their numbers. But one druid of that order, Njavesh, has taken her dedication to the foxes to another level.

Packs of frostbite foxes, led by elders awakened by Njavesh's magic, range out from Foxwood to patrol the tundra of central {@area Thrull|17f|x}, on the lookout for lost travelers. Their particular focus is seeking newly released thralls attempting the arduous journey south to their former homelands. These foxes lead their charges along safe trails, even hunting to help feed those unable to do so themselves, and as such, the foxes have gained an almost mythical status among thralls and travelers. It is said that those who sight the foxes have been graced by the gods with luck and are sure to survive whatever travails the harsh lands of Valika put before them.

entries.name: The Sentinel Woods
entries: These trees are the {@area Prismatic Circle's|25d|x} altars of sacrifice, so named for the druids' belief that the great oaks stand vigil above the underworld where {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24} sleeps, their roots drawing the blood of sacrificed creatures downward. In every season except the darkest months of winter, the druids of the Prismatic Circle make regular pilgrimages to the dozens of widely scattered oak groves known collectively as the Sentinel Woods.

These great clusters of trees are known to be magical sites by anyone cognizant of the normal range of flora found in the north—oaks are typically unable to grow in such a landscape. But an even darker magic makes its presence known to any who step into the eternal shadow of the groves, steeped into soil turned blood-black by generations of murderous sacrifice.

Four druids attending each sacrifice first speak {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}'s tale aloud. Then, working together, they flense each victim's skin from their body with ritual chanting and razor-sharp knives. During the humanoid sacrifices, the druids also sacrifice magic trinkets stolen from the southlands, incorporating them into their murderous rites. When the last humanoid sacrifice has been conducted, the druids and Völgr warriors eagerly watching the rites immediately depart, for by tradition, no one can linger in any Sentinel Wood between times of sacrifice. Animals and people are left where they died to feed the crows and wolves over long months, with the next group of druids who come to each Sentinel Wood entering another part of the grove where previous victims have long rotted away to nothing but scattered bones.

When Clan Völgr patrols pass a Sentinel Wood, the warriors always draw near it seeking intruders, not entering the trees but circling around them in search of trespassers who may wish to revive those who have been sacrificed, or who wish to prevent further sacrifices.

entries.name: Quest Hooks
entries: The following quest hooks provide ideas for adventures that might take place throughout the lands of the Valikan clans.

entries.name: Echoes of the Beast
entries: Hunting parties returned to {@area Borgund|189|x} with whispers that a great skeletal stag head was sighted in the fog. Shortly thereafter, hunters started lugging home various mutilated bestial corpses found along the trails. A scout has now returned with warnings that a warband of gnolls is roaming the tundra and adjoining forests. With the hunting grounds of the city endangered, the wardens of Borgund have requested that all able-bodied folk establish a plan to track down and eliminate the gnoll incursions and, if possible, determine if the Beast remains in the region.

{@i "Do not venture too far from the fire"} is advice which is commonly shared with travelers to the north, and all too frequently ignored. In this case, as the characters seek out the gnoll warbands, they must deal with the many creatures that lurk in the darkness, shrouded in the safety of the cold and gloom. Carnivorous cows or other mutated beasts stand between the heroes and their destinations. Patrols from Clan Sýr also inhabit the area, giving possibilities for either combat, roleplaying, or exploration to find the means to survive interactions with the most reasonable of the Thrull-based clans.

The characters finally begin to see signs of the warband's progress. Broken branches, mutilated carcasses, and jumbled tracks are among the signs to look for. For each character, there should be one {@creature Beast Gnoll|GHMG} and one {@creature Venomous Gnoll|GHMG}, along with one {@b {@creature Gnoll Brute|GHMG|Brute Gnoll}} for every two characters. Due to the mutations afflicted upon the gnolls, they are intent on destruction and fight with a vicious ferocity.
Returning with a trophy of a gnoll's hide or head will gain the characters a small favor with the wardens of Borgund. This could be helpful in gaining information or a respite at a later point.

entries.name: The Creeping Cold
entries: Clan Limgri have been the caretakers of Cold Iron Keep for many years, without much call for assistance, so when Queen Andrea receives an urgent request for aid from the keep, she reaches out for someone who can quickly make the journey to provide help, and who is not beholden to clan law...just in case the issue lies with the inmates of the infamous prison keep. The only way to reach the keep is by crossing Lake Jakkan, a frigid body of water that is also home to a terrible beast. The sailors that bring the prisoners to Cold Iron Keep whisper about sightings of this monster, sure that its antlers will cause the lake to freeze over, killing them all instantly. Some of the sailors even believe that the flickering coldfire on the creature's antlers are the souls of those it has dragged beneath the surface, down to a watery grave.

Whether the beast in the lake is real or not, the coldfire it is said to spread is certainly real, as the latest transport ship discovered.
