The guards awaiting the prisoner transfer began to worry when the ship had not arrived by daybreak on the second day after it was expected, but when it was finally spotted slowly sailing forward as the sun crested in the sky, their relief was short-lived. Upon boarding the small vessel, every soul on board was found to be frozen solid, with strange glowing puddles scattered across the deck. As the puddles coalesced into {@b {@creature Coldfire Ooze|GHMG|Coldfire Oozes}}, an alarm was sounded amid desperate orders to return the vessel to the lake.

With a half-dozen of their best guards claimed by the oozes, Clan Limgri is hesitant to deal with the oozes on their own, especially as it remains unknown how the oozes came to be aboard the transport ship. The transport ship currently rests several hundred feet from the shore, entirely too close for the warden's comfort. The oozes aboard the ship were hacked into by the guards who initially boarded the vessel, unintentionally proliferating their number, and further aggravation of the vessel is likely to cause them to spill over into the lake. It is unknown if they have the ability to freeze the lake solid. If so, doom is almost guaranteed for the keep. This adventure seed is intentionally written to be open-ended. The characters should seek creative methods for moving the vessel further from the keep to ensure the safety of the Clan Limgri guards and the prisoners within the keep. While the oozes are vulnerable to fire, this may not immediately be obvious to the characters, and even if it is, lighting the wooden transport vessel on fire could potentially lead to the oozes still being dumped into the lake. The DM has discretion, based on party skill and level, to decide if the oozes are capable of using their coldfire to freeze sections of the lake. Teamwork and cleverness should be rewarded. And definitely don't forget about the beast in the lake, which can be anything ranging from {@b {@creature Keppmir|GHMG}} for lower-level characters, to {@creature Gegazol|GHMG} for high-level characters.

entries.name: Sheltering Boughs
entries: This encounter is meant to demonstrate that even the well-meaning inhabitants of Valika can turn out to be dangerous in the wrong circumstances.

To the west of {@area Halsfjord|186|x}, the overseer of a small village has requested aid in locating their wayward healer. Sarvenka, a druid who dabbled in alchemy, was last spotted along a trail near a {@area Sentinel Wood|18f|x} foraging for mushrooms and herbs that she used to make various tinctures.

In her quest to gain standing within her clan, Sarvenka had gathered magical texts from various traveling merchants hoping to improve her magical abilities. Her latest scroll contained instructions on a method to gain a blessing from a holy tree, which she believed to be a straightforward enough task. However, once she had located a tree near a sacrificial grove, but still far enough away so as not to appear blasphemous to the {@area Prismatic Circle|25d|x}, things began to go wrong for Sarvenka. The tree she chose was an {@creature Ordeal Tree|GHMG} and her intentions were {@i somewhat} noble.

Unfortunately for the party, the night they discover Sarvenka's body shines brightly under the light of a full moon. The Redclaws that were patrolling the Sentinel Woods seeking to prevent ritual sacrifices by Clan Völgr have been drawn in by the scent of her blood and have fallen prey to their baser instincts. Four {@creature Werebear Ascetic|GHMG} and five {@creature Werewolf Ravagers|GHMG} creep upon the party as they examine Sarvenka.

entries.name: A Crucible of Fire
entries: Always seeking a solution to the spread of coldfire that threatens all of Etharis, the Order of Kentigern has hired an expedition to travel to {@area Cinderghast|18d|x} to research a resurgence of flame-bearing monsters from the Netherworld.

The expedition is destined a small warren of tunnels on the southern edge of the island alongside one of the larger volcanic peaks. In these tunnels, a group of four {@b {@creature Malcinder|GHMG}} and their {@b {@creature Malcinder Alpha|GHMG}} have made a cozy home, hunting and enjoying the relative solitude on Cinderghast. These creatures are content to be observed and will even allow small samples of their rock-like bodies to be harvested if approached with care.

Not quite as benign, the true cause of the recent uptick in volcanic activity has been caused by a pair of {@creature Mjorks|GHMG} and a {@creature Mjork Charger|GHMG}, none of whom are delighted to see researchers invading their home. These elementals, however, could be of value to the researchers at {@area Fort Kentigern|18c|x}, so capturing one alive would be a real accomplishment if the characters are able.

entries.name: The Fancy of Foxes
entries: Traveling the tundra alone is almost always a foolhardy mission, but especially above the circle of shadows in the time when the lands are blanketed in a perpetual darkness. Fortunately for one such traveler who recently lost their way in a blizzard, the characters were not far behind them, nor were the frostbite foxes that roam the tundra.

Angr knew better than to set out on a hunting trip when the wind promised a storm, but her brother had recently fallen ill and food supplies were running out, so she felt she had no choice but to brave the tundra. She felt lucky when she spotted the fresh tracks of an elk, but that feeling faded quickly as the wind picked up and sharp ice crystals began to tear at her skin. With no sun still to show for weeks, and the stars hidden by the storm, Angr soon became disoriented and resorted to digging out a crude shelter in the snow to protect herself, however she knows that she cannot survive through the night.

Two {@creature Frostbite Fox|GHMG|Frostbite Foxes} have been tailing the characters for hours, and now that the party has stopped to seek shelter from the storm, the foxes circle at the edge of the firelight, seemingly beckoning the characters back out into the storm. If the characters follow the foxes into the storm, and manage to keep track of them (tracking with survival skills, following prints with perception, or any other creative means) , the foxes will lead them to Angr's snow burrow where they will find her shivering from hypothermia, dehydrated, and ravenous.

Once Angr has been rescued, she could use assistance recovering her strength and health, but then she is more than happy to share tales of what brought her out in such a storm to begin with. She beseeches the party to help her hunt down the elk she was tracking to take back to her brother once the storm clears up, promising her gratitude and hospitality in return, along with whatever other meager payment she can scrounge up.

entries.name: Cold Iron Keep
entries: Far to the north, in the frigid peaks of the Jakkan Mountains, rests Cold Iron Keep. Silent battlements loom two hundred feet above the glassy surface of Lake Jakkan. To reach the step of the gate, guests must cross the large body of water—but not all who enter will exit Cold Iron Keep again.

In forgotten centuries, the wicked mage Mordu used spells to call this stronghold up from the living rock, filled its halls with perilous magic, and secured it against the outside world. Then the fearful folk of Valika stormed and ransacked the Keep, leaving it empty to the ravages of the elements—and to the lurking hunger of monsters.

Today, Cold Iron Keep locks away dangerous enemies rather than magical wonders. When Clan Mithra took the apparently empty ruin, Queen Andrea entrusted it to Clan Limgri, who converted its vaults into prison cells.

The sitting warden of Cold Iron Keep is Commander Bodil Garðr. Her manner as a former skald and doomsayer sets the tone of life in the Keep. She invites scholars, druids, and skalds from all over {@area Kandar|181|x} to reside above the dungeons. Few here thrive without her favor, a fact known by guests and prisoners alike.

Garðr's unorthodox leadership style includes an unexpected rapport with her prisoners. Though the uncooperative may find themselves subject to elaborate punishments (including experimentation by the warden's guests), the inmates who cooperate with Garðr get relaxed treatment—and might even be sent out of the prison on secretive missions.

entries.name: Granted New Life
entries: Isolation and a hostile environment make Cold Iron Keep difficult to reach. A single road, worn with use and disrepair, climbs the broad Jakkan slopes toward the stronghold. As the Keep's squared ramparts come into view, they hardly stand out against the snow-laden spires of jagged rock surrounding them.

The forboding walls of this place use no mortar. They bear mineral veins, strata, and other evidence of the living rock from which they were magically lifted. By a trick of design, the walls confine sound and their windows hamper light; even a grand festival would only be detected as a dull echo from the outside.

entries.name: Guests of the Warden
entries: Despite its cheerless exterior, the Keep is home to a growing community who share an interest in the stronghold's history and secrets. With Commander Garðr's leave, these free residents maintain quarters throughout the elaborate {@area Guest Halls|1a1|x} of the ground floor, drawn to the surviving magic of Mordu.

Over time, Cold Iron Keep drew the attention of adventurers and opportunists who schemed their way into the warden's favor. These daring sellswords spend most of their time exploring the {@area Deepvault|1a3|x}, the unmapped and half-flooded lowest levels of Mordu's citadel. Artisans, hirelings, and traders in the Guest Halls serve the delvers' occupational needs.

entries.name: Locked Away
entries: Below the Guest Halls lie the Cells. There, the clans of Kandar store prisoners who can't be dealt with by clan law, including those deemed most dangerous and impervious to death. Commander Garðr, under the consultation of her wisest guests, adapts the Keep's punishments and security measures to each inmate. The enemies languishing in its cells include cultists, political foes, giants, primordial druids, and creatures even more monstrous.

The Cells are only the uppermost threshold of the extensive vaults that once comprised Mordu's stronghold. Some two hundred prison chambers are maintained here, alongside a dozen or more laboratories where the least compliant inmates undergo various experiments.

Probing below the Cells (as many adventurers do) brings one to the Creep, a series of frozen corridors and chambers that typically end in rubble, magical seals, or barriers of frozen floodwater. Many passages in the Creep are deserted or picked clean, but every so often, a monster or magical phenomenon emerges from the Deepvaults below.

entries.name: The Rule of Law
entries: Above all this chaos sit the {@area Offices of the Warden|1a2|x}, a grand series of halls, observatories, and defunct magical sanctums refitted into a sort of administrative court. Here, Commander Bodil Garðr and her officers run the Keep. Their work is part prison staff part town council.

Adjoining the Offices of the Warden are extensive battlements and fortifications overlooking Lake Jakkan, though these are seldom used or maintained. Impassable mountain crags enclose its north, east, and west sides, making entrance or escape from those directions a dismal prospect.

entries.name: Vaults of Opportunity
entries: The Deepvaults that run hundreds of feet below Cold Iron Keep have become a wild fascination for those who dwell here. To the warden's guests, they represent plunder, power and potential discoveries; to the prisoners, they are a glimmer of hope—an avenue of escape lurking just out of reach.

At least five prisoners have ventured into the Deepvaults, but it was at Bodil Garðr's behest. The party was assembled from among the most favored inmates, each subjected to a {@spell Geas|XPHB} spell that would keep them on task under pain of death. Their charge was to chisel an entrance into a particular frozen vault, clear any monsters from the place, and return a peculiar set of funerary urns to the warden.

Few officers heard this plan, which proved fortunate for Commander Garðr. Whether the prisoners died in a monster attack, were struck dead upon defying the {@i Geas} spell, or simply escaped into the Deepvaults, no one knows.

Further experimentation and refinement of the {@i Geas} has given Commander Garðr hope that the attempt can be repeated. Perhaps, in time, the spell may allow her to evict the rabble of adventurers and undertake the clearing of the dungeons using her own obedient puppets.

The timeworn vaults of Cold Iron Keep share at least one thing with the inmates they confine: both lie hidden in the frozen waste, objects of exploitation by the warden and her entourage. Whether a new life awaits them at the end of the long dark depends on their ability to become useful.

entries.name: A History of Reclamation
entries: The earliest historical record of Cold Iron Keep is its despoiling. At that time, the Jakkan Mountains were already shrouded in dread tales—a lingering shadow from centuries of half-remembered evil.

Stories tell that the Keep's builder, Mordu, sent vile servants into nearby villages to abduct innocents and bring them to his laboratories for blood sacrifice. The beings he directed these offerings to are not recorded, nor are the secrets he won with this butchery.

In time, heroes trapped and killed Mordu in recompense for his foul deeds. In the aftermath of that victory, folk from the lowland villages stormed Cold Iron Keep, ransacked what treasures they could find, and left it to the elements. Abandoned in the inhospitable mountains, it became a forgotten ruin.

Soon after, Clan Mithra discovered the empty hulk of Mordu's citadel and garrisoned it. Many centuries later, it was entrusted to Clan Limgri. Queen Andrea Helsing, convinced Cold Iron Keep lacked strategic value, agreed to station her garrison further north if Limgri converted the Keep into a prison and staffed it.

entries.name: New Management
entries: Life at Cold Iron Keep took a strange turn when the Røkkva skald Bodil Garðr was made warden. As a laureate of {@area Solheim|18b|x}, Garðr railed against the power structures that prolong the end of the world and the rebirth of the gods; to Garðr, this doom is to be embraced, not feared.

When her performances sparked unrest, Clan Limgri forced Garðr into exile as warden of Cold Iron Keep, which she transformed into a haven modeled after her own image. Her refurbished Guest Halls teem with skalds, scholars, and adventurers eager to garner favor and emerge from the {@area Deepvaults|1a3|x} with wondrous treasure.

Life in the Guest Halls is often wild and jovial; just as Garðr prefers it. Her taste for rare and irreverent entertainment has become a signature custom in Cold Iron Keep. Shocking tales, predictions of doom, and the occasional shower of treasure from the Deepvault compensate for the Keep's makeshift habitations and unruly culture.

entries.name: Keeping the Peace
entries: The Commander oversees Cold Iron Keep's three administrative boards. Correctional Affairs, headed by Constable Ulherik Vander, directs the security and treatment of prisoners. Hospitality Affairs, commanded by Constable Heiga Iðun, keeps the peace in the Guest Halls. Provisional Affairs, under the direction of Constable Agnes Harlech, imports supplies, collects and disperses pay, and manages the Keep's other resources.

Instead of citizenship, Garðr offers her "hospitality" to new arrivals. When revoked, the guest's property is confiscated and they're cast from the Keep into the elements. This threat carries significant weight. Those who secure Garðr's favor with might be released from their cells on a limited basis. Disrespectful prisoners are allocated poor quarters and food, arduous duties, or even subject to experimentation. Garðr is a crafty and vindictive enemy.

entries.name: Notable Locations
entries: The following places of interest are found within Cold Iron Keep.

entries.name: Approach to the Keep
entries: The solitude and despair of the arctic are enough to deter any but the most dogged traveler from reaching the slopes of the Jakkan Mountains. Just when any wise soul would turn back, Lake Jakkan, Ferryman's Sty, and the face of Cold Iron Keep itself come into view through the mist and snow.

entries.name: Lake Jakkan
entries: Several miles wide before it reaches the gate of Cold Iron Keep, the road from {@area Hrist|187|x} plunges beneath the surface of Lake Jakkan, whose waves lap against the castle's foundations. Once, perhaps, the road wound around the shore, but the bounds of the lake have grown over the centuries, filling in any clear path to the doors of the Keep. This natural moat serves only to enhance the Keep's defense and seclusion.

By order of Commander Garðr, no boats are kept on the shore of Lake Jakkan, but a ferryman named Huskol dutifully watches for approved passengers to convey to the Keep. Crossing the lake takes hours and costs nothing, eliciting the question of why Huskol serves the warden and what he lives on.

A fearsome creature is said to lair within the caves beneath the lake, often appearing to inmates who first cross the waters toward their captivity. Reports describe a shapeshifting monstrosity combining the features of a caribou, a whale, and a dire wolf. It's unclear whether these fears are stoked by illusion magic or formed from sightings of a real monster.

entries.name: Ferryman's Sty
entries: Huskol, the ferryman of Lake Jakkan, lives on an island of detritus near the center of the lake, which the Keep's guests call Ferryman's Sty. The precise origin of this scrap heap isn't known. Giant beams and plates of metal protrude from a mound of broken stone rubble whose merest crest is visible from above the water but whose bulk reaches far down to the bottom of Lake Jakkan.

Tales tell that before the lake was formed, a village slumbered here in the shadow of the mountains. In some versions of the legend, the displaced rock and runoff from Cold Iron Keep's magical emergence first buried the village, then drowned the survivors. In other variations, Mordu sent mudslides and avalanches down upon a besieging army before he was at last defeated. Whatever the truth, Ferryman's Sty is believed to hide any number of secrets from before the rise of Cold Iron Keep.

Though old and bent, Huskol the ferryman has strong arms and keen eyes. The explorers of Clan Mithra who first sighted the keep claimed to have found the old man already living here in his leaning hut on the sty. Some say he sleeps for days, waiting for passengers approved by the warden to hail him from either shore using an ivory horn. By all accounts, Huskol's devotion seems to be to the Keep itself, not its warden.

entries.name: Guest Halls
entries: The ground floor and its adjoining chambers are collectively called the Guest Halls. They include Kettle Row, Maker's Market, Chalkstick Hall, and nearby vaults converted into living quarters for the Keep's guests.

entries.name: Kettle Row
entries: The rattle of pots and the din of dining fill Kettle Row, where drinking wells, food carts, miniature taverns, and apothecaries gather to provide what food and drink can be had at Cold Iron Keep. The selection isn't bad, considering the castle's remote surroundings. By the light of cookfires and hanging braziers, the warden's guests cling to whatever cheer Kettle Row can offer.

entries.name: Maker's Market
entries: The shouts of Maker's Market echo throughout the most populated areas of Cold Iron Keep. In this intersecting passage, suppliers of every stripe set up shop to serve the needs of the daring adventurers heading into the {@area Deepvaults|1a3|x}. Hospitality Affairs keeps a strong presence in Maker's Market, ensuring that any cheating or thieving is done with Commander Garðr's approval.

entries.name: Chalkstick Hall
entries: This wide hall featuring six frozen fountains is where adventurers gather to descend into the dangerous levels of Cold Iron Keep. A rift in the floor, known as Chalkstick Chasm, is thickly overgrown with pale cold-weather succulents—the "chalksticks" that give the place its name. A dangerous climb down the chasm bypasses the magical seals that prevent ordinary entrance to the Deepvaults.

entries.name: Offices of the Warden
entries: Most of the visible portion of the castle belongs to the Offices of the Warden, where behind closed doors, Commander Bodil Garðr and her trusted circle hold the reins of the Keep.

entries.name: Battlements
entries: Towering over the natural moat of Lake Jakkan, the three battlements of Cold Iron Keep provide an impregnable defense against invasion. Parapets, towers, barbicans, arrow loops, and murder holes all form redundancies against assault from outside and inside the Keep. Commander Garðr has diverted some of the garrison from defense to peacekeeping, leaving the fortifications somewhat understaffed; the guests must be kept in order, after all.

entries.name: Hall of Black Rot
entries: Garðr's highest reception hall in the Keep is reserved for the raucous nightly revels of her most favored guests. The chamber remains stained by its namesake: a hideous black mold removed during the Keep's refurbishment. An invitation to the Hall of Black Rot marks one as being among the most respected guests (or prisoners) in Cold Iron Keep.

entries.name: Seersalir
entries: The highest tower of Cold Iron Keep is crowned by Seersalir, the hall of the seer, where Commander Garðr has spent years restoring a large scrying contraption made by Mordu. Under the guidance of her most discreet scholars, she believes she has unlocked a great key to understanding the magic of Mordu and the Deepvaults. With Seersalir under her control, she can keep a close eye on both the prison and the world outside.

entries.name: The Deepvaults
entries: Extensive, mysterious, and diverse, the vaults beneath Cold Iron Keep are layered one after another, plunging hundreds of feet into the stony roots of the mountains—perhaps even beyond. One could spend a lifetime exploring the wonders of the Deepvaults and barely scratch its surface—in fact, many plan to.

entries.name: The Creep
entries: The uppermost level of the Deepvaults is plagued by coldfire. Curtains of the magical scourge move slowly through the tunnels in irregular patterns, allowing for careful plundering by adventurers who can keep their wits. The Creep, as it's called by the renegades who navigate it, is disproportionately armed with traps and defenses designed by Mordu to keep them out. Perhaps half of the Creep has been mapped and plundered, but somehow, new monstrous denizens appear with regularity.

entries.name: Fountain of Twilight
entries: Under the caverns on the south end of Lake Jakkan lie a collection of hidden vaults originally accessible only from the deepest and most dangerous levels of the Keep. In the centuries since Mordu first built them, these chambers have been worn away by dripping water until they opened into a cascading waterfall two hundred feet high. The waters feed bioluminescent fungal forests that somehow thrive in the cold twilight. Few explorers prove skilled enough to enter here, either from the lake above or the vaults below.

entries.name: Hidden Lake
entries: The labyrinthine chambers, passages, and stairs that make up the Hidden Lake are partially flooded by water flowing down through the Fountain of Twilight into the Deepvaults. The edge of this lake crashes in waves against the stairs, balconies, and sealed gates of the vaults. Unlike the Flooded Deepvaults beneath them, the Hidden Lake's elevated balconies and balustrades form a sort of shoreline through the maze of chambers.

entries.name: Flooded Deepvaults
entries: Sealed by magic and deathly cold water, the Flooded Deepvaults are among the most mysterious and inhospitable features of Cold Iron Keep. The secrets of Mordu's most obscure magic lie inaccessible here, disturbed only by the floods. What life, if any, survives within the mage's lowest vaults is too terrible to imagine, but those who stumble upon the seals have heard bellowing calls from beyond to open the forbidden doors and let the flood rise into the upper levels.

entries.name: Bruhvelving
entries: The most remote known chamber in the Deepvaults, Bruhvelving can be accessed via twelve magical seals scattered about the Deepvaults in the form of animated relief sculptures. Each seal is depicted as a different image matching its personality: the archfey, beast, butcher, fool, lamb, raven, ruler, scholar, troll, warrior, wyrm, and youth. Each is said to hold secrets about the Deepvaults, and can be beguiled or entreated to allow entrance into their hidden vault.

Inside, Bruhvelving is believed to conceal extensive passages to vaults even deeper and more secret beneath the flooded levels. Savvy adventurers have theorized that because it's so deep and unflooded, Bruhvelving might have no physical connection to Cold Iron Keep other than the sealed portals, and might have been installed in a separate part of the mountains.

entries.name: Commander Bodil Garðr
entries: As an accomplished skald in {@area Solheim|18b|x}, Bodil Garðr never imagined she would one day spend her days tending the locks of a prison. When her Røkkva prophecies of cataclysm and bloodshed caused unrest, Queen Andrea Helsing gave her the "great honor" of becoming warden of Cold Iron Keep—away from the ears of the people. In her honorable exile, Garðr redoubled her desire to hasten the end of the world, and found she had been handed just the tools to do it.

Garðr is tall, with rich walnut skin and a knifebright smile. Everything in how she presents herself is calculated for effect. Her shield and iron club can be used for metal-drumming in the skaldic tradition. She wears no emblem of her rank, dressing like the commonfolk whose respect and awe she has earned. Even her hair is plaited in runic designs that create a subtle protective aura over her.

Runesmithing provided Garðr insight into the seals and enchantments left at Cold Iron Keep by its builder, Mordu. Excited by the possibilities held within the mage's Deepvaults and high chambers, she invited scholars and like-minded Røkkva believers to glean what they could from its magic. In time, she became hostess and patron to a wide variety of mages, entertainers, and scoundrels, all vying for her favor.

Most recently, Commander Garðr obtained significant resources through Queen Andrea for master runesmith {@area Tove av Eikenbu|1ab|x} to develop a method of inking {@spell Geas|XPHB} enchantments onto the bodies of select prisoners. These runic tattoos allow the warden to assign the prisoners tasks outside their cells, keep them under tight control, and even kill them remotely. Combined with the strange and dangerous nature of Cold Iron Keep's prisoners, this project has become Garðr's utmost priority as she sends them to plumb the depths of the Deepvaults, halt the raids of Clan Völgr, and secure herself against the ending of the world.

entries.name: Important Individuals
entries: Notable people associated with Cold Iron Keep include those described below.

entries.name: Astar, Queen of Midnight
entries: Ancient as the Keep itself, Astar is a medusa known in the folk tales of Clan Mithra as the Queen of Midnight and the Barrow's Eye. Nearly a hundred years ago, clan hunters entered a battle of wits with her and, after three days, succeeded in trapping Astar in an iron mask emblazoned with an image of a smiling child. Held as a mead-hall trophy, then nearly lost in the calamity of Cinnabar, the bound medusa was eventually brought to Cold Iron Keep.

Once inside, Astar was sealed in a cell walled by mirrors. Far from the murderous monstrosity of folk tales, she nurses her hatred for her captors with cold detachment. But the divinations and unsought counsels she whispers from behind her cherub's mask are as dangerous as her glance was said to be.

entries.name: Fregr Ulfborn
entries: The retired adventurer Fregr Ulfborn (known as the Winter Wolf) holds court in a shabby drinking hole called the Torchbearer at the center of Kettle Row. His weathered face and silver hair belie the strength in his axe hand. Ulfborn's personal adventuring guild, the Vaulters, boasts over fifty members, and now dares to openly disobey the warden without losing her hospitality. The streetwise guests say a turf war is brewing between Commander Garðr and the Winter Wolf that will finally clear some space in the Guest Halls.

entries.name: Gorm, Heir of the Wyrm
entries: Gorm is a red-haired madman who burns continuously with coldfire from head to foot. Whispers of him echo all through Cold Iron Keep, claiming he has seen the cataclysm of the Great Wyrm, that the coldfire obeys his commands, and even that he moves freely about the Deepvaults with the blessing of the warden.

Indeed, speaking with Gorm was one of the foremost reasons Bodil Garðr accepted the post in the first place. She wasted no time tattooing her magical controls on him and sending him into the Creep. After all, if his prophecy is true and the touch of his foot upon the snow of Valika heralds the end of all things, that would suit Garðr just fine.

entries.name: Jormund the Dreamer
entries: The quietest vault in the Keep's prisons is occupied by a thirty-foot sarcophagus made of quartz. Within lies the sleeping bulk of Jormund, a frost giant vampire. Kept in a state of torpor by the enchanted sarcophagus, Jormund dreams so vividly that the images of his nightmares can be scried from the magical device in Seersalir. Commander Garðr fears Jormund too much to wake him, but there are others in Cold Iron Keep who can think of many ways to use such an ally, if he can be swayed to their cause.

entries.name: Riven Kreig
entries: Recently apprehended in the streets of {@area Hrist|187|x}, Riven Kreig is a fanatical devotee of the {@area Prismatic Circle|25d|x} whose talent lies in absorbing a person's life essence at the time of their death. Even stranger are the rituals Kreig employs to wear the face of an absorbed victim. Since his imprisonment, the assassin has refused to assume his true form, if it could even be identified.

As another who invites the end of the world (albeit for different reasons), Commander Garðr hopes to turn Riven Kreig to her Røkkva point of view. Until then, he remains a delicate laboratory experiment.

entries.name: Tove av Eikenbu
entries: This Clan Mithra runesmith converted to the Røkkva religion four years ago when he first met Bodil Garðr. Tove av Eikenbu is a thin, wiry man in his fifties. Despite the Keep's raucous culture, he maintains an austere, almost disquieting focus on his studies.

Eikenbu is perhaps the only person in the Keep that Garðr fully trusts. As the first of twelve prominent scholars who took up residence in Cold Iron Keep when Garðr became warden, his research and encouragement were instrumental in developing the runic {@spell Geas|XPHB} tattoo that controls certain prisoners.

entries.name: Quest Hooks
entries: The following quest hooks can be used to flesh out adventures set in Cold Iron Keep.

entries.name: Beguile the Seal of Bruhvelving
entries: Since entering Cold Iron Keep, the player characters heard many descriptions of Bruhvelving, the lowest and most hidden vault in the complex. Many name it, but few claim to have reached it. Down Chalkstick Chasm, through the Creep, and into the unmapped portion of the Deepvaults, one can find Bruhvelving's twelve sentient seals.

If the rumors can be believed, choosing a seal is as important a step as contriving a way to open it. Whether by guile, knowledge, or bitter sacrifice, it is believed the figures in the seals can be overcome. Once past the seals, Bruhvelving's vault remains a largely untouched trove of magical wonders—but such things are not to be tampered with lightly. Beyond the magical wards and lurking monsters, Bruhvelving may hide truths about the beginning and the end of existence that challenge the integrity of reality itself.

entries.name: Black Rot Revels
entries: Days of petitioning and favors have landed the player characters a rare opportunity: a stolen invitation to the Hall of Black Rot, where Cold Iron Keep's warden hosts her most favored guests. This is where the real power rests—where deals are brokered and trust gained. Siv Hrothigvar, purveyor of the vilest delights in Valika, will be in attendance and off her guard. What foul crimes will the characters commit to take a place beside her and avenge the innocent souls she preyed on?

entries.name: Forage the Fountain of Twilight
entries: Plague is spreading through the Cold Iron Keep's Guest Halls. Medicine is scarce until new shipments arrive. The situation worsens as the plague victims rise from their deathbeds with a taste for living flesh. Rare fungal growths in the Fountain of Twilight hold the secret to defeating the undead plague, if only a party of survivors skilled enough to navigate the vaults could bring them back before the Keep's denizens tear one another limb from limb.

entries.name: Interview With a Vampire
entries: Through careful investigation, the player characters have learned that the prisoner {@area Jormund the Dreamer|1a9|x} appears in tales alongside the Great Prismatic Wyrm defeated by the hero Kentigern. In an age long past, his thirst for blood stained the Jakkan Mountains red and drained the blood of entire generations—until he was betrayed. If indeed the vampire frost giant is over a thousand years old and harbors hatred for the Great Wyrm, he would make an invaluable ally in the confrontation to come.

entries.name: Lost in Ferryman's Sty
entries: Queen Drejv the Betrayer's five deathless heirs were decapitated, and the heads scattered across Valika in a forgotten age. The player characters' search for the heads—which still live—has led them to Lake Jakkan, where the head of Økerhardt was cast upon Ferryman Sty. To obtain it, the characters will need to research accounts of the lake's mysterious shapeshifting denizen. They must avoid or defeat the creature to scour the scrap heap for what they seek. If Økerhardt can be coaxed to speak, his knowledge may well reach back to the days of Mordu and the mysterious creation of Cold Iron Keep itself.

entries.name: Oysters of the Hidden Lake
entries: Where the tides rise and fall along the grand staircases and ramped corridors of the Hidden Lake, beds of giant oysters congregate, visible only in rare instances. The pearls these oysters form are the most valuable in Etheris. Some are even said to deposit souls within their shells rather than pearls. Perhaps a dark patron sends the player characters to the Hidden Lake to retrieve a specific soul, or the Vaulters require it as a token to join their adventurer's guild.

entries.name: Revolt at the End of the World
entries: The Winter Wolf and his adventurer's guild, the Vaulters, have heard of Commander Garðr's Røkkva devotions and her plan to hasten the world's end. With frightening tales of her human experiments, decadent revelries, and favoritism toward notorious enemies of {@area Kandar|181|x}, the Vaulters have convinced the player characters that now is the time to rise up and end Garðr's reign at Cold Iron Keep for good.

entries.name: Rescue in the Creep
entries: Dungeon delvers come and go in Cold Iron Keep. Sometimes, they strike a promising trail and disappear for days. Other times, they meet a quick end far from help. Averik Olherdottir and her companions descended into the Creep fifteen days ago. Today, she called for help with a {@spell Sending|XPHB} spell. It seems her companions were killed by a wraith, so Averik jumped blindly into the dark and broke her legs.

With no hope of climbing to safety, no food, and exhausted from crawling to avoid coldfire, Averik has only a few days left to be rescued. The player characters must pick out her trail amidst the treacherous passages of the Creep, avoid the specters of her former companions, and get her out from the ever-shifting clutches of the coldfire.

entries.name: Uninvited Guests
entries: Having failed to impress the warden, the characters are shut out from Cold Iron Keep and its opportunities. But the fortified gate is only one way into the castle's complex.

Perhaps the characters will dive into the lair of Lake Jakkan's mysterious creature and follow the narrow chute into the Fountain of Twilight. From there, passage to the ground level of the Keep is a matter of picking paths through the flooded portions of the Deepvaults up into the Creep and Chalkstick Hall. They might instead scale the formidable glacial cliffs that rise above the third battlement to enter the Offices of the Warden. Or, should their skillset lean more toward disguise and deception, their plan might be to impersonate prisoners and get locked into a cell with the hope of courting Commander Garðr's favor.

data.name: Chapter 7: The City of Liesech
entries: Liesech is a poisoned apple many powerful hands have tried to grip. The city-state festers inside the Bite Bay like a rotted tooth just north of the {@book Charneault Kingdom|GrimHollowCG24|5}. On a clear day, the peaks of the Rock-Teeth Mountains are seen in the distant east. But there are few clear days in Liesech.

Travelers that find a stop in Liesech necessary are cautious of the creeping green haze in the air, the disturbingly serene looks on the peoples' faces, and the dreadful clamor of {@area The High Belfry's|1d0|x} bells that abruptly captivate Liesech's citizens, before they return to their day. Visitors don't stay long enough to learn more.

Liesech is the ‘seed' of {@area The Weeping Pox|1bc|x}, the deathly disfiguring and mentally dismantling plague. Those that brave a prolonged stay here soon learn that behind every friendly smile is abject terror.

entries.name: Geography
entries: A green haze perpetually hovers over Liesech's skies, ostensibly blamed on the vapors coming off the waters. It does not hinder the day's offering of warming light nor prevent the moonlight adding to a hauntingly beautiful, emerald glow that blankets the city. On the clearest nights, it is visible for miles around.

Liesech is primarily reached by boat. The Bite Bay's open waters and deep-sea ridge allow vessels to anchor closer to the shore. Transiting ships can skirt the coastlines in inclement weather, but even so, ship captains must be vigilant. Surrounding waters harness turbulent currents that have been known to throw vessels off course into sea stacks, exciting sea monsters rumored to dwell in nearby waters. This area is called the "{@area Eye of the Sea|1cf|x}." It has dark, historical significance in Liesech's story.

Liesech's plentiful channels and ditches keep the wild tide swells at bay. Landforms corral coastal winds, keeping temperatures in the region mild and warm most of the time. Waning sea storms sprout light rains during warmer times, barely enough to aid in agriculture. Outsiders opine that Liesech's sparse rainfall is the result of the haze that hangs over the bay city. Locals shrug their shoulders when asked.

During the wintry season, snowfall is dry and winds are merciful. Liesechers watch for regional predators, who fearlessly wander from the forest, mountains, or waters into the city in search of food to keep them during hibernation.
Be careful. Be vigilant. We're close to shore and facing the most danger of the entire journey.

entries.name: History
entries: Liesechers trace their origins to the story of {@area Otto Bouwengracht|1c7|x}, whose family was influential when the city was just a large fishing village. Each family eked out a living with their meager catches, never quite managing enough to live comfortably. Bouwengracht was ambitious and daring. He decided to fish out beyond safe waters, where he hoped to net larger catches. He did indeed net his biggest catch in the waters of the Eye of the Sea.

A powerful creature called {@area The Filth Grazer|1c9|x} sensed the ambition of the fisher and invaded his thoughts in a soothing, feminine voice. She offered Otto a bargain—she would provide a font of knowledge beyond the minds of Etharis's greatest scholars and the counsel to use it to turn Liesech into a dominant, thriving city, with wealth beyond his dreams.

All the Filth Grazer desired in return was to feed upon the city's waste, including their dead. Otto was required to also find other devotees to fulfill the Filth Grazer's charge, under guise of ceremonial sea offerings. Bouwengracht agreed.

Otto Bouwengracht became a wealthy fisher and merchant. His family, and the families of others who trusted him, rose to prominence. Liesech prospered under his leadership. Bouwengracht used his acquired genius to develop canal and irrigation channels that would allow for easy transport of supplies and waste from the city, while also keeping the ‘unexplained' damaging tides ebbing from the {@area Eye of the Sea|1cf|x}.

Furthermore, Otto Bouwengracht helped to construct a remarkable fleet of sailing ships. He sent his fleet to establish trading routes across the Emerald Sea and further. Liesech continued to grow, and with the revered Bouwengracht family as its voice, Liesech pronounced itself a free city.

The {@book Bürach Empire|GrimHollowCG24|3} and the {@book Charneault Kingdom|GrimHollowCG24|5} benefited from Liesech's ports. As a midway point for trade ships traveling between regions, this provided crews with needed respite and Liesech's merchants opportunities to acquire goods from afar. However, Liesech's tradeway taxes grew beyond the Bürach Empire's ability to tolerate.
Without fear of retaliatory response by the Liesech-allied Charneault Kingdoms, the empire boldly planned to bring the Bouwengracht family back under Bürach rule.

entries.name: Annexation by the Bürach Empire
entries: The {@book Bürach Empire|GrimHollowCG24|3} discussed a resolution to Liesech's autonomy. They could ill afford other regions demanding independence. Ultimately, they decided to punish Liesech by unleashing a monster.

Analita von Raiza's chained coffin was discovered beneath the soil of Rauland. Starved by centuries of bloodless imprisonment, the Bürach Empire believed von Raiza could be wielded as a pawn. She was freed from her imprisonment and sent to Liesech as an emissary of the Empire, where she was to use her vampiric gifts to compel the Bouwengracht Family to agree to the Bürach Empire's terms of annexation.

With the staging of an agent in Liesech, the empire planned to slowly influence mercantile matters to their advantage and disburse the Bouwengracht's wealth as they desired. This plan promised discretion without destabilizing Liesech's mercantile industries.

Alas, the entire enterprise relied on trust in a vampire's word. The Bürachers believed von Raiza was a trained monster they could manipulate through her need for blood. They did not realize her ambitions matched their own

entries.name: The Red Mourning
entries: Upon arrival in Liesech, von Raiza immediately sensed that a strange pall covered the city. As she began her investigation of the city and its power structure, she noticed the dark influence within Liesech was especially strong around the {@area Bouwengracht|1c7|x} manse.

Her vampiric hunger and her intellectual curiosity were piqued. Through her natural charm, von Raiza's meeting with the family was soon arranged; their domination came easy. She learned much about Liesech's founding family, all of the intricacies among Liesech's holdings, and most importantly, the true source of the Bouwengracht's rise to power: the bargain with the Filth Grazer and the ceremony that keeps the creature at bay.

After completing her mission, von Raiza enjoyed her newfound luxury. As the Büracher leaders foresaw, her basic vampiric nature returned. While her hunger was sated by Liesecher blood, her hunger for power grew. As the Bürach Empire embraced the puppet Bouwengracht's support of annexation and tariff reduction, their imperious demands continued. {@area Countess von Raiza|1c8|x} grew tired of being their puppet. With the knowledge she now had, the vampire acted.

On the morning of the next annual {@area Ceremony of the Nurturing Sea|1c0|x}, the Bouwengracht family, along with other noble families, were found brutally killed—the blood entirely drained from their bodies. While word of these deaths shocked many, von Raiza was not fazed. Shortly after these deaths, the vampire felt Liesech's magic wane. The Filth Grazer responded. On the eve of the day known as "the Red Mourning," von Raiza flew to the Eye of the Sea to deal with the jilted otherworldly force.

entries.name: Battle with The Filth Grazer
entries: The deaths of the Bouwengracht family and their supplicants severed the contract with {@area The Filth Grazer|1c9|x}, abruptly ending its long-satiating feeding. The Filth Grazer's vengeance was immediate. On the evening of the Red Mourning, a long, deep-droning wailing rippled from the {@area Eye of the Sea|1cf|x}. After the rage-filled crying faded, destructive swells of flotsam and waste rolled into Liesech's shores; toppling buildings, drowning many, and delivering The Filth Grazers minions—undulating, many-mouthed and tentacled monsters who consumed any in their path.

Analita von Raiza had to act. While brave Liesechers took up steel against the monsters, the vampire flew out to the Eye of the Sea to destroy the Filth Grazer. But how can you fight a force that transcends corporeal form?

The vampire spilled more blood of the Liesech noble families who worshiped the Filth Grazer through necromantic rituals intended to poison the Filth Grazer and break its hold on the city. In retaliation, the Filth Grazer sent monsters from the deep to slay anything in their path.

Analita responded by sending scores of animated corpses into the ocean, leaving the Eye of the Sea clotted with blood and viscera. If the rumors are true, those very same corpses were sent back into the city bearing the Weeping Pox.

In the end, the seas calmed. The newly crowned Countess von Raiza returned to her home at the {@area High Belfry|1d0|x}. The Filth Grazer minions returned to the sea. In the wake of the monsters wrath, the city mourned. Numbing shock and engrossing gratitude consumed survivors. They began to rebuild. Many wondered; who won the battle at the Eye of the Sea?

Countess von Raiza's and the Filth Grazer had endlessly attacked one another, with no clear victor. Desperate to defeat the monster, the countess had used powerful magic to reach into the Filth Grazer's mind to dominate its will. Instead of finding anything like a mortal mind, the Countness found the same soothing voice that had spoken to Otto Bouwengracht long ago.

The voice promised a wealth of knowledge, luxury, respect, and servitude—at a very small price: the Filth Grazer's appetite for Liesech's waste must be sated, and a future tither would be paid. As show of power, the Filth Grazer devoured Countess von Raiza's traumatic memories. Among the Liesechers, the creature did the same—supplanting any fearful thoughts of the Filth Grazer with pleasant ones. No longer suspicious, the vampire agreed to the Filth Grazer's terms and sealed the bargain in her blood, a vampire's most binding act. She settled down in Liesech to enjoy the fruits of her deal.

entries.name: The Weeping Pox
entries: In the days following the conflict, Liesechers mourned the loss of their first families. {@area Countess von Raiza|1c8|x} nursed her wounds and observed the processions from afar. During this time, the {@area Filth Grazer|1c9|x}'s penance was paid.

When the bargain at the {@area Eye of the Sea|1cf|x} was sealed, the Filth Grazer's reality-bending essence shrouded the city again. It placed within each corpse of the honored dead a virus, which quickly spread among funeral attendees and those they contacted. Once the funeral skiffs delivered the Bouwengracht dead to the Filth Grazer, it delivered its revenge.

The disease, named {@disease Weeping Pox|GrimHollowCG24|the Weeping Pox}, was the manifestation of the Filth Grazer's bargain with Countess von Raiza and its vengeance for the vampire's disruption of the ages-long alliance. The symptoms and consequences of the disease were like nothing Etharis had never seen.

After first exposure, the victim expressed hyper-vigilance and excitability, followed by bouts of delirium and fever. In its final stages, flesh takes on a bone-white pallor, and two bright red pustules appear on the neck. Right before death, melancholia sweeps over the victim as it weeps all the blood from its body and loosens a final powerful, painful wail.

It wasn't until Countess von Raiza's vampiric minions sought out other blood servers did she realize what had happened. The victim's blood that sated the countess was also infected. The vampire's nature repelled the Weeping Pox's mortal effects, but blood nourishment came with a terrible price. The vampire became grotesque and twisted, with boils and pox and sores that never healed. The Weeping Pox was the Filth Grazer's warning to von Raiza. She dared not dishonor the blood bargain.

As von Raiza suffered her penance, she continued to cultivate her influence. And the Weeping Pox claimed thousands. The countess sent representatives to lead the {@area Nurturing Sea Ceremony|1c0|x} processions, ensuring that her bargain to the Filth Grazer was fulfilled. She feigns concern over Liesech's people—going so far as to request aid abroad to help cure the Weeping Pox.

In time, allied aid arrived, in the form of the {@area Morbus Doctore|25b|x}. Their arrival sparked false hope, furthered by the countess' façade. The masked countenances of the medically trained doctors are a constant reminder of the death and danger that strike down citizens of Liesech every day.

entries.name: The Spread of the Pox
entries: Trade ships bound for the {@book Bürach Empire|GrimHollowCG24|3} and beyond brought the Weeping Pox with them. Realizing the contagion's source, the empire sent a quarantine fleet to block maritime trade routes into Liesech. This move created incited outrage between the empire and other regional leaders, who still desired to do business in Liesech.

A conglomerate of Etharis's aristocrats and merchants send vessels to dock in Liesech despite the blockade and the Weeping Pox. Missives sent to Countess von Raiza went ignored. The Bürach Empire's hold in Liesech diminished for fear of the disease and Countess von Raiza's apparent betrayal of their subterfuge.

The mercantile forces of all nations, in addition to smugglers, pirates, and black-market traders continue to use Liesech as a hub. Indecision among mercantile leaders in the Bürach Empire grows as they seek to learn how other regions benefit from trade in Liesech, where the deadly pox is in a state of perpetual pandemic.

Countess von Raiza, and perhaps the Morbus Doctore, claim that the Weeping Pox is now under control. They speak of new cures. But they have done so for years. The vampire's ambition benefits Liesech, much like Otto Bouwengracht's determination did ages ago. The city's people share in its prosperity, but also its sickness.

entries.name: The Liesecher Way
entries: In {@area Otto Bouwengracht's|1c7|x} youth, the fisherfolk were humble and hard-working. Communal survival drove decisions. Business was conducted on barter and trust. Commonplace among them, was a friendly smile and greeting or a blessing to the old sea gods.

The Weeping Pox, Filth Grazer, and {@area Countess von Raiza|1c8|x} have changed that. Visitors are still met with pleasant greetings and smiles. There is always, however, something dark behind the pleasant expressions. While speaking, their focus may drift off and their speech may slow. Any mention of the Weeping Pox to locals is met with silent reverence, a head turn toward the bay, and on occasion, a tear. They then return to matters at hand.

Most Liesechers make their way from their homes to their fishing boats and businesses after five morning bell tolls from the {@area High Belfry|1d0|x}. After six evening bell tolls, business dealings move from district streets to inside buildings. Those folks out beyond six bells are believed to be under employment of the{@area Gold Barge Company|1cc|x}—or one of the foolish that risk becoming an addition to the accepted nightly disappearances.

The Weeping Pox is a part of everyday life in Liesech, and the populace does whatever is necessary to keep up their spirits in the midst of death and misery. Locals call the affliction a necessity. They insist it prevents overpopulation and keeps visitors fearful from staying too long. They note with feigned relief how the disease spares children—although the children still bear its disfiguring marks and spread the disease like anyone else.

Despite many losses. it has not claimed the city's revered Countess von Raiza, who the locals say miraculously managed trade to break the {@book Bürach Empire|GrimHollowCG24|3}'s oppressive blockade. Citizens point to the arrival of the Morbus Doctore, who aid the sick and troubled.

entries.name: The Ceremony of the Nurturing Sea
entries: This annual ceremony sees citizens come together to offer their thanks to the old sea gods, who provide their families continued nourishment and prosperity. The ceremony starts with a solemn, white-cowled, and white-robed procession that starts at the High Belfry and ends at Bite Bay's docks.

After honorific prayer and song, families place their offerings on old boats that are then pushed out into the water. It is believed that the power of the sea currents, faith, and winds guide them to the old gods. Families return home with their devotion renewed. And the Filth Grazer and Countess von Raiza continue to honor their contract.
Something else may be watching this tradition in hiding from watery depths. Perhaps the old sea gods, or a representative of those ancient powers, are not as far gone as some might assume.

entries.name: The Week of the Grand Citywide Market
entries: Proclaimed by some to be the Bouwengracht family's greatest trademark, this quarterly celebration of prosperity and trade brought ships from all reaches of Etharis. Liesech opens all of Main Town and Dock Town streets for entertainers and vendors to set up booths and stages. During this time in ages past, the High Belfry opened for visiting aristocrats to enjoy Bouwengracht family hospitality while exploring or solidifying business ventures.

Since the death of Liesech's first families and the spread of the Weeping Pox, this celebration now takes place twice a year, with fewer participants. Visiting vessels undergo inspections by the Gold Barge Company and Morbus Doctore before debarkation. Affluent merchants and aristocratic guests are only invited to the High Belfry Open House after six evening bell tolls, for reasons explained by the countess's majordomo: "The Countess prefers company at night when she is her most radiant."

Despite these inconveniences, trade relations grow. The Market Week continues to draw more regional and renown celebrants, much to the ire of officials of the Bürach Empire, who refuse the countess's invitations. Of recent note, the acclaimed magizoology scholar Professor Terrence Myron von Küfen was spotted setting up a vendor booth when he was surrounded by {@area Morbus Doctore|25b|x}. Witnesses claim that he was escorted to {@area The High Belfry|1d0|x} and has not been seen since.

entries.name: Roleplaying Liesechers
entries: Citizens of Liesech have lived with death for years. They are well past wondering what is happening to them or why. Even the most generous and empathetic of the Liesecher population may not have the same caring attitudes of others throughout Etharis.

This traumatic tableau throughout Etharis can be portrayed in not just the villains, but the common folk and heroes as well. What others might consider a horrible catastrophe might be seen by the Liesechers as a better way to die than suffering through the Weeping Pox. While characters can still find help in the citizens, they are less likely to find sympathy or kindess.

entries.name: Hauthoff-Liesech (Main Town)
entries: The city has grown from dirt paths, cabin homes, and shore-tethered boats. The population lives among the dark stone and shingle-roofed buildings and cobblestone streets. Each street intersection has a prominent water drain in the event of sea-storm deluges.

A bell post stands at each corner; only rang to alert the Morbus Doctore when someone has contracted the {@disease Weeping Pox|GrimHollowCG24} or has died. Liesech's sewer and street network connect to one main sewer pipe and road into Dock Town. While most locals travel by foot, the {@area Golden Barge Company|1cc|x} offer various modes of transport: bicycle, pony, or stagecoach. Only two structures are gated and walled off from the rest of the city: {@area The High Belfry|1d0|x} and {@area Zielenhaus|1d1|x}.

entries.name: Traughafen (Dock Town)
entries: Liesech's docks stretch across the Bite Bay's shoreline, reaching both borders of the {@book Castinellan Provinces|GrimHollowCG24|6} and Rauland of the {@book Bürach Empire|GrimHollowCG24|3}. The Golden Barge Company sees to the city's maritime fleet from its boathouses, shipyards, and training grounds.

Docktown's businesses can provide visitors with a range of goods and services. Inns and taverns accommodate modest lodging needs. Most business arrangements are conducted within Handelzimmer buildings, where sanitized rooms are provided and monitored by the Golden Barge Company. This calms merchants fearing exposure to the Weeping Pox and grants them a respite from the reeking odor of fish scales and entrails that lingers about.

entries.name: The Bouwengracht Family
entries: The family responsible for Liesech's prominence is immortalized in history. However, accounts of Otto Bouwengracht's bargain with the Filth Grazer are nowhere to be found. In Liesecher hearts, the Bouwengrachts and their supporters were the epitome of hard work, humility and kindness. With their mysterious and tragic deaths, the families' manse and other holdings were assumed by Countess von Raiza.

As their temporary executor, the countess would guard these holdings until such time as any blood relative lay claim to them. The Bouwengracht family has familial ties to Rauland, yet none have stepped forward. Rumors that a Bouwengracht child, Wilhelm, was whisked away to safety by the families' mother-nurse as the families murder took place.
As the city continues to prosper, open reverence of the Bouwengrachts dwindles. Ironically, Countess von Raiza lauds Otto Bouwengracht's contributions to Liesech's success in public conversations.

entries.name: Countess Analita Von Raiza
entries: Liesech's interim leader is one of the oldest known vampires. Nearly impossible to kill, she was captured and imprisoned by the Bürach tribes of centuries past. Confinement to a sarcophagus carved and enchanted by old magic nearly shattered her mind.

Before that end, she was released by powerful Büracher loyalists and pressed into service as an agent of the empire. After fulfilling her mission in the subterfuge and assimilation of the Bouwengracht family, she manages the aftermath of her betrayal of the {@book Bürach Empire|GrimHollowCG24|3}.

This placed her face-to-face with the Filth Grazer, in prolonged battle that left her disfigured by the {@area Weeping Pox|1bc|x}. In the end, the Bouwengracht influence ended, a blood contract was made, and her rise to power and prominence began. She still nurses her wounds in the High Belfry. Her advisors, the vampiric {@area Cult of the Black Lady|1cb|x}, aid her in stately matters and see to her nourishment. This helps the countess improve relations abroad, carry on Liesech's traditions, and monitor the Weeping Pox and Morbus Doctore.

entries.name: The Filth Grazer
entries: The origin of the otherworldly thing that dwells within Liesech's waters is a great mystery. Those that encounter the Filth Grazer are left with wracking disbelief as to what they sensed. But in the mind of Countess von Raiza, the image is vivid: a miasmic mass of eyes, mouth and tentacles that speaks with a cacophony of soothing, alien-like voices.

The creature, or at least its most-used physical form, feeds on all forms of waste: physical and metaphysical. The Filth Grazer incorporates it into its being, fueling its intellect and persuasive power. Its waste carries a sliver of its sentience with it. This flows through sea water, rises into the air, clings to other life forms. This creates a far-reaching telepathic bond, which the Filth Grazer uses to torment victims as it sees fit.

In vengeance against Countess Analita von Raiza for killing the Bouwengracht's and other minions, the Filth Grazer excreted a powerful waste sludge among its tidal attacks on Liesech. This sludge's mark remained. It festered. It was the catalyst for the Weeping Pox. Every pox carrier is a thrall for the creature to sate its hunger, expanding its knowledge of Etharis and spreading its influence.

entries.name: Cult of the Black Lady
entries: In order to execute Liesech's annexation, Countess von Raiza needed help. She beguiled a group of Liesech's youth to slowly generate curiosity and mystery among Liesech's elite. They did their job well enough—within days of the countess's arrival, she received an invitation to attend a gathering at the High Belfry Manse of the Bouwengracht family. She thanked her help with a special gift—vampirism.

As von Raiza's spawn and family, they perform overt and covert acts to bolster her popularity and to discreetly remove any opposition. They hunt for people unaffected by the Weeping Pox to help their mistress recover with untainted blood.

The cult has vampiric and non-vampiric members, allowing them to protect the {@area High Belfry|1d0|x} and the countess, day or night. They distrust the Morbus Doctore and are hyper-protective of the countess during the healers' visits to the High Belfry. During the day, cultists claim they have heard the Countess cry out in her sleep about someone some powerful force in the sea. They don't know who or what that is.

entries.name: Golden Barge Company (G.B.C.)
entries: The company's founders trace their history to the Bouwengracht family. Their ancestors were devotees of Aphaleon, the Arch Seraph of Temperance. They dismissed Otto Bouwengracht's prophetic claim that the fishers would grow rich through offerings of their dead to the ocean, claiming that this blasphemed the reverent honoring of the dead.

While they did not participate in the {@area Ceremonies of the Nurturing Sea|1c0|x}, they served the village loyally. This commitment continues to be the core of the Golden Barge Company duty. They protect and transport Liesechers and their interests within the districts. They do not discriminate when there is profit to be made. The company boasts a staunch fleet of vehicles, tools and supplies.

In their long service to Liesech, they have come to understand the importance of commerce, and the power they wield serves that commerce. They are not afraid to remove obstacles to free trade through means that might cause others to blanche. They employ thieves and assassins when necessary, chalking it up to the greater good.

They know the city streets, sewers and waterways well and use the most expedient routes to complete their tasks. Few among their leaders suspect that the events of the {@area Red Mourning|1ba|x} and the {@area Weeping Pox|1bc|x} are related, and trust that Aphaleon will reveal the truth to these tragedies in time.

entries.name: Morbus Doctore
entries: The Morbus Doctore formed shortly after the Weeping Pox claimed its first victims. Healers and apothecaries from the city were at a loss, and they reached out to colleagues and contacts from across Etharis. Slowly, help arrived.

Before long the presence of these healers was felt. Wearing dark long coats, hats, and bird-like masks, they solemnly set to task. With Countess von Raiza's and the Golden Barge Company's assistance, a city area was cleared, and high iron walls erected around it. This quarantined area is called {@area Zielenhaus|1d1|x}. In the following weeks, plumes of a mysterious green smoke rose from Zielenhaus' chimney stacks. It spread over Liesech's skies and then settled into a ghostly haze. Locals continue to ponder its purpose.

Behind Zielenhaus walls, the Morbus Doctore bring the afflicted. Painful examinations and extractions take place; often with the same result: death. Even so, the Morbus Doctore are seen as merciful seraphs by most. Even though few are cured of the disease, suffering is lessened. They contain the suffering behind their walls. They treat the sick with reverent respect. They play with the children. They offer hope for a cure of the Weeping Pox.

A dark shadow looms over the organization, however. Whispers of procedures and experiments taking place in Zielenhaus with no connection to the disease echo through Liesech's streets. Sightings of plague-masked figures, covered in blood and skulking through dark alleys, have become more frequent. Whether these rumors are true remains to be seen.

entries.name: Eye of the Sea
entries: At the center of Bright Bay are dark, turbulent waters where, historically, ship crews that failed to heed warnings were lost and never seen again. Yet the mystery of the area continues to be the stuff of minstrel musing and tavern tales. Its waters are believed to hide anything from utopian settlements of enchanting sea creatures, gilded schools of fish that can feed families for a lifetime, and great treasures left behind in the death of the old gods.

It was such stories that drove young {@area Otto Bouwengracht|1c7|x} to row out to the Eye of the Sea, where he encountered none of these things. He came under the sway of a being called the {@area Filth Grazer|1c9|x}, whose presence lends truth of Liesech's historical prosperity. {@area Countess Analita von Raiza|1c8|x} is the only being that knows that the Filth Grazer's corporeal form resides there.

entries.name: The High Belfry
entries: Liesech's first major structure was a village town hall that sat at the village center. As families prospered, they collectively renovated this structure. It would become the single-story home built off of the old town hall, and with a small chapel within it. This grew to become the multi-story manse that housed the entire Bouwengracht clan, with a council hall and a chapel that also serves as the cities bell tower.

It was never referred to as the Bouwengracht Mansion. Otto Bouwengracht was humble; instead, choosing the name High Belfry. Here Liesech's leaders made decisions, meted out justice, entertained foreign guests and worshipped the old gods of the sea.

Since The Red Mourning, the manse has been renovated to accommodate Countess Analita von Raiza and her servitors, The Cult of the Black Lady. The High Belfry has visitors on special occasions, namely during the {@area Week of the Grand Citywide Market|1c1|x}, but those visits are kept to public areas of the mansion, council hall, and church.

entries.name: Zielenhaus
entries: The Morbus Doctore's iron-walled compound encompasses a four city-block region. Its gates open only to Morbus Doctore members—with one peculiar exception: Liesech children often stand at the gates and call for the doctors to come out and play with them. Some of the healers do for a bit—and even offer some sweets, before ushering the children home. Or at least most of the children.

Within its many dark-stoned structures are a labyrinth of cells, examination rooms, laboratories, libraries and vaults filled with instruments the Morbus Doctore need to carry out their efforts against the {@area Weeping Pox|1bc|x}.

Its tallest structure is the Morbus Plinth. It's a factory-like complex built of thick iron plating and strong rivets. Inside are the Morbus Doctore's most secret laboratories, including the central dispersal chamber that expunges experimental waste into the sky, feeding the ever-looming green haze.

entries.name: Quest Hooks
entries: The following quests hooks provide ideas for adventures that might take place in the city of Liesech.

entries.name: Golden Knights
entries: The {@area Golden Barge Company|1cc|x} seeks mercenaries to complete a series of night-time supply deliveries throughout {@area Traughafen|1c5|x}. Recently, deliveries have not reached clients, and its employees have vanished. Continued failures threaten to mar the reputation of the G.B.C.

An iron, banded barrel, has drawn the attention of a skulk of Plague Carrion, who want the barrel, filled with samples of the Weeping Pox brought from abroad for the Morbus Doctore, to study. This barrel has also caught the interests of the delivery robbers, {@area Cultists of the Black Lady|1cb|x}, who believe that the content may hold the secret to curing the Pox.
This draws characters into a confrontation with senior cult leaders, who arrive to accept these gifts. If the thieves and their paramour are defeated, the characters arrive at the Zielenhaus gates.

entries.name: The Relic
entries: The Froth Church was Liesech's historical house of worship, founded by the {@area Bouwengrachts|1c7|x} to not only worship the old gods of the sea, but to veil Otto Bouwengracht's eternal contract with the {@area Filth Grazer|1c9|x} and founding of the cult and the {@area Ceremony of the Nurturing Sea|1c0|x} into tradition.

In the wake of the {@area Red Mourning|1ba|x}, the church was destroyed. Liesechers don't recall anything about it—their memories were consumed by the Filth Grazer. It's ruins jut from a sandbar near Liesech's shore, and none venture out to it. The church's tithe coffer, ceremonial instruments, and gilded altar still remain somewhere among the ruins. Over time the ruins have sunk further into the bay, areas consumed by the sea may now be occupied. At worse, something touched by the Filth Grazer essence remains.

entries.name: Captured Envoy
entries: Ivik Garpok is a wulven diplomat who serves the {@area Crimson Court|2fc|x} of Ostoya. He was sent to Liesech to procure information on the {@area Weeping Pox|1bc|x}—or more precisely, on how to possibly weaponize it to use against the rebels from Raevo, the {@area Order of Dawn|31e|x}, or other enemies of Ostoya's rulers.

Ivik's bodyguards were found dead in the streets of Liesech, and the diplomat is missing. The characters are asked by representatives of Liesech's police force to quietly and discreetly investigate the disappearance and learn the fate of the wulven diplomat.

The characters can follow the trail of kidnappers who captured Ivik on behalf of the Order of Dawn. That group is looking for a way to turn the Weeping Pox into an affliction that can also affect undead in the same way it does living creatures.

data.name: Chapter 8: The City of Morencia
entries: Ship captains know when they are near Morencia. The gulf's water becomes choppy. The temperature noticeably drops. Skies abruptly become overcast. Passengers are ushered below decks. And the mist obscures everything from view.

On islands in the Gulf of Lions, between the coastlines of {@book Castinella|GrimHollowCG24|6} and {@book Charneault|GrimHollowCG24|5}, lies the city-state of Morencia. Travelers successfully navigating its protective mists and harsh, oppressive reefs find islands swept up in profiteering, power-plays, penitence, and purging.

Visitors to Morencia soon realize that behind every daily dalliance, trade deal, or duty fulfilled are nights filled with decadence, deception, and death. Among these contrasts, Morencia's people cautiously live and mysteriously die. Factions and leaders overtly and covertly act to their own benefit. A prevailing fear looms in the air, much like its guardian mists. But what lies behind it all?

entries.name: Geography
entries: An unnatural fog wall rolls across the seascape surrounding Morencia, slowly turning into an enveloping, eerie mist. Upon entering the {@area Misty Reefs|1f0|x}, the helmsman commands silence. The captain hawkishly watches the crow's nest for lookout warnings. A first mate listens for crew calls from the port, starboard and prow to any dangers.

The crew works with careful synergy; along with the life-saving {@i Nautantlicus} obtained from Morencia's {@area Navigators Guild|1ec|x}. Captain and crew utter prayers that they have the most current one. If they don't, they'll know soon enough: the hull will burst, bringing in dark water. Spectral voices of past perished cry from the mists and water below, welcoming their new dead.

Otherwise, after a gripping passage through the mists and among the reefs, vessels safely emerge to enter Morencia's harbor. Here, an armada awaits them. While waiting to be escorted to the quarantine bay, crew and passengers take in the boats and the dock-riddled shorelines, canal entryways, bridge connected isles, and arch- and crenellation-carved stonework of its buildings.

Morencia sits upon once-desolate swamp isles. Beyond its harbor, narrow canals wind through building-lined waterways. Even the city's brightest mornings bring a sunlight-stifling haze. At night, the city's canal lamps join a dim moonlight's effort to keep nightlife safe.
Temperatures are fitting for the season. However, there are two short periods during summer and winter, where temperatures grow extremely deadly—but are celebrated by citizens.

entries.name: History
entries: Morencia's settlers—whose culture, beliefs, magical prowess, and ways of life were challenged by the strictures of the Bürachers—fled that land in search of safe haven from subjugation. They pressed beyond the region's borders, far south, into the {@book Castinellan|GrimHollowCG24|6} lands. Here, they thought themselves safe, but the long reach of the {@book Bürach Empire|GrimHollowCG24|3} pushed all the way to the southern coast over time.

Fearful of the assimilation and possibly persecution of her people, the refugees' spiritual leader, Mother Frida Morenc, urged them to construct boats and sail into the Gulf of Lions. Frida, along with a few other refugees, remained behind: a dooming act. Their sacrifice lured the pursuing Bürachers away from their peoples' sea escape.

Many perished on the water. Dehydration, hyperthermia, and hunger drove people to cruel, cold consensus and horrific, heated acts. Boat hulls, damaged by reef and rock outcroppings, forced them to lighten loads. First supplies were dumped. Then, people willingly leapt from the boats—sacrifices worthy of Mother Morenc herself.

Despite their anguish, they clung to hope. The seemingly supernatural fog that shielded the area was studied by the most magically learned among them. Through trial and error, and after many deaths, the combined magic of several refugees allowed the fog to be pierced.

Soon enough, the refugees happened upon a group of dark, swampy islands. Once ashore, they forgave, healed, and mourned the tragic journey. They scoured the islands for resources with which to build a new life. In honor of the loss of their beloved leader, they named their new home Morencia.

With the mysterious fogs and the almost fiendish reefs and tides to protect them, Morencia flourished. From a swamp settlement, a city grew. Over time, their focus turned outward to navigation and trade. Descendants from its first settlers were not only skilled in magic-craft, but were adept carpenters, sailors, and shipwrights. With these talents, they constructed stalwart buildings and bridges. They built highly sought ships. They meticulously charted their surrounding waters. And they perfected the magic to pierce the supernatural protections of the area.

Within each Morencian citizen there is an innate determination and resiliency that contributes to their city's prosperity and remembrance of its settlers' sacrifices. Never will they have to fear invasion from a greater power, for the very island protects them.

entries.name: Politics
entries: As a recognized trade center, Morencia invites travelers, merchants, and their representatives. This means the population of Morencia fluctuates drastically, and businesses catering to travelers flourish around the docks. Overpopulation is a fear, however, which has led to several city-wide policies.

Upon arrival, all ships are subject to harbor quarantine, where they undergo thorough scrutiny by harbormaster agents. During this time, crews and passengers are escorted to the {@area Vueami|1e2|x}, or the Guest District, where they can obtain most comforts. Agents from Morencia's guilds or government agencies conduct meetings here, providing discretion commensurate with the nature of the business.

The authorities frequently check with proprietors of businesses that cater to travelers to gain further insights into guests' business, meetings, activities, and plans. Service guides tend visitors' needs, providing escorts and passes to other districts. While travelers and visitors are welcome, they are not allowed to become permanent residents of Morencia without proper vetting and approval. The harbormaster shares these limitations upon arrival, and the time visitors spend in the city is monitored.

This policy limits foreign merchants' presence and influence, while Morencia's business entities greatly sway and manage holds beyond its shores. Chief among them is the {@area Augustine Trading Company|25f|x}, which dictates the imports and exports throughout Etharis's ports. The company benefits from a merger with the local {@area Banker's Guild|1ed|x} and strong influence upon the {@area Supreme Council|1eb|x}.

Throughout their history, {@book Castinella|GrimHollowCG24|6} attempted to conquer or destroy Morencia's people, militarily and politically. Morencian citizens hear stores of the cruelty and depravities of the Arcanist Inquisition, and they vow to never let its influence reach their shores. In return, the Inquisitors view Morcenia as not just as a cauldron of arcane power waiting to explode, but also as a cesspool of excess and frivolity.

Recently, the Castinellan armada captured a Morencian ship captain in an attempt to intimidate them into safely guiding the Castinellan armada through the {@area Misty Reefs|1f0|x}. If not for vigilant fisherfolk of {@book Charneault|GrimHollowCG24|5} who saw the potential invasion and got word to Morcenia's authorities, Morencia might not have been able to foil the plot.

The {@area Navigator's Guild|1ec|x} worked with the Charneault navy to catch the Castinellan armada off guard. With the allied attack, the Castinellan armada ships were forced to slink away out of the mists. Significant losses were dealt to all fleets. Since then, the {@book Castinellan Provinces|GrimHollowCG24|6} have not attempted an invasion of Morencia.

The Castinellan influence eventually surfaced-through surprising trade agreements with the Augustine Trading Company. These dealings reek of guileful motives, but the company's leader, Kolyhs, assured elitists that no treachery exists and that profiting from their historical enemies is the best vengeance they could achieve.

The Banker's Guild profited immensely from the Castinellan deal. The guild welcomed loans to Castinellan interests while offering a generous loan to the Supreme Council for reconstruction of Morencia's fleet.
