The Bankers Guild leader, also {@area Kolyhs Papinian|1e9|x}, assured them that this was done with the safety of Morencia people in mind.

Morencia's elected governor {@area Doge Lorenzo Flabenici|1e7|x} suspects something iniquitous in Kolyhs' motives. He attempts to convince the Supreme Council to forego the Bankers Guild loan and consider another avenue.

Doge Flabenici convinced the council members they should auction open council seats. Generous citizens would gain a voting seat and provide citizen voices while supporting the stabilization of Morencia's {@area arsenal|1f1|x}. The council agreed that this move would spark confidence in the council's decision-making and quell suspicions of reaching influence of the {@area Bankers Guild|1ed|x} and {@area Augustine Trading Company|25f|x} in matters of city governance.

The {@area Supreme Council's|1eb|x} decision incited chaos. Citizens and guild members liquidated assets and called in favors to outbid others. Bids became incredibly high as prospects reached out to the Banker's Guild for exorbitant loans.
In the end, open seats were filled, the rebuilding of Morencia's fleet began, and its citizens pleased as new faces took seats on the council and the guild's influence lessened. Or so they thought.

As the Banker's Guild profits from loan terms, they benefit from their purchased seats. This weakens the power of doge and other guild seats tremendously. Nevertheless, the reformed Supreme Council must veil their immorally mired state with seeming competence.

entries.name: The Moonless Night Murders
entries: Morencia's majestic nights have become increasingly dangerous as of late. A series of vicious killings have left bodies mangled in district alleyways and floating in canals. Arsenal investigators, with the help of mystics from the {@area Maegilate|1ee|x}, have discovered few clues.

The ritualistic killings take place on nights when the moon is shrouded in cloud cover. No witness steps forward, but many claim to have seen ghostly forms of the victims moving through the city in the hours after the murder had happened. All of the bodies also decompose rapidly despite chemicals or magic being used for preservation.

Rumors mill throughout the districts. Some claim the victims were those being punished for forgetting the sacrifices of their ancestors. Other believe {@book Castinellan|GrimHollowCG24|6} inquisitors have found their way to Morencia's shores. Scared citizens demanded answers and the Supreme Council has none. In hopes to solve the killings, the Supreme Council has hired the famous monster hunter {@area Vedemir Kaltstahl|1e8|x} to investigate. His arrival pacified Morencians but has caused consternation among armada sentries, who do not want citizens to lose faith in their own soldiery to protect them.
All I can say is that the people disappear, and then they die. Brutally. We don't know why, but they brought in some monster hunter to deal with it.

entries.name: The Morencian Way
entries: The people attempt to embody the virtues of their ancestors: determined, resilient, hard-working and family oriented. They understand that sacrifice for the community is honorable and the importance of fair trade. Morencians are naturally cautious. They don't need to be reminded of the ancestor's hardships.

Some hardships are harder to forget. It is believed that ancestral spirits visit relatives in their dreams, never allowing them to forget their suffering. Those that endure these unconscious reminders are pitied by others and discreetly consoled with a hand on the shoulder and a phrase, "Thank you for suffering." These tormented ensure that when the day demands it, they bear their strain behind welcoming smiles.

As with all virtues, anything taken to an extreme can become a vice with only a modicum of ill intent. Determination and resilience becomes mania and shortsightedness. A dedication to perfection leads to excessive celebration when no one is looking.

Those that profess faith in high powers often do so by relating their deity's dogma through Mother Morenc, their ancestral spiritual leader. For example, when a business deal goes sour, someone might say, "Mother Morenc is telling me I've fallen victim to Venin's hand," or "Morael once reminded Mother Morenc to be patient; prosperity awaits."

entries.name: Cultural Happenings
entries: Morencian hard work is balanced by Morencian celebration. A good say's work is often followed by a night of revelry. Beyond smaller district events, there are three times when its citizens join together to ostensibly remember their people's struggles, successes, and tenacity. More often, however, it turns into debauchery and sin—or worse.

entries.name: Memoria
entries: During the first new moon, the {@area Maegilate|1ee|x} presents a spectacle of conjuration and illusion magic. Against the canvas of Morencia's protective mists, the city's history is presented. Mother Morenc's story, the flight from Bürach persecution, the Battle of the Castinellan Armada, and more. This spectacle captures the reviled, shocking, and tragic events.

On more than one occasion the festivities have summoned forth spirits of Morencian dead, who threaten onlookers. Now mages and priests are on hand during these festivities, protecting the curious throngs who both hope and dread to have a repeat haunting.

entries.name: The Black Carnival
entries: At the peak of summer heat and humidity, the Morencians celebrate the lives of those who've come before them, ensuring their prosperity and survival. A parade sees participants wear heavy ornate costumes or carry boats upon their back as they march along the parade route in the intense heat. Others participate in gluttonous feasting, engaging in contests of eating raw animal flesh, rotten fish, and drinking salt water.

At night, carnival activities take on a more abhorrent and carnal tone: expressions of lust, violence, and slaughter are common. By Supreme Council decree, these darker events occur away from Morencia's streets and waterways. The council recently received reports of deathly decadent activities during the most recent Black Carnival, coincidentally as the discovery of the first victims of the {@area Moonless Night Murders|1da|x} occurred.

entries.name: The White Carnival
entries: Morencia's sister celebration occurs at the first few days of winter's onset. The {@area Misty Reefs|1f0|x} become even more difficult to navigate during this time, necessitating activities to distract citizens from a time of decreased trade, less prosperity, and few resources.

The activities of the White Carnival are meant to remind people that their forebears also had to endure such plights, and to hearten the people that spring is just a few weeks away. They demonstrate respect for those sacrifices by wearing the traditional mourner's mask: a white face mask in an expression of deep sorrow.

{@area The Supreme Council|1eb|x} sends a number of these masks to each district citizen's residence, where they are handed out. Should there not be enough masks for each member of the family, then the family must decide who gets them. Those without masks at the toll of the Mourner's Wail bell are solemnly escorted to Morencia's dock in procession. There they are placed on a skiff and exiled into the mists.

Those who resist or flee escort are pursued, subdued and exiled. Accusations of the selling off of Mourner's Masks to bidders grows every year, forcing public outcry. This reenacts the ritual from the early days of Morencia, where citizens who were not adding to the economy or prosperity of the city during times of want were sent out into the mists. If they returned unharmed, they were considered blessed by the ancestors and allowed to stay. Very few did return, however.

entries.name: Districts
entries: Morencia is organized into five main districts, with four surrounding the Government District, each reachable by bridge or canal. Arsenal inspectors at each checkpoint scrutinize everyone passing between districts.

entries.name: Arbrumel (Harbor District)
entries: Docks of varying sizes accommodate arriving vessels through the {@area Misty Reefs|1f0|x}, Local vessels with proper authorizations are given cursory inspections, and Morencian cutters quickly guide foreign ships to the quarantine bay waters. Boat and dockside vendors welcome arrivals with foodstuffs and ominous warnings of recent happenings. Flotsam scourers skirt about on skiffs, scavenging floating debris from vessels sank by the Misty Reefs for anything of value. Dark plumes of smoke rise from the imposing {@area Arsenal|1f1|x} of Morencia's shipyards. Dark steel walls surround the area, atop which murders of crows roost and watch.

entries.name: Vueami (Guest District)
entries: The taverns, inns, and fest halls here offer any range of comforts for Morencia's visitors. City representatives live in satellite house-offices, where they meet business contacts or review prospective trade offers. Business owners learn what they can about guests to help the city filter good business dealings from bad. Identified fraudsters are taken to Arsenal jails and never heard from again. Other visitors are met by Morencian greeters, who offer guided tours, goods both legal and illegal, recommendations for services both vital and recreational, and any other service one might imagine.

entries.name: Zitoean (Citizen District)
entries: The majority of Morencian citizens live here. Residential blocks are identifiable by numbers and by statues carved from building foundations. Corner stores and small vendor tents line most walkways and canals. Businesses that operate in Ke 'Centre have shops here: citizens need not go far to resupply, and at lower prices than elsewhere.

Foreign visitors are only allowed here under escort or with the necessary paperwork. At night, streets are eerily quiet. Very few travel unless they are going to or from work, or to or from some revelry. Dedicated lamplighters move quickly to light canals and walkway lamp posts. Arsenal patrol presence is robust. The district is constantly under renovation.

entries.name: Ke ‘Centre (Business District)
entries: The prominent {@area Augustine Trading Company|25f|x}, the {@area Bankers|1ed|x}, and the Carpenter's and {@area Navigator's Guilds|1ec|x} houses are headquartered here. Travel is slow at best or, at worst, impossible—taking hours to get from place to place. Citizens know when company or guildmasters travel: their dark wooden skiffs dart through canals.

Foreigners, ambassadors, businesspeople, and their intermediaries mingle here. Though visitors are watched carefully, they are free to travel as long as they have a good reason to provide to Arsenal sentries when stopped and questioned—which can be frequently and closely. Visitors can acquire paperwork to save themselves some hassle, but it is only granted temporarily and with good cause.

entries.name: Enducour (Government District)
entries: From the checkpoints of the {@area Water Strider Bridge|1f2|x}, the {@area Supreme Council|1eb|x}'s Hall is clearly visible, as is the spires of the {@area Maegilate|1ee|x}, the city's arcanum center. This is Morencia's smallest district, as only matters of governance and academia occur within its streets. Councilmembers, professors, guests, and students travel from and to other districts after their daily business is conducted.

Morencia's lifetime elected doge lives here in a three-story, alabaster stone manse surrounded by a thick bog. At night, rising swamp gases give the lantern-lit canals a sickly green glimmer. Occasional displays of lightning and thunder materialize above the Maegilate, believed to be the result of perverse magic research.

entries.name: Doge Lorenzo Flabenici
entries: There have been only three people to serve as the city's official representative. The doge serves a lifetime term. Traditionally, the election process is several weeks long. Flabenici's election occurred in mere days, so quickly that some Morencians saw this as a sign of hope. Others proclaimed this an omen of darker days to come.

Flabenici manages Morencia through turbulent periods. Overconfidence in the {@area Misty Reefs'|1f0|x} power almost led to invasion by the {@book Castinellan|GrimHollowCG24|6} armada. Unsolved murders connected to the {@area Black Carnival|1de|x} whittle confidence in Flabenici's influence. His successful lobby to auction Supreme Council seats to replenish the treasury angered district leaders. Rising accusations that the doge adjusted {@area White Carnival|1df|x} mask allocations of some of Morencia's outspoken and wealthy families weighs heavily on citizen confidence in him. Yet, Doge Flabenici demonstrates resiliency. By presenting the people's needs to the Supreme Council and negotiating with guildmasters, he maintains a tenuous but firm grasp on his position.

entries.name: Witch Hunter Vendemir Kaltstahl
entries: The legendary slayer of the Three Sister Sorceresses and hero to the {@book Bürach Empire|GrimHollowCG24|3}, Kaltstahl came to Morencia at the behest of the Supreme Council. They hope that he can solve the {@area Moonless Night Murders|1da|x} and restore lost confidence in the Supreme Council's ability to keep the city safe. Despite disagreement from some council leaders, the investigation began.

The monster hunter erratically moved between locations and evidence—perplexing veteran sentry escorts. After a time with seemingly little progress, Kaltstahl demanded to speak with each member of the Supreme Council in private. Most council-people were hesitant, the monster hunters interrogation techniques border on immoral.

Kaltstahl then conferred privately with Doge Flabenici, arousing suspicion. Shortly after, the doge presented a motion to the Supreme Council to bring in more monster hunters. The council heatedly debates this request. Kaltstahl is on the brink of solving the murders and bringing forth those responsible but needs help from afar. However, the monster hunter missed some recent appointments, making his whereabouts and dedication to this hunt a matter of speculation.

entries.name: Kolyhs Papinian
entries: Most Morencians readily admit that the personality dubbed "The Blazing Brain" is the real power in Morencia. As the Banker's Guild leader, she allowed the Augustine Trading Company to acquire a majority stake in the guild, while simultaneously acquiring a majority stake in the Augustine Trading Company through proxy. This clever ploy gave Kolyhs the insight and influence to write loans and secure trade deals.

In time, her proxy became leader of the trading company, then revealed her status as proxy. Most profited from Kolyhs efforts, so she was voted in unanimously. Control of both the money and the markets allowed Kolyhs to benefit tremendously through Morencian historical turmoil.

In the aftermath of the {@book Castinellan Provinces|GrimHollowCG24|6} invasion, lucrative loans were written. In the auction for Supreme Council seats, prospects ran to the Banker's Guild for leveraging funds. Of course, Kolyhs bought a seat herself and has proxies in other seats.

Councilmembers and citizens suspect Kolyhs corrupted the council, but they haven't quite figured the extent of the corruption. The entrepreneur avoids questioning by {@area Vendemir Kaltstahl|1e8|x}. While not thought to be complicit in the Moonless Night Murders, she fears that any investigations into the machinations of the powers-that-be in Morencia might lead to unwanted revelations.
Flabenici talks real nice, but every time he smiles, someone ends up dead or broke. Or both.

entries.name: The Supreme Council
entries: Morencia's main governing body is the highest legal and political authority in the city-state. It consisted of ten seats, nine of which were seated by Morencia's guildmasters, the Maegilate Headmaster, and other business and military leaders. The recently opened citizen representatives seats have expanded the council's number to twenty.

The council is always led by Morencia's {@area current doge|1e7|x}. This structure allows for the considerations and influence from each corner of Morencian society. While intrigue and self-interest roils through the council, some leaders have the best interests of Morencia's citizens at heart. The council sent the monster hunter Vendemir Kaltstahl to help solve a series of unexplained murders and voted to acquire more monster hunters to aid the effort.

entries.name: The Navigator's Guild
entries: Deeply rooted in this guild's history is the mystery of the {@area Misty Reefs|1f0|x} and the secrets of Morencian magic. The fusion of the two is the key to navigating Morencia's protective mists and the dangers that they veil. This knowledge is deeply guarded by the guild, and costly to maintain.

Morencian allies that have this knowledge carry an enchanted Nautantlicus, an artifact sextant magically imbued with a route through the mists. This enchantment is temporary and monitored by the Maegilate. If the recurring costs for having safe passage are not paid for or relations with its keeper and Morencia is severed, a magic contingency removes them.

The Navigator's Guild has been known to increase the "pathway tax" for merchants, leading to harsh criticism by leaders that the sextants and their secrets should be kept under balanced oversight of the Maegilate. Their guildmaster, Admiral Endoria Onaglie, assuages such concerns, asserting that tax increases are used to aid Morencia's defenders who have contributed to society with their sacrifices. Admiral Onaglie is a hero of the Battle of the Castinellan Armada, having lost an eye in the battle.

entries.name: The Banker's Guild
entries: The guild's influence is apparent. It is the most prominent money-lending entity in Morencia. As such, it provides loans to citizens, guilds, and government, with privately negotiate terms. Guild vaults offer asset protection bolstered by the Misty Reefs and magically warded by the Maegilate. The merger with the Augustine Trading Company expands their reach throughout Etharis and allows for investment beyond the mists.

Among its prominent clientele abroad are mercantile entities of the {@book Castinellan Provinces|GrimHollowCG24|6}. Trade relations with Morencia's enemy has not escaped citizen notice. The guild endures scrutiny for its exorbitant loan terms, but whispering also speaks to disturbing payments of client's blood, exotic animals, and other disturbing items.

{@area Kolyhs Papinian|1e9|x} denies the existence of such transactions to the public, but in private she frequently visits the vaults that contain these unique acquisitions. Only Kolyhs knows the true value of these payments.

entries.name: The Maegilate
entries: The first gatherings of Morencia's magically talented settlers evolved into this teaching body. Selected citizens master magic of their ancestors. Arcane academia also includes city service: guild consulting, Supreme Council escorts, {@area Arsenal|1f1|x} support, and duties within the Maegilate itself. The Magi Profesorie oversees the city's arcane interests and is the voice of the institute on the Supreme Council.

As an entity, the Maegilate strives for a balanced hand in council decision-making. The Supreme Council recognizes consultations with the Augustine Trading Company and the Arsenal. These meetings guide the testing of Morencia's armament advances. Its progress is kept guarded by these entities. These meetings halted in the aftermath of the last {@area Black Carnival|1de|x} celebration.

The Magi Profesorie, who provides updates on the collaboration, remains silent and visibly irritated at the arrival of the monster hunter, {@area Vendemir Kaltstahl|1e8|x}, and the Supreme Council's recent vote to bring in more hunters to aid Kaltstahl in the {@area Moonless Night Murders|1da|x}.

Groups with the Maegilate work on special projects, and not all are known to the public—or even to the Supreme Council. While the members of the Maegilate are treated with respect by the population, the distrust of magic continues to grow, even within a population that has relied upon it to ensure trade continues.

entries.name: The Misty Reefs
entries: Morencia's ghostly mist wall is spotted miles out by vessel lookouts. This is more than enough time to avoid it, or if entering, using the Nautantlicus given to the vessel by Morencia's Navigator's Guild. If the magical sextant isn't closely heeded, vessels know quickly enough. They can become lost in the misty labyrinth, wreck upon jagged reefs and sandbars, or attacked by spectral spirits of sunken ships.

The specialists of Morencia did not control the mist or the reefs; in fact, they struggle to understand the ever-evolving supernatural forces that feed it. The ancestral magic that originally allowed for navigation of the Misty Reefs has had to evolve along with the phenomenon. It is feared by some scholars that whatever fuels the magic of the Misty Reefs wants to do more than keep people away—it may want to trap people there. But to what purpose? Few dare to speculate.

entries.name: The Arsenal of Morencia
entries: All Morencians proudly boast that their navy is the most formidable in all Etharis. Few contest this. The mighty galleons, sleek cutters, and skulker skiffs are made from the finest craftsmanship. The fleet is spread in locations along Morencia's shorelines. Its largest presence floats in waters outside just outside the Arsenal shipyard walls.

Cutters vigilantly watch the Misty Reef walls for any sign of vessel arrival, to which they guide into three-day quarantine waters. There, the Arsenal auxiliary, consisting of flotsam skulkers and inspector dinghies, inspect cargo and hulls for anything out of the ordinary. Sailors rotate duty from ship to canal and walkway patrols. The Arsenal leaders are stern disciplinarians. Dereliction is not met with brig time, but with a mile long-swim along the Misty Reef's wall to whatever fate awaits.

entries.name: The Water Strider Bridge
entries: A breathtaking spectacle of Carpenter's Guild effort, this bridge connects the two halves of the islands of the {@area Enducour District|1e5|x}. Several smaller bridges connect it from the other districts, giving it the appearance of a swamp spider. Sculptors indulged further by carving these bridges into a majestic, life-like resemblance of the bog arachnid.

During the day, the view from the center of the main bridge allows a view into every district bridge was a favored place to watch the {@area Black Carnival|1de|x} events or {@area Memoria|1dd|x} presentation. At night, the bridge it lit by glowing orbs that naturally rise from the bog and fly about and above the bridge. This draws the curious who claim and fear that they are the remnants of something that lived here before the Morencians came. There has been no record of the lights attacking anyone. Yet.

entries.name: Quest Hooks
entries: The following quest hooks may take place in Morencia.

entries.name: Can I Go Home?
entries: Out on the docks, a Morencian family mourns the loss of their daughter, exiled during the last {@area White Carnival|1df|x}. Imagine their horror when they spot her floating out from the Misty Reefs! They collect her, they find her in possession of a Nautantlicus. Elated but afraid, they flee with her.

The {@area Supreme Council|1eb|x} soon finds out what has happened and sends emissaries to collect her and the magical navigation device to figure out what has happened. The fleeing family stumbles into the characters and begs them to help get their child to safety. The characters might face Supreme Council or Morencian loyalists, both of whom demand that tradition of the White Carnival be upheld.

Their flight to take the child to safety may take them through into the unsettled swamp, where natural dangers or into the Misty Reefs, where spirits long for the child to join them. They may reach out to Supreme Council members to argue for the life of the child and sway the council's decision.

entries.name: The Moon Hits Your Eye
entries: While traveling through any of Morencia's districts, the characters continually spot a rotting crow, perched on a nearby bridge, dock ledge, or rooftop. The crow annoyingly pesters the characters, leading them into the {@area Zitoean District|1e3|x}, home of Isaa Guillaume, daughter of the deceased hero of the ship Relentless, First Mate Brieuc Guillame. Upon mention of the crow, Isaa reveals that this might be Lunelle, her grandfather's pet crow. She shares what she knows about the historic {@book Castinellan Provinces|GrimHollowCG24|6}'s invasion and the heroic acts of Admiral Endoria Onaglie, adding that her father died heroically during the battle against Castinella. Isaa offers to compensate the character if they can capture Lunelle and bring him back home.

Chasing the crow takes the characters into the business of an antiquities collector with close ties to the Banker's Guild. This noble's trap-laden sub-basements are a macabre museum of historic treasures. Included in them is the skeletal remains of Brieuc Guillame, and the perfectly preserved dissected eye of Admiral Endoria Onaglie. A transcribed eye-witness account from that time insists that the Admiral lost her mind and killed Brieuc as a sacrifice to the spirits of the mists. If these discoveries were made public, they could outrage the city and may besmirch the reputation of one of Morencia's great heroes and member of the Supreme Council.
Some say those lights on the Water Strider are evil! Well, here's a crazy idea. What if they're the ones killing people? You know it sounds convincing, don't it? Might take it to the Council!
entries.by: {@i Tavern Patron, After Three Too Many}

entries.name: History of Etharis
entries: The histories of Etharis have been written in the blood of the fallen by those who remain, honoring their ancestors through recorded and oral traditions. Yet Etharis is an ancient continent of diverse cultures. Knowledge has been lost or misremembered, great heroes have been glorified beyond their actual deeds, and calamities have erased much of Etharis's past.

This timeline charts the history of Etharis in rough order of events, though it's impossible to pinpoint exactly when each moment occurred. The mythologies of different cultures clash. There are no records of whether the dreamers were already asleep when Ember Cairn fell. Therefore, these histories are left open for the scholars of Etharis to reconstruct and debate.

Furthermore, the order of events as we've imagined them may not suit the sorts of stories you wish to tell at your table. For example, the lore written in this book suggests the Darkfall is a recent tragedy. However, you may desire to set your campaign in Ostoya during a time after centuries of vampiric rule. Move the Darkfall forward or back on the timeline, or add new entries to the recent past of your Etharis, moving the timeline forward.
As with everything in this campaign guide, the intent is to give you the tools to craft stories in Etharis of your own dark fantasies!

entries.name: Era of Antiquity
entries: An era of myth and prehistory, filled with conflicting creation stories. The distance of time is unknown. Many events in this era have no recorded accounts, only the evidence of their occurrence, which has survived to the modern day.

entries.name: The Gods Awaken
entries: According to Büracher scriptures, the gods are the first beings to awaken and sculpt the mortal world.
{@area "The Aetheric War and Gods' End"|34f|x} in Chapter 13
{@area "Dangerous Celebrations"|1db|x} in Chapter 8

entries.name: The Primordic Cycle
entries: The kin of the Valikan Clans tell of {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}, the Great Prismatic Wyrm, as the first being to exist.
{@area "The Primordic Cycle"|370|x} in Chapter 13

entries.name: Hymn of Viduami
entries: In Ostoya, a myth is told about a death god, entombed in the depths of the City Below by its kindred deities.
{@area "Hymn of Viduami"|074|x} in Chapter 3

entries.name: Kentigern's Saga
entries: After {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24} is slain by the hero Kentigern, Valikan sagas say the Great Wyrm's corpse is used to craft the mortal realm.
{@area "The Great Prismatic Wyrm"|179|x} in Chapter 6
{@area "The Kentigern Saga"|371|x} in Chapter 13

entries.name: The Aetheric War
entries: The Aether Kindred emerge from the void and destroy the gods. Only Aurelia, Galt, Ulmyr, and Maligant survive.
{@area "The Aetheric War"|350|x} in Chapter 13

entries.name: The Netherworld
entries: The heavens are ruptured by the Aetheric War, forming the Netherworld. Arch Seraphs of dead gods fall from grace.
{@area "Netherworld"|34a|x} in Chapter 12

entries.name: Birth of Caer Neiada
entries: The archfey cultivate Caer Neiada. Some believe them to be fallen seraphs, while the elves of Charneault worship them as deities.
{@area "History"|0eb|x} in Chapter 4

entries.name: Twilight of the Dreamers
entries: The dreamers fall into a slumber that will last an epoch, escaping an unknown cataclysm.
{@area "Dreamers"|238|x} in Chapter 10

entries.name: Descent to the City Below
entries: A civilization rises and falls, swallowed by the earth beneath a realm that would one day be named Ostoya.
{@b "The City Below" in Chapter 3}

entries.name: The Ulufey
entries: The ancestors of the elves, believing themselves children of archfey, settle Caer Neiada.
{@area "The Ulufey"|0c4|x} in Chapter 4

entries.name: Fall of Ember Cairn
entries: Ember Cairn, the ancient home of the dragonborn, is destroyed. Their people scatter across Etharis.
{@area "History"|163|x} in Chapter 5

entries.name: Kingdoms of Stone
entries: The dwarves build their fortress cities in the heartland of Etharis, raising kingdoms of their own.
{@area "Dwarves"|22d|x} in Chapter 10

entries.name: Human Tribes
entries: Humans wander the Büracher forests, forming into small tribes that grow into kingdoms.
{@area "History"|037|x} in Chapter 2
{@area "Humans"|235|x} in Chapter 10

entries.name: Exodus of the Orcs
entries: The people known as orcs sail from northern Etharis, heading west to distant lands.
{@area "Grudgels"|23a|x} in Chapter 10

entries.name: Era of Expansion
entries: Humans spread from west-central Etharis to settle in regions to the north, south, and east. This is an era of bloodshed and mourning. As history unfolds through centuries of human expansion, smaller kingdoms rise and fall until the modern civilizations of Etharis take shape.

entries.name: Four Kingdoms of the Divines
entries: The Norden, Raulfolk, Unters, and Abendlanders become the dominant human kingdoms in central Etharis.
{@area "History"|037|x} in Chapter 2

entries.name: Cold Iron Keep
entries: The wicked mage, Mordu, raises Cold Iron Keep as his fortress. He's soon after slain by the Valikans.
{@area "Cold Iron Keep"|196|x} in Chapter 6

entries.name: Thorgard's Saga
entries: Valikan hero, Thorgard av Holgar, unites the clans and conquers southwards. He then sails his fleet west in search of new lands and his orcish kin.
{@area "Thorgard's Saga"|17a|x} in Chapter 6

entries.name: Writings of Numena
entries: Raakan el-Adakar's research on the nature of arcane magic forms the basis for magical theory.
{@area "Arcane Magic in Etharis"|335|x} in Chapter 12

entries.name: Rise of the Prismatic Circle
entries: The archdruid Kybard has a vision that {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24} merely slumbers, forming the Prismatic Circle.
{@area "The Prismatic Circle"|17b|x} in Chapter 6

entries.name: Arrival of the First Men
entries: The earliest human settlers reach Caer Neiada, journeying through the Grove Maze.
{@area "Arrival of the First Men"|0c5|x} in Chapter 4

entries.name: Wars of Stehlenwald & Grabenstein
entries: The dwarven city of Grabenstein falls to the Unters, whilst Stehlenwald endures.
{@area "Unterland"|025|x} in Chapter 2
{@area "Dwarves"|22d|x} in Chapter 10

entries.name: The Lord Edda
entries: The {@i Lord Edda} is written, compiling Valika's myths and sagas from earlier centuries.
{@area "History"|178|x} in Chapter 6

entries.name: The Bürach Empire
entries: Emperor Indorius I is ordained by the Hearthkeepers, founding the Bürach Empire.
{@area "History"|037|x} in Chapter 16

entries.name: Crossing the Grey Spine
entries: Büracher migrants cross the Grey Spine, discovering Kaer Vena and new lands in the east.
{@area "History"|0a1|x} in Chapter 3
{@area "History"|0a1|x} in Chapter 3

entries.name: Flickers of Coldfire
entries: The first sightings of coldfire occur on Volgen, and the beginning of the ice bridge forms.
{@area "Geography"|177|x} in Chapter 6

entries.name: Castinellan Peninsula
entries: The Bürach Empire extends its reach to the Castinellan peninsula, discovering the shattered kingdom of the dragonborn.
{@area "Dragonborn"|22b|x} in Chapter 10

entries.name: Founding of Morencia
entries: The city of Morencia is founded as a settlement, and it becomes a haven for folk fleeing the Bürach Empire.
{@area "History"|1d8|x} in Chapter 8

entries.name: Pact of the Sacred Land
entries: Thuridan Sarador crowns Aymeric Noblecoeur, signing the Pact of the Sacred Land and founding the Charneault Kingdom.
{@area "Pact of the Sacred Land"|0c6|x} in Chapter 4

entries.name: Era of Descent
entries: At the height of its dominance, the Bürach Empire endured two hundred years of cascading tragedies and defeats, which eroded its might and culminated in the Gods' End. Historians call this period the Era of Descent.

entries.name: First Ostoyan War
entries: Ostoya secedes from the Bürach Empire, declaring its independence and winning victory at Fallowheart.
{@area "History"|0a1|x} in Chapter 3
{@area "Lady of Vengeance"|07f|x} in Chapter 3

entries.name: Unification
entries: The Unifier, Sagrado Valieda, unites the Castinellan Provinces of Faro, Toletum, and Therpena, founding the Arcanist Inquisition.
{@area "Unification"|133|x} in Chapter 5
{@book "Arcanist Inquisition"|GrimHollowCG24|14|Arcanist Inquisition} in Chapter 11

entries.name: Company of Free Swords
entries: Darion Harkin founds the Company of Free Swords in the wake of the Falaris resistance.
{@area "Company of Free Swords"|27c|x} in Chapter 11

entries.name: Augustine Trading Company
entries: Manoch Augustus leaves the clergy to found the Augustine Trading Company in Morencia
{@area "Augustine Trading Company"|25f|x} in Chapter 11

entries.name: Ebon Syndicate
entries: Hiram Greyman narrowly escapes death by the fortune of a hawk moth and founds the Ebon Syndicate.
{@area "Ebon Syndicate"|29c|x} in Chapter 11

entries.name: Ulmyr's Gate
entries: The mages of Ulmyr's Gate attempt to open a permanent rift to the Ethereal Plane, destroying their entire city.
{@area "Nordenland"|021|x} in Chapter 2
{@area "Disembodied"|247|x} in Chapter 10

entries.name: The Red Mourning
entries: The Bürach Empire attempts to annex Liesech through Analita von Raiza, culminating in unleashing the Weeping Pox.
{@area "The Red Mourning"|1ba|x} in Chapter 7

entries.name: Crowning of Emperor Leopold I
entries: The coronation of Emperor Leopold I leads the Bürach Empire into civil war, and the emperor's assassination leads to the Gods' End.
{@area "Gods' End"|01b|x} in Chapter 2

entries.name: Era of Beast
entries: In the current dark age of Etharis, humanity clings to survival. Every realm is haunted by the shadow of annihilation through this godless era, marked by the arrival of the Great Beast.

entries.name: The Gods' End
entries: The last gods of Etharis--Aurelia, Galt, Ulmyr, and Maligant—destroy each other, leading to a new dark age.
{@area "Gods' End"|01b|x} in Chapter 2

entries.name: Watchers of the Faithful
entries: The Castinellan theocracy ordain themselves the Watchers of the Faithful in the aftermath of the Gods' End.
{@area "Watchers of the Faithful"|2d8|x} in Chapter 11

entries.name: Second Ostoyan War
entries: The Bürach Empire invades Ostoya, trying to stabilize its strength by reclaiming territories.
{@area "The Darkfall"|080|x} in Chapter 3
{@area "The Darkfall"|080|x} in Chapter 3

entries.name: Shadow of the Great Beast
entries: The first stories of the Great Beast begin circulating in the Bürach Empire.
{@area "Rising Shadows"|01d|x} in Chapter 2

entries.name: Order of Kentigern
entries: The Order of Kentigern becomes the last shield against the coldfire crisis, now erupting from Volgen.
{@area "Fort Kentigern"|18c|x} in Chapter 6

entries.name: The Darkfall
entries: The sun vanishes from the Ostoyan Empire, giving rise to the Crimson Court, who seize power.
{@area "The Darkfall"|080|x} in Chapter 3
{@area "The Darkfall"|080|x} in Chapter 3
{@area "Geography"|1d7|x} in Chapter 3

entries.name: The Raevo Rebellion
entries: The province of Raevo secedes from the Ostoyan Empire, and the Order of Dawn is founded.
{@area "Raevo"|066|x} in Chapter 3
{@area "Order of Dawn"|31e|x} in Chapter 11

entries.name: The Dark Mist
entries: Princess Tiphaine de Sauveterre disappears, and the Pact of the Sacred Land is broken.
{@area "The Dark Mist"|0cf|x} in Chapter 4

data.name: Part 2: Denizens of Etharis
entries: When the sages and scholars began to chart the records of history in Etharis, the Elder species each held their own realms within the world. Interaction between these different realms was infrequent and limited in scope. Survival was hard enough to achieve without the confrontations inherent in inter-species contact.

Then the humans changed everything. Advances in science, particularly in warfare, drove them to look at the other realms as prizes to be won. The humans' inevitable march into the rest of Etharis ended with the carving of separate regions. The other species were either driven to the edges of the continent, driven deep underground, or integrated into human societies.

Today, humans are still the most numerous and powerful species in Etharis. The Elder species survive either in isolated remnants of their own domains, or incorporated into larger human kingdoms. But in this age of crisis, some leaders of the other species may see the opportunity to claw back what their ancestors lost.

Other types of creatures, particularly those of a more eldritch origin, find a home within the lands of Etharis, even if acceptance is hard won and begrudging. Some have always lived in Etharis, but don't have a society of their own. Some seem to have returned to Etharis after everyone thought they were gone. Still others are a result of a magical cataclysm or the fall of the gods.

Some truly unique folks find themselves mistrusted or even hunted based on their seemingly monstrous heritages. From those created by the faeries to fool humans, to those who are children of those suffering from lycanthropy, finding their way in Etharis society is just as challenging as fighting a daemon or a dragon.

One place the people of Etharis might find solace is in a group or institution that offers both understanding and protection. Secret societies, trade guilds, magic schools, knightly groups, and dark cabals are just a few of the types of organizations that have offered fellowship—but too often at a steep cost.
What do these species and the groups they belong to mean in the ongoing struggle against the dark forces attempting to turn Etharis into a nightmare realm?
Read on and find out!

data.name: Chapter 10: Folk of Etharis
entries: The people of Etharis tend to be hardy folk, as one needs to be when dealing with the hardship and strife of a world lacking in sympathy but teeming with monsters. Whether thinking about the common heritages of a typical fantasy setting, the rare heritages unique to Grim Hollow, or the eldritch heritages of magical and terrifying origin, the folk of Etharis leave plenty of room for interesting and dramatic stories.

{@area Humans|235|x} dominate the world of Etharis. The elder peoples—the {@area Dragonborn|22b|x}, {@area Dwarves|22d|x}, {@area Elves|22f|x}, {@area Gnomes|231|x}, and {@area Halflings|233|x}—share the human nations with those people who undertook the conquests of the Era of Expansion. The rarer and more unsettling heritages share these lands as well, although their presence may raise some eyebrows—and far too often pitchforks and torches.

As described in the {@book Grim Hollow Player's Guide|GrimHollowPG24}, the Heritage System allows the folk of Etharis to be far more diverse than a typical fantasy setting. The social structure of the different realms or locales sees an individual's traits breaking with that of someone typical of their species.

Even physiological traits can differ greatly within species, as the interweaving of different people—not to mention the mutative magic prevalent throughout Etharis—can give someone traits much different than even those of their ancestors. If a player can imagine a character with unique abilities, the Heritage System likely has a way for them to build it.

The rare and eldritch heritages described in this chapter have a special place in Grim Hollow's lore. These species, or in some cases, these created beings, might not have separate nations or even distinct communities within the landscape of Etharis, but they nonetheless have compelling stories about their origins or their relationship with the other folks who make of the population of the world.

Players might want to embody the lore of these less common heritages, playing a character that engages with the lore as a typical member of that heritage. On the other hand, they may want to be the exception of that heritage, breaking tradition and charting their own course within the social milieu of the world.

As with any campaign, the world of Etharis that you present to your players—and the stories of the people that make up that world—are yours to tailor for the stories and adventures taking place there. The folk described here, and their histories and legends, are a starting point for your imagination!

entries.name: Common Heritages
entries: Etharis's common heritages are found throughout the world across every social class, occupation, and belief system. While they are often associated with a particular region, any settlement of moderate size will have examples of every common heritage.

entries.name: Dragonborn
entries: With scales like Armor and eyes that betray an ancient lineage, the dragonborn move through the shadows of Etharis, inspiring both terror and reverence in those they encounter. Their draconic features—jagged scales, deadly talons, and fearsome jaws—mark them as living echoes of legendary beasts. Though they lack the massive wingspan and imposing stature of true dragons, dragonborn carry within them the living essence of these primal creatures: dragon's breath and terrible majesty flow through their veins.

Whispers claim the dragonborn's lineage predates all others in Etharis. When human explorers first ventured into the southernmost territories during the {@area Era of Expansion|038|x}, they discovered in Castinella not a thriving civilization, but the hollow remnants of one—a kingdom already fallen to ruin. The granite metropolis of Ember Cairn stood as a crumbling monument to forgotten glory. Even the dragonborn themselves could not recall what calamity had befallen their once-proud society. Their prayers echoed unanswered through abandoned temples, their gods silent as the grave. They dwelled amid the broken pieces of their inheritance, like orphans in their own ancestral homes.

The human settlement of what would become {@book Castinella|GrimHollowCG24|6} required little effort. The dragonborn, plagued by spiritual emptiness and growing despair, abandoned their ancestral territories to wander Etharis in search of divine answers that never came. Those who remained were receptive to the teachings of human missionaries who spoke of {@area The Aetheric War|34f|x} and the Divine Seraphs. A terrible revelation took hold: their gods had not abandoned them but had been obliterated by the {@area Aether Kindred|350|x}. This epiphany filled the void of their lost faith, transforming the dragonborn of Castinella into the most fervent believers in the Eternal Dogma, their devotion born from ancient wounds.

In recognition for their conversion, Castinella granted the dragonborn dominion over a fragment of their ancestral lands. From this meager foundation, they reconstructed Ember Cairn atop its own ruins. The dragonborn who were scattered across the continent began returning on grim pilgrimages to their reborn city, where they embraced the worship of the Divine Seraphs with desperate intensity. Finding their prayers answered at last—or so they believed—many dragonborn felt called to become clerics, missionaries, and inquisitors. They spread their adopted faith with a zealotry matched only by the searing flames that erupt from their throats—twin instruments of conversion that leave either ashes or believers in their wake.

entries.name: Using Dragonborn in Your Campaign
entries: Dragonborn make zealous inquisitors. They hunt heretics through mist-shrouded villages, and their intimidating draconic features make them perfect enforcers of religious orthodoxy. A fanatical dragonborn inquisitor can be a terrifying antagonist hunting your players for real or imagined transgressions against the faith. However, if the characters convince the inquisitor of their innocence, they could earn a potent ally.

Alternatively, dragonborn characters may question their culture's typical religion. A young dragonborn adventurer may discover ancient texts suggesting their gods weren't destroyed but imprisoned, leading to forbidden archaeological expeditions. This could unveil dark secrets about what truly happened to dragonborn civilization.

The party could ally with a dragonborn chaplain who witnessed the horrors of border conflicts firsthand. This weathered veteran has a pragmatic interpretation of the Eternal Dogma that prioritizes compassion. While maintaining respectable standing within the church, he provides succor to all who seek it, regardless of faith, heritage, or history. His position grants access to sensitive information, making him an excellent source of intelligence. The weary warrior-priest walks a dangerous line between official duties and personal principles, whose help risks discovery by more orthodox elements within his faith.

entries.name: Dwarves
entries: Carved from the very stone they venerate, dwarves emerged in Etharis in times before history's quill first touched parchment. Their presence as old as the continent itself, these stoic beings established their realms within the valleys and shadows of Etharis's most primeval mountains: the Rock Teeth and the Grey Spine. Deep within these ancient ranges coursed veins of mithral and gold, glittering promises in the darkness. Those dwarves who extracted and transformed these precious metals into works of exquisite craftsmanship soon garnered renown across the lands of Etharis. Today, dwarves are most common throughout the {@book Bürach Empire|GrimHollowCG24|3} and {@book Valika|GrimHollowCG24|7}.

When the blood-soaked {@area Era of Expansion|037|x} descended upon the continent, dwarven clans retreated behind the walls of their greatest strongholds: the terraced citadel of {@area Stehlenwald|032|x} and the mountain fortress of Grabenstein. These bastions were once deemed unconquerable. The defenders of Grabenstein fought with grim determination to protect their ancestral holdings, enduring siege after relentless siege until finally their formidable walls crumbled, their thanedoms scattered to the bitter winds, and their mineral-rich mines were seized by human warlords whose descendants would later establish the foundations of the Bürach Empire.

The dwarven kingdom of Stehlenwald persisted, though not without cost. Rather than suffer their northern kin's fate, the Stehlenwald dwarves delved further into the mountain's lightless heart. There, in chambers untouched by surface light, miners uncovered a metal that defied their enemies' Weapons: adamantine. Clad in impenetrable adamantine Armor and wielding axes, spears, and blades that cut through bone like paper, dwarven battalions succeeded in driving invaders back to their homelands. Victory came at a terrible price—Stehlenwald's population was devastated by the twin specters of war and famine. For the survivors, isolation within their mountain sanctuary became their sole means of preservation.

By the time human kingdoms rose to prominence, the once-proud dwarves of Stehlenwald had recovered their strength, if not their numbers. They emerged from their mountain seclusion to find the world utterly transformed, their realm now encircled and vastly outnumbered by the Bürach Empire's legions. Recognizing the single path that might ensure his people's survival, the dwarf king negotiated a pact with the Bürach Empire. The dwarves would relinquish their territories and join the empire, provided they retained independent governance over their own affairs.

To this day, countless dwarves cannot bring themselves to forgive the immense loss of life and sovereignty suffered by their forebears. A common saying in Etharis holds that one might sooner move a mountain than change a dwarf's mind. Though deep-seated grievances still fester within the Bürach Empire, dwarves have risen to become among the most affluent and accepted peoples throughout all the nations of Etharis—their outward prosperity masking generational scars that never truly heal.

entries.name: Using Dwarves in Your Campaign
entries: Consider portraying dwarven settlements as outwardly prosperous trade centers hiding dark secrets beneath. Their mountain homes and ancient mining tunnels might harbor forgotten shrines where traditionalists perform rituals to ancestors who died defending their sovereignty. These cults could be harmless expressions of cultural memory—or sinister groups planning vengeance against human bloodlines.

Dwarven characters make excellent representatives of generational trauma. A merchant dwarf who smiles at imperial officials by day might harbor centuries-old grudges by night. This duality creates morally complex NPCs who assist players while pursuing their own agendas of reclaiming lost artifacts or sabotaging imperial interests.

While in {@book Liesech|GrimHollowCG24|8}, the party might run afoul of a dark dwarven magistrate. The bureaucrat was expelled from the Magister College decades ago, finding a career in mundane public policy. They study the transformative curses of Etharis with particular fascination, viewing them as evolutionary pathways. The failed arcanist conducts experiments on unwitting subjects lured through his legitimate connections. Their most insidious scheme involves coins infused with essences harvested from lycanthropes, vampires, and other transformed creatures. These enchanted coins gradually alter those who handle them, creating subtle physiological and psychological changes. The twisted wizard meticulously documents these changes in coded ledgers, viewing the city as his experimental laboratory.

entries.name: Elves
entries: Ancient and hauntingly beautiful, the elves were among the first to walk the primeval forests of Etharis. Their history intertwines with half-remembered myths from time immemorial. Tall and ethereal in their grace, these ageless beings claim descent from the nature spirits that first breathed life into the mortal realm. The dense woodlands and meandering riverlands of Caer Neiada—now part of the {@book Charneault Kingdom|GrimHollowCG24|5}—are their ancestral territories. Within these shadowed groves, they crafted realms of terrible beauty, forever bound to the whispered guidance of the forest's ageless spirits. Etharis's elves are still most commonly found under the boughs of Charneault.

Elves possess unnaturally keen vision and deadly accuracy with bow and arrow. Their intimate communion with the woodland's secrets and their fey bloodline allows them to vanish like ghosts within their territories. In Etharis's dawn, their nimble and lethal armies inspired dread in all who encountered them. Yet centuries of unchallenged supremacy over their forest domains, coupled with their extended lifespans, cultivated a fatal arrogance within the elven psyche. They regarded themselves as elevated beings, particularly in comparison to humans, whom they dismissed as crude and ephemeral creatures. Thus blinded by pride, the elves failed to perceive the rising human threat until it was too late.

{@area The Era of Expansion|038|x} extracted a terrible toll from elven civilization. Their once-great kingdoms shattered, countless ancient forests reduced to smoldering ruins. Though their martial prowess never faltered, the elves found themselves overwhelmed by relentless human armies that burned and desecrated their sacred woodlands. Forced to retreat into the deepest, most inaccessible reaches of the forests, they clung to the desperate belief that the Spirits of Nature would shield them from extinction. When the elves eventually returned from their exile, they formed an uneasy pact with humanity that gave birth to the {@book Charneault Kingdom|GrimHollowCG24|5}—a compromise born of necessity rather than desire.

The profound grief of the elves for their vanished paradise now manifests in their haunting artistic expressions. They have composed soul-rending melodies of a lost golden age that echo mournfully through the trees when darkness falls. In these melancholic ballads, they name themselves ulufey—"mortal fey" and descendants of the fey sidhe. The faerie courts that once guided their ancestors did not perish during the Era of Expansion, and many elves harbor the silent hope that these Nature Spirits might yet aid in restoring their lands to their former, terrible splendor—though what price such beings might extract for their assistance remains unspoken.

entries.name: Using Elves in Your Campaign
entries: Elven enclaves—and nearly anything the elves create—are remarkable works of art, but each hides a certain darkness and melancholy. Visitors might find themselves unable to leave, stricken by profound beauty or encumbered with the unending sorrow of the long-lived elves.

The ulufey make compelling antagonists as ancient elves seeking vengeance for lost forests, perhaps kidnapping children from logging communities or binding human spirits to withered trees. Alternatively, they might serve as tragic figures—the last guardians of sacred groves whose protection requires increasingly desperate measures.

The party could encounter a once-celebrated guardian of Caer Neiada's sacred groves, who survived the burning of her ancestral forest during the Era of Expansion by forming a desperate pact with an ancient, nameless entity hiding in the forest's heart. Now seemingly ageless, she maintains her ethereal beauty through a horrific ritual requiring the lives of young humans with fey-touched bloodlines. Her court appears as a haven of preserved elven splendor deep within a recovering forest, but visitors who look closely notice how the trees grow in unnatural patterns, their bark inscribed with arcane symbols. Each of the lady's dogmatic followers bear a notable self-inflicted scar—assurance they will never usurp their leader's beauty.

entries.name: Gnomes
entries: Restless of mind and slight of frame, gnomes are believed to share distant ancestry with dwarves, though they lack the stature and bulk of their mountain kin. These diminutive beings are marked by ceaselessly churning intellects and an unsettling gift for creation. Gnomes possess minds that never truly rest—clever, inquisitive, occasionally mischievous, yet bound by rigid cultural devotion to their obsessive crafts.

The most terrifying mechanical marvels of Etharis have emerged from gnomish workshops. Thundering cannons, unsettling clockwork automatons, smoke-belching flintlocks, and devastating explosives all bear the fingerprints of gnomish ingenuity. For gnomes, the fevered process of creation often eclipses the creation itself. They conjure new inventions merely to demonstrate possibility, with little thought to consequence. A common saying among other folk warns that to locate a gnome in any populated area, one need only follow the trail of destruction to its smoking origin.

When the bloodshed of the {@area Era of Expansion|038|x} engulfed Etharis, gnomes abandoned their vulnerable settlements beyond the mountains' protection, seeking shelter within dwarven strongholds like {@area Stehlenwald|032|x}. There, they shared in the grim fate of their cousins as human armies laid siege to the towering stone ramparts. While dwarven miners unearthed adamantine in the mountain's depths, the unquiet minds of gnomish inventors conceived different applications for the mountain's gifts.

Gnome alchemists labored without respite, combining volatile compounds and mysterious powders until they perfected a weapon of unprecedented devastation: explosives. As dwarven warriors clad in impenetrable adamantine charged the human forces, the air shattered with concussive blasts as gnomish artillery rained cannonballs and dynamite upon the enemy lines. Beneath this barrage, human forces broke in terror, witnessing what seemed a horrific new sorcery unleashed upon the world. Victory came at grievous cost to both gnome and dwarf. The gnomes followed their dwarven allies into centuries of isolation beneath the mountains—and when the dwarf king eventually led his people into the {@book Bürach Empire|GrimHollowCG24|3}, the gnomes accompanied them, trailing smoke and whispered secrets.

Strangely, gnomes maintain an uncanny connection to the natural world. Those who did not seek sanctuary within the mountains felt themselves inexplicably drawn back to ancestral territories deep within Etharis's ancient forests—a peculiar affinity suggesting a shared lineage with the elves, though neither species acknowledges such kinship. While gnomes are common throughout the Bürach Empire, the often seek solace within {@book Charneault|GrimHollowCG24|5}.

entries.name: Using Gnomes in Your Campaign
entries: Gnomes provide Game Masters with perfect vessels for exploring themes of innovation gone awry and obsession's dark consequences. Their inventive nature fits seamlessly into Grim Hollow's gothic horror landscape when properly framed.

You could portray gnomish settlements as smoke-shrouded laboratory-towns where the line between genius and madness has long since blurred. Gnomish inventors might create clockwork creatures that develop unexpected sentience or explosives that produce supernatural side effects, like trapping souls or creating localized time distortions.

A reclusive gnome artificer makes an excellent recurring character—perhaps they supply the party with unusual gadgets while gradually revealing concerning signs of mental deterioration. Their workshop could feature increasingly disturbing experiments, with the gnome convinced they're on the verge of a breakthrough despite the horrific costs. Strange mechanical chimeras whir in shadowed corners, half-alive and suffering. Walls covered in obsessive calculations contain subtle errors that worsen over time. The gnome's inventions grow more effective yet ethically questionable—devices that harness pain, machines that whisper to users, or contraptions incorporating biological components harvested from missing townspeople. Most disturbing, the artificer genuinely believes their work serves a greater good, unable to recognize their own descent into mechanical madness.

entries.name: Halflings
entries: As humanity's relentless conquest of Etharis left countless civilizations broken in its wake, most peoples earned human wrath through resistance. Those who opposed the human tide were met with ruthless violence. Yet one people survived by embracing a different path—not through confrontation, but through calculated accommodation to the shadow of human dominance.

The blood-soaked Era of Expansion touched halflings differently than other folk. These diminutive beings place little worth in material treasures such as gold or gemstones, making them willing to surrender such trinkets as tribute to the better-armed humans who offered a veneer of protection. Though possessing quiet courage and indomitable spirit, halflings harbor no grand ambitions of nation-building. Their modest settlements seamlessly transformed into provisions for human empires and kingdoms—fertile fields feeding the forces that had subjugated them. Halflings infiltrated human society by assuming roles deemed valuable: merchants who smile while calculating odds of survival, advisors who whisper half-truths, educators who teach only what is permitted, and scholars who record history as the victors wish it remembered. Though halflings privately abhorred humanity's capacity for brutality, they maintained their silence—a calculated decision that ensured their continued existence through generations of human violence.

Halflings remain a tenacious yet outwardly peaceable folk. Most of these small-statured beings seek only tranquil corners in which to settle, far removed from the conflicts that periodically engulf Etharis. They cultivate appreciation for haunting melodies, exquisite cuisine, and moments of laughter when circumstances allow. They nurture a particular fascination for ancient lore, forbidden knowledge, and tales whispered in shadows. Yet this apparent docility masks an unexpected resilience. When cornered by threats they cannot evade through cunning or flight, halflings display a surprising ferocity. Chronicles of halfling adventurers who pursue hidden and dangerous knowledge rank among the most chilling tales recounted in hushed tones around their hearths.

For generations, halflings have methodically compiled the fragments of wisdom they gather into extensive private libraries devoted to particular subjects. Within these countless volumes reside centuries of accumulated knowledge concerning the peculiar interests and obsessions of these seemingly simple folk—domestic crafts that conceal secret purposes, the whispers of nature where no human ear listens, and histories unaltered by conquerors' blades.

entries.name: Using Halflings in Your Campaign
entries: Halflings offer Game Masters unique opportunities to explore themes of hidden resistance and desperate survival under oppression. Their outward docility conceals depths that can generate compelling narratives.

A quaint halfling village could be an idyllic community harboring dark secrets beneath a pastoral facade. A simple small-town brewery ran by a halfling family might serve as a waystation in an underground network that smuggles forbidden relics or helps fugitives escape persecution. Behind their disarming smiles, halfling innkeepers could maintain elaborate spy networks, collecting secrets from world-weary travelers while appearing harmless.

A halfling librarian makes an excellent recurring villain—perhaps they've spent decades meticulously gathering forbidden knowledge while projecting an image of harmless scholarly dedication. This character could control an extensive network of seemingly innocent halfling travelers who collect dark artifacts or identify potential sacrifices for ancient rituals. Their specialized library might contain grimoires bound in human skin, records of successful possessions, or maps to places where the veil between worlds has thinned. When confronted, this villain could reveal generations of resentment against human dominance, twisted into a plan to summon entities that will restore "proper balance" to Etharis—regardless of the cost to innocent lives.

entries.name: Humans
entries: Though ancient peoples like elves and dwarves claim the first kingdoms of Etharis as their legacy, humanity now casts its shadow across these lands. Humans infest every corner of the continent, from the bitter, unforgiving tundra of the North to the parched, cracked plains of the South. Historical records first note their emergence from the temperate woodlands in what would become the Bürach Empire—unremarkable creatures that the dominant races of Etharis dismissed as insignificant. Left unchecked, they multiplied with disquieting speed. Their adaptable nature and restless ambition eventually drove them beyond the boundaries of their birthplace.

The blood-soaked chapter of history known as the {@area Era of Expansion|038|x} is often portrayed as an unending tide of violence that swept across the continent for centuries, as humans spread like a contagion to every corner of Etharis. In truth, this era encompasses countless separate conflicts that erupted in the wake of human migration. Humans did not initially dispatch armies, but settlers—seemingly innocuous in their individual intrusions. They felled ancient trees from the Grove Maze to construct their dwellings. They journeyed to the distant reaches of {@book Valika|GrimHollowCG24|7} to escape the yoke of their own southern kingdoms. When they claimed territories already inhabited by other peoples, conflict became inevitable. By the time the mighty kingdoms of elves and dwarves recognized the creeping danger of human encroachment, the opportunity for containment had already passed. The slaughter that followed forever altered the face of Etharis, washing it in rivers of blood.

Humanity's terrible success stems from its adaptability. Their brief lifespans—mere moments compared to other peoples—cause their customs to wither quickly, preventing them from becoming ensnared by their own traditions. Every human culture bears the mark of another heritage in the territories they have claimed. In the duchies of the {@book Charneault Kingdom|GrimHollowCG24|5}, humans pay tribute to the Spirits of Nature as taught by the elves. The imposing fortresses and sprawling cities of the {@book Bürach Empire|GrimHollowCG24|3} incorporate dwarven stonework and architecture, appropriated rather than honored. In the present age, humans are acknowledged as the dominant people across Etharis, controlling vast swathes of land and sea. Yet their realms are far from uniform, each carrying its own blood-soaked history and amalgamation of peoples from other heritages—stories of conquest thinly veiled as cultural exchange.

Among all human vices, ambition proves the most pernicious. Humans maintain their stranglehold on positions of power within their societies. Many have laid claim to the High Throne of Altenheim, hungering to control the most formidable empire in existence. Known as the Era of Descent, the years following the {@area Era of Expansion|038|x} have witnessed the slow decay of each human domain. While common folk attribute this decline to the death of the gods, people of other heritages whisper that human hubris is the true cause. When no more realms remained to subjugate, humanity turned its greed, ambition, and violence inward—consuming itself as surely as it had consumed the lands of others.

entries.name: Using Humans in Your Campaign
entries: Humans provide Game Masters with perfect vessels for exploring Grim Hollow's themes of corruption, ambition, and fall from grace. As the dominant heritage across Etharis, humans represent both the mundane terror of political power and the supernatural horror of those willing to make dark bargains for advancement.

If venturing north to {@book Valika|GrimHollowCG24|7}, characters may meet a renegade longship captain. Commanding the longship {@i Frostfang} with ruthless efficiency and unexpected honor, the captain was born to a prestigious Valikan clan. She fled an arranged marriage by stealing a vessel and gathering a crew of outcasts, fugitives, and wanderers. Now she sails the treacherous waters between her raiding past and a potential future as a legitimate merchant-adventurer.

Unlike most Valikan raiders, the {@i Frostfang's} captain refuses to take captives, though she readily relieves southern ships of their treasures. A distinctive jagged silver scar runs across her right fist and occasionally gleams with faint blue light when her heart races—the result of a childhood encounter with coldfire.

entries.name: Rare Heritages
entries: The rare heritages of Etharis have long existed in seclusion, inhabiting the shadowed boundaries between more numerous peoples and their domains. Individuals of these scarcer lineages confront hardships familiar to all who walk Etharis, yet they frequently harbor perspectives on existence that the common folk find alien or unsettling.

entries.name: Dreamers
entries: History consumes itself in endless cycles. Long before humans embarked upon their blood-soaked expansion, before elves sang in ancient groves or dwarves chiseled their first runes into mountain halls, another civilization rose to impossible heights before plummeting into oblivion. Beneath present-day {@area Stehlenwald|032|x} once stood the heart of a nameless empire thought to have eclipsed any that followed. For centuries, this civilization existed as whispered myths among elves and dwarves. Legends claim their downfall came when mortals dared challenge divine authority. Other tales speak of an empire devoured whole during the first cataclysm of {@area The Aetheric War|34f|x}, leaving nothing but dust and silence.
But something lingered in the dark.

As humans spread across Etharis, forcing the Stehlenwald dwarves to carve deeper into their mountain sanctuaries, the desperate miners broke through chambers far below even the adamantine veins that became their salvation. These sealed vaults bore markings of inexplicable origin. When the ancient barriers were breached, forgotten enchantments dissolved, and the occupants of these lightless chambers stirred from a slumber that had lasted millennia.

In the final days before catastrophe consumed their civilization, a secretive order of mystics known as dreamers foresaw the approaching annihilation and conceived a desperate gambit for survival. Time flows differently in the realm of dreams, and these ancients wielded the forbidden magic of oneiromancy to sever themselves from time's merciless current. Suspended in the liminal space between reality and dreamscape, they could exist in perpetual slumber for as long as necessary, outlasting the cataclysm that would erase all trace of their kind from the waking world.

Their desperate plan succeeded, but at a terrible cost. Existing for countless ages within the dream realm wrought profound and irreversible changes upon their minds. Upon waking, the dreamers discovered they could no longer distinguish between dreams and memories—both faded like morning mist from their fractured consciousness. What emerged was a people stripped of their own history, carrying only fragmented images and dreamlike impressions of a civilization that may have been real or merely the collective fantasy of their dream-addled minds.

Now these dreamers struggle to find their place in a world never meant for them. Predominantly found in settlements throughout the Rock-Teeth Mountains, where dwarven excavations first breached their ancient chambers, they maintain an uneasy coexistence with their discoverers. Whatever their true origins, they have demonstrated remarkable adaptability, their minds quick and bodies surprisingly resilient in this alien realm. Their prolonged slumber infused them with preternatural energy, allowing them to labor beyond even the legendary endurance of their dwarven neighbors. Yet sleep remains the state in which dreamers feel most authentic, most at home. They can slip into sudden slumber whenever no immediate task demands their attention—their consciousness sliding back into the dream realm as if drawn by unseen currents to a place more real than the waking world they now inhabit.

entries.name: Using Dreamers in Your Campaign
entries: Dreamers offer unique storytelling opportunities centered around lost knowledge, fractured identity, and the boundary between reality and dreams. Their presence in the Rock-Teeth Mountains creates natural adventure hooks.

Mysterious dreamers could be guides to ancient ruins only they can partially remember, their fragmented recollections revealing locations of pre-cataclysmic artifacts with dangerous powers. Their ability to slip between waking and dreaming states might allow them to perceive threats others cannot—spectral entities, dimensional anomalies, or psychic disturbances.

A dreamer NPC might experience prophetic nightmares of coming disasters, their warnings dismissed as delusions until too late. Alternatively, dreamer communities could unwittingly serve as conduits for ancient entities attempting to return through the dream realm.

entries.name: Grudgels
entries: Ancient whispers murmur an age when orcs cast long shadows over the land. This heritage, rooted in the continent's frozen North, is described in hushed tales as having spread like a plague to terrorize other peoples. Orcs were said to be fearsome warriors with an insatiable thirst for bloodshed. Yet accounts of their hordes nearly subjugating all of Etharis almost certainly represent the fevered imaginings of terrified outsiders, distorted by generations of fearful retelling.

These same dark legends recount how the orcs abandoned their territories in a mass exodus—not fleeing from invaders or natural calamity, but heeding some mysterious ancestral summons to sail westward across the infinite sea. While historical records confirm that orcs indeed departed Etharis in a vast armada, a calling that would later echo in Thorgard av Holgar's mind a century afterward, the notion that an entire people could simply vanish beyond the horizon strains credulity. The truth, as ever in Etharis, festers beneath layers of half-truths and deliberate obfuscation.

Those who carry the bloodline of the orcs in present-day Etharis name themselves grudgels. Their appearance, at odds with the nightmarish depictions of their forebears, leads the ignorant to believe orcs and grudgels constitute entirely different peoples. Yet what truly divides them is merely the relentless march of time and the metamorphosis of culture across generations.

Grudgels remain imposing figures whose physical prowess has not diminished since the days when tales of orc warriors struck terror in the hearts of other folk. Yet even in the {@book Valikan Clans|GrimHollowCG24|7} where their numbers are greatest, grudgels demonstrate no greater inclination toward violence or the warrior's path than any other people. Among their ranks are skilled artisans, solitary wanderers, wielders of arcane forces, and those who commune with the night sky. They alone preserve the arcane craft of forging stryllum—an otherworldly substance formed when starlight congeals into glass through processes they guard jealously. Grudgels are industrious, peaceful among allies, and possess an uncanny ability to maintain composure in dire circumstances. Yet they remain perfectly capable of unleashing terrible violence upon those who threaten their existence or seek conflict in the merciless wilderness they call home.

Beyond the North, grudgels remain such uncommon sights across Etharis that most are more likely to encounter terrified rumors of their kind than an actual grudgel in flesh and blood. While those who serve as protectors or sell their martial skills may exploit such fearful whispers to their advantage, the single thread that appears to bind all grudgels is a profound reluctance to discuss or acknowledge the ancient legends of their orc ancestors. Whether this aversion stems from a desire to distance themselves from their forebears' violent legacy, or from some secret knowledge regarding the true reason for the orcs' disappearance from Etharis, remains a mystery they keep locked behind their tusked grimaces.

entries.name: Using Grudgels in Your Campaign
entries: Grudgels can be at the center of stories dealing with forgotten history and misconception in Etharis. Their imposing presence combined with many grudgels' unexpected calm demeanors creates a compelling character contradiction.

Consider introducing grudgel craftspeople who trade in mysterious stryllum artifacts with unusual properties—perhaps items that glow in proximity to supernatural threats or allow glimpses across dimensional boundaries. Their workshops might contain star charts mapping celestial alignments with ominous significance.

A grudgel NPC could serve as a stoic guide through northern territories, their knowledge of ancient paths suggesting familiarity with secrets predating recorded history. Their calm in the face of horror might stem from ancestral knowledge of worse things lurking beyond Etharis. As the party journeys with them, they might notice the grudgel performing subtle rituals at certain crossroads or placing carved stryllum tokens at seemingly random locations. When questioned, they offer only cryptic explanations about "keeping boundaries intact" or "honoring old agreements." This behavior suggests grudgels aren't merely descendants of orcs who left—they're guardians maintaining ancient wards against whatever drove their ancestors to flee.

entries.name: Laneshi
entries: Far beneath the churning waves that crash against Etharis's eastern shores lies the sunless realm of the Llana'Shi Empire, home to beings that surface-dwellers call laneshi. Hauntingly alien in appearance, these pallid humanoids with writhing tendrils of kelp-like hair trailing from their skulls are creatures born of the abyssal depths, possessed of the unnerving ability to commune with the flora and fauna of their lightless domain. Laneshi society functions through a worldview of stark, uncompromising absolutes and fundamental duality. Light or dark. Acceptance or banishment. Ally or adversary. Their culture writhes along the permeable boundary separating life from death. They converse freely with spirits seeking guidance from beyond mortality's veil and regard their eventual demise not with dread but as merely another state of being. Every element within laneshi society must occupy its predetermined place, their civilization built upon a rigid caste hierarchy that reflects this unwavering perspective.

The mystic caste encompasses all laneshi born as twins—a disturbingly common occurrence among their kind. The first-born twin is invariably inducted into the mystic caste, while its sibling is designated as their spiritual anchor. Through a potent necromantic ceremony, the second twin is sacrificed, its soul forcibly bound within the surviving sibling's flesh. Each member of the mystic caste therefore houses two souls within a single vessel—one living and one eternally trapped—granting them perception into the realm of spirits and enhancing their necromantic capabilities. Mystics preside over burial ceremonies, crafting, construction, historical documentation, and the preparation of sustenance. The more laborious responsibilities of this caste are performed with the assistance of reanimated corpses—a sight that would inspire madness in most surface-dwellers who value the sanctity of final rest.

The warrior caste comprises not only the martial forces of the laneshi but those charged with diplomatic relations, cultivation of food sources, and the rearing and instruction of young. This caste roughly doubles the mystic caste in size, organized as a meritocracy where exceptional deeds elevate one's standing within the hierarchy.

Laneshi warriors engage in perpetual conflict with other denizens of the deep. Yet simultaneously, the rulers of the Llana'Shi Empire have begun to direct their gaze toward the world above the waves for purposes yet unclear.
