Perhaps some ancient horror stirs in the lightless trenches of the ocean floor, driving the laneshi to seek desperate alliance with those who breathe air. Or perhaps there is terrible truth hidden within fearful whispers that these aquatic visitors have forged unholy pacts with primordial evils, and now the laneshi search for fresh territories to subjugate in service to an unspeakable master whose hunger knows no bounds.

entries.name: Using Laneshi in Your Campaign
entries: Laneshi characters are powerful tools for introducing cosmic horror, a core theme of Grim Hollow, to your campaign. Their alien culture and necromantic practices create natural tension when they interact with surface dwellers. Furthermore, the deep sea has always been a place as mysterious as the stars.

Your stories could feature laneshi emissaries suddenly arriving at coastal settlements to offer strange gifts or seek arcane knowledge. Their emotionless demeanor and casual use of undead servants would horrify locals, providing a distraction from much darker intentions.

A laneshi mystic makes an excellent recurring character—perhaps seeking surface allies against some greater deep-sea threat while struggling to understand surface taboos about death. Their dual-souled nature could manifest as sudden personality shifts or conversations with their imprisoned twin that others cannot hear. Players might witness the mystic arguing with themselves in moments of stress, their voice alternating between commanding and pleading tones. The imprisoned twin could possess knowledge unknown to the living host, revealing secrets at crucial moments or temporarily taking control in situations reminiscent of their sacrifice. This internal conflict might intensify as the mystic spends more time among surface dwellers, with the dead twin growing increasingly resentful of their eternal imprisonment or perhaps developing unexpected empathy for the surface world's perspective on death—creating a moral dilemma that challenges the mystic's rigid cultural assumptions.

entries.name: Ogresh
entries: The prevalent cultures of Etharis whisper myriad tales of the ogresh, though few have encountered these imposing beings in the flesh. Folklore portrays them as reclusive, sagacious figures who serve as wellsprings of arcane knowledge and counsel for neighboring communities. Countless legends describe protagonists receiving cryptic guidance from an ogresh before embarking upon perilous journeys, while others speak of court advisors with distinctive broad features who whisper secrets into royal ears. Such narratives contain kernels of truth, as ogresh often possess insights gleaned during extensive wanderings in their formative years. Yet even beyond these mythic accounts, those familiar with the ogresh frequently regard them with a mixture of fascination and unease—as aberrations that have somehow assumed humanoid form.

The true rarity of the ogresh stems from peculiarities of their biological nature. Young ogresh develop at an achingly slow pace, and throughout a protracted adolescence spanning decades, they are driven by an insatiable wanderlust that borders on compulsion. This primal urge forces them to roam in search of territories suitable for permanent habitation—areas possessing abundant natural resources, established settlements of sentient beings, and a conspicuous absence of other ogresh. Upon selecting a location, an ogresh transitions into the second phase of their existence, marked by a voracious, almost unnatural appetite and an increasingly sedentary lifestyle. A single ogresh could effortlessly consume the surplus provisions of an entire village, explaining the evolutionary necessity of their wandering instinct and solitary nature.

Given their dependence on other folk for sustenance, many ogresh naturally develop an uncanny mastery of social manipulation. Yet some who have gazed too long into their deep-set eyes maintain that the ogresh capacity to extract insights from others possesses a disquieting, almost supernatural quality—as though they perceive more than merely what is spoken, gleaning secrets others would never willingly divulge. Whether this represents an evolved survival adaptation or something more sinister remains a subject of hushed debate in settlements harboring these enigmatic giants.
Sometimes I forget my own strength. I was carrying a fat woolly sheep under each arm the other day, and I couldn't understand why the farmers at the market were staring.

entries.name: Using Ogresh in Your Campaign
entries: Ogresh let Game Masters play with the expectations many players have about giants. These giants are not brutish or single-minded and can make powerful, if alien, allies. Their imposing presence and unsettling insight, coupled with a generally friendly demeanor, creates opportunities to present Grim Hollow's themes of hidden darkness.

Consider portraying an ogresh as a village's respected advisor whose counsel, while effective, subtly serves their own appetite for resources. This advisor might have decades of experience manipulating local politics to ensure continued prosperity—and surplus food—while maintaining a benevolent facade. Their home could be filled with mementos and journals from their wandering years that contain valuable but cryptic information about distant threats.

For adventures, an ogresh settlement site might contain remnants of previous civilizations they've outlived, with disturbing implications about their longevity and the fate of communities that no longer produced sufficient resources. Ancient ogresh might possess knowledge of forgotten rituals or the true origins of arcane curses.

entries.name: Eldritch Heritages
entries: Grim Hollow's eldritch heritages represent people born of arcane workings, otherworldly powers, or the effects of ancient curses. These people are often feared and do not incorporate themselves into Etharis's societies. These heritages are often found well outside of city walls, making a hard life for themselves in the darkness.

entries.name: Accursed
entries: The accursed stand as the most enigmatic and scarce among all heritages that walk Etharis—for each accursed exists as a bloodline unto themselves. Whether forged through aberrant magical convergence, delivered into the world through yawning planar rifts or shimmering extradimensional doorways, or embodying the final vestiges of a lineage presumed erased from history's pages, an accursed being possesses traits that defy categorization, manifestations of a singular existence unbound by conventional heritage.

This haunted designation stems not necessarily from their origins in malevolent enchantment, witch's compact, corrupted arcane formula, or other traditional interpretations of "curse" within the realms of sorcery. Rather, "accursed" reflects the grim reality of how the wider populace of Etharis perceives such individuals—as walking omens, living aberrations whose very existence violates the natural order. Only those rare souls who venture beyond fearful whispers to comprehend the accursed's true nature might glimpse the person beneath the unsettling exterior. This heritage exists to liberate imagination, encouraging the creation of characters whose essence transcends established bloodlines while remaining tethered to Etharis's bleak tapestry.

Unlike other heritages, the accursed rarely represent a collective people or shared culture, instead standing as solitary anomalies in a world unprepared for their existence. One such being might manifest as a halfling-sized corvid possessed of human speech, hatched from a petrified basilisk egg during the witching hour of a blood moon. Throughout the breadth of Etharis, no other creature shares their peculiar composition, their chosen heritage traits reflecting this singular nature. Another might embody the form and capabilities of a lizardfolk—a heritage absent from Etharis's recognized bloodlines. Whether this accursed represents a solitary wanderer from some forgotten scaled enclave lurking within the fetid depths of the Black Mire, or the offspring of elven parents subjected to a malevolent sorcerer's spite, their selection of heritage traits carves out a unique narrative within a world that instinctively recoils from their presence.

entries.name: Using Accursed in Your Campaign
entries: The accursed invite players and Game Masters to explore what it means to be truly alone in a world that fears the unknown. Many accursed may seek vengeance on a world that hates them simply for how they were born while others may find purpose in their situation and seek to change Etharis for the better.

The appearance of an accursed in a remote village might trigger superstitious panic, witch hunts, or desperate rituals to ward off perceived omens. These reactions create immediate tension as players must navigate between protecting the innocent accursed and addressing the legitimate fears of locals who may have experienced previous supernatural horrors.

Your campaign might feature a traveling carnival known for showcasing "living curiosities," including an accursed known as the Ashen Songbird, whose haunting voice produces melodies thought impossible. This slender figure appears mostly humanoid but with skin that shifts between translucent gray and wisps of smoke, revealing glimpses of organs composed of smoldering embers. Their eyes glow amber, and when they sing, listeners hear multiple voices in impossible harmony.

The performer wears ornate manacles inscribed with binding runes disguised as decorative bracelets, preventing them from straying beyond the carnival grounds. The carnival master introduces them as a being born from the ashes of a great fire that consumed a village, though the tale changes regularly.

Between performances, the accursed remains confined to a reinforced wagon with a special chimney to vent the smoke that occasionally billows from their mouth. Fellow performers maintain their distance—some from fear, others from guilt.

entries.name: Arisen
entries: The arisen exist in hushed, trembling discourse throughout Etharis, all too frequently condemned as abhorrent anomalies beyond trust or salvation. Although they appear humanoid, each arisen stands as a singular construct born of arcane manipulation, obscure science, or the unholy marriage of both disciplines. Arisen are incredibly rare and often live lonely lives but are most often found in {@book Ostoya|GrimHollowCG24|4} and {@area Stehlenwald|032|x}, where dark arcane experimentation is commonplace.

No arisen enters the world through natural birth. They are not the undead, nor creatures restored from death's embrace by divine intervention. Rather, each arisen is meticulously assembled and granted the spark of consciousness, typically by the hands of a creator whose motives remain their own. Arisen are indeed thinking beings, possessing distinct personalities, intellects, and lived experiences like all sentient creatures. Yet the question that haunts their existence remains—do they harbor souls within their fabricated forms? This metaphysical quandary plagues each arisen throughout their unnatural existence.

Arisen bodies consist predominantly of organic material. They are not mere automatons, though many bear mechanical components to varying degrees. One might possess a single limb of articulated brass and steel, while another's vital organs float suspended in a network of fluid-filled vessels. Some arisen feature exposed wiring penetrating their flesh, connected to arcane power sources fused to their spines, while others appear deceptively ordinary save for telling scars and the enchanted gemstones embedded within their eye cavities.

Some arisen are entirely fabricated, constructed according to precise specifications or esoteric designs. Others begin their existence through seemingly benign experiments to replace failing organs or severed limbs, with successive procedures inexorably eroding all connection to their original selves. Still others emerge from living beings who perished or suffered catastrophic injury before being "reconstituted." Regardless of the process that spawned them, an arisen's creation typically proves traumatic—manifesting as fractured recollections of arcane laboratories and the first flickering moments of awareness, or as the persistent nightmares that constitute the only remnants of a former existence.

Their scarcity among Etharis's populations and the grim rumors that cling to them like shrouds often force arisen into isolation. Yet not all succumb to nihilism in the face of their bleak origins and lonely existence. From that very trauma, many develop a penetrating self-awareness that fuels an insatiable curiosity and a uniquely profound perspective on matters of existence, mortality, and their precarious place within a world that regards them with fear and suspicion.

entries.name: Using Arisen in Your Campaign
entries: Arisen characters bring classic body horror to your Grim Hollow campaigns. Their unnatural existence raises profound questions about personhood and invokes visceral discomfort. In a world full of horrors, NPCs may rightfully fear an arisen, providing a great opportunity to create tension. Additionally, you could present a character arc wherein an NPC's worldview is challenged by a particularly humane arisen.

Shadowy academies might secretly produce arisen as experiments in mortality, with escaped specimens becoming either allies or antagonists in your campaign. A brilliant but amoral physician operating from a hidden laboratory could create arisen servants using parts harvested from fresh graves, drawing the party's attention when local cemeteries are desecrated.

The party may come into conflict with an arisen physician-wizard who works to improve the lives of other arisen. They have altered their appearance to be somewhat more accepted by the commonfolk and use the wealth they have amassed to lobby for and support their fellow arisen. Their medical work is dedicated to making life more comfortable for arisen.

However, their compassion toward arisen contrasts horrifically with their attitude toward "the soft." Viewing humans, elves, and others as merely raw materials for experimentation, the arisen doctor abducts subjects under cover of darkness. These unwilling donors provide components for their procedures, their screams muffled by thick stone walls.

When confronted with their crimes, they display genuine confusion, unable to comprehend why society values "soft" lives while rejecting the arisen. This moral blindness makes them tragically sympathetic yet unquestionably monstrous, creating difficult ethical questions for characters who discover their work.

entries.name: Dhampir
entries: A dhampir bears the weight of a terrible inheritance—a mortal half-damned by vampiric taint, existing in the agonizing threshold between life and undeath. These cursed beings carry the sinister aspects of their undead progenitors, yet their lingering mortality spares them from a vampire's most crippling weaknesses. Like the predators whose blood flows within their veins, dhampir hunger for the crimson essence of the living, though they are not wholly enslaved by this craving if their resolve proves sufficient to withstand the constant whispers of thirst.

Invariably born from other heritages, dhampir manifest with extreme rarity and display vast diversity in appearance and disposition. The sole trait binding all dhampir is the grim legacy of their unnatural conception. True vampires embody death itself, mimicking vitality by twisting corpses into undead servants or spreading their affliction to spawn more of their kind. Yet vampires cannot naturally reproduce or create anything genuinely alive. Thus, dhampir typically emerge from some manner of necromantic aberration—a child at death's threshold revived through forbidden arts, an alchemist blending herbs with vampire ichor, or a desperate attempt to reverse vampirism gone terribly awry.

Regardless of their origins, most dhampir endure an existence rooted in profound trauma and pervasive isolation. Common folk who fear these half-bloods view them as blood-thirsting abominations, despite the fact that most sustain themselves primarily through ordinary nourishment. Simultaneously, pure-blooded vampires despise them as diluted, imperfect shadows of true vampiric power. A dhampir's life thus becomes an endless search for belonging, whether struggling to assimilate into mortal society or attempting to earn approval from their blood-drinking ancestors.

A dhampir who chooses to dwell among mortals often suffers relentless guilt. Their craving for blood may grow overwhelmingly intense as they desperately cling to their humanity and capacity for compassion. Dhampir are branded as monsters by the superstitious masses. Yet even those rare mortals who embrace a dhampir as family can never truly comprehend their internal battle against the predator that lurks beneath their skin, waiting for a moment of weakness to emerge.

Other dhampir surrender to their darker nature, embracing their bloodlust without reservation. Some even contend for position within {@book Ostoya's|GrimHollowCG24|4} vampire nobility. Despite lacking conventional parentage, a dhampir may form a perverse familial bond with the vampire they consider their sire. Vampires who exploit this relationship gain a valuable servant capable of walking in daylight and integrating more seamlessly with mortal communities. A dhampir in such circumstances might serve as spy, steward, or herald to their master. Yet they remain acutely aware that they shall never attain positions of genuine authority within vampire hierarchy—forever caught between two worlds, belonging fully to neither.

entries.name: Using Dhampirs in Your Campaign
entries: Dhampirs embody the quintessential gothic horror theme of the monster within. Their dual nature creates perfect story vehicles for exploring inner darkness and societal rejection. On the other hand, dhampirs could be insurrectionists, looking to overthrow their vampire masters using the strength granted to them from the same bloodline.

Whispered rumors of a dhampir apothecary selling miracle cures might draw players to a reclusive figure whose medicines contain traces of their own blood—effective but potentially addictive. Those who consume these remedies develop subtle connections to the dhampir, experiencing glimpses of their memories or cravings.

Noble vampire families might secretly employ dhampir "cousins" to protect their interests during daylight hours, while vampire elders dispatch dhampir agents to investigate threats without risking themselves. These agents walk a dangerous line, neither trusted by their masters nor accepted by those they infiltrate.

entries.name: Disembodied
entries: To pierce the veil between worlds remains the forbidden aspiration of countless arcane practitioners, and nowhere did that ambition come closer to fruition than within the vanished city of Ulmyr's Gate. Established in the {@book Bürach Empire|GrimHollowCG24|3} by a cabal of audacious wizards who bristled against the constraints imposed by imperial authorities, Ulmyr's Gate offered unfettered study to all mages who sought refuge behind its walls. The Great College dedicated to arcane pursuit quickly transformed into a sanctuary for practitioners of all disciplines from across Etharis, ushering in a brief, brilliant epoch of magical advancement within its boundaries.

This luminous age collapsed in a single night of hubris, when the founding mages of Ulmyr's Gate attempted a ritual of unprecedented scope designed to cleave the veil and establish a permanent threshold to the {@area Ethereal Plane|344|x}. Instead, their spellwork rent an immense wound in the fabric of reality, and in one catastrophic moment, the entire city was cast into the void between worlds. Ulmyr's Gate and all its inhabitants were presumed obliterated. The disaster precipitated another savage inquisition against arrogant arcanists who meddled in forces mortals were never meant to comprehend. Soon after, the empire's attention turned elsewhere, and the tragedy faded from memory.

Years later, unsettling accounts began to circulate of spectral figures glimpsed in the region where Ulmyr's Gate once stood. These haunting entities appeared strangely insubstantial or incomplete—witnesses reported that they would dissolve into nothingness as abruptly as they manifested. Gradually, the terrible truth emerged: these wretched souls were survivors of the Ethereal Rift, now trapped in the liminal space between dimensions, desperately striving to maintain their tenuous connection to the Material Plane.

Those former denizens of lost Ulmyr's Gate who have summoned the will to return to the mortal realm have been designated "the disembodied" by the scholars who have examined their condition. These rare individuals have abandoned or escaped their former home, traversing the Ethereal Rift to wander the mortal world. Though they bear semblance to their former selves, they are simultaneously more and less than human—entities existing between realities, perpetually haunted by memories of their city's annihilation, of which they seldom if ever speak. Their eyes reflect glimpses of the void between worlds, and in moments of distress, their forms may flicker or partially fade—reminders that their grasp on corporeal existence remains forever precarious.

entries.name: Using Disembodied in Your Campaign
entries: The haunting presence of disembodied beings offers unique horror elements perfect for Grim Hollow campaigns. Their fractured existence bridges physical and ethereal realities, creating encounters where the laws of nature bend unpredictably.

Small rural communities near the former site of Ulmyr's Gate might experience periodic "bleeds" when the barrier between worlds thins. During these events, buildings partially transpose with ethereal counterparts, landmarks appear that aren't there by daylight, and disembodied visitors manifest seeking objects or people they've lost. These incidents create perfect investigation scenarios where players must piece together fragmented realities.

Ambitious nobles might secretly capture disembodied to extract ethereal essence for arcane experiments. These nobles believe studying these beings could unlock the secret to transcending death itself, creating unsettling scenarios where seemingly benevolent patrons have sinister motivations.

The ruins of Ulmyr's Gate itself exist simultaneously in multiple planes, with different areas accessible depending on atmospheric conditions, magical resonances, or specific rituals. Players venturing into these ruins might find themselves shifting between planes unexpectedly, encountering fragments of the city's final moments frozen in time.

A disembodied arcanist might serve as a reluctant mentor to magic-wielding characters, offering knowledge of forbidden spells while exhibiting disturbing behaviors—disappearing mid-conversation, speaking to entities others cannot perceive, or unconsciously manipulating reality around them during emotional moments.

entries.name: Downcast
entries: At the culmination of {@area The Gods' End|351|x}, thousands of divine souls fell from the sky like burning stars. A host of celestial beings were thrown from the heavens, condemned to live in the mortal realms among the people of Etharis, bereft of divine powers they once held.

Without their deities, each of the {@area Arch Seraphs|352|x} took their patron's mantle and attempted to impose their will upon the world cast into disarray. The Arch Seraphs were the most powerful divine beings that remained who still possessed a semblance of their divine power following Gods' End. But an angel is not a god. The Arch Seraphs cannot embody all aspects of the divine domains they once served, with some focusing on a narrow set of virtues. Others cast off their divine grace to instead become feared Arch Daemons who cultivated mortal vice. Those divine souls who fell from the heavens to endure mortal lives are called the downcast.

Many downcast are no more. For some, the sickness and despair they suffered after their fall from grace was too much to bear. Some of those who remain still serve the Arch Seraphs seeking to recover the divine order that was lost. Others have accepted their new mortal existance, ignoring the divine host to which they once belong. Still others have chosen to follow the Arch Daemons, embittered by their fall and gaining pleasure by spreading fear and destruction.

Those who were cast down from the heavens still retain a small portion of their previous angelic form. This might manifest in their eyes, which still faintly glow with divine light. For others, faded Celestial runes still mark their once perfect skin. Those who have embraced an Arch Daemon's corruption might present subtle fiendish traits such as cracked skin, devilish horns, or a faint scent of sulfur that follows in their wake.

entries.name: Using Downcast in Your Campaign
entries: Downcast rarely settle into communities with others of their kind. For some, seeing the constant reminder of who they once were in the mortal form of others is too painful. Some prefer the company of true mortal beings, soaking in everything that it means to live in the constant pain and doubt of mortality. Others still seek to dwell in the realm of sin and debauchery, and they don't want the judging glares of their peers haunting their revelry.

Downcast characters are affected in some way by the loss of their immortality and divinity—one cannot simply forget when they were once immortal and imbued with divine power. That loss might be crushing, an excruciating loss that leaves an unfillable void. The loss might be a challenge, a call to prove one's worth even though the tools to achieve glory are much duller than before. It might be a call to oblivion, a tacit permission to rush headlong toward a void that is waiting to be filled with your mortal essence and weakened, but potentially immortal, soul.

Other mortals may not notice a downcast's former divine presence unless they look closely. Once they recognize what they are—or more precisely what they were—others might look at the downcast with a variety of emotions and attitudes. Pity is common, as mortals try to grapple with the loss they can only imagine must have been endured. Hope can shine through others' eyes towards a downcast's direction, as they are reminded that if gods once oversaw their lives, they might again someday, but fear is never far behind. Divine powers once wielded for good may still be adrift in the world, untethered from their once altruistic forms, and perhaps, now used for ill. There's always something else, some emotion that is unknown and unnamable but that nevertheless reminds the downcast that they are different, and always will be.
He walked into the tavern, and it was like an angel fell from the sky.

entries.name: Wechselkind
entries: A mother wakes to a faint sound in the midnight hours and anxiously inspects her slumbering infant. All appears undisturbed. Yet weeks later, an unseen enchantment dissolves and a nightmare is unveiled: her true child has been spirited away by the fey, a wechselkind abandoned in its place. For the faerie are ancient, callous entities, unchanging through centuries—and nothing ignites their terrible fascination and bitter envy more than the malleable, radiant essence of a young mortal child.

A wechselkind is a construct meticulously fashioned from wood, clay, and porcelain fragments in the semblance of a small child, animated through inscrutable faerie enchantments and shrouded in illusion that renders them indistinguishable from the stolen mortal infant. Once that glamour dissipates and the cruel deception stands revealed, a wechselkind is typically banished by the abducted child's family, if not destroyed outright in grief-stricken rage. On rare occasions, a family might show mercy to the forlorn creature and attempt to nurture them, only to discover that while their mind develops naturally, a wechselkind remains eternally imprisoned in the unchanging form of a childlike doll—a grotesque mockery of growth and maturation.

Whether grudgingly tolerated or violently rejected, most wechselkind eventually find themselves existing as societal outcasts, forced to survive through whatever means their artificial nature permits. The lingering echoes of their faerie glamour allow a wechselkind to disguise themselves for brief intervals, either as the child they replaced or as a diminutive adult such as a halfling, gnome, or other being of comparable stature. With minimal physical requirements for survival, a wechselkind can drift between settlements, observing the mortals they encounter with covetous eyes, eternally searching for acceptance that perpetually eludes them.

With the devastating spread of the Weeping Pox across Etharis, many wechselkind have emerged from self-imposed exile. Their immunity to disease enables them to assist healers in plague-ravaged communities, earning a measure of respect—or even begrudging admiration—from those capable of seeing beyond their unsettling origins. Nevertheless, even wechselkind who achieve conditional acceptance among mortals typically remain vigilant, haunted by the certainty that once their utility expires, they will inevitably face abandonment once more—eternal children with ancient eyes, never truly belonging to either the mortal world or the faerie realm that created and discarded them.

entries.name: Using Wechselkind in Your Campaign
entries: Wechselkind inject profound tragedy into Grim Hollow's horror landscape. Their dual nature as both victims and unwitting agents of fey mischief creates morally complex scenarios that challenge player assumptions.

Rural villages might harbor old superstitions about wechselkind, leading to witch hunts when children fall mysteriously ill. Players must determine if an accused wechselkind is responsible or merely a convenient scapegoat for human cruelty. These investigations reveal how fear transforms communities into monsters more terrible than those they hunt.

The party may come across a dilapidated workshop at a village's edge housing a wechselkind artisan whose exquisite, handcrafted toys display uncanny lifelikeness. Rejected decades ago, they create these playthings as gifts for children who can experience the childhood the toymaker will never know.

The wechselkind may seek help bringing their creations to market, unable to venture into town without facing persecution. Their toys—dolls with glass eyes that seem to follow movement, music boxes that play melodies no one taught them, and wooden animals that occasionally move when unobserved—sell remarkably well despite their unsettling quality.

Unknown to their creator, faint fey magic is woven into the toys, causing children who possess them to experience vivid dreams of the faerie realm. Some begin sleepwalking toward the woods, others develop sudden personality changes, and a few report "invisible friends" who whisper secrets.

entries.name: Wulven
entries: Throughout Etharis, from wilderness edge to settled heartland, in woodland hovels and isolated homesteads, dread of lycanthropy burrows deep into mortal hearts. Yet few ever comprehend that the towering stranger they glimpsed on the fog-shrouded trade road at twilight, the forest recluse who materialized without warning to caution of lurking perils ahead, or the enigmatic druidic devotee guarding an ancient spring harbor no such terror. These beings have already been marked by the lycanthropic taint, and have embraced the primal gifts it bestows upon their blood.

Wulven are the natural offspring or descendants of one cursed with lycanthropy, inheriting not the curse in its entirety but instead bearing its feral essence within their veins. Those born with this bloodline are most commonly associated with werewolves in the fearful minds of common folk, inspiring the name thrust upon them. Yet wulven are equally likely to descend from werebears, wererats, wereravens, or more obscure and terrible forms of lycanthropy that haunt the darkest corners of Etharis.

The diluted strain of lycanthropy flowing through a wulven's lineage means they cannot assume animal or hybrid forms like their accursed forebears. They remain as composed and sociable as any folk, suffering neither loss of control nor mindless bloodthirst beneath the full moon's pale light. Nevertheless, the untamed nature of the beast they carry within their blood drives most wulven toward lives of isolation and the hushed solitude of wilderness realms.

Wulven embody the mysticism of the primal world rather than its brutality. Where they traverse forest and untamed scrubland, their wild countenance and solitary habits often lead them to be mistaken for fey spirits, druids, or true lycanthropes. Many forge existences as members of secretive druidic circles or as silent guardians of small, sequestered communities. Some serve as emissaries or champions of the fey courts, though the stain of cursed magic within wulven blood causes certain fey beings to regard them with suspicion. Ultimately, most wulven exist as perpetual outsiders, wary around strangers for fear their solitary disposition and subtly bestial features—elongated canines glimpsed during rare smiles, eyes that catch the light in unnerving ways, the occasional low growl that accompanies moments of stress—will be misinterpreted as signs of the very curse they have been spared, yet can never fully escape.

entries.name: Using Wulven in Your Campaign
entries: Wulven bridge the gap between civilization and wilderness in Etharis, creating perfect intermediaries for stories exploring humanity's relationship with nature's darker aspects.

Villages on the edge of forests in {@book Ostoya|GrimHollowCG24|4}, {@book Valika|GrimHollowCG24|7}, and {@book Charneault Kingdom|GrimHollowCG24|5} might rely on wulven guides who navigate territories where true lycanthropes hunt. These guides walk a precarious line—valued for their knowledge yet always suspected when villagers disappear. This suspicion intensifies during full moons, despite the wulven's immunity to lunar influence, creating dramatic irony as players witness witch-hunt mentality forming against the very protectors keeping true monsters at bay.

Mountain passes guarded by solitary wulven sentinels offer safe passage in exchange for small offerings or news from distant lands. These gatekeepers maintain ancient traditions, marking territorial boundaries between human settlements and primal forces best left undisturbed. The wulven might warn travelers away from certain paths not because of physical danger, but because those routes lead to places where reality thins, allowing influence from {@area Primordial Realms|345|x} to seep through.

Druidic circles might include wulven members who serve as intermediaries between the circle and local communities, appearing during festivals to perform rituals ensuring continued harmony. Their presence creates tension—does their magic protect the village from supernatural threats, or are they subtly preparing the community for some darker purpose guided by ancient woodland powers?

data.name: Chapter 11: Factions of Etharis
entries: People of similar beliefs and goals come together to increase their power. This is true in any world, and Etharis is no different. Many organizations and factions hold sway in Grim Hollow campaigns.

The major factions of Etharis have some striking similarities, regardless of their motivations and goals. They have come together after enduring tribulations or in response to threats, and overtly or covertly they permeate every stratum of society, whether they hold sway in one nation, multiple nations, or throughout all of Etharis.

All of the factions discussed here—and so many of the lesser factions in Etharis—are driven by one overarching goal: power. Sometimes this power comes from the collected magical abilities of its members. Other times it comes from the knowledge and influence that the group's leaders and members can bring to bear. The power may even come from the fear that the faction inspires in those it controls. Regardless, it's this power that makes Etharis's factions so formidable.

With power, of course, comes the will to use that power. Many times, this power works on behalf of the characters, and for society as a whole. The characters may have a symbiotic relationship with these factions: the factions can offer the characters a small piece of that power, as well as a sense of direction and goals to achieve.

On the other hand, each of these factions may abuse that power, making them institutions that the characters must oppose. Just as the characters in a Grim Hollow campaign must constantly be on the guard against their own corruption as they gain power, these factions have the capability to perform and propagate great evil in the name of doing good.

entries.name: Factions in Brief
entries: The following are quick descriptions of each of the factions illuminated in great detail later.

items.name: Augustine Trading Company
entries: Amid the splendors of {@book Morencia|GrimHollowCG24|9} sits an organization that guards its treasure as jealously as a dragon defends its hoard. The Augustine Trading Company is an ancient and venerable institution that maintains a presence in every country, from the southern reaches of {@book Castinella|GrimHollowCG24|6} to the misty city of Castalore. The Trading Company puts profit above people, meaning that missions often end with the members having to decide if they are going to do the proper thing or the expedient, profitable thing. Those who choose the latter are rewarded. Those who chose the former find themselves named enemies of the Augustine hierarchy.

items.name: Company of Free Swords
entries: An ancient and elite mercenary company, the Free Swords have tipped the scales of war for many nations and provinces. The company includes warriors, wizards, priests, druids, rogues, and spies among its ranks. It has a branch in nearly every corner of the continent. They have maintained a code of honor since their founding: stay loyal to the company, be honest in your dealings, and take vengeance on those who betray you or your comrades. They live simple lives free of excess, spending most of their time honing their fighting skills.

items.name: Ebon Syndicate
entries: The Ebon Syndicate is an organization with a finger in every major criminal trade. Assassination, kidnapping, drug trafficking, theft, and the black market all fall within their purview. The Ebon Syndicate prefers to keep its actions and schemes in the shadows, especially in areas where they may not have the strongest foothold. In these cases, the Syndicate tends to hire mercenaries and freelance charlatans to do the most dangerous work.

items.name: Thaumaturge
entries: The Grand Thaumatic Order of archmages is a myth. Its existence has been a rumor throughout history, yet most commonfolk regard it as a fairy tale intended to frighten the conspiratorial. The truly downtrodden look to the Thaumaturge as a symbol of rebellion against authoritarian and intolerant regimes.

items.name: Watchers of the Faithful
entries: Composed of priests, paladins, chaplains, and other clergy, the Watchers of the Faithful have dedicated themselves to preserving the old faiths in a new form. They follow the Eternal Dogma: revere the {@area Arch Seraphs|352|x}, follow their laws as handed down by the High Priest, and destroy all that is unholy and evil in their sight—through any means necessary.

items.name: Arcanist Inquisition
entries: The Arcanist Inquisition is a religious order that hunts down arcanists to prevent them from using their powers to threaten the established political and social structures they prefer. They patrol the provinces of Castinella and beyond, spreading their teachings about the dangers of magic, stirring mistrust of arcanists among the local populations.

items.name: Crimson Court
entries: The sun-starved nation of {@book Ostoya|GrimHollowCG24|4} is ruled by a cabal of vampire nobles called the {@area Crimson Court|05c|x}. This loose system of nobility and feudal servants oversees every aspect of the nation, from the highest noble decrees to the lowest elements of criminality. As the ruling body of a nation, the Crimson Court has a great deal of power, but also a great deal of responsibility.

items.name: Monster Hunter Guilds
entries: Monster hunting is an ancient trade. What separated hunters from common warriors was curiosity—a genuine desire to understand one's enemy. Knowledge rose as the monster hunter's most lethal weapon. Recent times have seen an increase in organized hunting guilds. Killing monsters has become a corporatized trade as hunters band together and share knowledge, resources, and contracts. Some guilds have arranged themselves into a formalized structure like a mercenary company, with captains accepting quests and assigning them to teams of hunters.

items.name: Morbus Doctore
entries: The Morbus Doctore was created to combat {@area the Weeping Pox|448|x}, The members are easy to spot: boots and gloves dried with blood, strange coats and satchels hung with vials, and frightful masks meant to protect them from the disease. Established in the city of {@book Liesech|GrimHollowCG24|8}, they now operate in other major metropolitan areas of Etharis—looking for newly infected people to treat.

items.name: Order of Dawn
entries: The Order of Dawn formed when Ostoyans fled the rule of their vampire overlords and settled in the sympathetic province of Raevo. These dissidents occupied a fort outside of Castalore, where they became a militia fully committed to the destruction of the undead and the liberation of the rest of Ostoya. Members are trained and battle-ready, prepared to give their lives for their cause.

items.name: Prismatic Circle
entries: The Prismatic Circle is an order of druids who believe that only war and bloodshed can keep the world safe from the reawakening of {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}, the Prismatic Wyrm. Several of the {@book Valikan Clans|GrimHollowCG24|7} follow their teachings, turning their home region of Thrull into an area of constant warfare and occasional human sacrifice.

entries.name: Tips for Using Grim Hollow Factions
entries: Regardless of whether the factions mentioned here are global or regional; economic, social, political, or religious; beneficent or malicious, they all have the capacity to have a great influence over the adventures that the characters in your campaigns undertake. The trick for using these factions to their greatest effect is subtlety and a light touch.

Every one of them could be a patron organization for the characters, asking them to perform important, and possibly even life-saving, tasks. At the same time, each of them is capable of perpetuating great evil, whether in a mistaken striving for good or through purely diabolical motives.

The most resounding and impactful use of these organizations is not to use them as monolithic machines that churn only in the direction of ultimate good and pure evil. Likewise, it is suggested that you don't simply paint all members of a faction with the same brush. One member of a group could seem benevolent as they hire adventurers to deal with a terrible problem that terrorizes innocent people. Then, as the characters get more invested in the goals of that individual, they could realize the person is the face of a group with less-than-noble intentions.

It should only be after several interactions with members of a faction that the characters even realize that there is a larger faction behind the contacts. Only after foiling the plans of a local den of thieves might the characters realized they've actually struck a blow to the Ebon Syndicate, and now they'll have to deal with that group.

entries.title: {@i Success in all ventures and circumstances requires the unhindered flow of goods and services.}

items: A bastion of free trade, champion of education, and at the forefront of innovation, the venerable Augustine Trading Company is everywhere in Etharis. Behind its shining image is a ruthless corporation with a draconic thirst for coin. From its headquarters in {@book Morencia|GrimHollowCG24|9}, the Company's tendrils reach every corner of the known world, and even some corners long forgotten.

Founded by the disgraced clergyman Manoch Augustus, the Company originated as a local weapon distributor and evolved to become a global powerbroker. Though nominally headquartered in Morencia, the Company is found anywhere shipping lanes, caravans, or black-market networks do business.

Administered by a council of five called the {@i Montarri} lead by the financier Kolyhs "The Blazing Brain" Papinian, the Company controls various shell companies and governments via proxy ownership, debt-leverage, or economic entrapment. It funds wars, builds cities, collapses markets, and breeds monsters for battlefield use. It even maintains elite universities to develop the next generation of talent. The Company always takes the long view.

To the public, the Augustine Trading Company is a necessary engine of civilization, but in truth, it is a gilded machine of exploitation creating an empire bound by ledgers and blood. Adventurers who serve its interests are well-paid but expendable, and those who cross it face economic ruin... or worse. To many, the Company isn't just business—it's destiny.

entries.name: History
entries: Manoch Augustus began life as a priest. While in the priesthood, he came to work in the Secretariat of Coin, managing the church's funds. There he discovered his talent for growing funds through unscrupulous means. When his peers did not applaud his financial acumen, he left the church amid scandal. With few options left, he turned his talents to commerce. He tried to learn the error of his ways and started several honest businesses, but all failed. Turning to what he was good at, Augustus found a more profitable profession: the weapon trade.

Using the profits from the arms trade, Augustus diversified into lending, encouraging the wealthy to offer land as their collateral, knowing most nobility did not comprehend the ruinous terms he offered. Foreclosing on these properties after his clients defaulted on his predatory loans, Augustus was able to enter into yet another lucrative business: real estate. Land acquired was not sold but rather rented to tenets who worked it in exchange for a share of their labors, which were in turn sold. Building a pyramid of wealth, where the fruits of one business created the next, the Augustine Trading Company became the wealthiest faction in Etharis.

But it was not until an orphan turned banker became Treasurer, that the Company grew more powerful than most governments. Kolyhs "The Blazing Brain" Papinian has grown the Company to become the dominate economic force in Etharis; so powerful that most governments no longer dare cross it. The company has expanded into every trade and industry including warfare, security, lending, food production, medicine, construction, labor, crafts, resource extraction, automatons, and education. It doesn't matter if it is legal; the Company stocks it and sells it somewhere.

entries.name: Secret Projects
entries: Ever fearful of stagnation, Kolyhs urges a system where the Company trains academics at a host of corporate universities called Augustine Centers, and the most promising are then directed into secret research programs to develop the next profitable product or service. The most famous is Project Warbreed, which creates lycanthropic super soldiers and continues to revolutionize by crossing these thralls with other cursed creatures like vampires, creating further useful mutations. Of late, interest has grown in the arisen. If an employee dies and returns, do they still have rights, or is their now reanimated body owned by the Company?

Warbreed is not the only secret project. There are dozens. Project Fumarialis designs new magical and black powder Weapons of war. Magical automatons that look more and more like humanoids or animals are being created to embed spies and assassins in a target's city or home. Project Mandragora uses alchemy to create performance-enhancing Potions and drugs. Not only can they endow the average guard with the strength of ten men and the senses of a bloodhound, the Company can also lace the supplement with addictive substances, so guards stay loyal to get their high. Mandragora also seeks to treat, not cure, the symptoms of many ailments, allowing the Company a steady stream of revenue from desperately ill patients.

entries.name: Market Adaptation
entries: The Augustine Trading Company tailor their business plan to make them indispensable, no matter the local laws or customs. If a trade is looked down upon, such as arcane items in {@book Castinella|GrimHollowCG24|6}, they emphasize other goods, or bribe local governments to allow their trade to go unimpeded. If one {@book Valikan Clan|GrimHollowCG24|7} raids them, they bring vital resources and wonders from the south to another, emphasizing that trade can only continue if their customer can keep the trade routes open and safe. There is always a market, and always someone desperate, to buy from the Company.

This leaves many of the other factions with a conundrum. The Arcanist Inquisition and the Watchers of the Faithful generally despise what the Company stands for and the methods it uses, but they also need the goods only the Company can provide. They generally ignore the Company when they can but come down heavily if they catch it violating any laws. The Ebon Syndicate sees the Company as a competitor, but also a kindred spirit that it can deal with. One season might see the Syndicate purchasing narcotics from the Company, and another might see it robbing the lab where the pharmaceuticals are made. The Thaumaturge finds the Company useful for acquiring arcane reagents, but new spells or apprentices might be stolen by the Company.

Perhaps only the Company of the Free Swords understands the Augustine Trading Company. The latter pays good coin, provides powerful Weapons, and then expects the former to complete a contract. When the contract is closed, there is no further connection until the next contract is negotiated. Their relationship is coldly transactional, as it should be.
Behind every problem is a deal waiting to happen.

entries.name: Goals
entries: The Augustine Trading Company focuses on expanding resources to maximize its profits. It pursues its goals by ruthlessly opening new markets, developing new technologies, and removing the competition. As a soulless organization, it has no moral direction but profit, and it has the resources to hide the lengths it will go to achieve its goals.

entries.name: Profit by Any Means
entries: The Company pursues profit by any means available. This includes opening trade in all major, and not-so-major, cities, or sending explorers to find new resources to exploit. The Company uses less savory methods as well.

Using economic manipulation, the Company buys up essential industries to fix prices; sabotages rival businesses through bribery, blackmail, or "accidents;" and uses predatory high-interest loans to seize assets when the borrower defaults. They have been known to trap whole towns or cities in generational debt, creating de facto serfdom.

The Company militarizes commerce with programs like Project Warbreed, creating enhanced monstrous troops to be sold or rented. They hire adventurers to remove indigenous populations unwilling to cooperate and supernatural protectors of desired resources. The Company "redeems wayward souls," capturing and trafficking magical beings and cursed individuals for research projects.

entries.name: Protect the Company
entries: The members of the Augustine Trading Company value the Company over any individual. Those who put the company first are rewarded, until they are no longer useful. People are disposable. Institutions endure.

To protect the Company, it employs hordes of lawyers and bankers. It wears contracts like Armor and wields its wealth like a bludgeon. It maintains dossiers on everyone who has ever worked with or crossed them, saving these secrets for the future leverage. Whistleblowers are permanently silenced, and problematic adventurers sent on "suicide missions." Governments and churches are incentivized to aid the Company through bribes, favorable deals, and financial support. Laws are rewritten to criminalize competition or make it illegal to buy from them. Corrupt religious leaders issue holy edicts encouraging the faithful to buy from the Company. Those who refuse the Company's gold can always be blackmailed or disappeared.

entries.name: Secure the Future
entries: The Augustine Trading Company strongly believes in education and growing talent. It funds colleges and granting scholarships to their Augustine Centers found throughout Etharis, training the next generation of innovators. Applicants are carefully screened for flexibility, prizing accomplishment over morality or dignity. The accepted are given lavish scholarships, contingent on service and success, creating indentured servants afraid to lose their position.

Students and their instructors delve into all sorts of research that might become profitable: new spells to increase production, magizoology to develop new sources of magic or super soldiers, or even black powder and steam engines for Weapons or locomotion.

entries.name: Leadership
entries: Kolyhs Papinian was orphaned by the age of eight and grew up on the streets, where she learned that being unobtrusive has its virtues, but the right words can control any situation. The woman who would become "The Blazing Brain" grew up to understand three things she would use to gain the power and control that she needed.

entries.name: Truth is a luxury. Control is a necessity.
entries: The world is a dangerous place. Without family or position, any random calamity could ruin or kill her. To control the world around her is to survive. Truth is too expensive to afford. Lies and proxies are safer, and secrets keep you untouchable. This became the foundation of her plans.

entries.name: Gold buys silence faster than truth buys trust.
entries: Kolyhs schemed her way to enough money that she could afford an apprenticeship to the Banker's Guild. Along the way, she learned that loyalty, justice, and friendship could be purchased. Coin is a weapon that she wields more shrewdly than any blade.

entries.name: Every system has a door, and every door has a price.
entries: Any system—religious, economic, legal, or magical—always has hidden loopholes that can be exploited to gain leverage. To master a system is not to follow it, but to break and own it. Using the legal system, she could turn her position as Head-Executor of the Banker's Guild into a spot on the {@i Montarri} of the Augustine Trading Company. Using the financial system, she could use the Company's money to secure a position on the Supreme Council of {@book Morencia|GrimHollowCG24|9}. Using the political system, she could bind all three together to make her unassailable.

There may be no shrewder creator of contracts short of the Arch Daemons than Kolyhs Papinian ({@creature Archmage|XMM}). The gnome who rose to be on the Bankers Guild, the Augustine Trading Company, and the Morencian Supreme Council is disarmingly charming. When first met, she seems little more than a minor functionary, but in front of a crowd, her charisma blazes to life, enthralling all who listen. Kolyhs understands what people want, and she is always ready to sell it to them.

entries.name: The Montarri
entries: As one of five members of the {@i Montarri}, Kolyhs is head of the Augustine Trading Company, holding the simple title of Treasurer. In addition to the Treasurer, the other directing councilors include:

items: Senior Preceptor Melchora Rosalle, who manages the researchers of the Augustine Centers.
Master of Ledgers Enzo Reiff, who is in charge of trade operations.
Lord Barrister Anlon Katzenbach, who is in charge of contracts and legal agreements.
Intendant of Compliance Odriana van de Nijveldt, who is the Company's spymaster.

entries: Each of the {@i Montarri} control any number of agents, which they use to scheme against the Company's enemies, and sometimes each other. Accusations of corruption are common but rarely bear fruit. The {@i Montarri} are essentially unquestionable within their spheres of influence. Forcing one to sacrifice a handful of pawns or lose some profits is the best any of them can hope for against each other. Only Kolyhs might oust one of the other {@i Montarri}, and why would she do so when she is the one who chose them for their positions?

entries.name: Agents
entries: The Augustine Trading Company has employees from middle managers and Trade Factors, to specialists like Preceptors and containment agents who specialize in working with magical beasts. The most talented agents are usually headquartered in Morencia and dispatched as needed.

Local branch directors cultivate their own specialists, deploying them only for the most important missions where their talents are critical to success. Instead, mercenaries and adventurers make disposable employees when situations are dangerous or require deniability. Pay is quite generous, as are the perks that often accompany such missions, so being selected by the Augustine Trading Company is quite a triumph for an adventuring party. The tasks are usually quite clear and uncomplicated, as long as the party has few morals and even fewer scruples.
Even a child understands that more coin means more leverage.

entries.name: Important Locations
entries: Towering over the mist-choked canals of {@book Morencia|GrimHollowCG24|9} like a black and gilded fang is the August Spire, a fortress-like palazzo that is equal parts palace, bank, and office building. The innovation of the Company is on full display, with gas lamps and waterwheels churning beneath it, powering arcane engines. The headquarters of the Augustine Trading Company has the expected boardrooms, offices, and vaults, but that is only a small portion of the nexus of power of the Company. The Company's most prized possession is a portal made of twisted flesh and enchanted iron that allows the Company to dispatch couriers and spies across Etharis if the need is great enough. Powering the portal is ruinously expensive and requires vampire blood, the flesh of fiends, metal tainted by the primordial elements, and essence drained from rare magical items whose creation has been lost to time. No one but the Augustine Trading Company could afford to activate it, and even they do so only under the most important circumstances.

entries.name: Trading Hubs
entries: Outside of Morencia, the Company establishes trading hubs that blend with the dominant aesthetic of the region. They are always well-staffed and well-defended public storefronts, selling luxury goods and necessary staples. They also include a backroom where mercenaries might be hired, gray market deals can be struck, or more "complicated" shipments are handled. Nearby is always one or more warehouses, strategically placed near roads and waterways. Each trading hub is overseen by a Trade Factor who reports to the August Spire in Morencia.

entries.name: Research and Development
entries: The Company maintains the Augustine Centers, where they fund research and develop their latest talent. These elite schools are extremely expensive and frequently require sponsorship or significant tuition to enter. However, actual aptitude always outcompetes wealth, as the Company cannot afford to allow talented agents to work for their competitors. Graduating students can expect to move into senior field roles or coveted regional trading hubs, giving them more chances to succeed and further indebting them to the Company.

Nearly every major city has at least one Center. The most famous include the Collegium of Progress and the Augustine Academy of Applied {@skill Arcana|XPHB}. The former is located in Morencia, near the Company's headquarters, training the next generation of middle managers, teaching law, economics, accounting, contract negotiation, and ethics. The later is located in Castalore and specializes in surveillance arcana, information warfare, and marketing.

entries.name: Secret Sites
entries: The company maintains several secret facilities. Under the frozen lands of Grarjord is Facility Null-9, home to the cutting-edge research of Project Warbreed to design controllable super soldiers. Here, new warbreed are created, combining lycanthrope and vampiric curses to make new thralls. The thralls are made obedient via trauma-induced memory erasure that makes them lethal command-following automatons...usually. Some say the facility itself howls when the wind dies.

Hiding in the {@book Castinella|GrimHollowCG24|6} mountains of Corova is Ashhold Alpha, a war college run off the books, where mercenaries and monstrous clientele test prototypes designed by Project Fumarialis.

In the forests of the {@book Charneault Kingdom|GrimHollowCG24|5} is the Unlisted Garden, home to Project Mandragora, distilling strange fey-tainted plants to create arcano-reactive reagents for alchemical and transformational drugs. Poisonings are, unfortunately, very common among the staff, both unintentional and intentional.

entries.name: Quest Hooks
entries: The Augustine Trading Company is always looking for specialists, especially disposable assets like mercenaries or adventurers. No matter the Company's shiny propaganda and lucrative pay, their assignments are almost always morally gray. Those in service to the Company must often choose between serving the Company's ruthless interests or preserving their own conscience.

entries.name: Widow's Curse
entries: The Company needs an escort for a trading vessel or caravan. Hidden amidst harmless cargo is the Widow's Veil, a cursed, tattered black veil that is always damp with cold, unseen tears. Once the property of a grieving noblewoman from the {@book Bürach Empire|GrimHollowCG24|3}, she cursed death itself when her love was taken by war. Now the veil attracts incorporeal undead. This highly dangerous artifact is destined for a Company black site where a new project intends to capture unquiet souls for experimentation. The characters will have to deal with frequent attacks and possession attempts until the source is uncovered. Destroying the cargo forfeits payment, but completing delivery may be morally problematic for some characters.

entries.name: Ambushed Trader
entries: The party witnesses a violent ambush against a Trade Factor named Herlinde. If they rescue her, they can begin an investigation uncovering a conspiracy implicating rivals in the Augustine Trading Company. If they fail to save her, the party is blamed for Herlinde's murder, and agents of the Company are hired to eliminate the "murderers," or potential witnesses.

entries.name: Monster Salvage
entries: The Company hires the characters to salvage parts from various monsters, not all of whom are evil. Once the hearts of their quarries are realized, the party must choose whether to harvest the monster anyway or stop the Company from murdering an innocent monster.

entries.name: Abduction
entries: The characters are hired to abduct Humusi, a teen the Company claims murdered one of their Trade Factors during a street robbery. In reality, some quirk of Humusi's birth renders her particularly resilient to the ravages of Transformations. The Company plans to send her to Facility Null-9 and use her as a new vampiric werewolf hybrid Warbreed. If the characters hand her over, Humusi proves a natural host, but eventually breaks her programming and begins hunting the party out of vengeance.

entries.name: Plant Management
entries: A mutated sentient plant with mind-controlling spores has gotten loose inside the Unlisted Garden, and the characters must rescue the researchers. There is a significant bonus that dwarfs the rescue pay, if the characters can capture the creature alive.

entries.name: Bandit Control
entries: Gorlga Zverferrin, a bandit captain and self-styled merchant prince, has been harassing Company caravans and threatening a local trade hub. The characters are offered a reward to eliminate the threat, but double if they can convince her to become an agent of the Company and harass its competitors instead.

entries.name: Mage Rescue
entries: The Arcanist Inquisition is no friend of the Augustine Trading Company, and they are good at capturing mages. The characters are hired to rescue a Preceptor from the Augustine Academy of Applied {@skill Arcana|XPHB} before he can be burned. There is an extra reward for determining what spell or Magic Item they used to contain the potent spellcaster.

entries.name: Debt Collection
entries: Lord Léo Vaillancourt owes a significant sum to the Company and is no longer able to make his payments, defaulting on his loan. The characters are sent to collect on the debt. While money is fine, the Company would much rather have his land and family heirlooms, which are said to include several unique magical items, as well as a vast library on monster hunting. Unfortunately, for the characters, the Vaillancourts are skilled monster hunters and include fey and hag Transformed among their number.

entries.name: Tainted Pirates
entries: The Galling Visage has been taken by pirates and the characters are hired to recover it. Most pirates, would never risk tangling with a Morencian ship, especially a Company floating fortress, but Captain Ix'ilettal and his crew are twisted aberrant horrors of the deep. Now crewing the Galling Visage and the Ix'ilettal's Tyrant's Mercy, the crew seeks to spread its taint to even more crews.

entries.name: Bookeeper's Secrets
entries: Lonzol, a Company bookkeeper, turns up face-down in a Morencian canal, pockets empty. Worse, his ledger's missing. The Augustine Trading Company wants it found before the wrong kind of curious mind cracks it open. The characters are told it contains only numbers, but nobody murders over simple math. The party must follow the trail through rain-slick alleys, bribed guards, and Ebon Syndicate criminals who'd rather see the truth of what's in the ledger.

entries.name: Burning Warehouses
entries: A warehouse goes up in flames; the third one this month. Officially, it was an accident. Unofficially, it was sabotage, and the Company's losing patience. They want the party to find out who's torching their assets and make an example of them. The problem is, the more they dig, the more they realize these fires might be the only honest thing they've been told. Each warehouse contained off-books caged daemons, to be sent for experimentation by Project Warbreed. Exposing the truth risks alienating another department of the Company.

entries.name: Faction Rewards
entries: For those with flexible morality, the Augustine Trading Company provides financial rewards in excess of any other faction or nation. Loyal and successful minions might expect coin, luxurious gifts, land, servants, jewels, fashions, and objects of art. Characters might even expect powerful Magic Items, especially if the item would be useful in service to the Company. Should the characters hunt an elemental in service to the Company, they might requisition {@i {@item Armor of Fire Resistance|XDMG}} and then be allowed to keep it if they are successful. Werewolf hunters might be gifted {@item Silvered Weapon|XDMG|Silvered Weapons}. In search of a {@creature Griffon|XMM}, {@item Wings of Flying|XDMG} or a {@item Figurine of Wondrous Power|XDMG} makes an appropriate gift.

Agents that become indispensable might expect even more lucrative or exotic rewards. Characters might be given shares in a trading caravan or vessel that generate a passive income for the party, and provide them a reliable mode of transportation, as well as a cover for visiting settlements that might not look kindly on the approach of adventurers. Such passive investments might earn the party as much as 100  {@item Gold|XDMG|GP} per level each month.

The Company might also offer experimental Potions or drugs that provide combat enhancements. Some of these experimental Potions might even start a{@book Transformations|GrimHollowPG24|6}, especially if the loyalty of the characters is in question. Once the {@book Transformations|GrimHollowPG24|6} is advanced enough, such characters might be sent on particularly dangerous missions to test the efficacy of using the drug to create other super soldiers.

Loyal characters that get into trouble can expect the finest financial and legal support, as long as their survival is more useful than their mysterious demise. Show unwavering devotion, and you'll be wrapped in layers of legal Armor, fed coin by the chestful, and praised in quarterly reports as a model of progress. But the Company doesn't traffic in sentiment. It runs on ledgers, not loyalty. The moment a character's survival costs more than their silence, contracts go missing, testimonies get rewritten, and the next assignment is a one-way expedition. You won't get a farewell party. You'll get a eulogy buried in a profit-loss statement and a plaque in a hallway no one walks.

entries.title: {@i Blood and Honor, Strength in Steel}

items: The Company of Free Swords is the pre-eminent mercenary organization on the continent, with branches in most major cities. The company takes all manner of contracts—from ridding a village of a monster, to protecting a town from bandit raids, to sending hundreds to fight a full-fledged war for a well-paying lord.

The company has a code of honor and an elite reputation they make sure to spread. Despite the name, its services do not come for free. If you want the best loyalty money can buy, a company known for slaying daemon lords and sending {@area Aether Kindred|36b|x} into the void, then you want the Company of Free Swords, and you better be ready to pay.

Despite their love for coin, the Free Swords are not unscrupulous. The company's honor, its soldiers' well-being, and the outright refusal to commit atrocities are core principles. While individual branches may at times be swayed by coin to stray from their code, the Free Swords are known to "discipline" their own, so none take that chance lightly.
Skill and honor are the main qualifications for joining the Free Swords, and they have attracted warriors, priests, druids, rogues, spies, and monster hunters into their ranks.

entries.name: History
entries: The founding of the Free Swords dates back nearly two centuries, to a bloody conflict in the Falaris province of southeastern Bürach. Here, conscripted soldiers of the rebel tyrant King Vaclav II fought an increasingly brutal guerrilla campaign against the Bürach army.

Things came to a head when the soldiers were ordered to slaughter a rural village who had refused to join the tyrant's army. Commander Darion Harkin not only refused the order but also led a mass desertion, with many soldiers choosing to follow his tough-but-fair leadership.

Vaclav II sent the remainder of his forces to punish the deserters, but Harkin anticipated the move. By the time the tyrant's forces arrived at the deserter's encampment, Harkin and a small group of his most loyal soldiers had snuck into the king's own keep and assassinated him.

Harkin then sued for peace with the {@book Bürach|GrimHollowCG24|3}, offering to "purchase" Vaclav's keep with the very treasure that they'd looted from it. The Bürach were happy to have an end to the conflict and a controlled presence in the area.

In what eventually became known as Harkin's Hold, the Company of Free Swords was founded with Darion Harkin's famous words: "If we must deprive this world of fathers, brothers, and sons, and be killed as we do so, let it be for the highest coin, that we may die content."

Despite the mercenary mindset, the company took much of its ethos from its founding conflict. Harkin instilled a code of honor comprised of three core principles: stay loyal to the company, be honest in your dealings, and take vengeance on those who betray you or your comrades.

Having their own space to rest and train, and a location close to plenty of opportune conflicts, the Free Swords soon became respected and feared. As their reputation grew, so did their ranks, and it wasn't long until new branches opened—across Bürach and beyond.

Over the years, the Free Swords have fought in many notable conflicts, large and small. Encouraged to learn and recount (and perhaps embellish) their history, some of the Free Swords have also gained a bard-like reputation.

entries.name: The Battle of Kovich Crag
entries: An early conflict that helped established the Free Swords occurred during the early days of the Bürach-Ostoya conflict. After a failed over-the-mountains push, a battered Bürach regiment retreated through the Kovich Crag—a low but narrow pass through the Grey Spine. Emboldened, the Ostoyans readied a counterattack and marched up the pass.

With a contingent of Free Swords nearby, the Bürach forces quickly contracted the Company to hold the pass. Further complicating matters, an Ostoyan spy among the Bürach warned the Ostoyan troops, allowing them to prepare.

Against the odds, the Swords emerged victorious. Stories are still told of their hit-and-run feints and a detachment that climbed rock walls to decimate the Ostoyan flank. In a brazen move, Harkin sent the defeated Ostoyan commander back with a scroll offering the company's services in the future, planting the first seeds of their expansion into Ostoya.

entries.name: The Daemon Lord of Kravburg
entries: Few events in the company's history have brought as much glory and scrutiny as the story of the Silent Seven. The company had received a lucrative commission from the Burgermeister of Kravburg—a small but affluent town of nearly a thousand souls. There were reports of ritualistic murders, foul magic, and whispers of a daemonic presence.
Two dozen well-trained mercenaries traveled to Kravburg. What happened next is subject to wild speculation and the stuff of legends.

After two weeks, the town was burned to the ground, with half its population dead, with the remaining having fled to the surrounding woods. In the middle of what was once the town square smoldered the charred remains of an enormous horned creature. Many experts claimed it was a daemon lord—a creature of mythical power, thought immortal. Seven members of the Free Swords survived, but aside from a well-rehearsed phrase of: "we killed the daemon, we saved the people," none ever spoke of what took place.

The legend of this grew. Without exception, the surviving townsfolk spoke of the mercenaries giving their lives to get them to safety, but there were also tales of them killing possessed townsfolk, of readying for an unholy ritual, and of fighting each other with unnaturally glowing eyes.

None argued about the dead daemon lord, and the company continues to leverage the tale decades later. When things don't go as planned, the Company's more sardonic members still grimly say: "We killed the daemon. We saved the people."

entries.name: The Company Today
entries: Today, Company branches have spread across Etharis, with the four most profitable interests found in {@book Bürach|GrimHollowCG24|3}, {@book Ostoya|GrimHollowCG24|4}, {@book Castinella|GrimHollowCG24|6}, and {@book Charneault|GrimHollowCG24|5}. Free Swords chapters maintain the same tenets but vary greatly by geography.

In Castinella, the Free Swords are often engaged by the Arcanist Inquisition, who have strict rules regarding mages in the chapter. In the knightly courts of Charneault, chapters are often contracted on a prolonged basis, requiring an oath of service for the duration. There is even a small chapter of the company that is spirit-sworn, who only take contracts in line with the spirit's wishes—conveniently, this spirit is easily aggravated.

Despite their growth, the Free Swords maintain a stellar reputation for their skill, fearlessness, and honorable conduct. They stand ready to face any danger, provided there's enough coin. Their renown for collecting payment "one way or another" is also well known, making crossing them unadvisable.

entries.name: Goals
entries: The Company enables its swords to live grand, action-filled lives, and it does so by embracing its core goals.

entries.name: For the Highest Coin
entries: Nothing's free. The mercenaries are well aware that their exciting lives are often destined to end in painful and inglorious deaths far from home. They're equally aware that the mitigating factor to this is coin.
Gold. Treasure. Payment. The Free Swords take their money seriously—nothing gets done without it, and woe be to any client that can't pay up.

However, coin does not trump loyalty or integrity—at least not for the Free Swords. The Company firmly believes that trustworthiness, and knowing that your ally won't turn on you mid-battle, is worth a premium. Honor is good for business.

With varied independent chapters, there have been instances of a unit not honoring a contract or switching sides. One of the most prominent of these took place during the Ostoyan civil war. A company of Free Swords was hired by Raevans to reinforce the Order of Dawn at a key skirmish. When the Somans brought out the Swords, several members refused to break their oath, resulting in a bloodbath that that saw the Free Swords slaughter each other.

The battle caused philosophical debate to ripple across the organization, with members torn between the ideals of duty to their brothers versus the need to honor contracts. There are rumors (which the company tries to suppress) of a secret group who call themselves "The Red Swords," who take the highest coin regardless of moral implications.

entries.name: Never Leave a Debt Unsettled
entries: The Free Swords will take payment one way or another. They consider it acceptable to pillage lands if they feel their work hasn't been justly compensated. They're not brigands, however, and will first pursue the lord who disrespected them rather than the land's peasants.

entries.name: Life Well Fought; Life Well Lived
entries: In addition to the code he set in place, Darion Harkin's own work ethic and disciplined approach to the mercenary life became an example to the rest of the company. When on the job, Free Swords were known to live free of excess, stay loyal, and focus on the work. Only once the job was done, their coin collected, and the company's reputation upheld did they celebrate. And celebrate they did! Harkin encouraged a gregarious nature in his people, telling them to spend widely, and pay generously for things, not only out of fairness, but because success begets success.

This mindset remains in place today. When people speak of the two faces of the Free Swords, they speak not of duplicity, but of the difference between the colorful, well-natured big spenders at the tavern and the stone-faced soldiers on the job.

entries.name: Leadership
entries: The Company of Free Swords are organized into local chapters, which are typically run independently, with only nominal oversight and sharing of contracts. However, it is common for several chapters to be run out of the same headquarters, and in such cases, there's a Council of Captains and a Chapter Head.
