Certain rules and obligations of the greater organization must be met by each chapter, but the company's strong discipline means that these are most often enforced by exception.

entries.name: Yrsa Bitterwind
entries: The Company of Free Swords today is led by Yrsa Bitterwind, a towering, musclebound Valikan woman. Her tale of coming to run the largest mercenary organization in Etharis is one for the Sagas.

entries.name: Early Life
entries: A capable warrior from childhood, Yrsa grew up in Thrull amongst the brutal raiders of clan Völgr. By teenage years, she had killed more men than most do in a lifetime and was leading raids. She was soon seen as a potential successor to Nölgr Magnusson, the Völgr clan chief and Keeper of the Three Tribes. She yearned for ascension and recognition, but clan politics would not allow her to challenge Nölgr. She grew increasingly brash and brutal, striving ever harder to prove herself in a situation where nothing would be enough.

entries.name: Pivotal Battle
entries: The turning point came when her raid into Kandar was met by a far superior force of Free Swords mercenaries, under the leadership of Brom Ven Torrig—a bear of a man himself, and a famed warrior. Amidst a bloody conflict, the two leaders met on the field of battle and called a halt when Yrsa issued an unusual challenge. Knowing that the battle was lost to her, but that many of her soldiers were yet to die, she challenged Brom to a duel. Win or lose, her people would retreat. But if she won, Brom's company would be hers to lead.

Knowing that it would save many of his people's lives, and confident in his own skills, Brom did not hesitate to accept the challenge, but the man had drastically underestimated Yrsa's ferocity. By all accounts, it took her all but one strong kick into the warrior's shield and two swings of her greataxe to leave both Brom headless.

"Return to Thrull," she told her raiders, "tell them that since they did not find Yrsa Bitterwind fit to lead them, she has gone to earn great victories. Tell them that she will return stronger, and to claim what is hers."

entries.name: Rising Leader
entries: Yrsa left as the leader of a small company of Free Swords who stood in both awe and fear of her and dared not oppose her claim. Yrsa had no interest in staying in {@book Valika|GrimHollowCG24|7}, and the company quickly returned south. Her raider mindset translated well to leading brute force mercenary missions, and she took well to learning more complex tactics and even subterfuge. Her imposing stature and personal battle reputation grew quickly, with southerners taking to calling her "The Valikan Queen."

The recognition she had missed in her homeland came easily for Yrsa in the company, where deeds, merit, and earned coin were all that mattered. It took but a few years for Yrsa to find herself far from Valika, and at the head of the Free Swords.

Yrsa Bitterwind has brought the company greater recognition and prosperity, but not all her impacts have been positive or well received. Her temper in negotiations and her utter brutality on the battlefield, while welcomed by some, have made others apprehensive. Some say that her drive for personal accomplishments and "besting of worthy foes" puts them at risk. Others worry that everything she does is motivated by her eventual return to Valika, and not for the advancement of the Company.

entries.name: Chandra Veln
entries: Yrsa's right-hand and second in command is Chandra Veln, a mysterious elf sorcerer who acts as the chief spymaster of the Free Swords. Also prominent among the Company's other leaders is Hans Tova, an ancestral Büracher and the son of one of the infamous Silent Seven. Hans is known for being a shrewd negotiator, a keen tactician, and drawing upon powerful unnatural abilities in combat.

entries.name: Agents
entries: Historically, new recruits into the Swords—regardless of their training or military service—spend at least a year in regimented training. The action they see is plenty dangerous, but only under strict veteran oversight. The training is as much to ensure their combat prowess as it is to instill the Company mindset and tenets.

Since Yrsa's unorthodox yet well-documented ascension, there have been more exceptions made for those with proven records and skills. This has been met with decidedly mixed feelings among the veterans.

entries.name: Ranks and Pay
entries: The Free Swords embrace their "simple soldier" mindset and avoid a complex hierarchy. A recruit who's completed their first-year training and initial engagements becomes a Free Sword—the typical foot soldier of the company. All Free Swords of the same rank are paid the same share.

After at least five years and having proven themselves, one becomes either a Veteran or a Specialist, gaining a bigger share than a regular Free Sword. Specialists are Veterans with unique skills. These include sappers who use engineering, alchemy, or magic to affect the battlefield, battlemages who are field-level combatants that bring powerful spell support, and infiltrators who are skilled in stealth, poison, and neutralization of individual targets. Other specialists—including several types of ranged combatants—also exist. Specialists must be proven in their area and receive a higher coin share than a Veteran. Finally, those who lead are promoted to Captains. Captains get the highest share of the coin.
Although there is no combat rank higher than Captain, there is a leadership hierarchy among the different chapters, which determines rank among the Captains.

In the field, a Captain can lead as few as one other Sword and as many as several hundred. For large battles, temporary military-style designations are given to different Captains to create the necessary hierarchies. This allows the company to field units of dramatically different sizes.

entries.name: Equipment and Visual Identity
entries: Free Swords are always proficient with the sword and {@item Shield|XPHB}, and many choose to carry these tried-and-true implements into battle. The company itself does not mandate equipment and encourages its members to take pride in the procurement and maintenance of the best Weapons of war for themselves.

In times of mass battle, the Free Swords don their distinctive red and gold "Double Lion" tabards or those provided by their employer. Otherwise, the mercenaries are allowed to wear what they want. All are issued a sturdy double lion brooch, which they wear with pride, except in cases of espionage or other subterfuge.

While the company rarely provides more than the very basics of equipment, they do have access to some of the best merchants and artisans across Etharis, including many who set up permanently on-site at company locations. After all, where better to get a good price for Weapons of war than amongst well-paid mercenaries for whom such things could mean the difference between life and death?

entries.name: Experienced Leaders
entries: Veterans of the Company of Free Swords are formidable foes on the battlefield. They are well equipped and combine the tactics of a well-oiled military machine with individual flare.

entries.name: Free Swords Specialists
entries: Specialists of the Company of Free Swords are some of the company's best Veterans and pack nasty surprises beyond their sword and shield. Four specialists are detailed below: {@creature Free Swords Sapper|GrimHollowCG24|Sapper}, {@creature Free Swords Battlemage|GrimHollowCG24|Battlemage}, {@creature Free Swords Archer|GrimHollowCG24|Archer}, and {@creature Free Swords Infiltrator|GrimHollowCG24|Infiltrator}.

To create a Free Swords Specialist, take the {@b {@creature Free Swords Veteran|GrimHollowCG24}}, increase its {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB|HP} by 10, increase its {@variantrule Challenge Rating|XPHB|CR} by 1, and apply the indicated changes to its stat block.

entries.name: Important Locations
entries: For several years after their founding, the Free Swords operated out of Harkin's Hold—the keep they'd won as part of their founding insurrection. Although the company soon outgrew its confines, the small keep remains a Free Swords outpost. Darion Harkin's grave and many artifacts from the company's founding are kept here, and company members often make a pilgrimage to see the original tenets—painted by Harkin himself upon simple planks of wood. Harkin's Hands—a small chapter of the company, many of whose members trace their lineage back to the original members—operates out of the outpost.

entries.name: Expansion
entries: When the company ran out of space at Harkin's Hold, they realized they needed an independent keep, with privacy for both clients and mobilization, as well as space for barracks, stables, and training areas. By this time, many members of the original company (those still alive) were ready to retire and invested their substantial fortunes into the building of the Löwengard Fortress. Located in {@book Bürach|GrimHollowCG24|3}, the impressive fortress stands in the shadow of the Grey Spine, north of the Grove Maze and south of Vollendorf. The strategic location allows the company to travel by road, river, or mountain pass to {@book Bürach|GrimHollowCG24|3}, {@book Charneault|GrimHollowCG24|5}, {@book Ostoya|GrimHollowCG24|4}, and beyond.

Löwengard can house over a thousand Free Swords, although typically only three hundred active and two hundred recruits are stationed here. The reinforced fortress boasts training facilities, armories, and over two dozen expert craftsmen, including an enchanter. In addition to soldier barracks, the Fortress has a reception hall, several private war rooms, and a council chamber where the largest Council of Captains met for nearly a century before the Lions Den in Voros became the more centralized location. It is often said that: "If you want to start a rumor, go to Voros; if you want to start a war, go to Löwengard."

Farms and small holdings have sprung up in the fertile areas around the fortress and now provide it with most of its basic needs without having to source from nearby cities. The company pays well for such goods.

entries.name: Lions Dens
entries: Even as the Löwengard was built and populated, the company needed guild-style representation offices in larger cities. Thus, the Lions Dens were created to serve as the company's urban face, to drive recruitment and drum up local business. Most often a Lions Den is located in an urban center near an associated rural fortress of the Free Swords—a smaller, local version of Löwengard.

Lions Dens vary greatly by city and location. In some towns, they're but a single-room shack run by a representative from the local keep. In other cities, they are resplendent guild halls with meeting areas, services, and lodgings for visiting Free Swords.

The most impressive of these is located in the city of Voros in {@book Castinella|GrimHollowCG24|6}. This Lions Den was built as a purposefully impressive display, designed to establish the company's presence in both the Provinces and nearby {@book Charneault|GrimHollowCG24|5}. Despite increasing business, securing lands for new keeps and dens in the new regions proved slower than expected, and the Voros den grew in size and stature.

This was further helped by Armand Savranti—Yrsa Bitterwind's immediate predecessor—who hailed from Castinella and took to holding many Councils of Captains in Voros instead of Löwengard. Few objected to this, as the High Captains very much enjoy their urban comforts when not out in the field.

Yrsa, on the other hand, prefers the cold mountain air of the fortress, and holds more and more councils there once again. The Voros location remains geographically and politically vital for the Free Swords.

entries.name: Quest Hooks
entries: The characters' interactions with the Free Swords can range from uncomfortable interactions while on competing missions to full membership. Unaffiliated characters can find themselves hired by the company to fix urgent issues or to assist in times when extra hands are needed. Those in service to political powers or small settlements may find that the Free Company has been hired as an opposing force.
Characters who gain full membership are only allowed to leave the company with permission, which typically requires them to be in good standing and to maintain discretion regarding past dealings.

entries.name: A Captain's Death
entries: A brutal murder has been committed in one of the towers of the Löwengard Fortress. The victim was a fervently loyal captain, but widely disliked. Yrsa Bitterwind herself may hire respected outsiders to investigate. Plenty of swords disliked the gruff captain, but was his death a personal vendetta or had he uncovered evidence of a Red Swords cell within the fortress?

entries.name: Local Reinforcement
entries: The Blades of Bressil, a distant Free Swords branch, have taken a monster-hunting contract to track down a werewolf terrorizing the town of Claussein. When one werewolf turns out to be a pack, and one of the Free Swords is taken during an initial skirmish, the company needs immediate local help.

entries.name: Unpaid Protection
entries: Kutor Bracht, the Burgermeister of the small Bürach village of Narskenn, has failed to pay a group of Free Swords for protecting his village from bandits. The Free Swords have taken most of the village's cattle hostage, threatening to slaughter the herd and come after Bracht if they're not paid. The Burgermeister argues that the bandits never attacked while the Swords were under contract, suggesting the mercenaries colluded with the bandits and asks for help to resolve the situation.

entries.name: Bandit Company
entries: The Swords of Swamach, a small and distant branch of the company, have turned to banditry to survive, attacking nearby villages. The Free Swords sent another company to deal with them, but they were waylaid by monsters and decimated. The few who survived offer significant coin for aid in the assault on the renegade company's fort.

entries.name: Contractual Delay
entries: The Swords of the Canopy, a famed Free Swords branch in Charneault, were approached with a monster-investigation contract in a small coastal town south of Camont. They want to take the job, but their current contract prevents them from engaging in other work for two more weeks. They seek to hire others to begin the investigation on their behalf, intending to assault the monsters once their existing contract allows. They do not expect the monster attacks to escalate during the two weeks.

entries.name: Sword Proxy
entries: The Golden Glaives, a well-respected Free Swords branch in Castinella, were approached by the wealthy owner of a mine in the hills of Corova. Her mine has been invaded by fiendish creatures coming through a magical rift. Some miners were slaughtered, while others have fled and won't return until the mine is cleared and the rift sealed. The problem clearly requires magical intervention, but the Arcanist Inquisition is slow to respond. The Golden Glaives do not wish to directly intervene because of political concerns with the Inquisition. However, they are willing to discreetly hire an independent group to intervene, thereby maintaining good relations with the mine owner and preventing unwanted attention from the Inquisition.

entries.name: Faction Rewards
entries: For characters who join the Free Swords individually, payments increase with rank in the company, and are always a share of the total contract and any spoils. Characters who join as a group or (in time) create their own chapter can expect lucrative contracts to be sent their way through the Free Swords offices, and via the company's reputation.

Beyond coin, larger Lions Dens and company keeps can offer exclusive and difficult-to-procure equipment. This includes Armor, Weapons, Potions, Mounts, and other more unique implements of war. Some locations even offer enchanting and spellcasting.

In practical terms, the equipment offered may include {@book Advanced equipment|GrimHollowPG24|6|Advanced Equipment} from the {@i Grim Hollow Player's Guide}, or even Magic Items themed around Weapons and Armor. However, the more exclusive and rarer offerings are likely reserved for company members in good standing and not offered to outsiders. Then again, coin does tend to solve many arguments with the Free Swords.

Finally, simply being associated with the Company of Free Swords can help earn bigger rewards. Nobles are willing to pay more, and villains may be more willing to surrender to those associated with the formidable Free Swords.
Individuals who are highly favored by the company might be offered a stronghold or the command of a warband or military division.

items: If behind every unexplained success lies an undiscovered crime, then the Ebon Syndicate's successes are as innumerable as their crimes. The syndicate's tendrils stretch out from the seedy shadows into every aspect of urban life in Etharis, and often beyond.

They are the beggar on the corner inconspicuously eavesdropping on nobles' conversations. They are the thief cutting a purse string. They are the traceless spy planting evidence before the militia raid. They are the hooded assassin coming for you in the dark alley, and the smiling waiter serving you poisoned wine should you escape that alley. They have the thing you want, and the thing you need, and their eyes on the thing you hold most dear.

The syndicate primarily operates in Etharis's biggest and richest cities, where targets and opportunities are most plentiful. Lesser organizations and freelance operatives soon find themselves facing a deadly offer: stop, join, pay, or find yourselves bleeding in a gutter. The latter is not an idle threat, but a quickly fulfilled promise—those who step on the Syndicate's toes or try to take their territory are mercilessly dispatched.

Etharis's other major factions may look down upon the syndicate's activities, but they know enough to respect its capabilities. Even groups like the Augustine Company and the Watchers that publicly claim to oppose the syndicate have been known to take advantage of its unparallelled spy network.
The syndicate pursues every kind of under-the-table opportunity. From petty crime to organized drug smuggling, extortion, and assassination—if it needs to be done outside the law, they get it done.

entries.name: History
entries: "All roads lead to Altenheim, eh? So do you fellows want your roads to end here, or shall we make a deal?" These were supposedly the words of famed bandit Hiram Greyman as he hopelessly tried to bluff a group of seven city watch who'd cornered his band of three after a botched heist.

The watch did not in fact take his deal, and the roads of both of his companions ended in that alley. Gravely injured himself, and delirious from blood loss, Hiram desperately fled into the city's hedge maze. It was there—as he told the story—that a hawk moth led him to safety.

Hiram saw the moth as a sign of divine favor, sent by the gods themselves. As he recovered and rebuilt his organization into what eventually became the Ebon Syndicate, he took the hawk moth as his personal symbol. When he died, the organization kept the symbol. The nocturnal moth flies swiftly through the darkness, drawn through its short life to dangerous and shiny things. Over the years, different leaders have focused on its different aspects as inspiration for the organization.

As the syndicate grew in prominence late in Hiram's life, he became a more and more elusive figure by necessity. Few knew his true face, and getting an audience with "the Greyman" became a unique honor. This started the tradition of the Syndicate's leaders taking the title of "Greyman."
With the right motivation, any secret is within reach.

entries.name: The Red Gondola
entries: Once the Ebon Syndicate became a major power in Altenheim, having the connections and support there greased the wheels for expansion into other Bürach cities and beyond. While establishment of a major criminal organization rarely comes bloodlessly, it never came as hard as it did when the syndicate tried to get their foothold in the free city of {@book Morencia|GrimHollowCG24|9}.

One of the syndicate's most common targets and foils throughout its existence has been the Augustine Trading Company. The Company carries expensive goods that are always a tempting target for the Syndicate's thieves. Further, the Company practices what the Syndicate calls "above-table extortion," thus exercising control in areas where the Syndicate would prefer to collect. The Syndicate's spies, on the other hand, cause the Company no end of headaches by discovering its unsavoury secrets and selling them to the highest bidder.

Thus, when the Syndicate started to operate on the Augustine Trading Company's home turf of Morencia, the canals ran red with blood. The times of open conflict in Morencia saw assassinations in broad daylight and open skirmishes between syndicate enforcers and Free Swords hired by the Company. The conspicuous conflict filled the streets with dread, regularly flaring up over many years. But it all ended, at least in public, after {@book Castinella's|GrimHollowCG24|6} failed invasion.

Many rumors swirl around the political machinations that spurred the invasion. Among the more subtle is the story of the red gondola. In the aftermath of the failed Castinellan attack, an unmarked anchored ship was recovered. The mysterious dark ship held the bodies of more than a hundred well-armed soldiers with no insignias. They had been brutally slaughtered. Most had had their throats slashed, soaking the ship's wood with blood. Rumors spoke of a large Morencian-style gondola seen departing and returning in the dark among the chaos of the invasion.

Such a gondola—unmarked and painted fully red with fresh blood—was left the next morning near the home of the leader of the Augustine Trading Company. A bouquet of inky-black roses in the shape of a hawk moth was in the gondola. The truth of what had transpired has never been revealed. Was it an Augustine ship carrying Castinellan invaders? Was it a Free Company ship, and those who had hired them? Had the Ebon Syndicate known of the invasion, only to leverage it to their own benefit? Did the Syndicate recover proof of the Company's betrayal of the city? If they did, that truth has never seen the light of day.
Since that day, the Syndicate was more accepted in Morencia, and the war with the Augustine Trading Company grew cold, going off the streets and back into the shadows.

entries.name: The Syndicate Today
entries: With timely interventions (true or embellished) like the Red Gondola, the Syndicate is now well established continent wide.

Their headquarters are in Altenheim, and they are active across Bürach. The {@area Erlefurt|33d|x} syndicate is known for the use of faerie magic, and for a pair of famous wechselkind thieves who can get to any treasure. In Rauland, Ebon Syndicate agents have been instrumental in stealing and disseminating the mechanized Weapons of Stehlenwald. The city's fading fortunes have been the Syndicate's greater success.

In {@book Ostoya|GrimHollowCG24|4}, the Ebon Syndicate is much more prominent in Raevo, as the {@area Crimson Court|05c|x} has its own spies. In Castinella, mage smuggling provides a unique opportunity for the Syndicate. The latter work is hardly altruistic, as the Syndicate is just as happy to track down or assassinate mages for the Inquisition as they are to help them get away to safety.

Other than distant {@book Valika|GrimHollowCG24|7}, the Syndicate finds it most difficult to operate in {@book Charneault|GrimHollowCG24|5}. However, the rising chaos of the dark mist has dampened faith in the knights, creating opportunities for quicker and seedier solutions which the Syndicate is eager to provide. Fading relations with Tol Leyemil have also opened a door for the Syndicate, who are willing to trade with anyone, and more than keen to get their hands on rare elven Poisons and other potent natural products. Lord Sorcerer Diyanthar was particularly eager to facilitate such trade in exchange for arcane knowledge procured world over, and his acolytes continue the practice.

entries.name: Goals
entries: The greatest constant is change, and the Ebon Syndicate's goals are not only ever-changing but vary drastically by location and time. On any given day, the Syndicate could have spies undermining a government, assassins killing the government's rival, and thieves stealing from both victims.
If anything remains constant, it's that at the highest level the Syndicate seeks money, power, and deniability. How these balance out in any specific time and place is the most tangled of webs.

entries.name: Everybody has a Price
entries: Coin is a great motivation for the Syndicate, but more so as a tool than it is for the other factions. While the Augustine Trading Company and the Free Swords may be primarily concerned with how much they can get, the Syndicate's monetary motivations are much more practical: it's about what—or who—coin can buy. The Syndicate has no political offices or aspirations, so what influence they have is influence they've bought with coin or leverage. While a petty thief goes into the life dreaming of riches, it is not long until that dream turns into the realization that what they really want is a way out, and the cost of that is often more than gold.

entries.name: Control from the Shadows
entries: The Syndicate's lack of open political involvement should not be confused with a lack of influence.

While most obvious in the political spectrum, the Syndicate's preference to pull strings from the shadows is evident across all their dealings. Unless they're sending a message, the Ebon Syndicate remains unseen. They don't hesitate to use proxies, patsies, and mercenaries in tasks that require deniability. It's not uncommon for a freelancer to not realize who their true employer is. The lack of a public presence lets the Syndicate freely operate in more illicit industries than their reputable competitors, such as the Augustine Company.

While secrecy can be their greatest weapon, the Syndicate does not hesitate to play the boogieman when it suits their ends. The sheer size and scope of their operations is so great that it's impossible for them to go unnoticed. An occasional display of their mark can act as much to cover their tracks as to reveal.

entries.name: We Are the Hand Upon the Scales
entries: The Ebon Syndicate ensures that no authority grows too large to threaten their operations. They expertly manipulate to keep allies in power and undermine enemies.

It's not uncommon for the Syndicate's operations to receive public approval. When the Syndicate saves the kidnapped prince, assassinates a stalking vampire, or publicizes a Burgermeister's corruption, there are those who take them for heroes. In a way, the Syndicate's lack of allegiance and willingness to take payment as a debt make them the most accessible force for righting a wrong. However, it pays to remember that just because the Syndicate's goals aren't visible doesn't mean they aren't being served. A hand that can easily right a wrong can also wrong a right.

entries.name: Natalia Koscheii
entries: The syndicate's current Greyman is Natalia Koscheii, who has become as famous for her ability to escape assassination attempts as she is for her hands-on leadership skills. Stories of her unlikely escapes have been exaggerated to such an extent that some now insist that she is one of several identical children, several of whom are now dead. Others, citing her Ostoyan heritage, insist that she is a vampire who turns into a hawk moth to make her escapes.

entries.name: Ancestry
entries: Natalia is indeed of Ostoyan heritage, and of minor nobility at that. Her ancestry goes back to the Somans who defied the vampires and founded the Order of Dawn. As a teenager, Natalia's mother grew tired of the bleak war, fleeing Castalore aboard a smuggler's ship. She was taken to Castinella, only to find herself in danger from the Arcanist Inquisition. In a turn that perhaps the smuggler had been counting on, she chose to remain aboard the ship and put her arcane powers to use in a life of freedom. Some years later, Natalia was born to this Ostoyan refugee turned charming smuggler.

entries.name: Development
entries: Natalia was five years old when she participated in her first larceny. She inherited her mother's magical gift and her father's dexterity, and also their wandering spirit, so Natalia left the maritime life behind after falling in love with a wily Morencian thief during a routine port stop.

However, the life of young love and diversion was not her destiny. Things turned abruptly violent when Natalia's lover, a Syndicate novice, found herself on the wrong side of an upstart gang and killed. Left on her own and out for revenge, Natailia turned to the Syndicate. She infiltrated the gang, and after barely a month, led them into an ambush that saw seventeen thieves pay with their lives.

The experience awakened something within Natalia. She found that emotional detachment and secretive machinations came easily. Her ability to disassociate and manipulate both tactically and emotionally, and her skills with both magical and mundane trickery, helped her to quickly advance through the Syndicate's ranks.

Even in her early days, Natalia often changed locations. After her start in Morencia, she took over a mage-smuggling operation in Voros, oversaw a spy network out of Vollendorf, then finally seized leadership after bloody betrayal left the Syndicate's Altenheim headquarters in disarray.

entries.name: Leadership Style
entries: Natalia has brought greater stability and reason to the Syndicate, focusing on exerting greater political influence to create opportunities. Less brutal than some of her predecessors, she values loyalty above all. Her uncanny ability to infiltrate local Syndicate arms to uncover the disloyal keeps other leaders on edge.

entries.name: Larger Structure
entries: The Ebon Syndicate's network of agents, operators, and lieutenants is intentionally inscrutable, but this much is true: local gangs report to their individual bosses, while those bosses report to a Lieutenant—sometimes called a "Grayman" with a regional subtitle—who in turn reports to Natalia, the true Grayman.

For example, the Axehands weapon-smuggling gang in Stehlenwald reports to their boss, whom they've historically called "Picker." The current Picker is Eetra Strothklar. Eetra reports to the Grayman of Stehlenwald, a mysterious dwarf called Spinnebleck. He then reports to the Grayman of Bürach—Ardekii "Blackflag" Berntsen, who answers to Natalia.

One of the Syndicate's most famous lieutenants is Darion Aufir, the Greyman of Grabenstein. The debonair ogresh controls most of the criminal element in the city, and keeps a very public court within his lavish mansion.

Possibly the most brutal of the current leaders is Kallista de Locra, the young Grayman of Morencia, who has on multiple occasions proposed outright assassinating most of the Augustine leadership. There is suspicion that several recent attempts on Natalia's life were ordered by Kallista.

entries.name: Agents
entries: The unknowing agents of the Syndicate are many. A wash maid with access to everyone from soldiers to nobles is a perfect scout for a den of thieves. A duke's steward with a gambling problem may divulge an important delegate's arrival, enabling a road ambush that can't be traced. A gang of street thugs tipped off to a rich dandy's passage won't know that their attack forced the victim's influential father prompt a military reinforcement, leaving a distant outpost ripe for the taking. Such are the webs the Syndicate spins.

entries.name: The Shadow Academy
entries: For the chosen few identified to join as full members of the Syndicate, there is the Shadow Academy. The academy is not a school or training ground; there are no stern masked instructors preaching rules, and there is no graduation to a fancy moth mask. The Shadow Academy is itself a tenet, a concept of how agents are trained.

The core philosophy of the academy is of hands-on training: "Every lesson is an action; every action is a test." Passage through the Shadow Academy is typically overseen by the member who vouched for the recruit. Trust is paramount. If you're not willing to vouch for those you bring in, then they do not deserve anyone else's time.

Recruits are expected to bring some level of craft to the Syndicate, but more importantly, the right mindset. Once a recruit is brought in, they are mentored by different specialists, learning tasks on the job, before being passed on to the next. The recruit's performance is discussed by the leadership when they change mentors.

Promising recruits who fail at certain tasks are given chances to excel in other areas. Recruits that fail repeatedly, or who embarrass the Syndicate, tend to disappear. Many are killed, but some—if the knowledge they have poses little danger—may be released and watched or relocated. While there are no "instructors" per se, the Syndicate encourages skilled members to pass their knowledge on. In exchange, they get a skilled new member for their own crew.

entries.name: Specialists
entries: The Syndicate is not a military organization and has no official ranks other than Grayman. Members are referred to by name or alias, and often by their specialties, often using local slang. For example, spies might be called "ears," "listeners," or "flies," while assassins could be "blades," "blackmoths," or "fins." Slang varies widely across the continent.

entries.name: Local Enforcers
entries: Street-level agents deliver overt official messages. Thugs and brutes with some manners and basic business sense can beat both into a target when the need arises. In addition to common Weapons, Syndicate enforcers favor fighting chains or "chain-knuckles" that give them the choice to strangle or bind their opponents. These have the benefit of being less conspicuous than a blade.

entries.name: Elite Assassins
entries: The Syndicate's most skilled assassins concoct specialized compounds for each mission. They can make their target spasm in pain, suffocate in silence, or simply fall unconscious, allowing the agent to whisk them away or stage the murder as desired. Their smoke bombs help them escape or turn an unexpected fight in their favour.

entries.name: Important Locations
entries: It has been lost to history whether the Ebon Halls—a tangled maze of ancient tunnels and natural caves that run beneath Altenheim—were named for the Syndicate or vice versa. What is known is that even before Hiram Greyman's time, these passages were used by thieves, smugglers, and other never-do-wells. While Hiram may not have been the first to use them, he was the first to invest heavily in mapping and expanding them, laying the literal foundation for the Grey City.

The Grey City is an underground fortress that's impenetrable as much due to the elusiveness of its location as its ever-changing set of deadly traps and hidden doors. More than one cautionary tale tells of a novice misreading a hidden sign before withering to death in a walled-off room full of poisonous fungus.

Established by Hiram and heavily upgraded since, the Grey City remains the Headquarters of the Syndicate. On those rare occasions when multiple Greymen choose to meet, it is most often here. What makes it a true wonder, however, is not the fortress at the center, but the vast network of tunnels around it. They lead to all areas of the city, connect to the sewers, and even have several lengthy connections to the river. Today's Syndicate smugglers still utter thanks to the infamous laneshi Greyman Sorranna, who dedicated several years overseeing tunnel construction, nearly bankrupting the Syndicate in the process.

The Grey City is unique to Altenheim. Other Syndicate headquarters vary greatly in design. The Syndicate has operated out of anchored ships, dockside warehouses, hillside caverns, secret mountain castles, and so many tavern basements that experienced Syndicate operatives still tap their foot to check the creaky floorboards in whatever drinking establishment they enter.

entries.name: Quest Hooks
entries: Unless the characters have criminal tendencies, they are most likely to openly encounter the Syndicate as antagonists. However, the hidden hands of the Syndicate often secretly guide the actions of anyone accepting freelance work across Etharis.

entries.name: Liberation
entries: Already known for cruelty and paranoia, Baron Kronn Van Vosderg has begun hanging peasants from his castle walls, in what he calls "a search for traitors." The characters are approached by the wife of man slated for execution with a desperate plea for help. They are subsequently approached by a Syndicate spy with an offer of cooperation. The spy's partner, a skilled assassin sent to kill the Baron, has been detained by the paranoid guards, who have no idea of his true mission. If the characters can free the assassin to do his dark business, they'll be free to rescue others in the castle.

entries.name: Elderly Patron
entries: The party are invited to a lavish ball in Altenheim. There, they meet a humble but wealthy older woman named Serafille du Molenn, a collector of antiques. She offers the characters several opportunities to recover historical artifacts and Magic Items. The first assignments are perfectly legal, involving dungeon delving and negotiation. As the characters continue their work, the requests become more questionable, leading to the eventual realization that they're gathering objects of arcane power for the Ebon Syndicate.

entries.name: Novice Thieves
entries: The characters are engaged by Tobril Vortberg, a well-to-do merchant, to recover possessions stolen during a recent burglary. The thieves were a small gang of petty thieves on their first real job. Not knowing where to fence the goods, they talked about their loot all over town, leaving a trail to the Gandering Goose tavern, a secret Ebon Syndicate gathering spot. The gang went there, were scolded, and turned away. When the characters come looking, a "helpful" bystander gives them directions to the gang's hideout, and a warning that they're armed and violent. The Syndicate's interest is to silence the nascent gang.

entries.name: Castinellan Extraction
entries: The characters are approached by a Castinellan woman in Camont. She needs help getting her magically gifted brother out of Toletum before he's captured by the Arcanist Inquisition. She mentions that she'd been given the name of a woman known to arrange such things, but she refused her due to increased Inquisition scrutiny. Her contact, an Ebon Syndicate smuggler named Rella, can be persuaded to provide intelligence and travel routes, but only if they agree to pick up a special package for her.

entries.name: Past Ties
entries: Caravis Voss, the Burgermeister of Tolensk, hires the characters to root out an Ebon Syndicate cell that's trying to "muscle in" on his city. After some investigation, the characters discover that Caravis is a former member of the Syndicate himself and is trying to settle an old score rather than protect the town. To complicate things, the leader of the Syndicate cell sent to collect the debt is his former lover, Alexi.

entries.name: Walk Away
entries: The characters find themselves approached by several Syndicate enforcers, who tell them to walk away from whatever quest they are currently pursuing. If the characters refuse, the enforcers rough them up, attempting to teach them a lesson. If the enforcers win, they do not kill the characters; they just leave them beaten. The enforcers themselves do not know why they were sent, and the characters are left to wonder about the Syndicate's goals, or to investigate further.

entries.name: Faction Rewards
entries: The Ebon Syndicate trades in illicit substances, contraband, Poisons, sinister Magic Items, and of course, {@item Gold|XDMG|Gold}. There are few limits to the rewards for being in the Syndicate's service, but often the greatest and easily most valuable thing that they can offer is information. As outsiders, the characters are often left with information and leads for things the Syndicate wants them to pursue. However, this doesn't mean the information is good.

As the characters become wealthier and more renowned, they may gain contacts in various cities. These Syndicate contacts would seek out the characters as sources of information, offering goods, services, and opportunities in exchange.

If the characters require smuggling services, for themselves or for special goods, the Syndicate can make arrangements in most places on the continent and at sea. If they require a place to lay low, the Syndicate has plenty of hideouts in any major city.

Should characters join the Syndicate and progress within its ranks, exciting opportunities await—Travel the world! Meet interesting people! Leave them wondering what the hell happened!—is a common Syndicate spy refrain. Higher ranks mean not only more interesting missions but also access to the wider information network and political influence. High-level operatives may also be given an entire secret lair for themselves.

entries.title: {@i We who shape magic, shape the world.}

entries: {@i "Dear reader, it is only fair that you should be warned at this point, that if you've come to the pages of this fine tome for concrete information on the organization known as Grand Thaumatic Order of Archmages, you may find the words contained herein lacking. This is not for the lack of effort by the humble documenters of these things, but due to the simple truth that despite great effort, we have not been able to collect even a single piece of hard evidence of the organization's existence..."}

items: So reads the introduction to The Powers of Etharis—one of the best researched (if somewhat out of date) historical tomes known. If the Grand Thaumatic Order of Archmages, or Thaumaturge in short, does exist, then they have gone to great lengths to conceal their presence.

The existence of a secret society of archmages seems only logical. Between divination and arcane history, such individuals are likely to not only be aware of each other, but also to share many common interests and compete over high-magic knowledge and artifacts. The fact that there is no war amongst the world's highest mages implies a certain level of cooperation or oversight.

Whether the widespread theories of the Thaumaturge having unleashed {@area the Weeping Pox|448|x} or banished Ulmyr's Gate to thwart a competing arcane organization have any grain of truth is unknown. Even if not directly involved, the members of the order should at least know about them.
What is certain is that every great magical event, whether calamitous or benevolent, has been either blamed on or attributed to the order, and in many cases both.

entries.name: History
entries: Tales of a secretive cabal of archmages date as far back as there is recorded history. Unexplained manipulations accomplished via great magical force have been recorded across every nation of Etharis.

One of the most infamous conspiracy theories is that the Thaumaturge unleashed the Weeping Pox, only to lose control of it. They then allegedly equipped Morbus Doctore with scientific and alchemical knowledge to combat the epidemic. Members of Morbus Doctore maintain that all their knowledge is the result of their own scientific research.

Not all tales of Thaumaturge are dark. The Order is widely assumed to oppose the Arcanist Inquisition, and many a novice mage has an unbelievable story describing their escape from Castinella. Of these, suddenly teleportation to a distant land is most common. Others tell of charmed guards suddenly undoing their bindings and ushering their captive out the door. The least told tale is of a roomful of guards and Inquisitors suddenly combusting or imploding into a bloody mess. Some mages claim these events were the sudden heightened manifestations of the victims' own powers, but the truth points to them having magical help.

There are also plenty of tales describing a mysterious mage appearing at just the right time to help a village slay a monster or depose a tyrant. Conversely, other tales describe a dark-robed figure at the tyrant's side. Such figures tend to appear from nowhere and disappear just as suddenly. If Thaumaturge exists, the one thing that can be certain is that they keep their motivations to themselves. If one came across a true recording of Thaumaturge history, they would likely find it full of tales too grand to be believed.

entries.name: The Last Sunset
entries: Two women, or two figures who looked very much like women, stood atop a forested hill watching the sunset. Below them, shadows were already creeping across the tiny Ostoyan village of Drebjik. One of the women was very tall and lanky, slender even for an elf. Silhouetted against the bleeding sky, her head looked like a drop of dew bending a blade of grass as she spoke to the other:

{@i "It is notable because it is the last one. For now,"} she said. She wore a flowing purple dress with an elaborate high collar that wrapped her long neck, something entirely out of place for the setting.

{@i "He succeeds then?"} responded the other. She wore a long velvet coat, dark burgundy and ornately embroidered. The laces of her knee-high leather boots matched perfectly. She regretted not bringing a hat, but did not turn her gaze away from the spectacular fading light.

{@i "Yes. And fails. That is how these things go—every success holds sparks of failure."} A short silence was interrupted by a sudden and brief cacophony of distant shouts, like a tavern door swung suddenly open and immediately shut. The women did not turn around. A man, or a figure that looked very much like a man, joined them on the precipice.
{@i "Do you think it wise passing through aether? It can bring attention,"} said the woman in the coat. Her voice was heavy, annoyed rather than truly concerned.
{@i "It doesn't,"} said the tall woman, matter-of-factly.

{@i "I see you've decided on your elven self today,"} the man said. He paused to recall her elven name: {@i "Taer Sa'ail?"} he continued, with a barely quizzical inflection. The man was large, though not as tall as her. He wore a once-white tunic of heavy wool and many buttons, the sort favored by soldiers. It was well-worn, though the dust and stains on it were recent. He held a short staff of polished obsidian, and the sun's dying light reflected in its glossy surface like a vein of red marble.
{@i "I appreciate my name being remembered,"} responded Taer Sa'il, {@i "almost as much as appreciate it being forgotten."} Her lips curled into a razor-thin smile.

{@i "One day you'll have to tell us your name,"} interjected the other woman. The man grunted softly but said nothing. She returned to the previous topic: {@i "You were saying. He succeeds? Should we have stopped it?"}
{@i "Could we have?"} mused the elf.
{@i "I sent agents,"} said the woman.
The man said nothing.
{@i "Common adventurers? Soldiers? Hardly a foil. They fail."}
{@i "And succeed. Every failure holds the sparks of success."}
{@i "Such is the power of witnesses. Else we wouldn't be here."}
The man said nothing.
{@i "Is that all we intend, to witness?"}
{@i "It's different for us. It's an event of power."}
{@i "Would you draw on it?"}

{@i "I would be careful. What he's doing is unprecedented. Also,"} the tall woman paused, looking for the right words. "{@i Kasimir's necromancy—it is not just potent. It's contagious. Corrupting."} She paused again. Her attention drifted to the last vestige of light, to the darkened village, to a thousand futures. The silence lingered.
The man said nothing.
When she came back to the present, the elf started anew: {@i "They'll call it the Darkfall."}

The man said nothing. He too had sent agents. Not mortal ones. He didn't ask whether they would succeed. What he came for would happen, and he was here to draw on it. He doubted the corruption could affect him, given what he was. Regardless, the power would be worth it.
The three of them remained on the hilltop as night swallowed the land. Waiting in silence. Watching through the darkness.

entries.name: Thaumaturge Today
entries: The Order is no less mysterious in the present day than they ever were. However, as is the case when the names of the powerful are well-known, the suspicion of membership is more concrete.
The Lady of Masks in Ostoya is suspected to be a member of the order, suggesting they actively oppose the {@area Crimson Court|05c|x}.

In {@book Bürach|GrimHollowCG24|3}, the sudden magical appearance of Martorius' castle has people speaking the name "Thaumaturge" out loud in suspicion. Many of these whispers are perhaps in the hope that "a greater good" has come to save the land from the Great Beast. Whatever Martorius's purpose, he has not shared it, and the Hearthkeepers are as suspicious of him as they are of monsters.
At least a dozen prominent figures are rumored to be Thaumaturge members. In addition to these high-profile individuals, two characteristics have recently emerged to be associated with the Order.

The first is the renewed and very keen interest in the Sacred Artifacts. While these legendary items have long been sought, the mysterious source of present-day interest has stirred up rumors. These include that the Order has the artifacts and is seeking knowledge of how to use them, that they have some of the artifacts and seek the rest, or that they've lost the artifacts and are now trying to recover them from an immortal enemy.

The second rumour is of the Thaumaturge's connection to the {@area Aether Kindred|36b|x}. Strange arcane traces have been reported continent-wide by independent mages and the Arcanist Inquisition. Encounters with aberrations believed to be aetheric in nature have become more common. These events have led to suspicions that some members of Thaumaturge may be Aether Kindred, their puppets, or carelessly using connections to dangerous aether planes for travel and other unstable magic.

entries.name: Goals
entries: To say that the Thaumaturge's goals are obscure is an understatement. To say that any such goals are entirely united is ludicrous. Different powers within the organization pursue their own objectives, often competing and occasionally conflicting with one another. Despite the difference, they generally fall into one of three categories.

entries.name: Power is Knowledge
entries: If nothing else, the Thaumaturge believe that understanding the cosmos is key to progress and survival. To this end, members of the Order are collectors and procurers. They seek out knowledge in the form of tomes, scrolls, and objects and places of power. They create arcane laboratories and experiments—sometimes on a grand scale.

The Order also seeks people who can further their ends. Talented mages, those born to strange powers, and those in touch with greater beings are of most use and recruited, knowingly or not, into the ranks.
Because each member of the Thaumaturge often serves their own interests, their pursuits and methods vary. At the same time, such individuality may hint at the seeker's areas of interest or expertise.

entries.name: A Hand Upon the Cosmic Wheel
entries: If the order had a gilded hall, etched into its entry gate would be the saying, {@i "When the hands of fate align, the hand guiding them remain unseen."} The Thaumaturge understand that whatever the grand schemes of the powerful are, the common people need to believe their lives are their own. In order to guide things, events must be allowed to proceed.

The Thaumaturge believe in subtle and invisible manipulation. Their schemes and goals can span decades, centuries, and if one is to believe the transcendent nature of some of their members, millennia.

One thing of paramount importance to understanding the Thaumaturge is they believe non-action can be as powerful as interference. Sometimes, the greatest influence is in letting things proceed just as they are.

entries.name: Every Life a Candle
entries: The Thaumaturge tend to hold that every life is its own flickering candle, precious but ultimately doomed to be snuffed. All lives can be meaningful, yet none matter more than the tapestry of the cosmos. The Thaumaturge's methods and conspiracies can appear heartlessly cruel in their treatment of human life, but they often serve to avoid an even greater calamity.

The Thaumaturge is believed to be the world's best defense against the great cataclysms such as {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24} in {@book Valika|GrimHollowCG24|7}, the Great Beast in the {@book Bürach Empire|GrimHollowCG24|3}, and the vampires of Ostoya. Yet, it is never clear in the moment if they are trying to save lives, sacrifice lives, or both. How many must die before the way forward is found? The trouble with planning on a cosmic scale is that common human lives don't last long enough to see the result.

entries.name: Leadership
entries: There is no known central leader of the Thaumaturge, but conspiracies and suspicions abound. The most common are:

items.name: Raakan el-Adakar
entries: An archmage scholar whose research founded the basis of magical knowledge in Etharis. He's said to have perished in {@area Cold Iron Keep|196|x}, but his body was never recovered, and several of his apprentices mysteriously disappeared after his fall. Rumors insist that he survived and is gathering allies.

items.name: The Ravencourt Keeper
entries: Not even his students know the true identity of the Master of the {@area Ravencourt Sanctuary|33e|x}. While a common ghastly theory is that the Master is an inhuman entity that consumes some of his students as payment for power, a more likely theory is that the Sanctuary is a recruiting ground for the Thaumaturge, and that the most fitting student of every class is recruited to serve in a secret war.

items.name: Pharazorthok
entries: A terrifying Aether god said to resemble an enormous tree made of tentacles and countless eyes, which grows into the material plane and spies on everything that happens. A common suspicion is that this "God of Knowing" controls the Thaumaturge's most senior members as minions.

items.name: Martorius
entries: The mysterious mage whose glorious castle mysteriously and quite suddenly appeared atop a mountain in the Bürach Empire. Powerful and secretive, he is certainly the most prolific suspect for the leadership position.

entries.name: The Council Theory
entries: There is no proof that the Order has a singular leader. It is entirely plausible that the magnitude of power and independence within the Thaumaturge ranks make singular rule impossible. The members of the Order may co-rule as a council, or even meet as a caucus, to consult and share rather than to command.

In addition to the aforementioned possible leaders, several other individuals are suspected to be involved. For many of these, the very idea that they even know each other is itself scandalous. While all these mages definitely exist in Etharis, their association with the Thaumaturge is only hinted at.

items.name: Mina Taliesin, "Lady of Masks."
entries: An archmage ruler of northern Ostoya and the enemy of the {@area Crimson Court|05c|x} in the south. While her face is always mysteriously covered, her immense magical powers are no secret.

items.name: Professor Franciszka Welgirson
entries: Disembodied arcane tutor to the Bürach Emperor, conspiracy theories swirl around her and what she tells the Emperor in private. The source of her magic knowledge is also subject to juicy palace gossip.

items.name: Odis
entries: This elven mage from the {@area Sarmar Academy|33f|x} now travels Valika, seeking out stories of {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24} and coldfire. While the elf seems entirely independent, his very specific area of inquiry—and his timing—suggest that he was pointed in this direction by a greater power.

items.name: Volarius of Aloran
entries: The "knight-sorcerer" emerged out of obscurity in {@book Charneault|GrimHollowCG24|5} to both popularity and suspicion. Claiming to act in an oath to a powerful spirit, Volarious secured a place on the Royal Council, and is unafraid to wield his influence. Jealous rumors suggest that he is a powerful foreigner who has in fact enslaved the spirit.

entries.name: The Orb Is in Your Court
entries: The lack of concrete information on the Thaumaturge is both thematic and intentional. The Order is a mercurial organization, and their details can be molded to each individual campaign.

If needed, they can be a villainous organization pulling the strings at a cosmic level. This can be especially useful in freeform campaigns, and can use a final behind-the-scenes villain to tie it all together.
Conversely, they can be a force for good, with mystery mages showing up at opportune moments to inform or transport the characters.
The Thaumaturge can also be many things in between—providing an arcane distraction with a one-time quest or a minor foil in the form of a rogue member.
If the campaign is leaning heavily into the arcane, they could be a more typical faction with ranks, progression rites, and even the bestowing of robes, wands, or hats.

entries.name: Agents
entries: Like the Ebon Syndicate, the Thaumaturge uses many pawns throughout the echelons of society to keep themselves hidden. True members of the order are immensely powerful {@creature Mage|XMM|Mages} or {@creature Archmage|XMM|Archmages} of every stripe.

There is no strict hierarchy or list of titles in the Thaumaturge ranks. Even among its senior members, titles are chaotic, with individuals using whatever they fancy—Archmage, Thaumaturge, Grand Arcanist, Lord Sorcerer, and Witch Queen have all been used. Most commonly, however, individuals of such stature use their names rather than a title.

The most elite mages each have dramatic individual abilities and specialties. They can go beyond using the most powerful known spells, and some can control time and space themselves, and call upon cosmic powers.

entries.name: Initiated Mages
entries: Having been initiated into the Thaumaturge is in itself a badge of honour and recognition of magical power. Such initiates are not to be trifled with. Skilled in deception, illusions, and arcane trickery, initiates can create chaos in social and combat situations. These crafty mages can create patches of obscure areas, switch places with their enemies, and become invisible before unleashing a custom-crafted cantrip or powerful spell.

items: Full-fledged Thaumaturge mages are arcane powerhouses. They can easily manipulate magic thrown at them, and teleport around the battlefield while throwing spells at their opponents. They can summon devastating rifts to unleash destructive energy or summon monstrous allies. Multiple mages can combine energies for even grander feats.

entries: A weak mind sees only obstacles.

entries.name: Important Locations
entries: There is no known central stronghold of the Thaumaturge, and it is likely that if the Order holds large meetings or councils, these take place in magically created or sealed locations.
In Etharis, there are several notable locations where mages dwell, and it is likely that at any given time, at least some Thaumaturge members are on premises.

entries.name: The Ravencourt Sanctuary
entries: The mysterious island off the shore of {@book Ostoya|GrimHollowCG24|4} is easy to suspect as the home of the Thaumaturge. Virtually nothing is known about it, other than it is protected by a supernatural Mistwall, and that it both produces and devours mages. If it is the home of the Thaumaturge or acts as a meeting place for them, those who visit there do so via a secret or magical means rather than by taking the ominous boat that comes every seven years.

entries.name: Erlefurt's Magister College
entries: The grandest of the magical colleges of the {@book Bürach Empire|GrimHollowCG24|3}, the Magister College welcomes accomplished mages and arcane scholars from all over Etharis. More than any similar institute, the reports of secretive goings on in its ancient marble halls are legendary, leading to speculation that there is more to the school than meets the eye. Night-time access to several areas of the college grounds is forbidden, and powerful arcane protections are in place.

entries.name: Sarmar Academy
entries: The Charneault academy, founded as a place for elves and humans to share magical knowledge, has been a contentious venture from the start, much like the relations between the two peoples. Today—as much to protect the academy itself as anything else—membership is strictly limited, and it has gained a reputation as a place of reclusion. Despite this, it is still a notable arcane institution and a premier source of knowledge on ancient magic.

entries.name: Castle Martorius
entries: Like the Ravencourt Sanctuary, not much is known about the goings-on at the mysterious castle. It belongs to a mage, it appeared magically, and those who attempt to enter without permission never return. Those who have visited the castle speak of magical wonders, and that the dimensions of its interior do not match those of its exterior. If ever there was a perfect place for mages to meet in Etharis, this would be it. Whether that is actually the case depends on the whims of Martorius himself.

entries.name: Cold Iron Keep
entries: Nestled into the high mountains of {@book Valika|GrimHollowCG24|7}, the mage-created prison keep is not a comfortable place for a meeting. Yet, it has a certain appeal for a potential Thaumaturge council. It is distant and relatively unobserved. It has magical safeguards. Most importantly, its cells are home to several "notable individuals" whose knowledge or very nature could be of interest to the arcanely inclined.

entries.name: Quest Hooks
entries: Characters run-ins with the Thaumaturge are certain to be mysterious, arcane in nature, and most definitely lack any confirmation that the group involved actually is the Thaumaturge.

After thwarting a mage of the order, the party is likely to run into a dead end or evidence of a vastly more powerful mage "cleaning things up." After assisting a Thaumaturge member, these mysterious benefactors—even when they are named and known—tend to dramatically disappear, leaving a note, a reward, and few answers.

entries.name: Artifact Retrieval
entries: The characters are tasked by a Morencian noble with the retrieval of a powerful magical artifact. The object is located in a sealed ancient crypt on the grounds of an abandoned estate on a tiny island a short sail from the city. Upon arrival, the characters discover that someone else has recently arrived on the island. Is this mysterious mage a competitor for the prize or the noble's previously sent minion who has succumbed to the crypt's curse?

entries.name: Sarmar Invitation
entries: One or more of the spellcasters in the party receive a letter addressed to them. A mysterious patron has taken notice of their deeds and arcane skills, and inviting them to visit the famed but obscure Sarmar Academy. Travel to the distant location is complicated, requiring passage through spirit-controlled forests and knight-controlled lands. Upon arrival, the characters are offered an arcane reward in exchange for dealing with a corrupted spirit.

entries.name: Ravencourt Summons
entries: The name of one of the party members has appeared on the list of mages invited to the Ravencourt Sanctuary. Strangely, seven years have not yet passed since the last boat, nor have any of the previous mages yet returned. Among the other mages waiting at the docks is Arexa Fallora, a previous student of the Sanctuary. Arexa is sworn to secrecy about her time there, but she proclaims this summons is a malicious ruse, and that the boat is not from the sanctuary. Someone powerful is trying to abduct would-be mages, and the only way to find out who is to get on the boat.

entries.name: Strange Body
entries: General Kavalla der Voran, a famed Bürach leader, has been assassinated under the strangest circumstances. Her corpse was found in her castle, wearing a full dress uniform, but her body was entirely hollowed out from the inside, as evidenced by looking through her empty eye sockets. The murder is clearly magical in nature, but no motive or message is apparent. The characters are asked to find those responsible and stop them.

entries.name: Castinellan Extraction
entries: Grand Arcanist Valeria Vistra Ostavore's brother, Contrall Ostavore, has been captured by the Arcanist Inquisition. During the capture, he killed four Inquisition guards, and is now slated for a very violent and public execution. Valeria offers the characters a choice of powerful Magic Items and access to a mysterious arcane secret in exchange for her brother's rescue and delivery beyond Castinellan borders.

entries.name: Vampiric Service
entries: The characters are contracted by the {@area Crimson Court|05c|x}. A powerful necromancer has "stolen" several notable vampires and is suspected to have fled to Castinella, intending to use them to wrack havoc. The Crimson Court is willing to help the characters track the rogue mage in exchange for returning the vampires safely to them. News of brutal murders in Castinella has already begun to spread.

entries.name: Jailed Shaman
entries: Ertakon Cad'erval, the leader of a tiny remote enclave of Valikan elves, has sought out the characters' assistance. The enclave's shaman, Alfreja, has been imprisoned at {@area Cold Iron Keep|196|x}. According to Erkaton, the shaman keeps shadowy monsters away from their enclave, and by extension nearby villages, by absorbing them into herself and slowly suffocating them. Occasionally, the monsters break loose, which is what led to the inciting incident of her capture. Erkaton begs the characters to treat with Clan Mithra and find a way to return the shaman.

entries.name: Shadowsteel Experimentation
entries: Jurnalha Stohl, a dwarven ironsmith from Stehlenwald, asks the characters to investigate a mine, as a shipment of ore she ordered hasn't arrived. On their way there, the characters meet a veiled figure who tells them to kill everyone they find in the mine—for they are contaminated with fiendish power from their work with the Shadowsteel that is secretly mined there.

When the characters arrive, everything seems normal. The miners show no sign of anything being wrong, even under magical scrutiny. The mining captain says the shipment is late because they struck a rich vein of ore, and they wanted to excavate it before sending the shipment.

In reality, these miners are impostors working for the Cult of {@deity Tormach|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}. They recently invaded, killing or imprisoning the real mining crew, who were indeed suffering madness and mutation from working with the virulent ore. The characters can uncover the ruse, deal with the ore thieves, and find the captive miners who are being changed by the ore.

entries.name: Faction Rewards
entries: The Thaumaturge predominantly reward the party with Magic Items, particularly of the exotic and strange variety. These items could have come from other planes or be particularly dangerous, like Shadowsteel. The Thaumaturge also provide spell scrolls, Potions, or access to magic services such as creating a Magic Item, enchanting equipment, casting high-level spells or curses, providing access to libraries or arcane facilities, or teaching new spells.

Powerful mages can allow access to ally strongholds or to mysterious areas such the Ravencourt Sanctuary. They might also provide access to a network of teleportation circles between major cities or other means of magical transport. One of the greatest assets they can provide is information. When it comes to ancient rites, the planes, or mythical beasts, if information exists, it is likely accessible to the mages of the Thaumaturge.

Finally, the Thaumaturge might also offer education, allowing characters to progress in arcane studies of all sorts, gain introductions to mentors or patrons, and receive access to magical feats and boons. More nefarious mages seek out minions or subjects for experimentation or transformation.

entries.name: Watchers of the Faithful

entries.title: {@i Though the gods no longer grace the heavens, the Arch Seraphs still guide the way}.

entries: Born in the fires of {@area the Gods' End|351|x}, the Watchers of the Faithful rose to prominence first in {@book Castinella|GrimHollowCG24|6} as part of its theocratic government. Priests, paladins, chaplains, and other clergy rallied to the remaining Arch Seraphs to hold the fabric of society together. Throwing their faith behind the Arch Seraphs, the major churches reformed into a large organization dedicated to preserving the old faiths in a new form.

This young faction is utterly devoted to Sagrado Valieda's Eternal Dogma: revere the Arch Seraphs, follow their laws as handed down by the Divine Speaker, and destroy all that is unholy and evil in their sight through any means necessary. Where the Arcanist Inquisition is militant in rooting out corruption, the Watchers instead pursue Valieda's mandate to fight for the Arch Seraphs with fiery words. Swords are well and good in defense of the faith, but the real battle is in the hearts and minds of the faithful.

Through preaching and persuasion, the Watchers seek to make the change in the world that they believe the Arch Seraphs desire. Edicts declaring heresy inflame the Inquisition to hunt for monsters or tainted spellcasters. A public proclamation that a government is straying from the true path causes citizens to rise up and force change. Decrees are issued creating new ecclesiastical rules that the faithful embrace over secular laws issued by less religious rulers. Slowly but surely, the Watchers guide the world to a better place.

items.name: History
entries: After Sagrado Valieda unified the {@book Castinellan Provinces|GrimHollowCG24|6}, he formed a military theocracy devoted to {@deity Empyreus|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}, placing the High Priest of the Sword Saint in charge of the military and creating the Arcanist Inquisition to control and limit the use of magic in the provinces. The Unifier was a brilliant tactician and made the military strong, but it was clear that while the faithful of {@deity Empyreus|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24} had the fire and verve to rule a new nation, they were ill-suited to some portions of the ecclesiastical bureaucracy. Fortunately, the clergy of the other Arch Seraphs were ready to help.

The Unifier asked the other High Priests of each Arch Seraph to come together and form a covenant that would become the Watchers of the Faithful. From among the High Priests of each Arch Seraph, they would elect the Divine Speaker who would be first among equals. It was no surprise that Sagrado Valieda was chosen to lead them.

entries.name: Spreading the Message
entries: Thus, the Watchers of the Faithful began, integrated into the Castinella theocratic government almost by default, and driven to spread to other nations by {@area the Gods' End|351|x}. The Gods' End legitimized the Watchers' control of the government and furthered their outreach to the faithful in other nations, particularly the {@book Bürach Empire|GrimHollowCG24|3}. The Watchers of the Faithful ordered its missionaries to preach the light of the Arch Seraphs directly to the people of all nations, denounce corrupt clergy, speak out against sacrilegious laws and the rulers that make them, and condemn the exploitation of the poor. To the shock of those in power, the medaling of the Watcher's evangelists found support among their citizens. Thousands flocked to the Watchers, with many going on pilgrimages to far-flung areas to spread the word of righteousness through diplomacy or warfare. Money continues to flow from these pilgrims into the coffers of the Grand Temple.

Quickly the Watchers of the Faithful have become known as champions of the people, and to foreign powers, they are far more dangerous than the Arcanist Inquisition. The Watchers' evangelists use faith to motivate followers, making the faction more and more powerful as it gathers legions of people to its side. The Arcanist Inquisition might attack a certain settlement accused of heresy or dangerous arcana. The Watchers' watchful priests can enrage an entire regional populace into acts of religious fervor and violence. This is the power that keeps the leaders of other countries awake at night.

entries.name: External Relations
entries: The Watchers of the Faithful are found in all the nations of Etharis. They have tentative alliances with the Hearthkeepers in the Bürach Empire, and the Chapter Knights of Charneault, but the Watchers' unyielding doctrine makes such alliances terse at best. The Watchers send missionaries to {@book Valika|GrimHollowCG24|7} to convert those hardy folk away from the Primordials and into worship of the Arch Seraphs. The faction detests the undying {@area Crimson Court|05c|x} in {@book Ostoya|GrimHollowCG24|4}, but their relationship with the Magocracy of Raevo is tense due to the province's embrace of the arcane, so these natural allies are denied them.

The Watchers have complicated relations with other factions as well. The Watchers incense the Arcanist Inquisition, their only true allies, to undertake various holy missions across Etharis to stamp out all that is, in Cirdanal's words, "corrupt, heretical, iniquitous, or displeasing to the eyes of Heaven." The Watchers pursue the Augustine Trading Company for its efforts at weaponizing monsters, the Ebon Syndicate for its corruption, the Ostoyan nobility for falling to vampirism, and the Valikan tribes for raiding, worshipping the elements, and more raiding. They save a special hatred for the arcanists of the Thaumaturge. They claim it's because their magic spawns abominations and tempts others to heresy, but for some sects, it is their competition for the knowledge and ability to guide the world that is the Thaumaturge's true sin. Any information about the Thaumaturge is quickly passed on to the Inquisition with the Divine Speaker's compliments.

entries.name: Goals
entries: The six different religious sects publicly agree to the three unifying principles of Sagrado Valieda's Eternal Dogma, and internally, to a fourth goal that they see as the eventual outgrowth of their successful guiding hand.

entries.name: Revere the Arch Seraphs
entries: {@i Place your faith in the Arch Seraphs and they will hold you in their hands, protect you from harm, and raise you to the light}.

The Watchers of the Faithful are first and foremost, a religious organization. The constant trials the people of Etharis face can only be overcome through the grace of the Arch Seraphs. The most important facet of the Watchers' holy mission is the conversion of as many people as possible to the faith of the Arch Seraphs. The faithful can then be protected and guided to a new golden age.

entries.name: Follow the Sacred Laws
entries: {@i Sin and corruption are tools of Arch Daemons and their heretics lead the faithful unto chaos}.
The Arch Seraphs have a plan, a plan that is whispered to the High Priests of the Watchers of the Faithful.

Each High Priest imparts the sacred laws as received from their Arch Seraph to the rest of the conclave and from there to all the faithful. These laws tend to be broad but unyielding; honor the priests, uplift the downtrodden, make war on the Arch Seraphs' enemies, avenge heresies against the Arch Seraphs, suffer not the warlock to live, find purity in being of service, or eat not when thy neighbor is hungry.

entries.name: Destroy All that is Unholy
entries: {@i Suffer not the Unholy, for they hate the blessings of the Arch Seraphs and would cause you harm}.

While the Watchers of the Faithful disdain those who stray, they seethe with hatred for true monsters. Their followers are commanded to hunt any creatures that challenge the power of the Arch Seraphs and the blessed order, such as aberrations, daemons, fey, primordials, or undead. Some sects interpret the command to hunt the unholy more liberally and include heretics, anyone whose form is being corrupted by a transformation, criminals, or even magical artifacts and books that the sect believes to be blasphemous.

The Watchers of the Faithful scorn Arcane and Primal spellcasters, but they rarely hunt spellcasters unless their actions are otherwise profane. The faction's purpose is broader than the Arcanist Inquisition. Information about such casters is passed on to the Inquisition if they are deemed a threat.

entries.name: Theocratic Supremacy
entries: {@i From the whispers of the Arch Seraphs to the High Priest's words shall blossom a holy kingdom of the Light}.

While not shared publicly, the Watchers' leadership has a fourth goal: the subjugation of the world's governments through organized faith. By spreading faith in the Arch Seraphs, the Watchers of the Faithful hope to unify the common folk, and when that unified people rise up, they can and will force political change with new governments taking direction from the Divine Speaker and the High Priests.

You are blessed to hear the words of Sagrado Valieda in this dark time of temptation and wickedness. Let not magic's corruption taint your spirit. Only through the Eternal Dogma can you live a righteous life, for all other paths lead to evil and damnation. Open your heart to this truth and you shall know contentment.

entries.name: Leadership
entries: The Watchers of the Faithful are split into six sects, each following the teachings of a specific Arch Seraph. Each elects its own High Priest, who is said to commune directly with the Arch Seraph of their sect. Then they collectively elect a Divine Speaker from one sect to lead the faction.

For such a sacred institution, the conclave to elect a Divine Speaker is a surprisingly political affair involving the trading of arguments, favors, and threats as each High Priest tries to prove they have the support of the faction's members, and the Arch Seraphs themselves. While the Divine Speaker can theoretically come from any sect, in the faction's history, there have only been two that were not followers of {@deity Empyreus|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}. Once for {@deity Zabriel|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}, and once for {@deity Solyma|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}, and neither held the seat for more than a few years before it was reclaimed by an Empyreusian.

Day to day decisions are made by the Divine Speaker with the advice and guidance of the Council of High Priests. When the Council must decide the direction of the faction or there is a disagreement over policy, the six High Priests vote after a period of negotiation. Ties always go to the side the Divine Speaker voted with.

entries.name: Nola Cirdanal
entries: The current Divine Speaker is Nola Cirdanal, who is of course a follower of {@deity Empyreus|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}. She is a strong, middle-aged Castinellan, who prizes courage and persistence. She favors deploying large numbers of evangelists to spread the Light of the Arch Seraphs to all peoples of Etharis, regardless of the wishes of those people's rulers. Those rulers that complain loudest tend to find their realm plagued by the loudest firebrands the Watchers can field, accompanied by the most heavily armed guards.
