The language used to communicate might require a spoken phrase, a complex gesture, or a particular object. These combinations of verbal, somatic, and material components are called "spells." When mages cast spells, they weaken the border separating Numena from the Material Plane in order to draw and shape numen into a magical effect as woven by the spell.

Wizards learn the language of spells through study and years of careful practice. Learning to speak to the arcane is immensely difficult, so that even the most powerful wizards must utilize a spellcasting focus and a spellbook. Spells more complicated than cantrips require persistent study from the pages of a spellbook to ensure the components of the spell are performed accurately each day.

Those capable of casting spells without study or memorization are called sorcerers. For these casters, touching Numena is like flexing a muscle. For others, called warlocks, it's as if the knowledge or arcane spells is whispered into their minds from an unknown entity beyond the Material Plane.

Several theories have been put forth on what Numena actually is. One states that Numena is a word encompassing any realm other than the Material Plane. This hypothesis attempts to reconcile perceived differences between arcane, divine, and primal magic, arguing the process of casting a spell is the same for each, except for where the numen originates.

Other scholars believe Numena isn't a realm at all. Some suggest it's an invisible force not dissimilar to gravity, but which is imperceptible to all besides mages. Another theory infers that Numena is itself a living entity attempting to shape our dimension to match its own. This premise, though terrifying, explains why spells are so difficult to keep in mind: knowledge of their components slips from the mind like oily aberrations.

entries.name: Societal Mistrust
entries: Sadly, Raakan el-Adakar did not live to complete his research. His own thesis was that Numena was a traversable realm, and for his final magical experiment he opened a portal to some magical plane. When Raakan returned some years later from whichever realm he'd visited, be it Numena or some other distant reality, he was unintelligible. He spent his final days confined in {@area Cold Iron Keep|196|x} under the watchful eye of guards, who claimed to suffer bizarre visions and disturbing ailments until the day Raakan perished.

Society has always had a divided and suspicious view of arcane magic. Though its power is unrivalled, its mystery, unpredictability, and undeniable dangers make it feared and hated by those who cannot wield it. To rulers, magic represents a threat to their authority. To commoners, mages are a mutual enemy who can be blamed for all the world's unexplained troubles.

There is certainly merit in fearing the arcane. Raakan el-Adakar's fate is one among countless cautionary tales. Casting spells is fundamentally the practice of trying to speak a language nobody truly understands, and the unintended results of experimentation have been devastating, such as when countless souls were lost in the instant the mages of Ulmyr's Gate upended their city into the {@area Ethereal Plane|344|x}.

Even among arcane scholars there are fears that magic permanently thins the veil between the Material Plane and {@area Numena|34c|x}. Each spell cast is like picking at the threads of a tapestry until holes begin to form. If true, what horrors could have wriggled through? This anxiety is the basis of a theory that spellcasters are to blame for the advent of the {@area Great Beast|440|x}. Though entirely unproven, such a rumor has made those who wield magic even further detested by society.

Characters capable of wielding magic should be wary of when and where they choose to demonstrate their talents. Even friendly acquaintances may turn fearful or even hostile when confronted with the arcane. To a mage, the misunderstandings of the uneducated can be as dangerous as any magic.

A mortician willing to help a murder investigation might flee in terror upon witnessing a wizard speak with the dead. A bard's audience could quickly turn into an angry mob if they believe they've been enchanted by a bardic spell.

A wise mage's greatest asset is caution. Rumors of their magic could easily reach the ears of a truly dangerous foe, such as an agent of the Arcanist Inquisition. However, cultures across Etharis do treat their spellcasters with differing attitudes. In some corners of the continent a mage may yet find sanctuary.

entries.name: Regional Attitudes
entries: The attitudes of the population and the powerful toward arcane magic vary greatly from region to region. A wizard celebrated in one place may be put to death in another.

entries.name: Bürach
entries: In the {@book Bürach Empire|GrimHollowCG24|3}, magic is considered a means to project power, instill obedience, gather information, and maintain order. For these reasons, magic is highly valued, yet mages themselves are heavily regulated and their freedom restricted.

Those who cannot cast magic fear it. Therefore, strict laws govern the lives of mages across the empire. Licenses are given to mages who graduate their college, which stipulates not only which spells they're permitted to cast but also under what circumstances. Sorcerers and warlocks who operate outside official sanctions are outlaws and may become hunted if they're feared to be dangerous.

With the coming of the {@area Great Beast|440|x}, magic is now treated with even greater suspicion, as some have come to suspect that the entity was the result of an arcane experiment gone horribly wrong. The Hearthkeepers have not denied this notion, as it deflects their own culpability on the matter.

entries.name: Ostoya
entries: The {@book Ostoyan|GrimHollowCG24|4} view of magic is more varied. In the provinces still under the sway of the Crimson Court, the lower classes, still incapable of understanding the arcane, fear magic. They stay clear of magic and its practitioners to avoid inciting the jealousy and ire of the nobility.

The {@area Crimson Court|05c|x} makes it clear to all its subjects that the use of magic is theirs by right; anyone who uses magic must either become a servant of the Crimson Court or be labeled as a threat and destroyed. Thus, one most often finds Ostoyan mages in the Crimson Court's employ taking part in wars against the rebellious province of Raevo and the Bürach Empire.

In the province of Raevo, magic is by and large an accepted part of daily life. The workshops and markets of Castalore host wizards leading around magical constructs or trading occult objects among themselves. All this is done under the watchful eye of the {@area Ravencourt Sanctuary|33e|x}. Magic is valued to maintain sovereignty against the power of the Crimson Court and the forces of the Bürach Empire, as well as to repel raids from the marauding clans of {@book Valika|GrimHollowCG24|7}.

entries.name: Charneault
entries: In the {@book Charneault Kingdom|GrimHollowCG24|5}, the elves treat magic as an integral part of their lives. They hold skilled mages in high esteem, even as they tread cautiously around the cost of seeking greater magical power. While elven magecraft has achieved great magical benefits for its people, the price for missteps is often paid by the entirety of the kingdom, as is the case with the dark mist.

In the human-controlled lands of Charneault, attitudes vary greatly. Mages are treated with respect in more learned circles, but among Knight Chapters they are met with suspicion, and in the case of the Severe Templars, outright hostility. These human-centric forces hold elven magic accountable for the suffering endured by the populace, leading them to forego most arcane abilities in favor of strength in arms and divine fervor.

entries.name: Castinella
entries: It's worth noting the {@book Castinellan Provinces'|GrimHollowCG24|6} stance toward magic: deplorably, the theocracy is hostile to all forms of arcane or primal magic. It is the will of {@deity Empyreus|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}, they claim, that their people wage a righteous war against all magic users and those who harbor them.

To undertake this task, the Arcanist Inquisition has assembled a host of knights and common soldiers under the command of clerics and paladins. Backed by the power of belief in the inherent corruptive nature of arcane magic, they call on divine magic to locate mages and neutralize them.

As such, magic-using arcanists must be careful when entering and traveling within the {@book Castinellan Provinces|GrimHollowCG24|6}, lest they be arrested, imprisoned, and very likely put to death by the Arcanist Inquisition. Mages cannot expect sympathy from the common folk, as most peasants hold all magic as witchcraft and devilry, the power of demons brought to Etharis.

The Castinellans have yet to move against nations that employ magic, such as the Bürach Empire and the Charneault Kingdom. But should the theocracy grow more powerful, a "righteous war" would certainly be in the cards. It would only take one international incident of arcane treachery—real, imagined, or staged—to incite a holy war between Castinella and a neighboring nation.
Magic is Life. Life is Magic. There is no other truth.

entries.name: Colleges of Magic
entries: A force as potentially dangerous as magic has the ability to kill the powerful, alter the destiny of nations, and even destroy worlds. With that ever-present danger in mind, authorities across the nations of Etharis attempt to keep magic regulated to some degree.

One method of regulation is to control the teaching of magic via colleges, schools, universities, or other institutions of learning. In addition to memorizing spells and arcane theory, pupils are taught to respect the social hierarchy their nation places a mage within.
The most prevalent and relevant of the arcane colleges are listed here.

entries.name: Erlefurt Colleges
entries: Western Nordenland is the center for arcane learning in the {@book Bürach Empire|GrimHollowCG24|3}. The leaders of the nation wish to make their capital city, Erlefurt, the preeminent locale for magic users and other arcanists in Etharis. They have a saying: "Keep your friends close, your enemies closer, and the wizards safely under your thumb."

The capital city of Erlefurt has the highest number of magical colleges anywhere in the continent, with the Magister College being the oldest and most venerable of all. There is a healthy, and sometimes unhealthy, competition between the various colleges to recruit and retain the most promising students and the most powerful and revered instructors.

It's difficult for an aspiring mage to enter any of the Erlefurt Colleges, but the Magister College proves itself to be exceptionally stringent. The Magister chooses only the best among its applicants—not merely those with high magical aptitude, but also those most likely to bring greater honor to the school.

The Erlefurt Colleges see magic as a force that must be harnessed with wisdom, the kind that only those trained in the way of the Erlefurt Mages possess. As such, all students must swear to follow a set of rigid laws, listed in a tome called the {@i Magician's Codex}.
The primary laws in the Codex include the following:

items: Only mages who pass their final tests of the Erlefurt Colleges can carry the title of Erlefurt Mage.
An Erlefurt Mage shall defend their College against all who would despoil them or steal their secrets.
Only the Erlefurt Colleges may train a student. All students must be registered and approved by the Colleges.
All magical experiments must be registered and approved by the Colleges.

entries: Any Erlefurt wizard who breaks these codes is hunted down by the Enforcers, a group of elite abjurers highly adept at subduing one of their own. Once captured, offenders are taken to the Magician's Court and judged by the Grand Mage. If found guilty, transgressors may be magically imprisoned or even executed.

As for other arcane institutions who don't subscribe to these tenets, they are treated with contempt and even outright hostility. On the rare occasion that a mage trained at another college is deemed worthy to grace the halls of an Erlefurt college, they are watched carefully and forced to swear to the {@i Magician's Codex}.

As there are no formal magical colleges in the Grarjord or other parts of Valika, members of the {@book Valikan Clans|GrimHollowCG24|7} send promising students to Erlefurt for tutelage. Valikan students are considered oddities by their Bürach peers, even more so than students hailing from other nations of Etharis.

entries.name: Ravencourt Sanctuary
entries: East of Raevo's shores, hidden among the mists of its enchanted isle, the Ravencourt Sanctuary trains its mages in its own academy. Unlike its rival the Magister College, the Sanctuary believes that magic may only grow through freedom. Thus, the Ravencourt Sanctuary has few laws to guide the actions of its mages. Their primary rule is that the mages must keep all the secrets of the Sanctuary safe. Apart from that, a mage who successfully graduates from the Sanctuary is free to do what they will and teach whomever they want.

Despite their seeming lack of laws, the punishment for breaking the Sanctuary's primary rule is both harsh and unequivocal. Before they leave the Sanctuary, a Ravencourt mage swears a vow to protect their academy's secrets. They write their name on a page of an enchanted book, finishing their signature with a drop of their own blood. This creates a highly potent curse: if the graduate should break the vow, the page tears itself from the book and burns. The mage consequently dies an unspeakable death.

Mages from Erlefurt view those trained at the Ravencourt Sanctuary as dangerous occultists. Though their talents are undeniable, the apparent freedom with which Ravencourt magicians wield their magic marks them as a threat to the cautious spellcasters of Erlefurt. The secrecy of the Sanctuary also incites rumors of daemonology and pact-making within its walls.

The two colleges are technically at war. Mages from either side have standing orders to annihilate each other on sight, though this is rarely practiced. If the current stalemate between {@book Bürach|GrimHollowCG24|3} and {@book Ostoya|GrimHollowCG24|4} were nudged once more into war, perhaps members of the two mage schools could be propelled into open conflict.

entries.name: Sarmar Academy
entries: Having lived for thousands of years as a people, the elves are no beginners when it comes to magic. In their capital of {@area Tol Leyemil|112|x}, they have established their own college for wizards: Sarmar Academy, famous for its bards, mystics, and druids.

The Sarmar mages teach that magic is learned and controlled through careful observation. By watching and listening to the natural world, the elves believe the Spirits of Nature whisper the language of spells to those with the aptitude to listen. The school teaches their students to pay close attention to detail and focus on what they can sense. Before a single cantrip is cast, pupils spend months learning meditation, observation, and introspection. This emphasis on patience makes teaching slow. Magical education at Sarmar can last decades. For elves, of course, that's the blink of an eye—not so for the shorter-lived species.

Being an entirely elven city that forbids non-elves from entering, only elves may enroll in Sarmar Academy. The exception is Aemanyir House, a branch of the Academy located outside the city limits. This program was launched by an elven mage named Alren Corynthios as an experiment in teaching the elven way of magic to non-elves. Most of the Sarmar academics consider this a waste of time, but Alren's persistence and connections with the elven nobility have kept the program going for many years, if just barely.

One area of contention within the Sarmar Academy is its loyalties. The tension between the human rulers of {@book Charneault|GrimHollowCG24|5} and the elven leaders of Tol Leyemil makes humans wary of how Sarmar's mages would be used if the elves ever decided to push the question of independence. The presence of Aemanyir House is a slight reassurance to the humans that not all of Sarmar's mages would join a secession movement.

entries.name: Renegade Colleges
entries: There are several unsanctioned or unrecognized colleges in Etharis that claim to teach magic. As they tend to produce subpar mages (according to sanctioned magical schools), they're looked upon with scorn. They're commonly viewed as potential sources of magical mishaps, as without proper guidance, these mages may cause irreparable harm to the reputation of all mages—or worse, bring about a magical catastrophe.

The Erlefurt Mages have made it their protocol to capture any so-called renegade wizards and bring them to {@area Erlefurt|33d|x} for processing and possible reeducation. Those who refuse this polite entreaty are viewed as enemy combatants and dealt with harshly.

Castinella, with its omnipresent Arcanist Inquisition, has no official arcane college. Underground education centers in the land secretly provide instruction to arcane-sensitive individuals, hoping to help them control and harness their powers before they are noticed by the inquisitors. While this form of magical education would be considered basic and remedial by the standards of formal colleges, it is no less important to those trying to control their magical abilities within a hostile nation.

Magical training in {@book Valika|GrimHollowCG24|7} is handled in a much different manner. Clan Rune treats arcane magic much like any other skill: masters take on apprentices who learn the basics of the craft as well as the moral and social imperatives of using numen. After completing their apprenticeship, young Valikan mages are free to travel to other settlements in Valika to practice. Once they have gained the full experience of a life practicing magic, they might become a master and take on apprentices of their own.

Bardic training in Valika teaches practitioners a different type of arcane magic, with additional instruction in performance, history, mythology, poetics, and more. This training ranges from individual instruction to small schools with a master skald teaching students the skills necessary to act as a bard or skald.
A drop of blood. A lifetime of certainty.

entries.name: The Planes
entries: Almost every person to have lived was born on the Material Plane—the physical world where Etharis exists. A mortal realm bound by physics and nature, it was once thought to contain everything that is and was. Nevertheless, some have looked to the stars, or through the perilous cracks in reality, and wondered what else is out there.

Very little is understood of what lies beyond the Material Plane. Some realms are closely adjoined to the mortal one, such as the {@area Realm of Faerie|343|x} or the {@area Primordial Realms|345|x}, whose influence is felt frequently in Etharis. Other realities are far more alien. Dark truths lurk within the cosmos, and exploring outside the Material Plane is always perilous.

Talented mages, victims of misfortune, and those who dream of the {@area Aether Kindred|36b|x} occasionally bear witness to what lies in other planes of existence. Such experiences are generally horrifying, and far too often mind shattering. Mortals are small, and outside the veil of reality are things mortals aren't meant to comprehend.
Those who've never seen past the mortal realm might imagine the endless expanse of the multiverse and feel wonder. Those who have feel only terror.

entries.name: Planar Adventures in Dark Fantasy
entries: Tales of magical portals leading to wondrous realms are common in fantasy, especially in stories where magic frequently intersects with daily life. Faeries step from their magical kingdom to elicit aid from brave knights. Eclectic characters travel together across the stars, sailing aboard a ship on the astral winds to visit all types of peculiar realms.

Such tales are filled with exciting adventures, but they struggle to capture the dark fantasy atmosphere of Grim Hollow. The more planar travel becomes part of the everyday, the less forbidding it seems. Therefore, excursions into other planes of existence should be infrequent and occur only in the direst of circumstances.

In a dark fantasy story, the planes serve to explore themes of cosmic horror and existential dread more than providing exotic locations to visit. There are no welcoming cities or safe havens in the depths of other realities. The planes are not a frontier for exploration. They're a terrifying and crushing ocean threatening to drown the Material Plane, a reminder of the insignificance of mortal existence.

entries.name: Realm of Faerie
entries: The Realm of Faerie, sometimes referred to as the Feywild, is stitched into the fabric of the Material Plane. Its roads are hidden, like the threads on the underside of a tapestry, so that only faeries can easily find their way. It's a realm of wilderness, magic, mystery, and contradictions. The same road doesn't always lead to the same place. Stepping through a knot of trees might turn summer to winter, or diving beneath the surface of a blue lake might emerge into a glittering cave.

Entering the Feywild is often imperceptible to mortals, as gateways can be hidden in plain sight: a narrow forest ravine, the freestanding archway of an elven ruin, or a circle of toadstools. Each could be a door to the Realm of Faerie, where the wilderness blooms with a surreal beauty and commoners feel as if they're walking through a dream—whether they're experiencing a flowering paradise or a gloom-filled nightmare.

The Realm of Faerie appears differently depending on the seasonal court that holds sway in that area. The Spring Court is the most beautiful, with dazzling golden citadels rising from blooming forests and flower-laden hills. Regions belonging to the Summer Court are always humid. The sun never sets and the manic Spirits of Nature demand tithes from passing travelers. The Winter Court is filled with gloom and snow. War-hungry goblins huddle around bonfires, making their plans to invade Etharis as soon as autumn wanes. The Autumn Court is filled with rot. Cunning sprites play cruel and deadly tricks on mortals and other fey.

Druids or rangers can sometimes navigate the Realm of Faerie in safety. Doing so in small traveling bands can be an expedient way to journey through Etharis, if no less dangerous. Groups disappearing into the overgrown depths of the Grove Maze might reemerge from the wintery Skuggiskogr Forest, if appropriate tributes are made to the Spirits of Nature along the way. New portals can be opened with magic and woodland spirits conjured from within. However, only talented spellcasters should dare try. Offending the Spirits of Nature can be as dire as upsetting an Arch Seraph or a Fiend.

Druids don't speak of the Realm of Faerie as separate from the mortal one. Instead, it's described as deep corners of wilderness, folded and concealed by the magic of the Fey Courts. The elves believe the entirety of the {@area Material Plane|341|x} was once a paradise crafted by the Archfey, who retreated into their strongholds in the Feywild to escape the violence during the {@area Era of Expansion|038|x}.

entries.name: Ethereal Plane
entries: The Ethereal Plane exists behind the veil of reality. It's thought of as a river that encircles the Material Plane and is stepped through to reach all other planes of existence. It overlaps the mortal realm but cannot be perceived without the use of magic. There are no natural portals that exist to reach it, though the Ethereal Plane is commonly used by powerful mages to teleport or move unseen.

The Great College of Ulmyr's Gate once tried to establish a permanent portal into the mists of the Ethereal Plane. Instead, the entire city was torn through a rift in reality and presumed to have been obliterated, along with the thousands of people who called it their home. In the years since, the disembodied have emerged. These poor souls, perpetually caught between realities, are a reminder of the dangers of trifling with planar travel.

There's no knowledge of how many other realities the Ethereal Plane connects with. Eldritch horrors and {@area Aether Kindred|36b|x} are said to have been witnessed within, preying upon travelers who've spent too long outside the bounds of their mortal existence.
The veil between the Ethereal Plane and the mortal one is fragile, like fabric. It can be difficult to pierce without accidentally creating a tear, leading to unintended consequences.

entries.name: Primordial Realms
entries: {@book The Valikan Clans|GrimHollowCG24|7} believe that the {@area Material Plane|341|x} is cradled between the four Primordial Realms of Air, Fire, Water, and Earth. Where these roiling elements crash together, they form the mortal realm.

Each of the primordial planes has its own mythological name, though modern scholars tend to simply refer to them as the Elemental Plane of Air, Fire, Water, and Earth, respectively. Portals to these realms may exist where their element is strongest, such as the depths of a volcano or deep beneath the sea. Such locations are perilous for mortals, however. Access is usually possible only through magic.

entries.name: Flamebourne
entries: A realm of unbearable heat, Valikan mythologies describe the Elemental Plane of Fire as an ashen waste broken by mountains and veined by rivers of lava. Burning embers drift upon the air, scorching the lungs of any who breathe. Cloth smolders and skin melts. No mage or druid recommends visiting for longer than a few moments. Efreeti and fire elementals hunt mortals who stray into the realm of {@deity Lady Morakesh|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}, especially if arriving without invite.
Salamanders and mjorks, who spread fiery destruction through the mortal realm, are said to have smoldered up from Flamebourne.

entries.name: The Endless Sea
entries: Deep beneath the waters of Etharis exists the Endless Sea, deeper even than the realm of the laneshi. Lurking within that frigid, silent darkness are horrors which mortal eyes were never meant to witness. But mostly, there is loneliness—a vast, watery, emptiness which suits {@deity Alondo|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}, primordial of ice and water.
All rivers, lakes, and oceans in Etharis eventually flow back to the Elemental Plane of Water.

entries.name: Stonereach
entries: Everything in Etharis is built upon Stonereach, the Elemental Plane of Earth. Exploring the depthless caves of the Rock-Teeth Mountains or the Grey Spine may eventually lead there, where the rock awakens into an elemental or a stoneborn troll.

Those tight and twisting caverns are rich with precious minerals. The challenge is escaping from the deep subterranean labyrinth, where no aid can be expected from the stubborn denizens of that realm.

entries.name: The Whispering Void
entries: The Elemental Plane of Air is a vast and whispering void. The breeze tears at clothes and exposed skin. The biting cold cuts to the bone. There's no light here, and flames are quickly extinguished by the cruel breath of the wind.

An old Valikan tale once told that murderers and dishonored warriors were banished to the Whispering Void after death. Here, they're tormented at {@deity Ilhara|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}'s grim pleasure.

entries.name: Netherworld
entries: Scriptures write about the Netherworld fleetingly. What is said claims that these hellish dominions were created in the aftermath of {@area The Aetheric War|350|x}, when the {@area Heavens|34b|x} themselves were ruptured by the {@area Aether Kindred|36b|x}. Legions of seraphs were cast down by the death of their gods. Amongst the flaming wreckage of their former domains, leaderless and subsisting in darkness, these celestial beings twisted into fiends. Many of the strongest among these fallen angels, the Arch Daemons, tried ascending to the role of their former deities by creating a variety of twisted realms collectively known as the Netherworld.

For example, the death of Myria, goddess of joy, filled mortals with the existential dread of their own mortality. Pleasure in the common joys of life waned. {@deity Sitri|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}, the Arch Daemon of hedonism, tried to fill this void with every carnal delight that could be offered to mortals upon the {@area Material Plane|341|x}. His realm within the Netherworld is a grand pleasure house overgrown with polyps of flesh, like mold, distorted faces and limbs visible among the writhing mass. This fleshy amalgam is comprised of every soul {@deity Sitri|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24} has corrupted with desire. A connected nerve system shares every sensation experienced by the sinewy growth to each soul grafted inside, causing it to scream and convulse endlessly. {@deity Sitri|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}'s daemons take their delight from torturing the twisted mass.

Like {@deity Sitri|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}, each Arch Daemon lords over their own fiefdom of the Netherworld, which reflects their infernal domain. {@deity Malikir|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24} is the most towering among them—a fortress citadel with countless flights of steps to reach the top. The dismembered souls of those who displeased the Arch Daemon are hung from the parapets, appearing like dismembered corpses gnawed upon by Malikirian imps. Mortal souls grovel beneath the gates at the bottom. They beg to be let inside, lest they be preyed upon by the other daemons of the Netherworld.

This hellish plane is only for the dead. Those whose souls have been claimed by an Arch Daemon must spend eternity tortured within their domain, according to the scriptures. The threat of eternal damnation enforces a measure of morality among the populace of Etharis.

Unfortunately, the Netherworld is undoubtedly real. Travel between it and the Material Plane is exceedingly difficult unless one has died, in which case the journey is one way. Other avenues require wielding dark magic, such as lighting a blood-infused candle to conjure a candlelight daemon or casting a ritual to commune with a fiendish spirit.

Cracks in the surface of the Material Plane can sometimes open, leading down into the hellscape of the Netherworld. This usually occurs from concerted effort from a daemon or the result of a misuse of immense magic by mortals. Such incursions release daemonic horrors into the mortal realm, sowing terror and discord unless some way is found to close this hellish portal.

entries.name: The Heavens
entries: When at last their work was complete, the gods looked down upon their creations in Etharis from the mighty Heavens, a paradise of sublime wonder for blessed souls to rest. But it didn't last. When the {@area Aether Kindred|36b|x} sundered the Heavens during {@area The Aetheric War|350|x}, what remained was left to the {@area Arch Seraphs|352|x} to rebuild.

In scriptures, the Heavens are now a fragment of their former glory. They've been rebuilt as a fortress instead of a garden, to protect the last bastion of divinity and safeguard the souls of the virtuous.

The Heavens are more guarded than any other plane of existence. Portals don't miraculously open to the mortal realm. With so little of their paradise remaining, the Arch Seraphs and their legions guard the celestial gates with devotion and fury. Therefore, there are no accounts of the Heavens—no true knowledge of {@deity Empyreus|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24|Empyreus'} fort or {@deity Zabriel|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24} library—besides what is extolled by prophets.

Any attempt by mortals to open a path to the Heavens has always been met by calamity. The founding of Ulmyr's Gate was predicated upon the idea that humanity would one day rise to witness the faces of the gods. Such hubris is inevitably punished.

entries.name: Numena
entries: Mages and academics have argued for centuries about the nature of Numena, since that word first appeared in the writings of Raakan el-Adakar. Not even the archmage himself seemed entirely convinced of its meaning. Throughout el-Adakar's life, his writings described Numena differently, leading to the branching theories that arcane scholars ponder to this day.

It isn't even certain that el-Adakar crafted the word himself, or had it whispered to him from beyond the veil of reality. To this day, wizards struggle to locate or travel to Numena. It's a conundrum, when contact with the {@area Ethereal Plane|344|x}, the {@area Realm of Faerie|343|x}, and even the {@area Netherworld|34a|x} has been made possible through intense and dangerous magical practice. Numena can be spoken to through spells. But its only responses are the manifestations created with magic.

If Numena is the realm of arcane magic, many fear it's also the realm of the {@area Aether Kindred|36b|x}: a place of pure, roiling chaos; a space beyond all else that exists, or a deep void of nothing save endless dark, distant stars, and the Aetheric entities. Depictions of such a place are found in the ancient carvings of the dragonborn beneath Ember Cairn and throughout the Shattered Peaks. They called it the Astral Plane, the reality between realities.

That scholars and mages continue to explore Numena's secrets is a cause of great discomfort for many commoners. Surely, with every spell cast, the veil between Numena and the Material Plane is further tattered as arcane energy is drawn through. The evidence of this are aberrant horrors, which increasingly crawl from beyond into the mortal realm. Such fears are behind the reasoning for the Arcanist Inquisition.

Then there's the theory that Numena has been visited many times by extraordinary individuals, who are treated as demigods among the mortals of Etharis. Whispers of such folk are associated with conspiracies concerning the {@area Thaumaturge|2b9|x}—that Etharis's fate is entirely controlled by a shadowy cabal of archmages. These astral travelers have arrived from distant realities either to destroy Etharis, or to rescue it from such horrors as the Aether Kindred, depending upon who is believed. Most arcane scholars dismiss this idea out of hand. If such individuals did exist, then the greatest minds of the mortal realm would be but children in their understanding of the cosmos.

entries.name: Divine Magic in Etharis
entries: Divine magic may seem contingent on the presence of gods, deities, {@area Arch Seraphs, and Arch Daemons|352|x}, but it is much more nuanced and complex. Divine spellcasters believe their deity sanctions them to draw from the power of the heavens—gifted magic in answer to prayers. Others believe a deity is not necessary to perform miracles, that faith is not so transactional, and that divinity itself chooses the virtuous in times when divine magic is in great need.

Less theological literature suggests a darker truth: a cleric's prayers are no different from a wizard's incantations. Divine spells commune with otherworldly energy and shape it to the caster's will, exactly as arcane magic weaves numen—perhaps the two sources of power are one and the same. Religious orders like the Hearthkeepers decry such theory as blasphemy. Of course, if it were true, by what right would the church limit the magic of others whilst celebrating their own spellcasters as prophets?

Yet as obscure as theology can be, evidence of the divine in Etharis exists. The downcast still remember the heavens as a glorious dream. {@area The Gods' End|351|x} itself undeniably diminished the light of divine magic across the continent, eradicating the population of divine mages until only a scattered few remained.

Of course, no one who records history witnessed the gods rise or fall—if there were ever gods at all. The cosmology and mythology presented here is just one that can be heard preached in the chapels, churches, and holy places of Etharis. What is seen as gospel in one area could be heretical if preached elsewhere.
But while mortal minds can accept their own lives are fleeting and small, the need to believe in a greater story that gives purpose to existence fills the gaps in knowledge with faith.

entries.name: The Aetheric War and Gods' End
entries: The gods came into being at the start of the Era of Antiquity. They opened their powerful eyes to behold a universe of wonder and chaos, and they were enamored. They wanted to shape it into something that resembled them. But to do that, they needed to impose order.

The gods banded together to form a pantheon. They shaped the firmament and the seas, then the land and all the creatures that followed. They named the land Etharis and created the people to populate it and beasts to roam it.

As these newborn mortals turned their minds to their creators, the gods found an endless font of believers. Their worship lifted the gods to greater heights, securing their ability to turn the wheel of the universe.

entries.name: The Aetheric War
entries: The gods were not alone. At the fringes of their ordered realm lurked the {@area Aether Kindred|36b|x}—incomprehensible beings of pure chaos who predated the gods themselves. The Kindred gnawed at the edges of reality. Occasionally the gods would find a piece of creation in disarray—a star gone missing, an ocean dried up, an entire city disappearing into a void of nothingness. Even the gods, in all their power and insight, could not fathom these discrepancies.

That's because {@area The Aether Kindred|36b|x} had no intent. No desires. They were an active threat to the gods' power by their mere presence, and the gods feared that one day all they had built would be stripped away. They had to strike now, while they still had the faith of their followers and stood at the height of their power. Thus, the gods banded together as a host and attacked the Kindred. This became known as the Aetheric War.

Scriptures differ on what happened to the gods who participated in the war. Some theologians say it was a slaughter. Others believe the gods were consumed by the eldritch horrors they tried to defeat. Others state that some gods retreated from this plane to someplace the Kindred could not reach them. While nothing is certain about what happened to the gods thereafter, it is clear they were utterly defeated.

Of the great pantheon, only the four youngest survived: Galt the Builder, Maligant Warclad, Ulmyr the Wise, and Aurelia of the Hearth. Perhaps out of caution or fear, they held back from joining the other gods in the Aetheric War. Thus, they were the only ones left to pick up the pieces, becoming responsible for the fostering of Etharis's future.

entries.name: The Gods' End
entries: As the surviving gods left to guide the mortals of Etharis, the Four Divines decided to band together. Their idea was to create an ideal empire, the "one above all" to act as a shining beacon for all mortals to emulate. This idea became the {@book Bürach Empire|GrimHollowCG24|3}.

Its first emperor, Indorius I, would be graced with the power to become a guiding light for all the nations of Etharis. To this end, the Four Divines gifted him with four holy artifacts that would enable him to rule wisely and protect his empire.

For a time, all was good. Unfortunately, this method of salvation carried the seeds of its own destruction. The Bürach Empire so loved their emperor that they decided to keep his bloodline eternally pure. The Church of the Hearthkeepers imposed the Edict of Eternal Blood, mandating that only those of the line of Indorius I may ascend to the throne. With that, they encouraged his descendants to intermarry, weakening their blood.

The wretched outcome of this practice finally manifested in the Mad Emperor, Leopold I. When he ascended to his legacy and touched the divine artifacts, his mind and soul were instantly connected with the Four Divines—and it drove them all to madness. Galt, Maligant, Ulmyr, and Aurelia warred among themselves, each vying to reign as the One True God of the universe. This culminated in the Gods' End, when these last remaining deities destroyed one another.
Etharis carries on as a godless world. While divine magic remains, it is but a shadow of its former glory.

entries.name: Arch Seraphs & Arch Daemons
entries: In the absence of the gods, the Arch Seraphs and Arch Daemons of Etharis have taken the mantles of their former deities. The machinations of these lesser divinities manifest in the deeds of their followers, providing a font of divine magic to the rare few of their most fervent worshippers. But they are not gods.

The Arch Seraphs and Arch Daemons can only embody aspects of their forebears' domains. Truth as an aspect of knowledge. Violence as an aspect of war. For this reason, the edicts of the Arch Seraphs and Arch Daemons are extreme and unyielding, encouraging their followers towards puritanical expressions of faith.

The Arch Seraphs appear to nurture the light that flickers in the heart of every mortal, while the Arch Daemons are considered to embody people's more heinous aspects. In truth, who belongs to which moniker is culturally dependent. A deity shunned as an Arch Daemon in one region may be celebrated as an Arch Seraph in another. The difference between fiend and celestial is a matter of morality. There are no gods left to point the way.
Below are descriptions of Etharis's most powerful Arch Seraphs and Arch Daemons, whose machinations frequently influence the lives of mortals.

entries.name: Healing Magic in Etharis
entries: The gods are dead. Divine power has waned in Etharis. Many temples that once revered the gods have now been converted to glorify the Arch Seraphs instead, and any new churches built are constructed in the Arch Seraphs’ names.

Most of the clergy are simple priests. Perhaps the most devout among them can conjure basic magic, but truly talented divine healers such as clerics or paladins are extremely rare. Viewed as living prophets, these individuals are celebrated in Etharis—or feared.

As possible wielders of divine magic, your characters are truly remarkable. This blessing is both a gift and a burden. Commoners are starved for guidance and want to believe in a power greater than themselves. They may look to a cleric as a beacon of hope and beg for aid or counsel, while equally, they flee from the gaze of a paladin who they fear has come to judge them.

The characters cannot rely on divine healers outside of their own group. A donation to the church does not guarantee a cure for disease or a resurrection. Such things are beyond the capabilities of common priests. Indeed, necromancy is reviled in many places, even when performed with good intentions, meaning most temples won’t revive a character even if it were within their capabilities.

If your characters are in need of healing, they’re more likely to find success seeking the aid of a surgeon, apothecary, or plague doctor. Such individuals are found in crowded cities where disease and danger are common, and there’s plenty of coin to pay for their services or {@item Potions of Healing|XDMG}. They might also be found travelling the roads between settlements. Alternatively, stories of herbalists living on the edge of villages or deep in the woods exist across Etharis, if you dare to trust such folk.

entries.name: The Aether Kindred
entries: After the {@area Aetheric War|34f|x}, the Kindred seemed to disappear. It's believed these horrors slept deeply after destroying so many of the gods, possibly requiring rest to heal from their wounds. Or perhaps they slumbered in the aftermath of much devouring.

The presence of the Kindred has returned to Etharis in more recent times. Abominations spawn from their dreams, and it's feared they may one day wake to take revenge on the rest of the gods' creations.

Spellcasters who wield either arcane or divine magic may draw their power from the Aether Kindred. One horrifying theory suggests that {@area Numena|34c|x} is an Aether Kindred, and thus why arcane magic is so dangerous. Whether the case or not, warlocks who make pacts with ancient gods and sorcerers whose minds are wracked with aberrant visions are undeniably touched by the Aether Kindred.

Worship of the Kindred is exceedingly rare, but not unheard of in Etharis. They have no formal church or doctrine. Indeed, few people even dare learn their names except the eldritch clerics who lead their cults. Their desires are truly unknowable. Their existence is an existential horror.
See {@deity Vraigoroth|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}
See {@deity M'rorcameleth|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}
See {@deity Pharazorthok|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}

entries.name: Primal Magic in Etharis
entries: Somewhere between arcane and divine magic is the magic of nature, called primal magic by those who differentiate between the three types. This force represents both the power of the elements and the power of things that grow, live, and die outside of the purview of either god or mortal.

Two main cultures in Etharis practice primal magic. The elves of {@area Tol Leyemil|112|x} believe the Spirits of Nature teach them how to cast spells by communing with the natural forces of the forests, mountains, and rivers. The {@book Valikan Clans|GrimHollowCG24|7} evoke their magic from the Primordials living within the Elemental Planes.

To divine spellcasters, the Primordials and Spirits of Nature are no different to the daemons and horrors from where arcanists draw their magic. Yet there is a deep spirituality to primal magic that is missing from the cold academia of arcane magic.

entries.name: The Primordic Cycle
entries: Earth. Fire. Ice. Air. These are the primordial elements that roiled and thundered at the beginning of time. The first living thing sparked into existence amongst this primal chaos, and the elements were all it consumed to survive. It shattered rocks with its teeth, gulped lava down its gullet, and devoured storms of wind and hail. The creature grew and grew, until its size encircled all that was. Every element tempested inside of it. This creature was called {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}, the Great Prismatic Wyrm.

For long millennia, {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24} was the conscious world. Then one day a mountain called {@deity Citrolach|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24} awoke on the Wyrm's back. {@deity Citrolach|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}'s heavy footsteps split the earth to create valleys, and here the ice seeped to feed the growing rainforests. From these trees emerged the first tribes of mortals. These tiny creatures were like gnats upon {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}'s back, and the Prismatic Wyrm hated them for how they itched. {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24} lashed {@deity Citrolach|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24} for having wrought them. Thus began {@deity Citrolach|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}'s hatred of the Wyrm.

Morakesh exploded from {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}'s breath like lava and fire spewing forth from a volcano. The {@deity Lady Morakesh|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24} was wielded like a weapon, forced to battle to prove the extent of her fiery destructiveness. When the gods came to challenge {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}'s strength, Morakesh burned them to husks—composing a symphony of agonized screams for the Wyrm's sick pleasure.

{@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24} tested even his own strength against Morakesh, extinguishing her flames to prove she was no match for him. The Lady harbored a burning hatred. The cinders of betrayal flared inside her heart.

The fires of Morakesh melted the remaining ice that formed the Endless Sea, releasing {@deity Alondo|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}, Lord of the Depths. Wishing to be left in solitude, {@deity Alondo|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24} tried to escape {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24} deep beneath the seas. The Prismatic Wyrm followed to show all Primordials that nowhere was beyond his reach.

{@deity Ilhara|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}, Queen of Air and Shadow, formed in the shadow and smoke of Morakesh's flames. Silent and unseen, she travelled throughout the realm learning of the discontent for {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}'s tyrannical rule. Ambitious to replace him, {@deity Ilhara|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24} found a hero among mortals to kill {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}.

entries.name: The Kentigern Saga
entries: Enduring {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}'s tyranny since time began, each of the Primordials ached to be free of their sire. They found their opportunity from an unexpected source: a man named Kentigern and his six companions.

{@deity Ilhara|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24} convinced her siblings to betray their sire and take the adventurers under their wing. Morakesh and {@deity Citrolach|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24} formed powerful magic Weapons that could pierce {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}'s flesh. {@deity Ilhara|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24} composed for them a song that would put the Prismatic Wyrm to sleep. {@deity Alondo|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24} taught them enchantments that would keep them alive, and he also told them where to strike {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}'s heart.

Thus, Kentigern and his companions slew {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24} and used his massive body to form the mortal realm. His flesh became the earth, and his spine the mountains that divide the center of Etharis. His blood became the frozen seas and therein his heart was thrown to form an island. Fragments of {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}'s scales can still be found today as precious stones. And thus the world was made.

Yet all four Primordials know that their sire was not truly destroyed, but merely slumbers in a state of hibernation. Even now, mortals obtain command over the elements from the Prismatic Wyrm. The day may come when {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24} awakens to wreak his vengeance across Etharis. The Primordials and mortals continually seek to prevent this from happening. Yet with the appearance of coldfire, it seems the Prismatic Wyrm's resurrection is coming ever closer.

entries.name: Contradicting Mythology
entries: There are many different faiths and religions in Etharis. The details between them can contradict each other, as is the nature of scriptures and ancient stories.

In some versions of The Primordic Cycle, {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24} clashed with the ancient gods of Etharis before the Aetheric War. The gods tried chaining the Prismatic Wyrm at first, but his size made him unable to be {@condition Restrained|XPHB}. {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24} sired the four lesser primordials as Weapons against the gods. All were defeated before being cast from the heavens.

In the Bürach Empire, it's explained that {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24} was created by the gods, though his appearance is rare in sermons or scripture. Valikan stories claim the Prismatic Wyrm awoke first and that the gods followed later.
The version of the story told depends on where one travels in Nordenland or Grarjord. Which version of the story is true, no mortal can ever know.

The tales the Prismatic Circle tells you about {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24} are lies. The Great Prismatic Wyrm is our salvation and the only thing holding back Valika's destruction. We must awaken the Great Wyrm before it is too late.
entries.by: Cult of the Great Wyrm Druid

entries.name: Primordials
entries: Each of {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}'s children rule over their own primordial realms. The {@book Valikans|GrimHollowCG24|7} believe Etharis exists in the gulf between the four Elemental Planes, where air, water, earth, and fire converge and clash together. It's said to be possible to enter the Elemental Planes where each element is strongest, and that druids draw their power from these realms like a wizard draws numen.
See {@deity Lady Morakesh|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}
See {@deity Alondo|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}
See {@deity Citrolach|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}
See {@deity Ilhara|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}
See {@deity Gormadraug|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24}

entries.name: The Faerie Courts
entries: Before the {@area Era of Expansion|038|x}, the Faerie Courts were worshipped like deities in Caer Neiada—the realm now called the {@book Charneault Kingdom|GrimHollowCG24|5}. The elves of that region believed they were descendants of the archfey and that magic was only possible by listening to the Spirits of Nature. In ancient times, it's said that many more elves were capable of magic than today.

In modern Charneault, the Faerie Courts are still revered. The culture is much more interlaced with the influence of Büracher religions, some settlements preferring to offer prayers to the Arch Seraphs while others make tributes to the archfey. The religions are not incompatible, however. There is rumor among scholars that the archfey are, in fact, seraphs themselves, downcast from the heavens to the mortal realm in one of Etharis's ancient cataclysms—perhaps the {@area Aetheric War|34f|x}. Here, the Spirits of Nature cultivated a second heavenly paradise, hidden within the folds of the {@area Realm of Faerie|343|x}.

Such beliefs are not commonly held. Many folk of Charneault consider the fey more devilish than divine. Some paladins might swear oaths to an Arch Seraph but are happy to receive gifts from the Spirits of Nature, while others bind their faith exclusively to the Faerie Courts.

Similar beliefs are found in eastern Nordenland and parts of {@book Valika|GrimHollowCG24|7}. Though these more human cultures feel less directly connected to the Faerie Courts, even regarding the Archfey like capricious devils, they still train mages and make pacts to wield primal magic.

data.name: Chapter 14: Magic Items
entries: No dark fantasy game or setting would be complete without Magic Items. The heroes, villains, and threats inhabiting Etharis find any advantage they can, and magic is a powerful tool in their arsenals.

entries.name: Magic Items in Etharis
entries: Like the world of Etharis itself, the Magic Items in a Grim Hollow campaign tend to be a twisted and dark reflection of the powerful relics in a fantasy world. After all, the same magic that casts a malicious pall over the people and creatures of Etharis is harnessed to create Magic Items.

Like any fantasy world, most Magic Items in Etharis can be picked up and used by anyone, or used after the wielder takes the time to attune to the item. Some rarer items are particularly useful for creatures who have started to undergo a {@book Transformation|GrimHollowPG24|6}.

The Arcanist Inquisition's philosophical leaders have agreed that using Magic Items that tap into arcane powers is no less dangerous and corrupting than channeling arcane energies through spellcasting. Worst of all, anyone willing to attune to a corrupting Magic Item is as culpable as a wizard or a sorcerer.

However, the Arcanist Inquisition agrees that attuning to or using divinely inspired items is permissible, especially if it aids in the hunting, capture, and ultimate redemption of arcanists. Not surprisingly, what is a safe Magic Item and what is not is often up to interpretation by the leaders of the Inquisition, who are likely attuned to a powerful item or two.

Using remnants of magical creatures, even the most horrible monster, is a common practice among the experienced adventurers of Etharis. The dreaded, feared, but ultimately necessary members of the various monster hunter guilds are most known for their manipulation of monster parts into tools of the trade.

entries.name: Magic Item Descriptions
entries: The following Magic Items may be found in Etharis.
I asked the gnome if it was safe. He said, 'Define safe.' Then the lightning came, and the bandits didn't.
Where is it? Where is it?! I know I set it down here somewhere. Will you help me find my flask?
Invisibility magic? How quaint.
Unfortunately, I can still hear you.
Hmm, normally that works better.
entries.by: {@i A Whip-Wielding Mercenary Facing a Wight}

entries: I missed? How is that even possible?
Alright, open up. We need to talk to you without any of your magic nonsense.
You'd think that having rocks and dirt stuck to your Armor would just slow you down, but have you ever tried shooting an arrow through a wall?
They took my sword arm. So I had one made. It doesn't get tired, doesn't bleed, and punches through plate like parchment. I'd say I came out ahead.

You should've seen it! It was surely one of the most terrifying creatures we've hunted. I landed a few blows before its massive claws threw me down. I was as good as dead, but then there was just this...rancid smell.
It was so bad that I think I woke myself up.
He said "Stand Still," and the next thing I knew, I was a bear!
Is it day? Night?
Why can't I see? Am I blind?
Why can't I move?
Just voices...your voice.
Why do you keep asking me the same damn question?
I like this coat, it keeps me warm.
And for some reason nothing happened when I was struck by lightning last week.

entries.name: The Four Sacred Artifacts
entries: Four Sacred Artifacts appeared at the dawn of the {@book Bürach Empire|GrimHollowCG24|3}, each a divine gift from the four gods: Aurelia, Galt, Maligant, and Ulmyr. These relics were given to Bürach's first emperor, Indorius I, and inherited by each of his successors.

Maligant provided the mighty sword, {@item Dämglesvir|GrimHollowCG24}, to slay Bürach's enemies. Galt placed a crown on Indorius I's head to signify his divine right. Ulmyr gifted the emperor an orb of foresight to make him a wise ruler. Aurelia clad him in a breastplate, wishing only to protect him and his heirs.

Many centuries later, after Emperor Leopold I was assassinated, custody of the artifacts sparked a civil war. Each of the holy treasures disappeared amid the fighting. One religiously fervent tale claims they blazed with radiant light before banishing themselves beyond mortal reach. Other rumors suggest they were each stolen or lost in various separate conspiracies. It's certain that whoever holds the Sacred Artifacts controls the fate of the Bürach Empire.

entries.name: Wielding An Artifact
entries: Before disappearing, the artifacts were tarnished and corrupted by Emperor Leopold I's delusions, cruelty, and malice. Darkness had seeped into them, transforming their divine power and purpose. Though it's heretical to suggest, some believe the artifacts' befoulment is somehow linked to the death of the gods and the growing influence of the Great Beast.

Wielding even one Sacred Artifact is difficult. Their true power can only be unlocked by practicing their use. Yet this necessitates more time exposed to the darkness that now infuses them, which can infest the wielder's body and mind.

entries.name: Attunement and Proficiency
entries: Each Sacred Artifact has three tiers of Attunement available to its user. When a character attunes to the item, they have access only to the benefits (and the curse) listed under the Aspirant tier. The Adept and Master tiers of attunement are unlocked over time (see Attunement Milestones, below).
Attunement to a Sacred Artifact can't be voluntarily ended. If the Attunement does end, the character can attune to the item again at the Aspirant tier.

Each artifact also curses its wielder. In addition to the curse effects listed for each tier, a character attuned to a Sacred Artifact is unwilling to part with the item, keeping it within reach at all times. If the artifact's curse is ended or suppressed, the character's Attunement with that item is broken.

entries.name: Attunement Milestones
entries: To ascend to a higher tier of Attunement and unlock additional powers of a Sacred Artifact, the attuned character must achieve a specific milestone. There is no minimum level required to achieve a milestone. However, Adept-tier artifacts are appropriate for characters of level 5 to 10, while Master-tier artifacts are appropriate for characters of level 11 and above.
The GM and players should work together to create appropriate milestones for the campaign, using these examples (themed to the four artifacts) as guidance:

items: Wield {@item Dämglesvir|GrimHollowCG24} to slay a mighty foe.
Take no damage during a difficult combat.
Earn the fealty of an important ruler.
You alter a future witnessed in {@item Ulmyr's Eye|GrimHollowCG24}.

entries.name: Shared Delusions
entries: The darkness that seeped into the Sacred Artifacts causes their wielders to experience the same paranoid delusions that plagued Emperor Leopold I.

All characters attuned to a Sacred Artifact suffer the same delusions, which could include the sense of being watched, believing a friendly NPC to be hostile, or mistaking an innocent person or household pet for a monster. Characters can even experience multiple simultaneous delusions.

A character suffering a delusion can use the {@action Search|XPHB} action to discern the truth, typically by succeeding on a {@dc 20} (or higher) Wisdom ({@skill Insight|XPHB} or {@skill Perception|XPHB}) check.

If the GM is using the Influence Dice rules from {@book Chapter 16|GrimHollowCG24|20}, the frequency and intensity of delusions increases while the number of dice in the Beast Pool exceeds the number of dice in the Resolve Pool.

entries.name: Might of Heaven
entries: {@i Dämglesvi} was one of the many Weapons wielded by Maligant himself during the Aetheric War. One of the god's most powerful armaments, its name means "might of heaven."

{@i Dämglesvi} was brought to Emperor Indorius I by a celestial honor guard. The weapon's siblings are wielded by Maligant's former lieutenants: the {@deity Empyreus|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24|Arch Seraph Empyreus} wields {@i Judgemen}, while the {@deity Tormach|Etharis|GrimHollowCG24|Arch Daemon Tormach} brandishes {@i Wrath}.
This sword was once one of the most powerful items in all of existence. Now it’s a twisted shadow of itself.

entries.name: Goddess's Protection
entries: After the Aetheric War, Aurelia was desperate to protect what remained of mortal life. Yet the goddess was heartsick from the slaughter and did not wish to create another weapon. Instead, she ensured that no danger to the emperor's life could ever threaten the stability of the realm.
The {@i Hearthfire Breastplat} sheathed the emperor in golden light, making their presence a beacon of hope and safety in the Bürach Empire.

entries.name: Divine Authority
entries: Galt imagined the Bürach Empire as a glorious society of fairness and order. To ensure that it operated effectively, it would need a unifying voice that spoke with divine authority.
{@i Indorius's Crow} is named for the emperor who wore it first, and each of Indorius's heirs have felt the weight of authority upon their own head.

entries.name: Blessed Wisdom
entries: Religious texts claim Ulmyr plucked one of their own eyes as a gift for the first emperor. With it, Emperor Indorius I and his descendants would act with a measure of Ulmyr's foresight and wisdom.

The eye was kept inside the {@i Sovereign's Or}, a golden reliquary inlaid with jewels mined from each of Bürach's provinces. These would remind the emperor of their duty to each of the realms they ruled.
Even those with eyes still cannot see.

entries.name: Magic Items in Grim Hollow
entries: The dark fantasy of a Grim Hollow campaign presents magic items as both powerful and dangerous -- just like the magic that creates and fuels them. Players accustomed to high fantasy campaigns and similar genres may expect to collect plenty of impressive magic items that work reliably and predictably, with no down sides. Such players may be surprised or bothered by the items in this chapter, some of which have sinister properties or are outright cursed.

Any magic item can also bear signs of its creator's sinister motives, from a twisted appearance to a lingering residue of evil. A wand created by servants of an Arch Daemon might have the innate capability to ensure that it serves dark purposes, refusing to function -- or even harming its user -- if used for other ends.

The GM should explore this concept with players beforehand, so that everyone is aware of this aspect of dark fantasy. When handled well, it can add to the mystery and dread of the world as the characters attune to items without knowing entirely what havoc that might bring.

data.name: Part 4: Game Master's Guide
entries: Grim Hollow campaigns are the perfect place for creating many kinds of dark fantasy stories. The {@book Grim Hollow Player's Guide|GrimHollowPG24} provides the means for players to build their characters within the maelstrom of conflict and horror that is the hallmark of dark fantasy play.

This section of the {@i Grim Hollow Campaign Guide} provides Game Masters with the tools for envisioning, creating, and running a dark fantasy campaign. Whether looking for advice on the types of stories that make up good dark fantasy encounters, adventures, and campaigns; incorporating new rules that highlight the types of tension and horror infused into dark fantasy gaming; or finding some potential creatures that can make a dark fantasy campaign memorable, there is something here for all kinds of Game Masters and campaigns.

One of the most important aspects of not just a grim, horror-filled campaign—but really any sort of themed campaign—is establishing the tone of the game early and reinforcing it often. This tone should be introduced at the beginning, as the players begin to make characters and the Game Master imagines the main threats, far-reaching conflicts, and first challenges. When the players have bought into the tone and the feel of the campaign, the Game Master has an easier time delivering a fun and memorable experience.

Once the campaign begins, new or alternative rules can take a versatile game system like 5e and fine-tune it to enhance the mechanics to mesh with the character options and story elements. Some of those alternative rule options are presented in this section, allowing Game Masters to ensure that players are getting the correct game flow and challenge level.

The delivery of the campaign, especially with regard to the types of encounters and adventures that comprise story arcs, can make a noticeable difference between a typical campaign and a gaming experience unlike anything that players have seen before. That delivery often revolves around a combination of encounter types, adventure framing, player and character choices, and a willingness to engage with the darker aspects of the heroic—or villainous—journey. These concepts are discussed here as well.

Finally, this section covers topics such as finding the right challenge level, changing the resource-management structure, placing conflicts and goals correctly in the context of the overall campaign, and more. Read on to become an expert on dark fantasy!

data.name: Chapter 15: Guide to Dark Fantasy
entries: What makes a dark fantasy campaign different from a typical 5e campaign? What does a Game Master need to do to bring a horror-flavored tinge to the encounters and adventures? How can the players—and the characters they bring into the story—add to the style of dark fantasy?

This chapter provides insights and tips into the genres and tropes that come together to make dark fantasy a reality within your games. From long-term goals to short-term tweaks, read on to gain more insight into bringing the potential of a Grim Hollow campaign to life.

entries.name: Dark Fantasy Styles
entries: Dark fantasy is a genre of story and game play that encompasses a number of different styles and subgenres. Each style is discussed here in greater detail. You may choose to mix elements from multiple styles in constructing your adventures or focus exclusively on a single style that is most enjoyable for your group.

Typical dark fantasy is like film noir, in the sense that both genres rely on one foundational truth: there are no truly benevolent heroes left—and those who are don't stay alive long enough to become famous. In both genres, there is a constant tension between doing the right thing for altruistic reasons and doing the smart thing that brings you closer to your goals and helps you survive.

In dark fantasy, characters and NPCs alike struggle with impossible moral and ethical choices. Even supposedly good people are flawed, capable of tragic and unspeakable deeds. Meanwhile, villains don't think twice about committing atrocities. A happy ending where everyone survives intact is unlikely.

Dark fantasy adopts realism, reflecting the world as we fear it to be rather than the one we hope for. The horror and ills of a real society exist in a dark fantasy world—all of the moral failings of people and the societies they create are on display, and fighting the status quo generally leads to unhappy and unfortunate consequences for the defiant parties.

When it comes to war, a dark fantasy setting is critical of any notion of glory and heroism in battle. Emphasized instead are sacrifices made to "win," the costs to innocent lives, the destruction of homes, the toll on the soldiers, and the horror witnessed by combatants on both sides of the conflict. And, of course, the individuals who seek (or seek to maintain) power as a result of the fighting rarely pay any price at all. War is a violent and devastating business. Yet human failings such as greed, jealousy, ambition, hatred, zealotry, and even the destructive aspects of love make conflict inevitable.

As for magic in a dark fantasy setting, its use often comes at an unspeakable cost and with terrible consequences when things go wrong. Infernal pacts cost souls and unleash eldritch horrors. Arcane studies take a toll on the body and the mind of the practitioners. The slightest miscalculation when channeling the power of magic can lead to dire consequences for the casters and the innocent people around them.

That said, hope glimmers into a dark fantasy world like a fragment of light through a narrow curtain. Plagued by malice and hardship, it's important that everything happens as a consequence of choices the characters made. A world that's cruel for cruelty's sake is not only illogical, but it's also boring and hard to navigate for players. Using dark fantasy themes purely for shock value eventually wears down your players, and likely yourself as well.

Dark fantasy serves a purpose—foregrounding the dangers of the campaign, engaging the players with realism, and allowing them to feel the impact of their choices, for good or ill. Hope is what makes your dark fantasy world meaningful.

entries.name: Grimdark
entries: Grimdark is an even more nihilistic subgenre of dark fantasy. Grimdark worlds aim less to mimic the realities of a corrupt society and prefer to magnify the depths of human desperation or depravity. In a grimdark world, society has already collapsed. There are no glimmers of hope and no possibility that evil will ever be punished. Indeed, evil deeds may be the only means for survival.

Mortal lives are cheap and frequently meaningless in grimdark settings. Enforced suffering, such as the sacrifice of a thousand innocent souls to sate an eldritch god, may be a necessary toll to keep the light of humanity alive. Resources are scarce and conflict is ceaseless. Rather than bastions of light in a perilous world, nowhere is truly safe. Death is inevitable and ever prowling, whether it comes in the guise of a maniacal priest, a flesh-eating affliction, or a terrifying, otherworldly monster.

Despite how dire grimdark worlds can be, the fun of such settings is in the excessively dystopian worldbuilding and the challenge of surviving such hostile circumstances. Your characters still have motivations and goals. But the question your adventures will explore is not whether you can succeed, but rather how long your characters can last?

entries.name: Horror
entries: Horror is by no means the easiest genre to create stories for. It relies on the right amount of tension, atmosphere, and imagery to work. If these are lacking, or if they're overdone, it can quickly turn into unintended comedy.

There are many nuances of horror, including terror, dread, and revulsion. Revulsion is easy to elicit; it can come from something as straightforwardly disgusting as slipping on a pile of corpses and falling into a pool of blood and viscera.

Dread is more difficult. It requires slow-building tension and a description of an atmosphere that is both immediate and mysterious. If you can give the players the feeling that something terrible is about to happen, but they don't quite know when it will happen, you have initiated a bout of potential dread. The longer you can sustain this buildup, the more powerful the release when the dreadful event happens.

You can describe the characters going down a dark corridor or a shadowy forest, where they catch glimpses of creatures moving from the corner of their eyes. They know they are being followed and an attack is imminent—but they don't know when or by what. False releases of the dread give temporary relief but ultimately heighten the buildup.

The final layer is terror, a release of emotion that comes from shock and the realization that something horrible is happening that is beyond the players' control. This is difficult to evoke since most players often feel their characters are in control of situations—thanks to their power and abilities. The key is in catching them unawares and upending their expectations.

For example, the party could defeat the seemingly corrupt inquisitor, only for his head to split open and reveal a monstrous, tentacled thing using human flesh as its shell. From a moment like this you not only get revulsion but a possible heart-pounding kick in the gut.

Horror pairs well into dark fantasy because it helps portray a world where frightening and dangerous things exist. NPCs are useful for building this atmosphere. A traumatized villager sitting in a tavern might fearfully describe their encounter with a monster from the woods, even thinking of it causing their hands to shake.

entries.name: Dark Fairy Tale
entries: A dark, overgrown forest where wolves and witches roam. An inn owned by an outwardly jolly old fellow who kills and robs weary travelers as they sleep. A cursed noble who must slay those they marry on their wedding night. Evil fairies whose gifts are never as they seem, helping and hindering those who accept them. These are some of the many tropes of dark fairy tales.

The dark fairy tale is another dark fantasy subgenre which leans toward fantasy instead of realism. Situations begin as outwardly normal, even hopeful. Then the characters discover that the reality of their situation is much more bizarre than originally understood, and far beyond their control.

The most important aspect of creating a dark fairy tale setting is an awareness of the elements used in such stories. Reading original stories of the Brothers Grimm will give you an idea, as well as more modern takes by fantasy authors. In these tales, the innocent are really monsters, the monsters often misunderstood, and the evil brought into the world can come from any source.

entries.name: Sword and Sorcery
entries: Exemplified in the works of authors Robert E. Howard and Michael Moorcock, the sword and sorcery genre sets aside idealism for a world soaked in mud, blood, and magic. Like the dark fantasy genre, sword and sorcery approaches a fantastic setting from an amoral perspective, with a particular focus on action. Characters live in a gritty, violent world of barbarism, corrupt kings, evil sorcerers, sword-wielding warriors, and horrifying beasts that threaten innocent folks. It can be set in a fallen kingdom or a post-apocalyptic desert wasteland. Most of all, the combat scenes are visceral, dynamic, and absolutely blood-soaked.

The other half of the equation, magic, is similarly important. Magic is essentially dangerous, difficult to control, and forbidden. Those who use it pay a high price in terms of blood, souls, or their own humanity.

entries.name: Conflict and Resolution
entries: Good encounters and adventures revolve around conflict, the engine that drives all stories. If characters must summon all their skills, wits, and resources to resolve a conflict, they are likely to be challenged and fulfilled. As such, the quality of the conflict determines the quality of the narrative.
When designing conflicts for narrative, pay attention to three critical questions:

entries.name: What
entries: Specifically, what is the conflict about? Who is involved, what is at stake, and what might be necessary to resolve the conflict?

entries.name: So What
entries: The most violent and dramatic conflicts fail to resonate if the consequences never touch the characters, the things that the characters care about, or the setting as a whole. The most impactful conflicts touch the characters and everything they care about in profound ways.

entries.name: Now What
entries: What do the characters need to do to resolve the conflict? How does the conflict and its resolution interact with the characters' immediate and long-term goals?

entries.name: Conflict Scale
entries: For games set in a large and complex world such as Etharis, it's best to focus on three kinds and scales of conflict: character-based, party-based, and world-based.

entries.name: Character
entries: In most campaigns that draw upon the characters' stories, each player develops their character's history or background early in the process of campaign planning and character creation. Before the players do this, it helps to explain the kind of world Etharis is to your players, allowing them to make informed decisions that fit into the setting.

When players establish their characters in Etharis, they should think about and communicate their characters' goals, desires, flaws, bonds, and personalities. In essence, they are bringing with them their own conflicts, which can form the seeds of a story. Take notes as they relate their backstories and character concepts, and listen for salient points that you can use for the plots and conflicts of your campaign. Not only do your efforts work to enrich your tale, you can draw your players deeper into the story, as they feel they have a stake in the game.

A character story contains elements the GM can use to motivate them on all sorts of adventures. The character should have a long-term goal (marry into the local merchant's family) and short-term goals (catch the thief of their family's stolen signet ring). If you offer them a way to get closer to these goals, the character probably takes it. Put an obstacle between them and these goals, and they'll try to get around it. (Conversely, if you offer them an adventure that doesn't get them any closer to those goals, they might ignore it.)

The rival suitor for the hand of the merchant's scion in marriage may make a good villain or foil in the initial adventures in the campaign. The thief of the signet ring could be tied to a larger threat that reveals itself later in the campaign as well.

entries.name: Moral Complexity
entries: With dark fantasy and the Grim Hollow character options that support play, you might have characters that are seemingly shady, evil, or twisted before the campaign even starts. Or these characters might be kind-hearted folk who have potentially sinister goals. Dark fantasy is a genre that accepts characters can have noble reasons for doing evil deeds, and evil characters often have a relatable cause to their malice.
