Enhance the character conflict of your campaign by offering flawed characters paths of light and goodness toward their otherwise evil goals. Helping the people of the town overcome a supernatural threat might be the fastest way to achieving revenge.

Conversely, characters that seem good and strive for positive goals should be challenged by offering them dark shortcuts or impossible decisions. The character that wants to become a paragon of light might have to do questionable things on that journey. It's in this juxtaposition of light and dark, good and evil, or bravery and cowardice that makes for the most engaging stories.

entries.name: Party
entries: Party-level conflicts are not when characters are in conflict with each other. Rather, they are conflicts important to the group that the characters must work together to resolve. A story hook or some other impetus leads them to the conflict, or the conflict comes to them directly.

If you find yourself stuck for ideas, this campaign guide contains plenty of cults, secret organizations, monstrous abominations, maniacal wizards, and eldritch entities to put in front of your characters. A string of antagonists leading to a primary foe can keep your party engaged for an entire campaign if presented correctly.

Remember to focus on what the characters are trying to achieve when imagining these conflicts. It's not enough for the party to learn about the sorcerer Gorm while they are saving the noble—they might decide it's not their problem, as long as the noble is safe. To ensure the heroes care about this new conflict, it needs to get in the way of the goal they are already pursuing.

entries.name: The Goal is Fun
entries: Whether a campaign is grimdark, horror-based, dark fairy tales, or sword-and-sorcery, remember the goal is still to have fun. A campaign full of relentless cruelty and darkness can get exhausting if that's all you present.

Dark fantasy can have a reputation that jokes and laughter are not welcome in the genre. On the contrary, these moments of brightness and levity can help accentuate the darkness and tragedy experienced later. Let there be moments of peace, triumph, humor, and lightheartedness to let the players rest before going back into the fray. As with anything, variety and contrast is crucial to highlighting all elements of the genre.

When tragedy and violence do return to the story, its impact is so much greater if your players enjoy spending time with the characters being affected. Encourage your group to embrace the mood, but don't let your campaign turn humorless.

entries.name: World
entries: World conflicts occur as the backdrop to your party's adventures and character- or party-based conflicts. The scale of these conflicts might be local, regional, or global. They include such things as the decimation of a village by the minions of the Great Beast, the war between nations like {@book Ostoya|GrimHollowCG24|4} and the {@book Bürach Empire|GrimHollowCG24|3}, a shadow war between various factions like the Arcanist Inquisition and an arcane college, ongoing disasters such as the Weeping Pox, or any trouble that is larger than a single string of adventures can address.

These conflicts are meant to ground the party's adventures in a living, breathing world filled with power shifts and constant change, where things happen that are much bigger than they are. The characters may influence the outcome of these conflicts or vice versa, should their story take them there.

Remember that tying a story into a world conflict adds texture to the story but doesn't necessarily give the characters more reason to get involved. Over time, you may find ways to organically weave character, party, and world conflicts together into a cohesive plotline. You don't necessarily have to do it overnight; take your time to plan how the story may unfold. See how a character's backstory may connect to the party's quest, and how that party conflict may link to a conflict currently brewing in the region.

Having said that, don't be married to your plans. Roleplaying is shared storytelling, and your players may have their own ideas that may ultimately prove more satisfying. Be open to these ideas as they may organically enrich your conflict, and therefore your narrative.

entries.name: Progression
entries: Just as the pacing of your sessions is a strong contributor to the flow of your campaign, so is the rate and the method by which your players level up. While the conventional method of level via {@variantrule Experience Points|XPHB} is a valid approach, it does have shortcomings.

In particular, it focuses mostly on killing enemies through combat encounters. If combat-based progression works well within your campaign, then this method is fine: your campaign can continue using what works well. However, if you want a more narrative-based game, encourage players to roleplay more, or simply want to try something new, consider one of the options below.

entries.name: Milestone Progression
entries: To follow the milestone progression process, at the end of a session simply ask the following question: "Has this session been a defining moment for the current story arc of our campaign?"

If the answer is yes, and you feel it makes sense to do so, award the party with a level increase. There is no perfect pacing set in stone when it comes to awarding milestones, and providing advice is difficult as every group is unique.
For some campaign types and players, leveling after ever session works. Sometimes this leads to the power curve rising too steeply in relation to the progress of the plot.

For some campaigns it's often best to do it about every 2-3 sessions. This is especially true if your group plays more frequently. However, growing in power too quickly can undermine the sense of danger in your dark fantasy setting, and so levelling up every 3-5 sessions may feel more appropriate. The important aspect is making the tone of the campaign match the rate at which characters grow in power.

Milestone progression focuses on the achievements of your party in a reactive manner. While it is possible to plan milestones ahead of time, the best results come from identifying and awarding them upon completion, or slightly before. After all, it is much easier to identify meaningful milestones after (or just before) they happen. If you have pre-planned milestones, you might be tempted to dictate the party's behavior, which your players will likely resent.

entries.name: Goal-Based Progression
entries: This is a proactive approach where the players direct the pace of the game by choosing their own milestones in the form of goals. While this can be more difficult to pull off, it can increase player buy-in and engagement. It can make planning in advance more difficult, you can mitigate this by asking players to choose the next session's goals at the end of each session. That way you have time to reflect on the goals as you set up the next session.

To run a campaign with goal-based progression, there are two important steps to take. First you should establish the world and ensure your players know enough about what's going on to set interesting goals for their characters. This is best achieved by running a short, structured story arc at the beginning of the campaign introducing major themes and influencing factors. Step two is to openly communicate with the party and ask, "What do you want to do?" offering suggestions when the party is unsure. This gets the party involved in shaping the story. Once the party has completed a goal milestone, allocate experience or give them a level depending on the difficulty of the goal.

entries.name: Encounter Difficulty
entries: Establishing the proper level of difficulty in your encounters is as much an art as it is a science. Set the encounter difficulty too low, and you'll hear complaints about the adventure being too easy. Set it too high, and you'll end up killing the party before you can conclude the story. Finding the right balance is key to an enjoyable and rewarding experience.

Before deciding on an appropriate level for combat encounters, it helps to inform players about what to expect in terms of difficulty. Without giving details away, let them know if you plan on introducing challenging encounters that could end in defeat. That way, your players aren't caught completely off guard when the difficulty scales up. When scaling combat encounters, you have several options to choose from.

entries.name: Alter the Resting Rules
entries: Rests are crucial for managing encounter, adventure, and campaign difficulty. Characters are more powerful and capable within an encounter when they have access to their full resources, especially spell slots and abilities that require Long Rests to recover.

For creating a more challenging and tense campaign, the next chapter provides alternative resting rules. If you use those rules, see how they change the dynamics and difficulty of your encounters and adventures before using the rest of the suggestions in this section. Moving to the new resting system might be more than enough to provide a heightened challenge to your players.

entries.name: Increase Monster Challenges
entries: A standard way for raising the difficulty level of encounters is to simply use more monsters or use stronger monsters.

entries.name: More Hit Points
entries: The first and least intrusive step is to use the standard encounter building rules for fifth edition, but increase the number of Hit Points that creatures have. Rather than using the average Hit Points based on a creature's Hit Point Dice, use 75% of their maximum potential Hit Points. To make monsters that last longer but not hit harder, give monsters maximum potential Hit Points.

For example, a monster with {@dice 10d8} Hit Point Dice and a 16 Constitution would have an average Hit Point maximum of 75: 45 Hit Points from their Hit Point Dice and 30 Hit Points for a +3 Constitution modifier per Hit Point Die.

If you used the maximum Hit Points possible, this would instead by 110 Hit Points instead of 75, allowing the monster to potentially survive a couple of extra attacks from the characters and perhaps do more damage themselves.

entries.name: More Monsters
entries: Adding one or more additional monsters to a combat also increases the challenge—usually more significantly than simply increasing a monster's Hit Points. This provides an extra threat to the characters with its own set of actions.

If using this method, start with the lowest {@variantrule Challenge Rating|XPHB|Challenge Rating (CR)} monster first, adding more fodder for the characters that are easier to kill but also potentially distracting the characters from the greater threat.

entries.name: Stronger Monsters
entries: The final means of making the threat greater within the realms of the opposing creatures is to use higher {@variantrule Challenge Rating|XPHB|CR} monsters than you normally would. This is often more challenging to do as a game master, for you need to find a monster that still fits the adventure and the story.

entries.name: Create Situational Challenges
entries: Another way to scale the difficulty is to design the battlefield in such a way that it hinders the party or helps the monsters. This can be highly effective—and sometimes too effective if not done carefully. Use this method sparingly, as if it is used too often and outside the bounds of the logical flow of the story and the setting, it begins to look overly punitive and even unfair.

entries.name: Difficult or Dangerous Terrain
entries: For example, the terrain of an encounter may include a substance that damages anyone on the ground. If the characters have no access to flight or other means of moving without touching the ground, they are damaged by the terrain. If all the monsters can fly or otherwise avoid the terrain without being hindered, this makes the encounter much more difficult that on an even playing field. Doing this once likely provides an interesting challenge. Doing this several encounters in a row seems punitive.

Lesser challenges, such as swamps with knee-high water, slippery glacial floors and walls, areas with high winds that make flying difficult, slime-covered surfaces that discourage running, rooms filled with poison gas, cold places that sap one's strength—are other ways to turn the terrain against the party.

Mechanically, these may cause effects such as making players move at half {@variantrule Speed|XPHB}, forcing them to make {@variantrule Saving Throw|XPHB|Saving Throws} against condition, preventing them from flying, and so on. More interesting are areas like this that the characters can find a way to use against the monsters with some effort or witty planning.

entries.name: Advantageous Terrain
entries: Intelligent monsters know to pick terrain that works with their skills and abilities. Thus, incorporeal undead like ghosts and wraiths can ambush characters in narrow corridors, where they can fade through the walls and floors to escape retaliation. {@creature Goblin Warrior|XMM|Goblin Warriors} can used ranged attacks through arrow slits, gaining {@table Cover|XPHB|Three-Quarters Cover} from the characters. Abominations can engage on slime-covered floors that are difficult terrain for everyone but themselves. Earth elementals can fight in pitch-black rooms where their {@sense Tremorsense|XPHB} is extremely effective.

You have the license to be creative in how you want to use the terrain to give your monsters the edge in battle. It makes them seem more of a threat to your players, even if it is only for a short time.

entries.name: Introduce Monsters in Waves
entries: Another way to subtly scale the difficulty is to have the monsters appear and attack in waves. These waves may come in at the top of each round or during a lull in combat. The first wave would function simply as cannon fodder, lulling the players into a false sense of security. Players who tend to use their most powerful spells and abilities early in a combat will easily defeat these weaker monsters.

Subsequent waves would hold deadlier monsters who arrive after the characters have likely unloaded with their most powerful attacks. This means the subsequent waves have a chance to survive for a few more turns than they would otherwise.

Thus, for the first wave, the characters may face a few weaker guards on duty in the castle's main hall. Later in the same combat, a squad of elite guards enters the fray, having heard the commotion. Subsequent to the elite guards engaging the character, a pair of heavily armored captains show up with their pet troll as the last line of defense.

This tactic has the effect of tempting players to exhaust their resources in early waves. Even against experienced players, you may mix in this technique to cause them to hold back their abilities early on, in case a more formidable foe could be on its way.

Finally, this technique gives you leeway to invisibly scale down your encounter when needed. If your players are having a difficult time with the first or second wave, you can reduce the monsters in the third wave, or do without further waves altogether.

data.name: Chapter 16: Dark Fantasy Rules
entries: Creating campaigns, adventure, and encounters that promote a dark fantasy feel can be difficult with the core rules of fifth edition. Those rules present and encourage campaigns where the characters are powerful and capable compared to the threats that they face. Frequent rests and resource renewal, forgiving encounter design, and a predictable encounter-rest loop: these elements make for a very stable and predictable game that but push against the essence of what makes the dark fantasy genre special.
The following suggested rules can be used to bring the joyful terror of that genre into a campaign.

entries.name: Alternative Rest System
entries: See {@variantrule Alternative Rest System|GrimHollowCG24}

entries.name: Grievous Wounds
entries: See {@variantrule Grievous Wounds|GrimHollowCG24}

entries.name: New Conditions
entries: The following new conditions are used by certain effects in the Grim Hollow rules. They follow the same rules as other conditions in the 2024 fifth edition rules.

entries.name: Great Beast Mutations
entries: The shadow of the Great Beast first rose within the {@book Bürach Empire|GrimHollowCG24|3} soon after the assassination of Emperor Leopold I. Stories of unspeakable horror began to spread throughout the realm. Although not every sighting of the Great Beast can be confirmed as real, and some consider it heresy to discuss the existence of something so dreadful, no one can deny the mark of the Great Beast's presence.

In areas where the Beast is said to have wandered, nature itself shudders in disgust and revulsion. Plant and animal life mutates, turning the flora and fauna into twisted aberrations. Even the most benign plants and animals become poisonous, physically corrupted, and aggressively hostile.

This change also affects people as well. Those who hear the Great Beast's trumpeting shriek, or explore places it has recently visited, risk changing completely and permanently into horrific creatures like gnolls or rougaugh. Such horrors further spread death and chaos. Even folk not entirely transformed suffer terrible mutations, temporarily altering their bodies and minds, if they survive an encounter with the Great Beast at all.

entries.name: Encountering the Great Beast
entries: The Great Beast is not a mere monster with a stat block and a set of limited abilities. This is because the Beast is as much an idea as a creature. Evil. Corruption. The impulse towards violence and destruction. The inevitability of annihilation. Gods and mortals alike can defeat almost anything if they are lucky, skilled, and powerful enough. But they can't outlast the sad truth that all things end. That's what the Beast represents.

The presence of the Great Beast is a forbidding backdrop for a dark fantasy campaign where the world can never truly be saved. Yet by its very nature, it's difficult to tell a satisfying story where the central characters confront the Great Beast directly. Doing so leads to almost certain death. Instead, an encounter with the Great Beast is a fight in the streets of a city where the citizens are clawing at each other whilst transmogrifying into gnolls. It's fleeing through a forest with aberrant beasts snapping at your heels, trying to escape the Great Beast's mutating aura.

A direct encounter with the Great Beast could be an epic climax to a grimdark campaign where the player characters were never expected to survive. Or it could be a prologue to a story set in the {@book Bürach Empire|GrimHollowCG24|3}, where the characters become monsters that are later encountered by the true heroes of the story. Deciding to feature an actual Great Beast encounter means you should ensure the players know the experience they're in for. This is a foe from which they must escape or perish.

While encountering the Great Beast or some other mutative effect, each character must make frequent Mutation checks. The frequency of each Mutation check is up to the GM. The closer the characters come to the Great Beast (or it to them), the more frequently the Mutation checks should be required—even as often as the start of each combat round. The farther the characters manage to escape from the Great Beast, the less frequently a Mutation check is required, possibly only the start of each scene or encounter.

Once the characters have fled far enough from the Beast, they can stop making Mutation checks entirely. Remember that the Great Beast is huge and ethereal. The GM must decide the means by which the characters can escape the Beast's presence, either by defeating groups of monsters obstructing them, accomplishing tasks like opening a city gate, or clever thinking such as the use of a {@spell Teleport|XPHB} spell or utilizing underground tunnels.

The deaths of characters in the Great Beast's presence should be spectacular, epic, and dark. Allow players to have final words as their character is horrifically eviscerated or transmogrified. If a character transforms into another creature, either by reaching level 6 of the {@area Corruption|439|x} condition or otherwise, the GM should consider allowing the player to control the resulting monster, challenging their former allies in one final battle.

entries.name: Dangers of Magic
entries: The Great Beast is not the only source of eldritch, mutative power in Etharis. Mages are hunted in {@book Castinella|GrimHollowCG24|6} because an untamed spell can yield similarly horrific consequences. The Coldfire Crisis in {@book Valika|GrimHollowCG24|7} sweeps through regions transforming the land. Fickle faerie magic in {@book Charneault|GrimHollowCG24|5} has caused its share of strife and pain. The cursed magical diseases of {@book Liesech|GrimHollowCG24|8} may manifest in something other than the Weeping Pox. While this table is titled {@area Great Beast Mutations|43f|x}, any of those other powers and afflictions could result in similar consequences for a creature.

Some rules and effects specifically call for a Mutation check. In addition, the GM can require a Mutation check whenever a character is exposed to powerful magic, curses, creatures, or other effects that put them in contact with eldritch horrors or unknowable forces.
Here are some examples of when a Mutation check could be relevant and evocative:

items: When a character fails a saving throw to avoid an effect created by a powerful creature who uses or was created by transformative magic.
When a caster loses {@status Concentration|XPHB} on a spell.
When a creature would receive a condition it already has.
When a deal with a powerful immortal being is broken.
When a creature from the Material Plane travels travels to another plane of existence.
When a shocking or devastating event occurs outside of combat.
When a character with two failed {@variantrule Death Saving Throw|XPHB|Death Saving Throws} succeeds on a {@variantrule Death Saving Throw|XPHB}.

entries: I ain't never seen it, but I know it's real. There were rumors the Great Beast was sighted near Niederhalde. Now the village is abandoned and overrun by monsters.

entries.name: Mutation Checks
entries: When a Mutation check is required, the character must make a Constitution saving throw, with a {@variantrule Difficulty Class|XPHB|DC} equal to 8 plus the character's level. The more power flowing through a creature, the more likely they are to succumb to mutation.

On a failed save, the character gains 1 {@condition Corruption|GrimHollowCG24} level and rolls {@dice 1d20} on the {@table Great Beast Mutations|GrimHollowCG24|Great Beast Mutations} table. If the result is a mutation that character already has, ignore the result and roll again.
A Mutation lasts until the character has no {@condition Corruption|GrimHollowCG24} levels, or until they finish a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}.

entries.name: Diseases and Afflictions
entries: See {@variantrule Diseases and Afflictions|GrimHollowCG24}

entries.name: Creating Legendary Foes
entries: See {@variantrule Creating Legendary Foes|GrimHollowCG24}

entries.name: Heroism and Corruption
entries: See {@variantrule Heroism and Corruption|GrimHollowCG24}
At first we saw just a pair of wolves, but soon there were dozens. I was one of the lucky few to escape.

entries.name: Using Optional Rules
entries: The rules in this chapter are offered as suggestions, not core rules that must be followed. Before implementing them, a discussion between GM and players is highly recommended to make sure everyone agrees these rules would add fun and flavor to the adventures and campaigns.

Similarly, don't be afraid to stop using them when appropriate. Better yet, groups should look to expanding, retracting, or customizing these rules to best fit the themes and play style of their game.

Once the group is accustomed to the Resolve and Beast Pool rules, the GM might decide to allow players to spend Resolve Dice to gain benefits listed for the Beast Pool, particularly when their actions align with goals of a villain. The GM might also be allowed to spend Beast Dice to gain benefits from the Resolve Pool to give adversaries a boost.

Another method of customization is by altering the abilities themselves. The Outnumbered ability from the Beast Pool, for example, can easily be adapted to summoning spells and moved over to the Resolve Pool. Inner Strength can be used to boost the healing of a fighter's Second Wind ability or to increase the level of a Cure Wounds spell. Summoning the Beast can easily be swapped out for an appearance by whatever villain the GM has set as the primary antagonist of the campaign. All of these are appropriate uses of Influence Dice.

If you find either dice pool remaining filled for an extended period of time, consider adjusting the rules to encourage more spending. For instance, a filled pool could lose one die at the start of the next session. Or, when a die would be added to a pool, the owner(s) of that pool could choose instead to remove a die from a filled pool. This creates a tug-of-war on the weave of Fate's tapestry.

The GM can also use the Beast Pool to discourage conflict within the party. When characters pit themselves against one another in a way that causes strife at the table, the GM can add dice to the Beast Pool as a reminder for everyone to cool down.

To summarize, the goal of the game should be for the players to have fun and tell memorable stories together. Where bending or even breaking the rules is necessary for that to happen, the rules should never be an impediment to fun.

data.name: Chapter 17: Curse Monsters
entries: Employing a rare substance called Shadowsteel to channel their power into spells that are irretrievably corrupt, Etharis's most cruel spellcasters have made spite into an art form. Shadowsteel curses and the creatures they spawn have been crafted specifically to spread pain and horror throughout the land, binding what might have been a goodly person into a single expression of vice so vile that their very existence causes strife, destruction, and death.

In every case, these horrific monsters are a blight upon the world until they're destroyed or, in rare circumstances, cured of their afflicting curse. Some of the creatures created by Shadowsteel curses are documented below, though it's entirely possible that this is a mere sampling of the horrors possible with this power.

entries.name: Using Curse Monsters
entries: Curse monsters come about when a victim of a Shadowsteel curse (see the {@book Grim Hollow Player's Guide|GrimHollowPG24}) fails to shake its effects. They finally succumb to the final stage of the curse, transforming into a terrible creature that loses all semblance of its former self. It is so rare for someone who's become a curse monster to be turned back into their original form that even the most studied sages just assume it's impossible. Of course, something is always impossible until it is done.

Should a character fall victim to this outcome, the newly created monster falls under the control of the Game Master while the player creates a new character. Assuming the monster isn't immediately killed by the other characters, it might escape and become an ongoing element of the campaign.

Characters too can wield the power of Shadowsteel curses, so it is possible that an NPC could fall victim to a curse as well. Again, the Game Master should weave this new monster into the ongoing campaign's story. Although the creature remembers nothing of its prior life beyond the languages it knew -- and thus shouldn't automatically have a vendetta against its tormentor -- it can and should return to plague the characters in some way later on.

Finally, these curse monsters can be used like a typical creature in a Grim Hollow campaign: as monsters to be hunted and destroyed before more innocent people are injured or killed. In the course of investigating or hunting the creature, the characters may learn more of the backstory of how it came to be. In some cases, the identity of the caster who established the Shadowsteel curse that led to the creature's existence might be learned.

In cases like this, a quest to seek justice on behalf of the creature, or even seek a way to reverse the effects of the curse, might be attempted. Such a storyline might be beyond the ability of the characters. Vengeance might be of no interest to the characters, who only want to defeat the creature that threatens the local populace. But for characters who fight evil, demand justice, or even just want to see the most wicked of individuals punished, tracing the curse back to the caster might be a satisfying arc.
Reversing a Shadowsteel Curse

The {@i Grim Hollow Player's Guide} says that only magic on the level of a {@spell Wish|XPHB} spell can reverse the culmination of a Shadowsteel curse. In effect, once the curse monster comes into existence, the victim is lost without the most powerful magic.

However, in your stories, you can always find a way to let the magic be reversed. This would likely involve many steps, including finding the caster of the original curse and convincing them or forcing them to perform a ritual. The ritual would likely require components that need to be procured, foci that need to be created from rare materials, and the presence of the curse monster in a sedated state.

Entire campaign arcs, or even campaigns, could focus on the story of the characters investigating and hunting the curse monster, learning about its past, retracing its steps, finding its instigator, and putting together the means to undo the curse.

The vile magic used to curse unfortunate victims is a blight that must be cut out with impunity. There is no return from such a taint. The only reliable cure for one afflicted by a curse is a swift and merciful death.

entries.name: Curse Monster Hooks
entries: Some potential story hooks for curse monsters are listed below, springboards for what is very likely a much more complicated situation. In all cases, it's important to note that each of these creatures represents a powerful element you can use within the context of the story of your specific game. Use these in your game as-is, or as inspiration for your own ideas.

{@creature Avatar of Slaughter|GrimHollowCG24}. At the site of a recent battle, the characters find both sides completely obliterated with no clear winner. In fact, apart from a single set of footprints, there's no evidence that anyone left the battlefield alive at all.

{@creature Bloated Gastromorph|GrimHollowCG24}. A country fair erupts into chaos when one of the contestants in a pie-eating contest suddenly transforms into a horrific monstrosity of epic proportions.
{@creature Body Snatcher|GrimHollowCG24}. What appears to be a string of serial murders has thrown the city into the grips of terror. All that remains of the victims is their skin.
{@creature Dream Whisperer|GrimHollowCG24}. The local magistrate is stricken with an affliction that robs them of their ability to speak the local dialect.

{@creature Herald of Calamity|GrimHollowCG24}. A glowing skeletal figure is seen on the roof of a local clockwork by the light of the moon. Afterward, countless accidents occur that put the local horologist in danger. They refuse to work until an exorcism has been completed successfully.
{@creature Mind Siphon|GrimHollowCG24}. Several prominent sages have gone missing from the local library. A massive insect has been spotted lurking in the shadows on the library grounds.
{@creature Plague Carrion|GrimHollowCG24}. A deadly plague has run rampant across the countryside, and the characters have been employed to find the source of the sickness.
{@creature Sightless Agony|GrimHollowCG24}. A strange gargoyle was spotted haunting the old, abandoned outpost. The characters are sent to investigate.

{@creature Verminous Abomination|GrimHollowCG24}. Hordes of rats have been plaguing the city. Strangely, it is believed they are also picking pockets and carrying away tiny inedible bits into a possible underground lair.
{@creature Weeping Willow|GrimHollowCG24}. Several local farmhands wandered into the woods and never returned. The sound of weeping has been reported coming from within those woods.

entries.name: A Note Regarding Curse Monsters
entries: As these are some of the most gruesome creatures you might use in your game, it's important to check in with your players regarding their personal tolerances before you do so. Knowing what to avoid can make the difference between a creepy good time, and a personal trigger.

entries.name: Avatar of Slaughter
entries: In any war, the most vicious combatants revel in carnage, destroying homes and lives without a second thought. Within the confines of such conflicts, a {@spell Curse of Uncontrollable Wrath|GrimHollowPG24} can be the most powerful instrument of vengeance.

Brave knights and bloodthirsty tyrants alike are reduced to a state of primal rage upon becoming an Avatar of Slaughter. Under the right circumstances, even a pacifist can fall prey to this curse if the perpetrator responsible is truly vile.

An Avatar of Slaughter appears as a daemonic minotaur with smoke fuming from its nostrils, existing only to kill any one unfortunate enough to cross its path. They often seek out friends and family first, as they unconsciously attempt to demolish their former lives.

Engaging an Avatar of Slaughter in combat is a task best left to the extremely brave or equally reckless. Born in the fires of conflict, they cannot be completely killed by it, though their affinity for violence means a defeated Avatar of Slaughter's essence seeks refuge within a weapon.

entries.name: Bloated Gastromorph
entries: Often whispered of in folk tales and legends, most frequently as a warning to children regarding the gruesome consequences awaiting those who recklessly indulge, the Bloated Gastromorph is an incarnation of gluttony and selfishness. This horrific creature has been transformed and twisted by the final stages of a {@spell Curse of Ravenous Hunger|GrimHollowPG24}.

Those afflicted become an enormous, slug-like monster possessing multiple fist-like claws, a wicked tentacular tongue, and a giant, voracious maw of gnashing teeth. Often dwelling in dank ruins of once-great castles, a Bloated Gastromorph cares about nothing but consuming every morsel of flesh it encounters, living or not. Bereft of memories of their prior lives, these creatures dwell fully in this new existence of unfettered consumption.

entries.name: Body Snatcher
entries: Body Snatchers are humanoids who have been transformed by a {@spell Curse of Damned Aging|GrimHollowPG24}. Notoriously difficult to locate, Body Snatchers frequently hide in plain sight, since they essentially resemble their host creature. It's common that when on the trail of a Body Snatcher, monster hunters and investigators find themselves trailing a step behind the creature. The only evidence that typically remains is the withered and discarded skin sack of their latest victim.
Body Snatchers possess an amorphous form, which they use to inhabit the bodies of their victims, living inside them as a parasite that consumes their being from the inside.

The Body Snatcher takes complete control of its host body, enhancing it with supernatural capabilities. The only sign that an individual has been invaded by a Body Snatcher is their erratic behavior and rapid aging—a host body can appear to age a lifetime in a matter of a few weeks.

While far from helpless, Body Snatchers prefer to avoid detection, engaging enemies only in dire circumstances or when changing hosts. They typically make every effort to complete the latter in seclusion to maintain their gruesome charade.
Jarmain was gone. All we found was his skin in a pile.

entries.name: Dream Whisperer
entries: Those learned individuals with aspirations of greatness, but who have fallen to fate's twisted ironies and been afflicted by a {@spell Curse of Lost Sentiment|GrimHollowPG24}, may emerge from such trials as a Dream Whisperer. Now a deformed, hunched creature covered in contorted arms, the Dream Whisperer uses its many hands to grasp and shred the hopes, dreams, thoughts, and memories of its victims.

Though animalistic in behavior, Dream Whisperers often reside within larger settlements, cities, and the like, where they can spread their influence and remain undetected for long periods of time. Preferring to attack at night, Dream Whisperers often sneak into unsuspecting targets' homes through windows and otherwise inaccessible openings, since their many arms allow them to scale otherwise impossible surfaces. Once inside, their arms serve a second function—to physically tear treasured memories from their victims and replace them with feelings of paranoia and doubt.

Fighting a Dream Whisperer is a dangerous task. Brave heroes have been driven mad by the Dream Whisperer's aberrant attacks. Comrades-in-arms have been compelled to turn on each other, and the weakest minds snap, leaving a drooling mess in the creature's wake. With each stolen memory, the Dream Whisperer seeks to slake its unquenchable thirst for its lost cognition.

entries.name: Herald of Calamity
entries: The tragic individuals who become Heralds of Calamity were afflicted by the Fates themselves, or so the story goes. As recipients of a {@spell Curse of Ill-Fated Fortune|GrimHollowPG24}, Heralds of Calamity bring tragedy upon all who linger in their presence.

Legends say these foul beings hunt unseen, lurking near vital locations such as a village well, bridge, or other communal site where they can inflict many casualties before survivors think to abandon the place.

When seen, Heralds of Calamity appear as incorporeal skeletal figures, though they are invisible except under the light of the moon. Unlike other curse monsters, Heralds of Calamity are not physically formidable, nor do they wield tremendous arcane power.

Many monster hunters, however, have fallen believing that a Herald of Calamity would be an easy challenge. Expert archers have misfired, fatally wounding companions. Seasoned adventurers have slipped on trivial obstacles and received debilitating injuries.
Regardless of the circumstance, all who have engaged a Herald of Calamity have the same advice: Rely on the maxim that if something can possibly go wrong, it will.
I know I'm a good shot. I don't understand how Darius was hit instead.

entries.name: Mind Siphon
entries: When an individual places too much value on their own opinions and ability, and simultaneously mistrusts all others, they risk drawing the ire of those capable of afflicting them with a {@spell Curse of Fastidious Pride|GrimHollowPG24}. The curse begins with an ever-growing sense of hubris regarding their own abilities and knowledge. As their narcissism grows, their feelings of being valued by others diminish.

Eventually the afflicted individual cannot rest, believing that tireless work and toil is the only way for them to live up to their own self-perceived greatness. The transformation into a hideous, malformed, multi-limbed abomination is coupled with an insatiable need to feed on the intelligence of others. As their victims multiply, the Mind Siphon uses their dismembered corpses to construct a macabre hive-like dwelling.

The Mind Siphon has a twisted insectoid appearance, possessing the torso of the original individual where the thorax would reside. In place of their original head is a gaping maw of fangs and tentacular tongues. From the base of the torso, the remainder of this creature resembles a chitinous abdomen with many appendages ending in hands upon which it walks. Lastly, a mouthlike protrusion at its front is topped by a dozen or more blinking eyes.
It was horrifying, even for me.

entries.name: Plague Carrion
entries: A Plague Carrion appears as a vile, decaying creature spewing pestilence across the landscape, destroying vegetation and poisoning living creatures unlucky enough to cross its path. A Plague Carrion is a sad creature that loathes its own existence and eschews combat, though such a creature defends itself when forced or cornered.

Despite being the least aggressive of all the curse monsters, individual Plague Carrions are believed to represent the greatest threat to the people of Etharis. Some even say Weeping Pox is a product of a Plague Carrion.

A {@spell Curse of Foul Blight|GrimHollowPG24} is most frequently used by hags and dark sorcerers to spite beautiful rivals out of jealousy. Those who fall victim suffer a miserable fate. If not cured quickly, the victim becomes a Plague Carrion, a deformed creature covered in gaping cysts that expel clouds of profane corruption. Venomous insects belch forth from these calloused apertures.

A Plague Carrion is compelled by its curse to constantly wander, tormented by its own corporeal decay if it lingers in the same place for too long. This cruel punishment ensures its foul blight spreads far and wide throughout the land.

entries.name: Sightless Agony
entries: Transforming into a Sightless Agony is the result of being afflicted with a {@spell Curse of Crushing Sensation|GrimHollowPG24}. Victims find themselves fleeing the company of others in search of peaceful solitude, as even the gentle sounds of nature become jarring and obtrusive to these poor souls. Such unfortunates have often left those behind who care for them, or perhaps even those who have the need for some rare skill the individual possesses.

A Sightless Agony seeks a dark, quiet place to hide itself. They often tear out their own eyes and claw away their skin to escape the agony of sensation, and the company of others becomes entirely unbearable. In such a state, they know only a boundless, white-hot rage.

When discovered, a Sightless Agony appears as a mutated gargoyle with skin flayed from its malformed figure. They attack with razor-sharp claws and a mouthful of fangs, and though a Sightless Agony possesses wings, they are loath to fly far, since the feeling of the wind is nearly unbearable. Even standing in one place, the Sightless Agony quivers and twitches as sensations from their immediate environment cause them to flinch at the unseen.

entries.name: Verminous Abomination
entries: Few demonstrate avarice like those who fall victim to a {@spell Curse of Insatiable Greed|GrimHollowPG24}. Those afflicted transform into a bloated, rat-like creature known as a Verminous Abomination. The Verminous Abomination is consumed with a desire for possessions—from beautiful riches to the discarded trash of city dwellers: the form or value of the trinket does not matter. All objects are prized by a Verminous Abomination, and for another to possess them is considered an offense.

Verminous Abominations dwell beneath large population centers, creating labyrinthine lairs to guard the hoards they amass. Adventurers who seek to clear out a complex—be it within sewers, mines, caverns, or wherever such a creature might dwell—rarely return to the surface, as fighting a Verminous Abomination in its lair is a challenge that few can overcome.

This giant, malformed beast spews swarms of wretched ratlings from its bulging stomach, while it navigates its filth-ridden lair. It emerges from the surrounding filth to ambush interlopers at opportune moments, and then fleeing when overwhelmed.

entries.name: Weeping Willow
entries: The wise have been known to provide this advice: if you hear crying in a woodland or forest that has become strangely quiet, run as quickly as you can—though it may already be too late. The cry of a Weeping Willow is difficult to ignore and has often led the unwary to an untimely demise.

A Weeping Willow is the unfortunate victim of a {@spell Curse of Conceited Obsession|GrimHollowPG24}. Once a vain or narcissistic individual, they become a twisted half-tree, half-humanoid creature warped by self-love and malicious intent.

A Weeping Willow is obsessed with its own appearance, calling for brave heroes to aid it in its distress. Upon reaching the Weeping Willow, however, heroes find only a vicious predator, a grove of unusual shimmering trees, and a very likely fate worse than death.

Combating a Weeping Willow unprepared often ends in disaster. The creature's enthralling cries and illusory beauty are difficult to resist, drawing its prey closer. The touch of a Weeping Willow can transform any creature's into a tree, bearing mirrored leaves for the vain creature to gaze into. Survivors of these encounters say that if you can resist the creature's malignant lure, it's best to leave them alone with their gardens of reflecting trees and self-admiration.
